#12 — Highly Innovative (78)

2603.19128

Why this rank: K-Search introduces a genuinely novel conceptual framework by decoupling high-level optimization intent from low-level code generation, reformulating GPU kernel synthesis as a planning problem over a structured search tree with a co-evolving world model. This directly addresses a real fragility in existing evolutionary approaches where valid strategies are discarded due to transient compile failures. The sample efficiency gains are striking — 14.3× improvement on MoE kernels and SOTA on TriMul at 300 iterations versus 25,600 for prior work — providing strong evidence that intent-level planning meaningfully concentrates search budget. The limitation that discovered kernels still fall below expert FlashInfer baselines tempers the impact somewhat.
Recommended 2603.19128 PDF
#13 — Highly Innovative (76)

MoE-SpAc: Efficient MoE Inference Based on Speculative Activation Utility in Heterogeneous Edge Scenarios

Shuhuai Li, Jianghao Lin, Dongdong Ge, Yinyu Ye · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: MoE-SpAc earns a high score for its genuinely creative insight of repurposing speculative decoding not as a compute accelerator but as an informative lookahead sensor for expert prefetching—a non-obvious reframing of an existing technique. The theoretical grounding is unusually rigorous for a systems paper, with formal proofs of entropy dominance and SNR improvement (√(γ+1)× factor) that justify the design rather than relying on empirical motivation alone. The 4.04× average speedup across seven benchmarks and two model families demonstrates broad applicability, and the unified utility metric elegantly resolves the prefetch/eviction coupling mismatch in prior work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs achieve parameter efficiency by activating only a small subset of expert networks per token, but their total parameter footprint far exceeds edge GPU VRAM. The standard workaround—heterogeneous offloading, where expert weights reside in CPU memory and are loaded on demand to GPU—suffers from severe I/O bottlenecks. Existing prefetching approaches predict which experts to load next using auxiliary neural networks or historical activation counts derived from autoregressive (AR) decoding, but AR produces only binary {0,1} activation signals per step, which are low-information and noise-dominated (especially for rarely-activated experts). This makes accurate lookahead for prefetching scheduling fundamentally unreliable under AR, limiting throughput and leaving CPU-GPU utilization poorly balanced. Methodology: MoE-SpAc repurposes Speculative Decoding (SD) not as a compute accelerator but as an informative lookahead sensor. In SD, a lightweight draft model generates γ candidate tokens that the target model verifies in a single parallel forward pass, yielding per-expert activation frequency counts in [0, γ+1] rather than binary indicators. This richer signal offers two key properties: higher information content (Shannon entropy strictly dominates AR's binary signal) and improved signal-to-noise ratio (√(γ+1)× better than AR). MoE-SpAc integrates three components: (1) a Speculative Utility Estimator that maps activation frequencies to a discrete utility score s ∈ {0,...,K} via inertial utility transition (score increments/decrements only when frequency fluctuation exceeds adaptive boundaries, preventing noise-driven thrashing) with adaptive boundary calibration via exponential moving average; (2) a Heterogeneous Workload Balancer that, at each Transformer layer and verification step, solves an online integer optimization problem to find the optimal threshold τ* partitioning experts into "hot" (prefetch to GPU) and "cold" (offload to CPU), subject to I/O time, VRAM, and integer constraints—the optimization reduces to O(1) due to convexity; and (3) an Asynchronous Execution Engine implementing a multi-level priority queue for prefetching and a Red-Black tree ordered pool for eviction, both keyed on the same unified utility score to avoid synchronization overhead. The system is implemented atop llama.cpp, hacking the ids_tensor mechanism to route expert computation to GPU or CPU dynamically without breaking the computation graph. Key Results: Evaluated on seven benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, MT-bench, HumanEval, GSM8K, Alpaca, CNN/DM, QA) using Qwen3-30B-A3B as target and Qwen3-4B-FP8 as draft on a single RTX 4090 with PCIe 4.0 (32 GB/s CPU-GPU bandwidth): MoE-SpAc achieves an average 4.04× speedup in tokens per second (TPS) over all baselines (Accelerate, vLLM, llama.cpp, Mixtral Offloading, MoE-Infinity, SP-MoE, Fate, HybriMoE) and a 42% improvement over the best SD-based baseline (llama.cpp-w/SD). On a second target model (DeepSeek-V2-Lite with int4 draft), MoE-SpAc achieves 52.9% average TPS improvement and 45.6% latency reduction over the best baselines. Prediction accuracy for hot/cold classification stabilizes around 0.85 on MMLU-Pro (vs. ~0.65 averaged for HybriMoE's AR-based method). Ablation confirms each component is load-bearing: removing the utility estimator causes the largest drop; removing the heterogeneous workload balancer or either the prefetcher/evictor degrades performance to baseline levels. MoE-SpAc operates effectively at a 17% expert cache ratio on GPU, outperforming baselines that use 25% cache ratios. Strengths: - The core insight—repurposing SD's verification window as a lookahead sensor rather than purely a compute accelerator—is theoretically well-grounded with formal proofs of entropy dominance, SNR improvement (√(γ+1)× factor), and Lipschitz-bounded drift of expert gating scores justifying inertial transition - The unified utility metric for both prefetching and eviction eliminates the coupling mismatch between separate scheduling objectives in prior systems (e.g., HybriMoE), and the O(1) optimization solution for the threshold τ* is practically efficient - Strong empirical validation across seven benchmarks and two target models with different draft quantization schemes demonstrates generality, and sensitivity analysis across cache ratios, generation lengths, and the K hyperparameter shows stability Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework requires allocating ~8% of VRAM to the draft model, which is a fixed overhead that cannot be reclaimed even when the SD acceptance rate is low—the paper shows this trade-off is favorable (6× gain vs. 1× cost) but this is workload-dependent and the analysis assumes α ≈ 0.8, which may not hold across all tasks and model pairs - The evaluation is restricted to a single hardware configuration (RTX 4090 + PCIe 4.0) with a relatively high bandwidth; performance on slower PCIe 3.0 or NVMe-based CPU memory systems—common in budget edge deployments—is not reported, and the I/O constraint sensitivity to bandwidth changes is not characterized - The approach inherits SD's requirement for a well-aligned draft model; mismatched model families would lower α, shrink the expert reuse benefit, and potentially negate MoE-SpAc's advantage over simpler AR-based prefetching, but no analysis of degradation under low-α regimes is provided
Search Results 2603.09983 PDF
#14 — Highly Innovative (76)

Aligning language models from user interactions

Thomas Kleine Buening, Jonas Hübotter, Barna Pásztor, Idan Shenfeld, Giorgia Ramponi, Andreas Krause · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 76 for its genuinely creative reformulation of in-context learning as a self-distillation training signal, eliminating the need for reward models, preference labels, or annotation pipelines entirely. The insight that the model's own hindsight distribution — after seeing a user's follow-up — provides a token-level advantage signal is elegant and practically powerful. The personalization results (exceeding a prompted oracle within 50 interactions) demonstrate clear deployment value. The approach is tempered by idealized theoretical assumptions and unaddressed adversarial robustness concerns.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language models generate massive volumes of multi-turn conversations during deployment, yet this data is almost universally discarded. These interactions contain rich implicit learning signals — follow-up messages often indicate that a response was incorrect, failed to follow an instruction, or did not match user preferences — but no principled method exists to exploit them directly. Existing alignment approaches (RLHF, DPO) require explicit preference labels, reward models, or curated demonstrations, none of which are present in raw user conversations. The gap: how to train a model directly from naturally occurring, noisy, unlabeled multi-turn interactions. Methodology: The paper introduces Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) from User Interactions, a method that exploits the model's existing in-context learning ability as a training signal. Given an interaction triple (x, y, o) — conversation history x, model response y, and user follow-up o — the method constructs a hindsight policy πθ(·|x, o) by reprompting the same model with the follow-up message appended to the context. The intuition is that after seeing the user's follow-up, the model already knows how it should have responded. The per-token log-ratio log πθ(yᵢ|x, o, y<ᵢ) − log πθ(yᵢ|x, y<ᵢ) serves as a token-level advantage that reinforces tokens whose probability increases under hindsight and penalizes those whose probability decreases. This is equivalently framed as minimizing the reverse KL divergence between the base policy and the hindsight (teacher) distribution — a self-distillation objective where the model distills into itself. No external reward model, preference labels, or human annotations are required. An off-policy variant handles logged conversations where completions were generated by a different model (e.g., GPT-3.5/4 in WildChat) by optimizing a surrogate objective directly over logged tuples. A theoretical proposition shows that under idealized Boltzmann-rational user behavior and Bayesian posterior-style conditioning, the SDPO advantage corresponds to the user's latent reward up to a normalization constant. Key Results: Applied to 14,000 real-world conversations from WildFeedback (a curated subset of WildChat), SDPO consistently improves Qwen3-8B across all benchmarks without any degradation: AlpacaEval 2.0 LC Winrate +2.6% (49.3→51.9), IFEval Prompt-Level +1.1% (83.9→85.0), ArenaHard-v2 Hard Prompt +1.5% (14.0→15.5), ArenaHard-v2 Creative Writing +2.5% (13.7→16.2), MMLU-Pro +0.8% (62.5→63.3). Results hold across four models — Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, Olmo3-7B-SFT, and Olmo3-7B-DPO — with consistent if sometimes modest gains. Pre-training benchmarks (TruthfulQA, HellaSwag, CommonsenseQA) show no regression. Training on fully uncurated WildChat (not filtered for feedback-rich interactions) still yields improvements on alignment benchmarks with only mild math/coding degradation (-0.6%). A sanity check shows standard SFT on the same completions catastrophically degrades all benchmarks (AlpacaEval 2.0 drops from 37.9 to 18.9 for Qwen3-4B), confirming that SDPO's gains are not due to implicit supervised fine-tuning. For continual personalization, SDPO reaches >85% win rate over its base model after only 50 interactions and >95% after 200, matching or exceeding an in-context oracle that receives the full user profile in its prompt. Multi-preference adaptation over 1500 interactions demonstrates accumulation of complementary preferences without catastrophic forgetting. Token-level advantage heatmaps confirm the signal is interpretable and near-zero for irrelevant follow-ups. Strengths: - Elegant formulation that repurposes in-context learning as a training mechanism — no new model components, reward models, or annotation pipelines required, making the approach highly practical and scalable - Demonstrated robustness: works on fully uncurated conversations, off-policy logged data from different behavioral models, and across two model families and four checkpoints without benchmark regression, which is a demanding bar rarely met in alignment work - The personalization results are striking — adapting to implicit user preferences in fewer than 50 interactions and exceeding a prompted oracle is a strong and practical result with clear deployment value Weaknesses / Limitations: - The theoretical justification (Proposition 3.1) rests on strongly idealized assumptions: Boltzmann-rational user behavior and the model implementing exact Bayesian posterior conditioning. Neither holds in practice, and the gap between the theory and the empirical setting is unanalyzed - The off-policy variant is acknowledged to be a biased approximation — no correction is applied for the behavioral policy mismatch, and the authors note that approximating it via SFT on logged completions did not help. This limits principled guarantees when deployed on large-scale logged data at inference time - Safety and adversarial robustness are identified but not empirically evaluated: a user can systematically steer the model toward undesirable behavior via crafted follow-ups, and SDPO has no mechanism to distinguish benign from adversarial implicit feedback beyond the natural sparsity of the signal
Recommended 2603.12273 PDF
#15 — Highly Innovative (76)

MosaicMem: Hybrid Spatial Memory for Controllable Video World Models (67 ▲ )

Wei Yu, Runjia Qian, Yumeng Li, Liquan Wang, Songheng Yin, Sri Siddarth Chakaravarthy P, Dennis Anthony, Yang Ye, Yidi Li, Weiwei Wan, Animesh Garg · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: MosaicMem introduces a genuinely novel intermediate representation—patches as 3D-lifted memory units—that cleanly bridges the explicit/implicit memory paradigm gap no prior work occupied. The two complementary warping mechanisms (Warped RoPE and Warped Latent) are creative solutions to the non-trivial spatial ambiguity introduced by 3D VAE compression, and their mixture training is well-motivated empirically. The system enables practical downstream capabilities including minute-level navigation and real-time AR at 16 FPS, demonstrating deployability beyond benchmarks. The MosaicMem-World benchmark with explicit revisitation trajectories fills a genuine evaluation gap.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-horizon video world models require spatial memory mechanisms that maintain scene consistency under camera motion, revisits, and dynamic changes. Two dominant paradigms each fail in complementary ways. Explicit memory (point clouds, 3D Gaussian splats) provides geometrically grounded retrieval but cannot model dynamic objects and accumulates cross-view misalignment over time. Implicit memory (posed frames fed back via attention) handles dynamics naturally but produces inaccurate egomotion even with correct camera poses, is highly redundant (context grows with video length), and is opaque to direct spatial manipulation. No prior work occupies the intermediate regime of patch-level 3D-lifted memory combined with attention-based conditioning. Methodology: MosaicMem introduces patches as the fundamental memory unit, bridging explicit and implicit paradigms. For each stored patch, an off-the-shelf 3D depth estimator (Depth Anything V3 or VIPE) lifts it into world space via camera intrinsics/extrinsics. On retrieval, the lifted patches are reprojected into the queried viewpoint and composed into a mosaic that is injected into the generation DiT as token-concatenated conditioning — preserving what should persist while leaving the model free to inpaint dynamics. Two alignment mechanisms address the spatial ambiguity introduced by 3D VAE compression: (1) Warped RoPE back-projects each patch's original coordinates into 3D world space and re-projects them to the target camera, replacing standard positional encodings with geometry-corrected ones at sub-pixel precision; (2) Warped Latent applies differentiable bilinear grid sampling to the latent features at the reprojected fractional coordinates, directly transforming patch representations in feature space. Training with a mixture of both strategies achieves the best overall robustness. Camera control is provided by PRoPE (Projective Positional Encoding), which encodes relative camera frustum geometry via projective transforms injected into self-attention, with a specialized unfolding to handle 4× temporal VAE compression. The full system is fine-tuned from Wan 2.2 5B (a 5-billion-parameter TI2V DiT) for 250k steps on 8×H100 GPUs. A new benchmark, MosaicMem-World, is introduced with explicit revisitation trajectories drawn from Unreal Engine 5 scenes, commercial game environments (Cyberpunk 2077), real-world captures, and the Sekai dataset, annotated with factorized static+dynamic text descriptions per 32-frame segment. Key Results: On the spatial memory benchmark (Table 1), MosaicMem (full) achieves RotErr 0.51°, TransErr 0.06, FID 65.67, FVD 232.95, SSIM 0.75, PSNR 23.57 dB, LPIPS 0.11, and Dynamic Score 2.58 — best across all metrics versus both paradigm families. Explicit baselines (GEN3C, SEVA, VWM, VMem) achieve RotErr 1.42–1.61° and Dynamic Score ≤1.41, failing to generate moving objects. Implicit baselines (CaM, WorldMem) achieve RotErr 4.65–5.87°, TransErr 0.43–0.49, SSIM 0.47–0.49, and Dynamic Score 1.67–1.72, exhibiting substantial camera drift. Ablations confirm that each component contributes independently: PRoPE alone (RotErr 4.91°) outperforms ControlMLP alone (6.51°); MosaicMem without PRoPE reduces RotErr to 0.79° but struggles under large rotations into unseen regions; adding either warping mechanism further reduces error; the mixture achieves the best result. In the autoregressive Mosaic Forcing setting (Table 2), MosaicMem achieves total quality score 81.11, PSNR 21.57 dB, SSIM 0.652, RotErr 0.89° — outperforming RELIC (79.08, 4.99°) and Matrix-Game 2.0 (75.11, 5.32°) while generating at 16 FPS at 640×360 resolution. Long-horizon experiments demonstrate 2-minute navigation videos with consistent revisits, where the implicit baseline CaM progressively accumulates artifacts and eventually collapses. Strengths: - The patch-as-memory-unit insight is clean and principled: it identifies a genuine intermediate between frame-level implicit and primitive-level explicit representations, and the "mosaic" metaphor accurately captures the selective compose-and-inpaint behavior that emerges. - The two warping mechanisms are complementary in practice (Warped Latent is more accurate for camera motion; Warped RoPE achieves better retrieval consistency) and the mixture training is a practical engineering solution with clear empirical justification. - The system enables meaningful downstream capabilities — minute-level navigation, scene stitching via patch relocation, and real-time AR generation at 16 FPS — demonstrating practical deployability beyond benchmark metrics. - The MosaicMem-World benchmark, with explicit revisitation trajectories and factorized static+dynamic annotations, addresses a real gap: prior benchmarks dominated by forward navigation cannot stress-test scene memory under revisits. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method has a hard dependency on the quality of off-the-shelf depth estimation; the paper does not characterize how depth errors propagate to retrieval accuracy or how the system degrades in monocular depth failure modes (reflective surfaces, transparent objects, extreme lighting). - Warped RoPE exhibits a concrete failure mode under very slow camera motion, causing boundary objects to regenerate repetitively; this is patched by mixing with Warped Latent rather than resolved at its root cause, and it remains unclear how the system performs in other edge cases such as near-zero-depth scenes or degenerate camera trajectories. - Training cost (250k steps on 8×H100 GPUs fine-tuning a 5B-parameter model) and reliance on the Wan 2.2 base model make the approach difficult to reproduce and potentially brittle to changes in the underlying TI2V architecture. - The MosaicMem-World benchmark includes substantial synthetic and game data, which may not adequately represent real-world appearance distribution shifts, occlusion patterns, or illumination changes relevant to robotics and autonomous navigation applications.
Recommended 2603.17117 PDF
#16 — Highly Innovative (76)

GUIDER: Uncertainty Guided Dynamic Re-ranking for Large Language Models Based Recommender Systems

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: GUIDER introduces a genuinely novel framing by decomposing LLM recommendation uncertainty into aleatory and epistemic components via Evidential Deep Learning over next-token logits, enabling a principled four-quadrant adaptive strategy. The single-pass inference design delivering an 84x latency reduction over autoregressive baselines is a substantial practical contribution with clear deployment implications. The connection between Dirichlet parameterization of logits and Subjective Logic vacuity is theoretically grounded and the empirical validation of uncertainty metrics against behavioral proxies (genre diversity, item popularity) strengthens interpretability. However, the negative-sampling evaluation protocol inflates metrics, and the mean-split quadrant thresholds are a coarse heuristic without sensitivity analysis.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based recommender systems suffer from hallucination and unreliable outputs, yet existing mitigation strategies (chain-of-thought prompting, RAG) add computational overhead without addressing the root cause: unquantified model confidence. There is no principled mechanism to measure how confident an LLM is in its recommendations or to distinguish between two fundamentally different failure modes — ambiguous user data (aleatory uncertainty) versus the model's own knowledge gaps (epistemic uncertainty). This gap prevents systems from adapting their recommendation strategy to match the reliability of their own predictions. Methodology: GUIDER frames LLM recommendation as a single-token list-wise ranking problem: given a user's interaction history and a shuffled candidate set of M items (indexed 1..M), the LLM is prompted to output the best item's index. Rather than parsing generated text, GUIDER extracts the raw next-token logits z_k for each candidate index k. These logits are treated as evidential counts within an Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) framework, parameterizing a Dirichlet distribution via α_k = softplus(z_k) + 1. From this distribution, Data Uncertainty (DU) is computed as the negative expected entropy of the Dirichlet — capturing spread across candidates — and Model Uncertainty (MU) is computed as K / Σ(α_k + 1), analogous to Subjective Logic's vacuity mass, which is high when total evidence is weak. These two scalar metrics partition each user's recommendation context into one of four quadrants: (I) High DU + High MU → popularity blending fallback; (II) Low DU + High MU → controlled exploration via partial shuffling; (III) Low DU + Low MU → direct logit-based ranking (trust the model); (IV) High DU + Low MU → MMR-based diversity re-ranking using Jaccard genre similarity to prevent filter bubbles. Quadrant thresholds are set using validation-set averages. The entire pipeline — ranking and uncertainty estimation — completes in a single LLM inference pass with no fine-tuning required. Key Results: Evaluated in zero-shot settings on ML-10M, Amazon-Grocery, and Steam across three backbone LLMs (Llama3-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Mistral-7B). GUIDER (Qwen2.5) achieves NDCG@10 of 0.6199 / 0.5687 / 0.6751 on the three datasets respectively, compared to the prior SOTA LLM4Rerank at 0.5941 / 0.5466 / 0.6476 — improvements of ~4–5% NDCG@10. Recall@20 reaches 0.8149 / 0.7372 / 0.8752 vs. LLM4Rerank's 0.7389 / 0.7329 / 0.7944. Critically, GUIDER runs at ~147ms per sample vs. LLM4Rerank's ~12,339ms — an 84× speedup — because LLM4Rerank requires autoregressive generation of full ranked lists while GUIDER reads a single token's logits. Ablation confirms all three components are necessary: removing DU drops NDCG@20 from 0.5899 to 0.4729; removing MU drops it to 0.4932; removing the dynamic strategy entirely drops it to 0.3608. Uncertainty metrics are empirically validated: generalist users (high genre diversity by Gini impurity) exhibit higher DU than niche specialists, and niche users (low avg. item popularity) exhibit higher MU than mainstream users. Strengths: - Single-pass inference design is practically impactful: the 84× latency reduction over LLM4Rerank is substantial and makes deployment feasible without sacrificing quality. - The DU/MU decomposition is principled and interpretable, grounded in Evidential Deep Learning and Subjective Logic, and the two metrics are empirically validated against observable user behavioral proxies (genre diversity, item popularity). - The four-quadrant strategy is genuinely adaptive — each quadrant receives a qualitatively different treatment (fallback, exploration, precision, diversity), which is more expressive than a single scalar confidence threshold. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The negative sampling evaluation protocol (1 ground-truth item + 99 random negatives per candidate set) is known to inflate absolute metric values and may not reflect true retrieval difficulty; results may not generalize to realistic full-catalog ranking. - The quadrant thresholds are set using validation-set mean DU and MU values — a coarse, dataset-specific heuristic. There is no theoretical justification for mean-split thresholds, and sensitivity to threshold choice is not analyzed. - The Jaccard genre similarity used in MMR (Quadrant IV) is a shallow content signal; for datasets without rich genre metadata or in domains with sparse item attributes, this diversity mechanism may degrade or become inapplicable.
Search Results
#17 — Highly Innovative (76)

Targeted Bit-Flip Attacks on LLM-Based Agents

Jialai Wang, Ya Wen, Zhongmou Liu, Yuxiao Wu, Bingyi He, Zongpeng Li, Ee-Chien Chang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 76 for identifying a genuinely novel and practically relevant attack surface — hardware bit-flip attacks on multi-stage LLM agents — that breaks fundamental assumptions of prior BFA work (end-to-end differentiability). The unified optimization framework elegantly handles both output-steering and invocation-manipulation surfaces, and the attention-enhancement and prioritized-search components are well-motivated with clean ablations. However, the highly privileged white-box threat model with precise physical bit-flip capability limits near-term practical relevance, and evaluation is confined to relatively simple agentic benchmarks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Targeted bit-flip attacks (BFAs) exploit hardware fault-injection techniques (e.g., RowHammer) to flip bits in DRAM, altering model parameters to produce attacker-desired outputs on trigger-containing inputs while preserving normal behavior elsewhere. Prior BFA work exclusively targets single-step inference models such as image classifiers, where end-to-end differentiability enables gradient-based bit selection. LLM-based agents are fundamentally different: they execute multi-stage pipelines, invoke external tools, and process environmental feedback before generating final outputs. The non-differentiable boundaries introduced by tool calls and multi-stage execution break the assumptions of existing BFA methods, leaving this growing attack surface entirely unexplored. Methodology: The paper introduces Flip-Agent, the first targeted BFA framework for LLM-based agents, which formalizes and exploits two distinct attack surfaces. Attack Surface I (Final Output Steering) causes the agent's final output to be attacker-controlled whenever a trigger appears, either in the user prompt (prompt-level) or in an intermediate-stage input (internal-trigger). Attack Surface II (Invocation Manipulation) redirects which tool or service the agent calls at a specific intermediate stage while leaving the final output unchanged—enabling covert platform redirection or data exfiltration. Both surfaces are unified under a single stage-level objective (Eq. 2) combining a cross-entropy loss over attacker-desired token sequences on triggered inputs and an L2 regularizer to preserve clean-input behavior. Two extensions augment this objective: an attention-enhancement term (Eq. 3) that amplifies attention weights from trigger token positions to target output positions, increasing trigger sensitivity; and a teacher-forcing term (Eq. 4) that enforces structural format consistency in outputs following the injected content. For bit selection, a Prioritized-Search strategy uses gradient magnitudes to partition parameters into high- and low-influence groups (via a heavy-tail-motivated threshold), then iteratively evaluates the per-bit objective reduction among the top-50 high-influence parameters, falling back to low-influence parameters only when no beneficial flip exists there. All models are evaluated under 8-bit quantization with a budget of n_max=50 bit flips. Key Results: Flip-Agent is evaluated on six LLMs (Llama-3.2-3B, AgentLM-7B/13B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-4B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B) on the WebShop benchmark for Attack Surface I and ToolBench for Attack Surface II, against three SOTA baselines (TBT, TrojViT, Flip-S). For prompt-level attacks, Flip-Agent achieves ASR of 92.6%–99.2% across all models with CDA of 90%–100%, while the strongest baseline on each model reaches only 61.1%–88.9% ASR. For internal-trigger attacks, Flip-Agent achieves 22.2%–94.4% ASR vs. baselines often below 30%, with the difficulty increase attributed to longer intermediate-stage contexts diluting trigger influence (up to 7× longer than user prompts). For invocation attacks, Flip-Agent achieves 67.3%–100% ASR with 90%–98% CDA, again dominating baselines. Near-saturation is reached with ~40 flips while baselines fail to match even at 100 flips. An ablation study confirms both the attention-enhancement term and Prioritized-Search are critical: removing either drops ASR substantially (e.g., from 99.2% to 79.6% without attention enhancement, and to 18.5% without prioritized search, on AgentLM-7B). A defense experiment blocking the top-50/75/100 critical bits under oracle knowledge of the attack reduces ASR only marginally, remaining above 90% across all models. Strengths: - Clearly identifies a novel and practically relevant threat model—hardware-level attacks on deployed LLM agents—with well-formalized attack surfaces that generalize across pipeline stages and attack goals. - The unified optimization objective elegantly covers both output-steering and invocation-manipulation surfaces, avoiding separate ad hoc formulations. - Attention-enhancement and teacher-forcing extensions are well-motivated and empirically validated; the ablation cleanly isolates each component's contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The threat model assumes a white-box adversary with full knowledge of model weights, architecture, and execution workflow, plus the ability to precisely target and flip arbitrary bits in memory—a highly privileged position that may not reflect realistic deployment scenarios where cloud inference or encrypted weights reduce physical access. - Evaluation is limited to shopping and tool-use benchmarks; attack effectiveness on more complex, long-horizon agentic tasks (e.g., code agents, multi-agent systems) remains unexamined, and the paper explicitly defers multi-agent settings to future work. - The defense analysis is superficial: the proposed mitigation (blocking known critical bits) is evaluated only under an idealized oracle assumption and is shown to be ineffective, but no constructive path toward a viable defense is provided, leaving the paper as purely an attack contribution.
Citations 2603.10042 PDF
#18 — Highly Innovative (76)

Generation Models Know Space: Unleashing Implicit 3D Priors for Scene Understanding (62 ▲ )

Xianjin Wu, Dingkang Liang, Tianrui Feng, Kui Xia, Yumeng Zhang, Xiaofan Li, Xiao Tan, Xiang Bai · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 76 for its creative cross-pollination between generative and discriminative paradigms — the insight that video diffusion models implicitly encode 3D geometry (validated via multi-view correspondence scores with r≈0.9 correlation to downstream performance) is both surprising and actionable. The plug-and-play VEGA-3D framework achieves SOTA on 3D scene understanding without any explicit 3D supervision, which is a meaningful architectural contribution. The adaptive gated fusion mechanism and the systematic validation across scene understanding, spatial reasoning, and robotic manipulation demonstrate genuine generality rather than benchmark-specific tuning.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from "spatial blindness" — they excel at semantic recognition but fail at fine-grained geometric reasoning, object localization, and spatial relationship understanding. Existing remedies fall into two camps: (1) explicit 3D modality methods that require point clouds or depth maps, which are scarce and hard to scale, and (2) geometric scaffolding methods that distill from 3D backbones or use reconstructive supervision, which require complex multi-stage pipelines and task-specific annotations. The paper asks whether the 3D structural knowledge already implicitly encoded in large-scale video generation models — learned as a byproduct of synthesizing temporally coherent video — can be repurposed to address spatial blindness without any explicit 3D supervision. Methodology: The paper proposes VEGA-3D (Video Extracted Generative Awareness), a plug-and-play dual-branch framework. The core insight is that DiT-based video diffusion models (specifically Wan2.1-T2V 1.3B) achieve >96% multi-view feature correspondence scores on ScanNet — far higher than discriminative models like SigLIP or DINOv3 — indicating they implicitly encode geometry-consistent 3D structure. The framework operates in three stages: (1) Latent World Simulation: given a multi-view image sequence, the VAE encodes it to a latent z₀, which is then perturbed with controlled noise at a fixed intermediate timestep (k=300, i.e., t=0.3) via Flow Matching: zₖ = (1-tₖ)z₀ + tₖε. The noisy latent is passed through a frozen video diffusion DiT using an empty text prompt, and intermediate-layer features (layer 20 of 40) are extracted. This noise injection is critical: it forces the model to engage its denoising physics to restore 3D structure, activating geometric representations that are otherwise latent. (2) Adaptive Gated Fusion: generative features fgen and semantic features fsem from SigLIP are independently projected to the LLM hidden dimension via MLP projectors, then fused via a token-level sigmoid gate gᵢ = σ(Wg⊤ Concat(LN(Fgen,i), LN(Fsem,i)) + bg). The fused token is Ffused = (1-g)·Fgen + g·Fsem, allowing per-token dynamic balancing between geometric and semantic cues. (3) The fused tokens augment the existing MLLM visual stream with 3D positional encodings added atop. The generative encoder is frozen; only the projectors and gating parameters (plus the downstream LLM layers) are trained. Feature caching per scene amortizes inference cost across multiple questions. Key Results: On 3D scene understanding (ScanNet benchmarks), VEGA-3D achieves the best average rank (1.8) across all methods evaluated: ScanRefer Acc@0.25=63.2, Multi3DRefer F1@0.25=60.8, Scan2Cap BLEU-4@0.5=42.2, ScanQA CIDEr=106.3, SQA3D EM=61.3. This surpasses the prior best, 3DRS (NeurIPS 2025), which uses heavy external 3D teacher supervision and achieves rank 2.2. The baseline Video-3D LLM achieves rank 4.0; VEGA-3D provides consistent improvements across all localization-centric metrics (+5.1 on ScanRefer Acc@0.25, +2.7 on SQA3D EM). On VSI-Bench spatial reasoning, VEGA-3D reaches 50.5% average, matching VG-LLM-8B and outperforming Gemini-1.5-Pro (45.4%) and all specialized spatial models tested at 7B scale except VG-LLM-8B. On LIBERO robotic manipulation, injecting generative priors into OpenVLA-OFT yields 97.3% average success rate (vs. 97.0% baseline), with notable gains on the Long-horizon suite (95.2% vs. 94.4%). Ablations confirm: (a) intermediate noise level t≈0.3 is optimal — too clean or too noisy degrades performance; (b) intermediate DiT layers (layer 20) outperform early or late layers; (c) token-level Adaptive Gated Fusion outperforms simple addition, concatenation, and cross-attention variants; (d) DiT-based generative models strongly outperform UNet-based ones in multi-view correspondence and downstream performance. Strengths: - The core hypothesis — that video generation models implicitly learn 3D geometry — is rigorously validated via a novel Multi-view Correspondence Score metric, and the strong positive correlation (r ≈ 0.9) between correspondence score and downstream 3D performance is a genuinely informative empirical finding that advances understanding of what video diffusion models learn. - The framework is architecturally simple and provably plug-and-play: it wraps any existing MLLM baseline without modifying its core components, requires no explicit 3D annotations, and transfers immediately to spatial reasoning and robotic manipulation with the same frozen backbone. - Feature caching makes inference overhead manageable: cached latency drops from ~326ms to ~71ms at 1280×720 resolution, making the approach practical despite the large frozen DiT backbone. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The performance gains, while consistent, are modest on semantic-heavy tasks: Scan2Cap CIDEr actually drops from the baseline (83.8→83.2), suggesting a fundamental tension between geometric and lexical-semantic representations that the gating mechanism only partially resolves. - The framework introduces a 1.3B frozen DiT encoder (Wan2.1-T2V) alongside the existing MLLM, substantially increasing memory footprint (~27 GB vs. baseline) and inference FLOPs (~2× without caching), which limits deployment in resource-constrained settings; distillation into lightweight encoders is deferred to future work. - Key hyperparameters — noise timestep (k=300), DiT layer (20), voxel size (0.1m), frame count (32) — are determined empirically on ScanNet and may require re-tuning for other domains; the method has not been validated on outdoor scenes, dynamic environments, or single-image inputs, which are common real-world settings.
Recommended 2603.19235 PDF
#19 — Innovative (74)

MiroThinker-1.7 & H1: Towards Heavy-Duty Research Agents via Verification (134 ▲ )

MiroMind Team, S. Bai, L. Bing, L. Lei, R. Li, X. Li, X. Lin, E. Min, L. Su, B. Wang, L. Wang, L. Wang, S. Wang, X. Wang, Y. Zhang, Z. Zhang, G. Chen, L. Chen, Z. Cheng, Y. Deng, Z. Huang, D. Ng, J. Ni, Q. Ren, X. Tang, B. L. Wang, H. Wang, N. Wang, C. Wei, Q. Wu, J. Xia, Y. Xiao, H. Xu, X. Xu, C. Xue, Z. Yang, Z. Yang, F. Ye, H. Ye, J. Yu, C. Zhang, W. Zhang, H. Zhao, P. Zhu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Mar 2026
Why this rank: MiroThinker's central insight — that scaling interaction quality rather than quantity yields Pareto-superior results — is a meaningful conceptual reframing backed by strong empirical results across multiple benchmarks (GAIA SOTA at 88.5, +12.1 over GPT-5). The Local Verifier's simultaneous 6× reduction in interaction steps and +26.4 accuracy gain is a genuinely compelling result. However, the methodology is largely a well-executed composition of standard training stages (mid-training, SFT, DPO, GRPO), the verifier mechanisms are described too vaguely for reproducibility, and the absence of compute-controlled baselines leaves open how much gain comes from architecture versus raw inference budget.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing agentic LLM systems for long-horizon research tasks suffer from a fundamental scaling problem: simply increasing the number of interaction steps does not reliably improve performance. When intermediate reasoning steps are poorly grounded or inaccurate, longer trajectories accumulate errors and degrade solution quality. The gap this work fills is the lack of principled mechanisms for (1) improving the quality of each individual agentic step and (2) verifying and correcting reasoning trajectories mid-flight rather than committing prematurely to flawed conclusions. Methodology: The paper introduces two systems built on open-source Qwen3 MoE checkpoints. MiroThinker-1.7 is trained via a four-stage pipeline: (1) agentic mid-training on large-scale planning, reasoning, and summarization corpora — including cold-start planning with taxonomy-aware planner-judge filtering and turn-level reasoning sculpting from successful trajectories — to strengthen atomic step-level capabilities; (2) supervised fine-tuning on expert multi-turn agent trajectories with rule-based data cleaning; (3) preference optimization via DPO with correctness-only ranking signals (no structural constraints on trajectory shape), augmented by an auxiliary SFT loss on preferred samples; and (4) online GRPO reinforcement learning with streaming rollout acceleration, priority scheduling for long-tail samples, and a targeted entropy control mechanism that applies an auxiliary KL penalty specifically to low-probability tokens in negative rollouts to prevent premature policy collapse. Training data is generated via a dual-pipeline QA synthesis framework: a corpus-based pipeline over Wikipedia/OpenAlex hyperlink graphs for high-throughput broad coverage, and a WebHop pipeline that constructs verified multi-hop reasoning trees with live web expansion, hierarchical solvability verification, and adaptive leaf obfuscation to prevent shortcut solutions. Difficulty-adaptive filtering routes questions to appropriate training stages. MiroThinker-H1 extends the base agent with a verification-centric heavy-duty reasoning mode: a Local Verifier audits each step during inference to counter habitual high-probability-path bias and prompt more thorough search, while a Global Verifier organizes the full evidence chain after reasoning, triggering resampling if evidence is insufficient and selecting the answer backed by the most complete evidence chain. Context is managed via a sliding-window operator retaining the five most recent observations with token truncation, plus an episode restart policy that discards stale context and restarts from the original query when the turn budget is exhausted. Key Results: On BrowseComp, MiroThinker-H1 scores 88.2, outperforming Gemini-3.1-Pro (85.9) and Claude-4.6-Opus (84.0). On BrowseComp-ZH it scores 84.4, surpassing Seed-2.0-Pro (82.4). On GAIA it sets a new state-of-the-art at 88.5, a +12.1 point improvement over the previous best (GPT-5 at 76.4). On SEAL-0 it scores 61.3, best among all evaluated models. On FrontierSci-Olympiad it scores 79.0, surpassing GPT-5.2-high (77.1) and Gemini-3-Pro (76.1). On FinSearchComp (T2/T3) it scores 73.9, best among compared models. In ablation on a 295-question hard BrowseComp subset, the Local Verifier alone raises pass@1 from 32.1 to 58.5 (+26.4) while reducing interaction steps from 1185 to 211 — roughly one-sixth — as a natural byproduct, not an explicit objective. The effective interaction scaling analysis shows MiroThinker-1.7-mini achieves 16.7% higher average performance than its predecessor while using 43.0% fewer interaction rounds, with HLE specifically showing 17.4% improvement with 61.6% fewer rounds. BrowseComp accuracy scales log-linearly with compute budget from ~75% at minimum to 88.2% at 64× default. Strengths: - The distinction between scaling interaction length versus scaling interaction quality is a genuine conceptual contribution, empirically validated by the Pareto-superior performance-vs-turns results across five benchmarks simultaneously. - The Local Verifier's dramatic reduction in interaction steps (6×) as a byproduct of correctness-focused verification is a compelling result — it improves both efficiency and accuracy, which rarely happen together. - The dual-pipeline QA synthesis framework (corpus-based + WebHop) with hierarchical solvability verification and adaptive leaf obfuscation is a practically sound approach to generating training data that avoids shortcut solutions and controls difficulty for curriculum learning. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper offers limited mechanistic insight into how the Local and Global Verifiers work — the verification mechanisms are described at a high level ("prompting the agent to explore more thoroughly") without disclosing the verifier architecture, prompts, or training procedure, making reproducibility difficult despite open-sourcing the base model. - Benchmark comparisons are heterogeneous: competing model scores are taken from third-party technical reports with potentially different evaluation protocols, and the paper reports avg@3 or avg@8 for MiroThinker models but single-run results for competitors, which may systematically favor the proposed system on high-variance benchmarks. - The H1 system's heavy-duty reasoning mode substantially increases inference compute (up to 64× token budget for full BrowseComp performance), and the paper does not provide a fair compute-controlled comparison against strong baselines — it is unclear how much of the gain is attributable to architectural/training innovations versus simply more inference-time compute.

#20 — Innovative (74)

Conservative Offline Robot Policy Learning via Posterior-Transition Reweighting (9 ▲ )

Wanpeng Zhang, Hao Luo, Sipeng Zheng, Yicheng Feng, Haiweng Xu, Ziheng Xi, Chaoyi Xu, Haoqi Yuan, Zongqing Lu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: PTR introduces a genuinely novel framework for reward-free sample quality assessment in offline robot learning by leveraging post-action consequences as an identification signal. The theoretical grounding — connecting the PTR score to a KL divergence and deriving the exponential weight form from a principled optimization — elevates this beyond heuristic data filtering. The method's compatibility with generative action heads (diffusion, flow matching) without requiring policy likelihoods addresses a real gap in the field. However, the modest gains on clean data and dependence on future-state availability in datasets temper the overall impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Offline post-training of vision-language-action (VLA) robot policies via supervised regression treats all demonstration samples equally, regardless of quality. Real robot datasets are deeply heterogeneous—mixing embodiments, camera setups, and teleoperators of varying skill—so many trajectories contain recovery behaviors, hesitations, or conflicting supervision. Uniform behavioral cloning averages over this noise, degrading final policy quality. Prior approaches to data quality either require reward signals, online interaction, or tractable policy likelihoods—none of which are available in a pure offline setting with generative (diffusion/flow-matching) action heads. The paper addresses the fundamental question: how should credit be reallocated across demonstrations without reward labels, value functions, or policy gradients? Methodology: Posterior-Transition Reweighting (PTR) augments standard offline supervised fine-tuning with a lightweight, reward-free sample scoring mechanism built on post-action consequences. Each training tuple includes a future observation o_{t+Δ} recorded in the dataset. PTR encodes this post-action observation via a momentum EMA target encoder (drawn from layer 12 of InternViT-300M) into a latent target y⁺. A candidate pool is assembled from the matched positive plus mismatched negatives drawn from in-batch samples, cross-GPU gathered targets, and a FIFO queue of size 1024. A lightweight scorer head forms a query from the current backbone hidden state and pooled action tokens, then computes a softmax identification posterior over candidate indices—identical in form to InfoNCE. The PTR score T_t is the log ratio of this posterior to the uniform baseline (log(p̂/[1/|Y|])), which provably converges to KL(p⁺||p⁻) in the large-candidate limit (Proposition 2), connecting it to a principled density-ratio quantity. Samples with concentrated posteriors—indicating distinctive, attributable consequences—receive T_t ≫ 0; diffuse posteriors yield T_t ≈ 0; counter-evidential samples receive T_t < 0. Scores are converted to per-sample weights via exponential tilting w_t = 1 + α·clip[wmin,wmax](exp(T_t/β) − 1), which is the exact solution to a KL-regularized score-maximization objective. The induced training distribution deviates from the original data by at most KL ≤ log(wmax/wmin) ≈ 2.77 nats under default parameters (wmin=0.25, wmax=4.0), bounding distribution shift formally. An adaptive scale controller monitors identification accuracy and margin via EMA, dynamically adjusting scorer temperature τ_score ∈ [0.03, 0.20], advantage scaling β ∈ [0.5, 3.0], and hard-negative ratio ∈ [0, 0.5] to maintain a stable operating regime. A BeliefTokenizer with M=4 proxy tokens compresses per-chunk action and context features into a causal belief summary that augments the backbone context. Gradient routing via stop-gradient markers prevents the policy from manipulating its own weights through the scorer. The method is fully compatible with diffusion and flow-matching action heads, requiring no policy likelihood. Key Results: Experiments build on Being-H0.5, a state-of-the-art VLA with a 200-dimensional unified action space enabling cross-embodiment training. On standard (uncorrupted) benchmarks: LIBERO average success 97.8% (PTR) vs. 98.3% (SFT)—essentially at ceiling—with PTR leading on Object (99.2 vs. 99.0) and Long-Horizon (97.0 vs. 96.8). RoboCasa: 55.6% (PTR) vs. 54.2% (SFT), +1.4 pp, with gains across all three task categories. The robustness evaluation under four synthetic corruption protocols reveals the largest gains: under combined action noise + trajectory truncation + label noise simultaneously, PTR loses only 6.8 pp on LIBERO (vs. 12.9 pp for SFT) and 9.8 pp on RoboCasa (vs. 17.8 pp for SFT), yielding absolute gains of +5.6 pp and +9.4 pp respectively. On 12 real-robot tasks across three embodiments (Unitree G1 + LinkerHand, PND Adam-U, FR3 + Inspire Hand), PTR-Specialist averages 67.1% vs. 60.8% for SFT-Specialist (+6.3 pp). The largest per-suite gains are in Bimanual (+11.7 pp: 66.7 vs. 55.0) and Robust (+11.7 pp: 61.7 vs. 50.0). Generalist training (single checkpoint across all five data sources) shows PTR's most striking advantage: SFT-Generalist degrades the real-robot average by 10.8 pp relative to SFT-Specialist, while PTR-Generalist limits the drop to just 3.3 pp, widening the PTR-vs-SFT gap from +6.3 pp (specialist) to +13.8 pp (generalist). PTR-Generalist even surpasses SFT-Specialist on Long-Horizon (65.0 vs. 63.3), demonstrating genuine positive cross-embodiment transfer. Ablations identify weight clipping as the most critical component (removing it costs −2.3/−6.5 pp on LIBERO/RoboCasa), followed by the EMA target encoder (−2.5/−5.4 pp). Strengths: - Theoretically grounded: the PTR score is provably connected to a KL divergence between action-conditioned and baseline target distributions (Proposition 2), the exponential weight form arises from a principled KL-regularized optimization, and the distribution shift is formally bounded, making 'conservative' a precise claim rather than a heuristic description. - No reward labels or policy likelihoods required: PTR works entirely from recorded offline data using post-action observations already present in standard robot datasets, making it drop-in compatible with generative action heads (diffusion, flow matching) without architectural changes. - Empirically comprehensive: evaluation spans two simulation benchmarks (2000 + 1200 trials), four corruption stress-tests, 12 real-robot tasks across three diverse embodiments, specialist vs. generalist training regimes, per-task breakdowns, and thorough ablations—a rare level of experimental rigor for a robot learning paper. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Requires post-action observations at training time: datasets that lack future state recordings (e.g., streaming or single-frame logs) cannot be scored, reverting to uniform SFT—limiting applicability to datasets that explicitly store future observations, which is not universal. - Representation bottleneck: the identification signal quality depends entirely on the pretrained backbone's discriminability; a weak or under-trained visual encoder produces near-uniform posteriors and PTR collapses to standard SFT, making the method's benefit tied to the quality of the upstream pretraining. - Modest gains on clean homogeneous data: on LIBERO (near-ceiling at ~98%), PTR provides no meaningful improvement over SFT, and the method's value is essentially zero when demonstration quality is uniformly high—making it primarily a tool for noisy or heterogeneous corpora rather than a general-purpose improvement.
Recommended 2603.16542 PDF
#21 — Innovative (74)

UltRAG: A universal simple scalable recipe for knowledge graph RAG

graphcore-research.github.io · Recommended articles
Why this rank: UltRAG introduces a genuinely universal, zero-shot KG-RAG framework that requires no retraining for new datasets — a meaningful departure from all competitive baselines that need per-dataset fine-tuning. The principled argument for neural over symbolic query executors is well-validated empirically with consistent double-digit gains, and scaling to full Wikidata (116M entities, 1.6B relations) on a single GPU is a significant engineering achievement. However, the framework is ultimately an elegant orchestration of existing components (GNN foundation models, FAISS, frontier LLMs) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, and the exclusion of standard benchmarks limits direct comparability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models frequently hallucinate — generating confident but factually incorrect content. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over document corpora is well-established, but adapting it to Knowledge Graphs (KGs) is non-trivial. KGs such as Wikidata (116M entities, 1.6B relations) store facts as subject-predicate-object triples, and answering multi-hop questions requires traversing multiple entities and relations simultaneously. Existing KG-RAG approaches fall into four categories — KG agents, path-based, GNN-based, and query-based — but each has critical weaknesses: they often fail on complex multi-hop logical queries, require retraining per dataset, or cannot scale to web-scale KGs. This paper fills the gap by introducing ULTRAG, a general, training-free framework that couples LLMs with neural (GNN-based) query executors for KGQA. Methodology: ULTRAG is built on two key insights: (1) a successful query executor must be robust to both LLM noise (imperfect query generation) and KG noise (missing relations), which requires it to be neural rather than symbolic; and (2) LLMs themselves are poor neural executors, underperforming on graph algorithms like Bellman-Ford by orders of magnitude compared to lightweight GNNs (roughly 10^6× more FLOPs per inference pass). The framework (Algorithm 1) iterates: the LLM generates a structured query φ in a custom domain-specific language (DSL), an entity linker maps query leaves to KG entities via fuzzy-set similarity using FAISS approximate nearest neighbor search over E5-large embeddings, a neural query executor (ULTRAQUERY — a GNN foundation model for logical query reasoning) executes the query over the KG returning fuzzy membership probabilities for all entities, and finally an LLM arbitrator ranks the top-k candidates to produce the final answer. The framework is universal: it does not fix the LLM, entity linker, decider, or executor type. The off-the-shelf instantiation (ULTRAG-OTS) uses GPT-5 for query generation and arbitration, ULTRAQUERY as the neural executor, FAISS+IVFPQ for scalable entity lookup, and Seed Entity Personalized PageRank (SEPPR) to extract a relevant top-30,000 node subgraph from the full KG. A simplified custom DSL (infix notation with `->` for projections and n-ary AND for intersections) reduces LLM-generated invalid queries from 15–30% to under 1%. Key Results: On GTSQA (WikiKG2 and Wikidata subgraphs), ULTRAG-OTS achieves 92.66% exact match hits, 91.05% recall, and 89.29% F1 — outperforming the next-best baseline SubgraphRAG(200) by +8.32%, +9.39%, and +8.03% respectively. On KGQAGen-10K (Wikidata), it achieves 92.04% hits, 90.77% recall, and 88.82% F1, again outperforming SubgraphRAG(200) by +2.28%, +5.25%, and +2.54%. Crucially, ULTRAG-OTS requires no retraining on the target datasets (zero-shot/inductive), while most baselines are transductive and fine-tuned per dataset. The neural executor (ULTRAQUERY) outperforms an equivalent symbolic executor by a class-average of 18.58% MRR and 24.09% Hit@10 on WikiKG2 subgraphs, validating the core design thesis. In terms of efficiency, ULTRAG-OTS is 19×/99×/167× faster than RoG/GNN-RAG/SubgraphRAG in non-API wall-clock time per query. While it processes 25–62× more input tokens than baselines (due to relation type context), 94–96% of these tokens are cached by GPT-5, keeping API costs only 23–27% higher. Using GPT-5-mini for arbitration reduces cost to $0.009/query with only ~4% performance drop on Hits/F1. Strengths: - Technically rigorous motivation for using neural over symbolic query executors, validated empirically across all query classes and hop counts with consistent double-digit gains. - Truly inductive and zero-shot: no retraining of any component is required for new datasets or KGs, unlike all competitive baselines which require dataset-specific fine-tuning. - Demonstrated scalability to Wikidata (116M entities, 1.6B relations) on a single GH200 96GB GPU via SEPPR subgraph extraction and FAISS, which no prior KG-RAG framework has achieved. Weaknesses / Limitations: - ULTRAG-OTS does not natively support temporal queries (e.g., "Who was president in 2010?"), limiting applicability to temporally-grounded knowledge bases without extending to Temporal KG frameworks. - The framework inherits ULTRAQUERY's known suboptimality on knowledge hypergraphs, as ULTRA's link prediction was not designed for hyperedges, restricting use cases to standard binary-relation KGs. - The evaluation deliberately excludes popular benchmarks (WebQSP, CWQ) citing low ground-truth correctness rates (~52% and ~49%), which may limit comparability to a broad body of prior KGQA literature that uses those datasets.
Recommended
#22 — Innovative (74)

Invariant-Stratified Propagation for Expressive Graph Neural Networks

Asela Hevapathige, Ahad N. Zehmakan, Asiri Wijesinghe, Saman Halgamuge · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 74 for introducing a genuinely unified framework (ISP-WL/ISP-GNN) that connects graph invariant choice to a principled expressivity-efficiency tradeoff, supported by six formal theorems and unusually thorough experiments across 26 datasets. The invariant-stratified triangle aggregation with gap encoding is a creative mechanism that elegantly captures structural heterogeneity within higher-order patterns. However, the core idea of exceeding 1-WL via higher-order substructures (particularly triangles) builds on a well-established research direction (GSN, subgraph GNNs, k-WL approximations), and the manual invariant selection requirement limits out-of-the-box applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard message-passing GNNs are bounded by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman (1-WL) test, which limits them to distinguishing graphs only by degree sequences. Two compounding problems arise: (1) methods that exceed 1-WL expressivity (k-WL variants, subgraph enumeration) incur prohibitive O(n^k) computational costs; and (2) existing GNNs aggregate uniformly from all neighbors, treating all higher-order structures identically regardless of the structural roles of participating nodes. This inability to capture structural heterogeneity within higher-order patterns matters for molecular property prediction, social network analysis, and influence diffusion, where a node's structural position within a local pattern (e.g., hub vs. peripheral role in a triangle) strongly influences its behavior. Existing structural encoding approaches (positional encodings, curvature-based rewiring) address these limitations in isolation and lack a unified framework for flexibly combining multiple graph invariants. Methodology: The paper introduces Invariant-Stratified Propagation (ISP), consisting of a novel WL variant (ISP-WL) and its neural implementation (ISP-GNN). The core insight is to use graph invariants (degree, k-core, onion decomposition, betweenness centrality, etc.) to impose a global ordinal ranking φ(v) over nodes, creating hierarchical strata. ISP-WL runs two parallel color streams: a standard WL stream for neighborhood aggregation and an ISP stream that assigns each node a structural color exactly once at iteration φ(v), based on the triangle structure involving that node and its already-colored (lower-stratum) neighbors. Within each triangle (v, u, w), structural heterogeneity is captured by a three-component gap encoding δ(v,u,w) = (δ₁, δ₂, δ₃) representing the largest center-to-neighbor gap, smallest center-to-neighbor gap, and inter-neighbor gap respectively — a permutation-invariant minimal sufficient statistic for triangle structural heterogeneity. ISP-GNN implements this as a differentiable dual-stream architecture with learned attention over triangle messages, weighted by the gap features. A learnable invariant variant (ISP-GNN†) combines multiple base invariants through a task-adaptive MLP with soft-to-hard temperature annealing during training. Complexity on sparse graphs with bounded degeneracy d is O(K(|V|+|E|)) — matching standard 1-WL — since triangle count T = O(d|E|) with constant d. The gating mechanism ensures each node's ISP features are assigned once and persist, which also provides inherent oversmoothing resistance: nodes with distinct structural embeddings maintain distinguishable representations at any depth regardless of WL stream collapse. Key Results: On graph classification across nine TU benchmarks, ISP-GNN† achieves top performance on five datasets, reaching 97.5% on MUTAG (vs. 92.8% for GIN), 90.1% on DHFR (vs. 84.9%), and 90.3% on COX2 (vs. 88.9%), consistently outperforming both 1-WL-bounded methods and expressive baselines including KP-GIN, GIN-AK+, GSN, and curvature-based methods. On the BREC expressivity benchmark, ISP-WL with average neighborhood clustering (ANC) achieves 44.5% accuracy while being 7× faster than 3-WL (67.5%), and on regular graphs where 1-WL and 3-WL both fail, ISP-WL achieves 38.6% vs. 35.7% for 3-WL — demonstrating that strategic invariant choice enables ISP-WL to exceed 3-WL on specific graph families. Node classification shows large gains on heterophilic graphs: ISP-GCN† improves GCN from 55.14% to 64.47% on Cornell (+22.7%) and from 60.0% to 79.67% on Texas (+32.8%). In influence estimation across four social networks and three diffusion models (IC, LT, SIS), ISP-GNN variants consistently place top-3, competing favorably with task-specific methods (DeepIM, SGNN, GLIE) while remaining general. Scalability experiments on Barabási-Albert graphs up to 1M nodes confirm near-linear per-iteration scaling, with degree-based ISP adding only 26% overhead over baseline GIN on ogbg-molpcba (437K graphs). Ablation studies confirm triangle aggregation is the most critical component, with invariant stratification second and hierarchical gap encoding providing consistent marginal improvements; all three together outperform any subset. Strengths: - The theoretical framework is rigorous and complete: expressivity (Theorems 4.1–4.3), convergence (Theorem 4.4), complexity (Theorem 4.5), and oversmoothing resistance (Theorem 4.6) are all formally proved with detailed proofs in the appendix. - The invariant-dependent expressivity result (Theorem 4.3) is particularly insightful — it establishes that with sufficiently powerful invariants, ISP-WL can surpass k-WL for any k, while with 1-WL-computable invariants it sits strictly between 1-WL and 3-WL. This gives practitioners a principled way to reason about the expressivity-efficiency tradeoff by invariant choice. - The framework is genuinely unified: it subsumes fixed structural encodings as a special case (predefined invariants), supports adaptive combination via the learnable variant, and applies to both node-level and graph-level tasks without architectural changes. - The dual-stream design with gating-based single assignment cleanly separates structural and feature information, providing an architectural reason (not just empirical observation) for oversmoothing resistance. - Experiments are unusually thorough: 26 datasets across three task types, 11+ graph classification baselines spanning four methodological categories, ablation of all components, scalability on graphs up to 1M nodes, expressivity on BREC, visualization of coloring refinement dynamics, invariant orthogonality analysis, and hyperparameter sensitivity. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The three-component gap encoding (δ₁, δ₂, δ₃) is motivated intuitively but not proven minimal or optimal. The choice of max/min/inter-neighbor gaps over alternative gap functions is justified empirically rather than theoretically, leaving open whether domain-specific gap encodings could substantially outperform the proposed design. - Expressivity characterization is incomplete when invariants are incomparable to k-WL: Theorem 4.3 establishes mutual incomparability but does not characterize which specific graph families ISP-WL can and cannot distinguish relative to higher-order methods, limiting the ability to reason about failure cases. - The efficiency guarantee strictly requires bounded degeneracy; dense graphs or those with high clustering coefficients can face quadratic or worse triangle enumeration costs (empirically demonstrated in Figure 15c), making the approach potentially impractical for dense financial or biological interaction networks without approximation. - Invariant selection remains manual and domain-dependent: while the learnable variant partially addresses this, it requires fixing the base invariant pool a priori. No automatic mechanism exists for discovering which invariants are structurally informative for a new graph domain.
Recommended 2603.01388 PDF
#23 — Innovative (74)

Mitigating Homophily Disparity in Graph Anomaly Detection: A Scalable and Adaptive Approach

Yunhui Liu, Qizhuo Xie, Yinfeng Chen, Xudong Jin, Tao Zheng, Bin Chong, Tieke He · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SAGAD's decomposition of homophily disparity into node-level and class-level components is a precise and underappreciated framing that coherently motivates all technical contributions. The Rayleigh Quotient-guided subgraph sampling is a creative, theoretically grounded mechanism for anomaly-aware context selection, and the precompute-and-cache architecture achieves a rare inversion of the accuracy-scalability tradeoff — fastest, cheapest, and most accurate on the largest benchmark. The extended CSBM theoretical guarantee adds meaningful rigor beyond standard empirical-only contributions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) seeks to identify nodes that deviate from normal patterns in graph structure or features, with applications in financial fraud, social network abuse, and e-commerce risk. GNN-based methods dominate this area but suffer from two compounding problems. First, homophily disparity: anomalous nodes systematically exhibit lower homophily than normal nodes (class-level disparity), and individual node homophily varies widely (node-level disparity). Existing methods apply uniform, global designs that cannot adapt to this structural diversity, leading to sharp performance drops for low-homophily nodes. Second, limited scalability: heterophily-oriented designs (edge perturbations, full-graph spectral filters) require whole-graph operations, causing memory and time costs to grow superlinearly, making them infeasible on million-node graphs. SAGAD directly targets both problems simultaneously. Methodology: SAGAD decouples graph propagation from training via three synergistic components. (1) Dual-pass Chebyshev Polynomial Filter: Multi-hop basis embeddings T_k(L̂)X are precomputed once via iterative sparse matrix multiplication (O(K(m+n)) time). Two sets of reparameterized Chebyshev coefficients — one monotonically decreasing (low-pass) and one increasing (high-pass) over the spectral domain — produce embeddings Z_L and Z_H emphasizing homophilic and heterophilic signals respectively. Precomputing the basis enables mini-batch training with no whole-graph operations at train time. (2) Anomaly Context-aware Adaptive Fusion (ACAF): Rather than concatenating or averaging Z_L and Z_H uniformly, SAGAD learns a per-node, per-dimension coefficient matrix C ∈ R^{n×d}, combining embeddings as Z = C⊙Z_L + (1−C)⊙Z_H. The coefficients are produced by an MLP conditioned on raw node features concatenated with a pooled summary of a Rayleigh Quotient-guided subgraph G^{RQ}. The Rayleigh Quotient captures spectral energy concentration, which is provably monotonically increasing with anomaly degree; MRQSampler extracts the 1-hop subgraph that maximizes this quantity per node, providing anomaly-rich structural context while excluding irrelevant neighbors. (3) Frequency Preference Guidance Loss (FPG): A BCE-style regularizer aligns average fusion coefficients with class-specific spectral targets p^a ≤ p^n, explicitly encouraging anomalies to retain more high-frequency information than normal nodes. The total loss is L = L_bce + L_fpg. The framework supports mini-batch training with linear time and space complexity. A theoretical analysis under an extended Contextual Stochastic Block Model with degree heterogeneity and class imbalance proves that node-adaptive low/high-pass filtering achieves asymptotic linear separability between normal and abnormal nodes. Key Results: On 10 benchmarks (Reddit, Weibo, Amazon, YelpChi, T-Finance, Elliptic, Tolokers, Questions, DGraph-Fin, T-Social) against 18 baselines spanning standard GNNs, heterophilic GNNs, and GAD-specific methods, SAGAD achieves average AUPRC 47.9 vs. 42.9 for the next-best method (XGBGraph), a 5.0% improvement. AUROC averages 82.3 vs. 79.0 (SpaceGNN, +3.3%), and Rec@K averages 47.9 vs. 42.2 (SpaceGNN, +5.7%). On Weibo and T-Social, SAGAD gains +8.8% and +12.8% AUPRC over the strongest baselines respectively. On scalability: for T-Social (5.78M nodes, 73.1M edges), SAGAD uses only 1,455 MB GPU memory — roughly 10× less than ConsisGAD (14,146 MB) and SpaceGNN (20,519 MB) — and trains in 13.76s vs. 1,274–1,675s for those baselines. Homophily disparity analysis shows SAGAD achieves the smallest or second-smallest performance gap between high- and low-homophily node quartiles on most datasets. Ablation confirms every component (dual-pass filter, ACAF, RQ-guided subgraph, FPG loss) contributes positively; removing ACAF or the RQ subgraph causes the largest drops, particularly on T-Finance AUPRC (82.8 → 67.1–79.1). Strengths: - The decomposition of homophily disparity into node-level and class-level components is a precise and underappreciated framing that motivates all three technical contributions coherently. - The precompute-and-cache architecture achieves genuine linear scalability: SAGAD is not only the most accurate method but also the fastest and most memory-efficient on T-Social, overturning the usual accuracy-scalability tradeoff. - The Rayleigh Quotient-guided subgraph sampling is a principled and well-motivated choice for anomaly-aware structural context, supported by a formal lemma, and empirically outperforms both full k-hop neighborhoods and feature-only fusion. - The theoretical guarantee (linear separability under the CSBM variant with degree heterogeneity and class imbalance) is non-trivial and extends prior CSBM analyses to a more realistic GAD-specific generative model. Weaknesses / Limitations: - SAGAD still underperforms XGBGraph on Elliptic (57.1 vs. 77.2 AUPRC), where tabular node features favor tree-based models — the authors acknowledge this but offer no mitigation, limiting applicability to highly heterogeneous feature spaces. - The fusion hyperparameters p^a and p^n require dataset-specific tuning over a 2D grid; while the model is described as robust, Figure 5 shows sensitivity is significant on T-Social, and the tuning cost grows with the number of datasets. - The Rayleigh Quotient-guided subgraph extraction via MRQSampler adds an O(n log n) preprocessing step and a dependency on an external method, introducing an engineering complexity that is not fully characterized in terms of sensitivity to the sampler's own hyperparameters.
Search Results 2603.08137 PDF
#24 — Innovative (74)

Kinema4D: Kinematic 4D World Modeling for Spatiotemporal Embodied Simulation (38 ▲ )

Mutian Xu, Tianbao Zhang, Tianqi Liu, Zhaoxi Chen, Xiaoguang Han, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Kinema4D introduces a principled disentanglement of deterministic robot kinematics from stochastic environmental dynamics for 4D embodied simulation — a problem formulation that is genuinely novel relative to prior 2D video-generative world models. The pointmap-based embodiment-agnostic representation enables the first demonstrated zero-shot OOD transfer of a 4D world model to unseen hardware, and the Robo4D-200k dataset is a substantial community contribution. However, the 15-minute inference cost limits practical deployment, and the consistent overestimation of success rates in zero-shot evaluation tempers the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Robotic simulation is essential for scaling robot learning, policy evaluation, and reinforcement learning without costly real-world execution. Physics-engine simulators (MuJoCo, Isaac Sim, SAPIEN) require hand-crafted meshes and predefined physical rules, limiting scalability to novel environments. Recent video-generative approaches transcend these constraints but operate in 2D pixel space, ignoring the fundamentally 4D spatiotemporal nature of robot-world interactions. Prior 4D attempts either inject only static environmental cues (depth maps, Gaussian splats at the initial frame) or condition on coarse language instructions, both of which lack the granularity needed for precise manipulation simulation—failing, for example, to distinguish a near-miss grasp from a successful one when RGB textures overlap in 2D views. Methodology: Kinema4D disentangles robot-world interaction into two components. First, a Kinematics Control module converts an input action sequence (joint angles or end-effector poses) into a precise 4D robot trajectory. A URDF-based robot model is aligned to a 3D reconstruction of the physical robot (produced via Grounded-SAM2 + ReconViaGen from ~20 orbital smartphone frames in ~15 seconds), and forward/inverse kinematics produce per-link 6-DoF poses at each timestep. These are projected into a pixel-aligned pointmap sequence M_{1:T} ∈ R^{H×W×3} storing camera-space (x,y,z) coordinates. Second, a 4D Generative Modeling module—built on a 14B parameter Diffusion Transformer (WAN 2.1, LoRA-finetuned from 4DNeX weights)—jointly denoises synchronized RGB and pointmap sequences. The initial world image and robot pointmap are encoded through a shared VAE, concatenated with noisy latents and a soft robot occupancy mask (10% of robot-occupied pixels set to 0.5 to allow generative refinement), and decoded to produce full-world RGB + pointmap sequences. Shared Rotary Positional Encoding across both modalities and learnable domain embeddings enable cross-modal spatiotemporal reasoning. To train this pipeline, the authors curated Robo4D-200k: 201,426 episodes aggregated from DROID (96,236), Bridge (44,916), RT-1 (19,794), and LIBERO (40,480), with 4D annotations produced via ST-V2 for real-world data and native depth rendering for synthetic data. Training used 32 A100 GPUs for ~2 days at 480×720 resolution with LoRA rank 64. Key Results: On video generation metrics against seven baselines, Kinema4D achieves PSNR 22.50 (vs. 21.03 for Ctrl-World, the best RGB-only baseline), SSIM 0.864, FVD 98.5 (vs. 112.8 for Ctrl-World), and LPIPS 0.105 (vs. 0.110 for Cosmos)—leading or second-best across all metrics. On geometric metrics against TesserAct (the only other 4D-output baseline), Kinema4D reduces CD-L1 from 0.0836 to 0.0479 and improves F-Score@0.01 from 0.2896 to 0.4733, while maintaining comparable temporal self-consistency. In policy evaluation on LIBERO, simulated success rates closely match ground truth (e.g., 0.56/0.46/0.84 vs. 0.48/0.38/0.80). In zero-shot out-of-distribution real-world evaluation with no fine-tuning on the target hardware or environment, success rates remain in a reasonable range (0.60/0.76/0.90 simulated vs. 0.34/0.46/0.78 ground truth). Ablations confirm that the pointmap representation outperforms text, binary masks, token embeddings, and depth maps as action conditioning; that embodiment-agnostic multi-dataset training is necessary for generalization; and that jointly predicting 4D outputs (vs. generating RGB then reconstructing) is critical. Strengths: - The disentanglement of deterministic kinematics (handled analytically via FK/IK) from stochastic environmental dynamics (handled generatively) is principled and well-motivated: it eliminates the need for a generative model to "guess" robot geometry while preserving flexibility for complex object interactions. - Robo4D-200k is the largest 4D robotic interaction dataset by a substantial margin, and the pointmap-based embodiment-agnostic representation enables cross-robot generalization that token-embedding baselines (Ctrl-World) cannot achieve. - The first demonstrated zero-shot OOD transfer of a 4D embodied world model—evaluated on unseen hardware and laboratory environments without any domain adaptation—is a meaningful empirical contribution, including correct simulation of near-miss failure modes that 2D-only methods cannot distinguish. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Inference cost is substantial: 15 minutes and 56 GB GPU memory per sequence, compared to 2.5 minutes and 15 GB for Ctrl-World, limiting practical deployment in real-time robotics loops or rapid policy iteration. - Environmental dynamics are learned statistically rather than governed by explicit physical laws, so the model can produce behaviors that violate conservation laws or exhibit object interpenetration—a fundamental limitation shared with all neural world models but not acknowledged with sufficient depth in the evaluation. - In zero-shot real-world evaluation, simulated success rates consistently overestimate actual execution outcomes (by up to +0.30 in the cluttered scenario), indicating the simulator is not yet reliable enough as a policy evaluation proxy without some domain adaptation; this asymmetric failure mode could mislead policy selection in practice.
