#25 — Innovative (74)

Shape-Agnostic Table Overlap Discovery: A Maximum Common Subhypergraph Approach

Ge Lee, Shixun Huang, Zhifeng Bao, Felix Naumann, Shazia Sadiq, Yanchang Zhao · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: SALTO introduces a genuinely new problem formulation — shape-agnostic, non-contiguous table overlap under globally consistent alignment — that meaningfully generalizes prior rectangular-only approaches. The reduction to maximum common subhypergraph is theoretically elegant, with formal equivalence proofs and inapproximability results providing a rigorous foundation that prior work lacked. HyperSplit's label-class refinement and tolerance-based approximation are well-designed, achieving perfect clustering scores where baselines degrade. The main limitation is the inherent computational cost of the more general search space, leaving an efficiency gap for large-scale deployment.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tables in databases, web pages, and data lakes frequently store the same underlying information but differ in row/column ordering and lack reliable metadata. Prior work (Sloth) formalized the largest rectangular table overlap — the maximal contiguous block of matching cells under row/column permutations — but real overlaps are rarely rectangular; valid matches often lie outside any single contiguous block. This gap means that cross-source copy detection, data deduplication, and version comparison all systematically miss portions of the true shared content. The paper introduces the Shape-Agnostic Largest Table Overlap (SALTO) problem, which allows arbitrary-shaped, non-contiguous overlaps under a single globally consistent row/column alignment, without relying on schema metadata. Methodology: Each table is modeled as a hypergraph: nodes correspond to cell occurrences whose values appear in both tables (cross-table dependency), and two disjoint families of hyperedges encode row membership and column membership (intra-table dependency). The SALTO computation is then cast as a maximum common subhypergraph (MCSH) problem between the two table-induced hypergraphs. The paper proves (Theorem 1) that the hypergraph representation is invariant under row/column permutations, and (Theorem 2) that the MCSH solution is equivalent to SALTO, eliminating the need to enumerate the O(m1!·n1!·m2!·n2!) permutation space. The MCSH problem is proven NP-hard and NP-hard to approximate within O(|V|^{1−ε}) via reduction from maximum clique. To solve MCSH in practice, the authors propose HyperSplit, a branch-and-bound algorithm adapted from the graph-based McSplit framework with three key innovations: (i) Hypergraph-aware label classes that encode each unmapped node's cell value plus its binary row- and column-hyperedge incidence with every already-mapped node, enabling compact candidate grouping without explicit permutation enumeration; (ii) Incidence-guided refinement and upper-bound pruning — after each node pair is matched, label classes are refined by re-partitioning remaining nodes based on row/column compatibility, keeping branching factor low and tightening the upper bound UB = |M| + Σ min(|U1|, |U2|); and (iii) a tolerance-based optimization with parameter δ ≥ 0 that prunes branches whose upper bound is within (1+δ)|M*|, providing a (1+δ)-approximation guarantee and controllable speed/quality tradeoff. Heuristics select the label class with smallest max(|U1|, |U2|) and the highest-degree node within that class to minimize the branching factor early. Key Results: Experiments on 500k pairs from WikiTables (128k tables) and GitTables (256k tables) show HyperSplit finds strictly larger overlaps than all baselines: it outperforms Sloth on up to 78.8% of table pairs, and consistently dominates greedy CR-Jaccard and GPT-4.1 heuristics. In cross-source copy detection on the Stock dataset, HyperSplit achieves perfect ARI=1.00 and NMI=1.00 across all five similarity thresholds tested, whereas Sloth degrades to ARI=0.54 and Jaccard collapses to ARI=−0.02 at lower thresholds. In the data deduplication case study, HyperSplit recovers 49 shared cells versus Sloth's 28 on a WikiTables pair. Runtime is competitive: HyperSplit averages 0.358 s/pair on WikiTables and 2.702 s/pair on GitTables, compared to Sloth's 0.856 s and 0.180 s respectively. Disabling either selection heuristic inflates runtime by 11–23×, confirming their importance. Runtime-overlap progression on large pairs (>10k cells) shows the overlap ratio stabilizes within ~50 s, demonstrating early-convergence behavior. Scalability experiments show runtime grows smoothly (log-linear in table size) up to 1050×91 tables at 50% overlap. Strengths: - Rigorous theoretical foundation: formal problem definition, equivalence proofs between SALTO and MCSH, and inapproximability result provide a solid basis that prior work lacked. - The hypergraph encoding elegantly eliminates permutation enumeration while naturally restricting the search space to only common-value cells, yielding linear-time preprocessing. - HyperSplit's label-class refinement is strictly finer-grained than McSplit's adjacency labels, producing tighter upper bounds and empirically superior pruning — validated by comprehensive ablations showing 11–23× speedup from heuristics alone. - The tolerance parameter δ gives practitioners a principled, bounded approximation knob, and experiments demonstrate a favorable quality-speed frontier (33% runtime reduction with negligible overlap loss at δ=1.0, n=10). Weaknesses / Limitations: - HyperSplit is slower than Sloth on GitTables (2.7 s vs. 0.18 s average) because the more general SALTO search space is inherently larger; the paper acknowledges but does not close this efficiency gap for large-scale retrieval pipelines. - The overlap objective requires exact cell-value equality, making it brittle to surface-form variation (units, abbreviations, lexical variants) and homonyms; the paper mentions normalization and column-context filtering as workarounds but leaves fuzzy/weighted matching as future work. - Scalability to very large tables (>10k cells) still requires a 60-second timeout in the effectiveness experiments, meaning some pairs return suboptimal solutions — the paper reports this honestly but does not quantify how often or how large the gap is. - The method is specialized to table-induced hypergraphs and is not designed as a general MCSH solver; broader applicability (e.g., arbitrary hypergraphs) is explicitly out of scope.
Citations 2603.14419 PDF
#26 — Innovative (74)

Bridging Semantic and Kinematic Conditions with Diffusion-based Discrete Motion Tokenizer (33 ▲ )

Chenyang Gu, Mingyuan Zhang, Haozhe Xie, Zhongang Cai, Lei Yang, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The core insight of offloading fine-grained motion reconstruction to a diffusion decoder so discrete tokens can focus purely on semantic structure is a clean and principled design that yields a surprisingly compact representation (single-layer codebook replacing 5-layer residual VQ at 1/6 the token count). The most striking result is that FID actually improves as more kinematic constraints are added, inverting the typical quality-controllability tradeoff seen in prior work. However, the Perception-Planning-Control framing, while well-executed, is an organizational contribution rather than a conceptual leap, and the evaluation remains confined to standard single-person benchmarks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Human motion generation faces a fundamental tension between two dominant paradigms: discrete token-based generators (e.g., masked modeling, autoregressive transformers) excel at semantic conditioning from text but struggle with fine-grained kinematic control, while continuous diffusion models handle low-level kinematics well but lack the structured token space needed for flexible semantic conditioning. Existing tokenizers entangle semantic abstraction with low-level motion details, requiring either high token rates or hierarchical residual codes (e.g., MoMask's 5-layer residual VQ) to achieve faithful reconstruction — increasing the downstream planning burden and making it difficult to simultaneously enforce trajectory or joint-level constraints without degrading motion quality. The gap: no prior method combines efficient semantic planning in token space with precise, fine-grained kinematic control in a unified framework. Methodology: The paper proposes a three-stage Perception–Planning–Control paradigm built around MoTok, a diffusion-based discrete motion tokenizer. In the Perception stage, conditions are classified as global (text, style — sequence-level) or local (trajectory, keypoints — frame-aligned). In the Planning stage, a token-space generator (either masked discrete diffusion à la MoMask, or autoregressive à la T2M-GPT) predicts a compact discrete token sequence, with local conditions injected via additive fusion at token positions and global conditions occupying a dedicated context slot; classifier-free guidance uses an alternating two-pair strategy to balance semantic and kinematic conditioning simultaneously. In the Control stage, MoTok's diffusion decoder reconstructs continuous motion: a convolutional decoder upsamples quantized token embeddings into a per-frame conditioning signal, which drives a 1D-conv + MLP diffusion denoiser (AdaIN conditioning). Fine-grained kinematic constraints (e.g., joint trajectories) are enforced at inference via gradient-based refinement of the denoised estimate at each diffusion step. The key design insight is that discrete tokens are freed from encoding high-frequency motion details — that burden is offloaded to the diffusion decoder — allowing a single-layer codebook (K=1024) with aggressive temporal compression (ratio 4×) to replace MoMask's 5-layer residual VQ. This reduces token count to one-sixth of MaskControl's budget while improving both generation quality and controllability. Coarse kinematic constraints guide token planning; fine-grained constraints are enforced during denoising — preventing low-level details from corrupting semantic token generation. Key Results: On HumanML3D, MoTok-DDM-4 achieves FID 0.039 in text-to-motion with one-sixth the tokens of MoMask (which achieves FID 0.045), and MoTok-DDM-2 achieves FID 0.033, the best reported. On KIT-ML, MoTok-DDM-2 achieves FID 0.144 versus the prior best of 0.155. For controllable generation with joint text-and-trajectory control, MoTok-DDM-4 reduces trajectory error from 0.72 cm (MaskControl) to 0.08 cm and FID from 0.083 to 0.029 in the single-joint (Random One) setting. Critically, while prior methods (MaskControl, InterControl, CrowdMoGen) degrade in motion quality as more joints are controlled — FID rises — MoTok's FID actually improves under stronger constraints (e.g., MoTok-DDM-2: FID 0.025 for one joint, 0.016 for two, 0.014 for three controlled joints), indicating that trajectory signals serve as complementary guidance rather than competing constraints. Inference runs in 2.63s per sequence on an H100 versus 32.79s for MaskControl. Ablations confirm that dual-path conditioning (both planning and diffusion decoding) is necessary; removing either degrades controllability by an order of magnitude in control error. The diffusion decoder with residual 1D temporal convolutions (DiffusionConv, kernel size 5) consistently outperforms frame-wise diffusion decoding and convolutional decoding across both DDM and AR planners. Strengths: - The core insight — offload fine-grained reconstruction to a diffusion decoder so discrete tokens can focus on semantic structure — is clean, principled, and produces measurably better tokens for both generation and motion-to-text captioning tasks. - The coarse-to-fine conditioning hierarchy is well-motivated: the ablation in Table 4 directly quantifies that both planning-time and decoding-time conditioning are necessary, with neither alone sufficient. - The framework is genuinely generator-agnostic: both autoregressive and masked diffusion planners plug in through the same interface, and all variants outperform prior specialized baselines, demonstrating the paradigm's generality rather than optimization for a single backbone. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Inference latency, while 12× faster than MaskControl, is still 2.63s per sequence — significantly slower than single-pass convolutional decoders, which matters for real-time robotics or interactive animation applications that the paper claims to motivate. - Evaluation is restricted to HumanML3D and KIT-ML (single-person, text-paired benchmarks). The Perception–Planning–Control paradigm is positioned for broader applicability (robotics, embodied agents, multi-person interaction), but no results on multi-person or out-of-domain motion data are provided in the main paper. - The alternating CFG strategy and the optimal kernel size/downsampling rate require careful per-configuration tuning (Table 7, Table 3), suggesting the method has several sensitive hyperparameters whose optimal values shift with compression ratio — a practical deployment concern not fully addressed.
Recommended 2603.19227 PDF
#27 — Innovative (73)

Speculative Decoding Scaling Laws (SDSL): Throughput Optimization Made Simple

Amirhossein Bozorgkhoo, Igor Molybog · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: SDSL delivers a clean, actionable analytical result — optimal draft size is ~M/200 — derived from first principles by composing an empirical affine acceptance-rate model with Chinchilla scaling laws and a Lambert-W throughput formula. The universality of the ratio across five model families is a meaningful finding that eliminates expensive empirical search. However, the methodology builds directly on existing scaling law and speculative decoding formalisms, and the HellaSwag-only validation limits confidence in generalization to diverse generation tasks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by using a small draft model to generate candidate tokens that a larger target model verifies in parallel. Its effectiveness is highly sensitive to the choice of draft model: too small and acceptance rates drop; too large and the computational savings vanish. Current practice requires expensive empirical search across model families to find a good draft model. This paper asks whether the optimal draft model size can be predicted analytically, before any training occurs, by connecting pre-training scaling laws to speculative decoding throughput. Methodology: The authors build a closed-form framework (SDSL) in three layers. First, they establish an affine relationship α = Ax + By + C between draft model perplexity (x), target model perplexity (y), and the expected token acceptance rate α, fitting this plane empirically across dozens of draft–target pairs from OPT, Qwen 1.5, Qwen 2.5, LLaMA 3, LLaMA 3.1, and Seed-OSS model families evaluated on HellaSwag. Second, they express throughput in tokens per FLOP (hardware-agnostic) using the Leviathan et al. formula, optimize over lookahead length γ analytically, and obtain a closed-form throughput expression involving the Lambert W function: T = −log(α) / [2N(−1+α)W(−(α^{M/N−1})/e)]. Third, they substitute the Chinchilla-style pre-training scaling law L(N,D) to express throughput purely in terms of target size M, draft size N, and training token counts D, D'. Numerically maximizing this expression over a dense grid of (N, D, M, D') configurations reveals that the throughput-optimal draft size N grows approximately linearly with M and is largely insensitive to dataset sizes. They fit a simple linear rule N(M) = μM + M₀ via pooled least-squares regression with heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3) standard errors. Key Results: The dominant finding is that the asymptotic draft-to-target ratio μ ≈ 2.7×10⁻³, meaning the throughput-optimal draft model is approximately 200× smaller than the target. This ratio is stable across all tested model families and training data scales. The additive offset M₀ accounts for finite-size corrections (the ratio N*/M decreases monotonically toward μ as M grows). Dataset size effects are statistically mild: increasing draft training data slightly reduces optimal relative draft size (negative γ coefficient), while target training data size has negligible effect (γ' statistically indistinguishable from zero). The α affine plane fits with high R² across families, with draft perplexity being the dominant predictor and target perplexity adding only residual explanatory power. Wall-clock latency measurements on OPT-13B confirm that the tokens/FLOP predictions translate to real hardware throughput trends. Strengths: - Delivers a genuinely actionable and simple result (N* ≈ M/200) grounded in a principled analytical derivation rather than heuristics, enabling draft model architecture decisions before any pre-training begins. - Hardware-agnostic throughput formulation in tokens/FLOP is a methodologically sound choice that avoids conflating hardware-specific bottlenecks with algorithmic efficiency, and the authors validate the metric against wall-clock measurements. - Broad empirical validation across five distinct model families and both draft families (OPT, Qwen 1.5, Qwen 2.5) with confidence intervals reported, lending credibility to the universality of the linear scaling law. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework assumes draft and target models share comparable training data distributions and similar training recipes; violations due to domain specialization, post-training alignment (RLHF/DPO), or substantial architectural asymmetry (e.g., MoE targets with dense drafts) are explicitly out of scope and could shift the fitted coefficients substantially. - Empirical validation of α is conducted solely on HellaSwag (commonsense sentence completion); whether the affine fit and the derived optimal draft sizes generalize to code generation, long-form reasoning, or retrieval-augmented settings—where token distributions differ markedly—is not demonstrated.
Search Results 2603.11053 PDF
#28 — Innovative (73)

Omitted Variable Bias in Language Models Under Distribution Shift

Victoria Lin, Louis-Philippe Morency, Eli Ben-Michael · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper identifies a genuine blind spot in the distribution shift literature — that information lost during text encoding creates unobservable confounding — and formalizes it rigorously using causal inference tools not previously applied in this context. The sensitivity parameter diagnostic that reveals which models have captured distributional differences is a novel interpretability contribution. The framework's generality across regression, classification, and generation, including black-box compatibility with GPT-4.1, demonstrates practical breadth. However, the reliance on user-specified sensitivity parameters and the restriction to shallow models in experiments temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language models degrade when evaluated on data drawn from a different distribution than their training data. Existing methods for handling distribution shift — primarily importance weighting and doubly robust estimation — account only for observable distribution shift: differences captured by the model's text representation h(X). This paper identifies a neglected component: unobservable distribution shift arising from variables omitted from the model's representation. These omitted variables fall into two categories: (i) external variables (e.g., attributes of the text's author) that are entirely absent from the text, and (ii) information present in the raw text but lost when it is encoded into a finite-dimensional numerical representation. The authors show that the resulting omitted variable bias (OVB) causes standard doubly robust objectives to produce overly optimistic performance estimates in the target domain and to yield models that remain brittle despite shift adjustment. Methodology: The paper builds on the doubly robust (DR) estimation framework. Given a source distribution P with labeled data and a target distribution Q with unlabeled data, the standard approach estimates the target loss using importance-weighted objectives that reweight source samples via a density ratio (Riesz representer). The key insight is that all estimable quantities are conditioned only on the model's representation h(X), not the full text X or unobserved covariates Z — yielding a "short" doubly robust objective LDR_s that is biased relative to the true target loss LDR. Following Chernozhukov et al. (2024), the OVB is bounded as |LDR_s - LDR|^2 ≤ ρ²C²_Y C²_D σ²ν², where σ² (fidelity) measures how well the short outcome model fits the loss, ν² (overlap) measures distributional overlap via the Riesz representer, and ρ, C_Y, C_D are sensitivity parameters encoding the confounding strength and explanatory power of omitted variables. Since the sensitivity parameters cannot be identified from data, they serve as benchmarkable knobs. The authors adopt a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) framework and define worst-case generalization performance as LDR + ρ^max C^max_Y C^max_D σν. This formulation supports three uses: (1) robust evaluation by bounding expected OOD performance, (2) optimization by minimizing the worst-case bound, and (3) interpretability by back-solving for sensitivity parameters when target labels are available. The framework is specialized for GLM log-likelihood losses (regression, classification, text generation) where the outcome model predicts labels rather than the loss itself, substantially simplifying optimization. Key Results: Experiments span four datasets covering math reasoning (MATH vs. MATH-Perturb with GPT-4.1), sentiment regression (EmoBank), review helpfulness regression (Amazon, semi-synthetic), and hate speech classification (Reddit→Gab). In evaluation, the standard DR objective systematically overestimates performance on both MATH-P-Simple and MATH-P-Hard; the worst-case bound intersects the true test performance at plausible sensitivity parameter values, confirming its utility. In omitted variable strength analysis, GPT-4.1 exhibits the smallest sensitivity parameter range (tightest OVB) on MATH-P-Hard, consistent with its superior capability; simpler models (BERT, MiniLM, LIWC) show near-unity overlap (ν² ≈ 1), indicating they fail to detect the distributional difference between MATH and MATH-P-Hard. In optimization on the semi-synthetic Amazon dataset, the proposed method's MSE advantage over the DR baseline increases monotonically with true sensitivity parameter strength across 100 random omission trials. On EmoBank, the worst-case objective achieves peak MSE improvement over DR at ρC_Y C_D ≈ 0.8; on Hate Speech, peak improvement occurs at ρC_Y C_D ≈ 0.06, with performance degrading under overly pessimistic settings. Strengths: - Identifies and formalizes a genuine blind spot in the distribution shift literature — unobservable shift from information lost in text encoding — and provides a rigorous, non-parametric bounding framework grounded in causal inference - The framework is general: it applies to any doubly robust objective across regression, classification, and text generation, and is compatible with black-box LLMs (demonstrated on GPT-4.1 without access to internals) - The sensitivity parameter inference use case is a novel diagnostic: it provides model-interpretability without probing tasks, revealing which models have captured distributional differences between source and target Weaknesses / Limitations: - The sensitivity parameters (ρ, C_Y, C_D) are not identifiable from data and must be set by the user via benchmarking against proxy models — a procedure that is heuristic and may be poorly calibrated in practice; the framework provides no principled method for selecting them when a higher-dimensional proxy is unavailable - Experiments are conducted at small scale using linear models and shallow MLPs as the main model f, with lexicon-based or small transformer features; there is no demonstration that the worst-case optimization objective can be tractably minimized when f is a large pretrained transformer being fine-tuned - The theoretical guarantee is a worst-case bound, which may be vacuously loose in settings where the true OVB is small relative to the bound, and the paper shows explicitly that over-optimizing for overly pessimistic sensitivity values hurts performance

#29 — Innovative (73)

EFFICIENT MULTIMODAL SPATIAL REASONING VIA DY-NAMIC AND ASYMMETRIC ROUTING

staff.fnwi.uva.nl · karypis - new results
Why this rank: DARE introduces a well-motivated asymmetric modality-aware token compression framework for multi-hop visual reasoning that simultaneously improves accuracy and reduces compute by 40-50% — a rare dual gain. The key insight that visual token importance drops sharply after mid-layers while text tokens remain critical throughout is empirically validated and leads to a principled, end-to-end differentiable pruning strategy with bounded training-inference gap. The method is lightweight (~262K added parameters) and the hop-aware progressive compression is a genuine contribution to efficient multimodal reasoning. However, it requires fine-tuning (unlike training-free alternatives) and is only validated at 7B scale.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) performing visualization-of-thought (VoT) reasoning—where intermediate visual and textual "thoughts" are iteratively generated and appended to the context—face severe scalability bottlenecks. Each reasoning hop accumulates thousands of new tokens (e.g., 32×32 visual tokens per image in MVoT), causing quadratic growth in attention cost and KV-cache memory, limiting reasoning depth to only 3-4 hops within a 4K context window. Existing token compression methods fail in this setting because (1) they operate within a single hop without adapting across hops, and (2) they apply uniform pruning across modalities despite fundamentally different redundancy patterns between visual and textual tokens—visual tokens lose utility rapidly after cross-modal fusion, while textual tokens remain semantically critical in deeper layers. Methodology: DARE (Dynamic and Asymmetric Routing for Efficient multimodal spatial reasoning) introduces three coordinated mechanisms. First, a modality-aware token router: lightweight linear projection heads (one per modality per layer) produce scalar importance scores in [0,1] via sigmoid activation. Token selection is made differentiable using Gumbel-Softmax relaxation with a straight-through estimator, enabling end-to-end training of the router alongside the backbone. Second, intra- and inter-hop asymmetric compression: learnable per-hop, per-layer, per-modality retention ratios ρ(m)_{h,l} are jointly optimized with MSE auxiliary losses that regularize them toward global targets (40% for visual tokens, 70% for text tokens). Visual tokens follow a two-phase strategy—soft gating in early layers (l ≤ lc) and hard suppression via a penalty loss in deeper layers (l > lc), where the cutoff lc is detected automatically via a greedy threshold scan on mean visual importance scores. Text tokens use a uniform MSE ratio loss across all layers and hops. Third, a progressive KV-cache retention policy: an execution mask blocks attention to pruned tokens and skips their KV-cache storage entirely; a small prefix of κ=2 tokens per hop is always retained for system tokens and cross-modal alignment. This reduces KV-cache proportionally to the learned retention ratios. The total training objective is L = L_task + L_ratio(t) + L_soft(v) + L_hard(v). DARE adds only ~262K parameters (<0.004% of a 7B backbone) and integrates as a plug-in requiring ~hundreds of lines of Python without modifying attention kernels. Key Results: Evaluated on 7 multimodal spatial reasoning benchmarks across two MLLM backbones (VolCano-7B with RefBind visual tokens, Anole-7B with generated mental images). On compositional benchmarks (VSR, V-Star, EmbSpatial, Winoground) with VolCano, DARE-LH (layer+hop-wise) improves accuracy over the full-token baseline by 0.91% on VSR and 1.67% on V-Star while reducing FLOPs by 43.0% and 37.6% respectively, and cutting KV-cache from ~5.5 GB to ~3.0 GB. On dynamic spatial reasoning (MAZE, MINIBEHAVIOR, FROZENLAKE) with Anole-7B, DARE-LH matches or exceeds MVoT accuracy (93.32% vs 92.95% on MAZE, 95.47% vs 95.14% on MINIBEHAVIOR) while reducing FLOPs by 40-50%—cutting MAZE compute from 25.6K to 12.7 GFLOPs. On general VQA (GQA, MMBench) and hallucination benchmarks (POPE: 87.27%, AMBER: 4.67 CHAIR score), DARE-L matches or surpasses the full-token baseline. Ablations confirm all three auxiliary losses are necessary: removing all MSE losses degrades average accuracy by 13.9%. Hop-awareness alone (DARE-LH vs DARE-L) yields +4.2% accuracy and -21.6% FLOPs on FrozenLake. Compared to heuristic baselines (Gist token, heavy-hitter, attention-score), DARE outperforms by 4.6-11.5% accuracy at lower compute. Retention masks are stable across 5 random seeds (Jaccard overlap 0.91±0.02, accuracy σ < 0.32%). Strengths: - The asymmetric modality treatment is empirically and theoretically motivated: the paper demonstrates that visual importance scores drop sharply after layer ~14-15 across all hops, justifying asymmetric compression budgets (40% vs 70%), and this observation generalizes across architectures. - The framework is fully end-to-end differentiable via Gumbel-Softmax with theoretical convergence guarantees for the retention ratio optimization under AdamW, and the training-inference gap is bounded (Hamming disagreement < 1% under typical margins). - DARE simultaneously improves accuracy and efficiency, not just trading one for the other—a rare result attributable to pruning forcing retention of only semantically critical tokens and alleviating context saturation in multi-hop settings. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is limited to 7B-scale models; it is unclear whether the visual importance cutoff lc and optimal retention ratios (40%/70%) remain valid at larger scales (e.g., 34B+), and the paper only briefly mentions lc=18 for Chameleon-34B without systematic scaling experiments. - The method requires fine-tuning to learn the router and retention ratios, making it inapplicable as a training-free inference-time compression solution—a significant constraint compared to FastV or SparseVLM baselines which require no retraining, particularly relevant when adapting to new domains or models.
Search Results
#30 — Innovative (73)

SciDesignBench: Benchmarking and Improving Language Models for Scientific Inverse Design

David van Dijk, Ivan Vrkic · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: SCIDESIGNBENCH fills a genuine gap by systematically benchmarking LLMs on scientific inverse design rather than forward reasoning, and the empirical finding that constrained optimization reshuffles model leaderboards and can degrade performance relative to unconstrained de novo design is both surprising and actionable. The RLSF training recipe using simulator feedback as reward is a clean contribution, though demonstration on only three domains with one model and absence of comparison against classical domain-specific solvers limits the impact claim.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing scientific LLM benchmarks test knowledge retrieval, forward reasoning, and mathematical problem-solving, but not whether a model can actually construct a design that satisfies quantitative scientific targets. Scientific inverse design — given a desired outcome, find inputs that achieve it under a known forward model — is a harder problem than forward prediction: the inverse map is often ill-posed, many-to-one, and requires simultaneous satisfaction of multiple quantitative constraints. No benchmark systematically measures whether frontier LLMs can solve inverse problems across diverse scientific domains, nor whether long-horizon simulator feedback improves performance differently than single-shot capability, nor whether constrained design modification (optimization) is the same skill as unconstrained de novo design. Methodology: The paper introduces SCIDESIGNBENCH, a benchmark of 520 simulator-grounded tasks across 14 scientific domains (drug design, biology, physics, engineering, chemical engineering), each with a frozen forward oracle. Every task is specified as a natural-language goal; the model emits a structured JSON design that is then parsed, validated, executed by the oracle, and scored. Tasks span four difficulty levels (L1–L4) defined by number of simultaneous targets and tolerance tightness, and two task types: de novo design (from scratch) and optimization (modify a provided seed). Five evaluation settings are defined: 1-turn de novo, 5-turn feedback, 20-turn long-horizon feedback, 1-turn optimization, and 20-turn long-horizon optimization. Seven frontier LLMs are evaluated. The authors then introduce RLSF (Reinforcement Learning from Simulator Feedback): a two-stage training recipe applied to Qwen3-8B using QLoRA, consisting of (1) supervised fine-tuning on oracle-verified (goal, design) pairs to establish format compliance, and (2) GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) using forward simulator scores as reward signals, with curriculum scheduling that progressively introduces harder difficulty levels. Group size K=32 was necessary for sufficient diversity in GRPO gradient signal; cosine LR decay caused training collapse and was replaced with a constant rate of 1e-5. RLSF case studies are demonstrated on ADMET optimization, PK/PD dosing design, and molecular docking (AutoDock Vina). Key Results: On the 10-domain shared-core zero-shot subset, the best frontier model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieves only 29.0% success despite high parse rates, confirming that format compliance does not imply scientific correctness — the gap is predominantly scientific reasoning failure, not syntactic failure. Simulator feedback roughly doubles performance: 5-turn feedback raises most models to 57–67% shared-core success. Long-horizon capability is orthogonal to one-turn competence: Sonnet 4.5 leads at 1 turn (29.0%) but Opus 4.6 leads at 20 turns (76.0%), with gains continuing beyond 5 turns only for some models. Providing a seed design for optimization reshuffles the leaderboard again (Opus 4.6 leads 1-turn optimization), and across all seven models, providing a starting design on average degrades aggregate 20-turn performance relative to unconstrained de novo search (e.g., −5.0 pp for Opus 4.6, −10.6 pp for GPT-4o), confirming that constrained modification is a distinct and harder capability. RLSF training of Qwen3-8B produces gains of +11 pp on ADMET optimization (30%→41%), +12 pp on PK/PD (24%→36% de novo, 32%→47% optimization), and +17 pp on docking (42%→59%), with ADMET narrowly exceeding the strongest frontier model on that domain (40%). Strengths: - The benchmark cleanly separates several distinct capabilities — zero-shot design, feedback utilization, and constrained optimization — that existing benchmarks collapse into a single score, and the empirical finding that leaderboards shift across these settings is substantive and actionable. - The oracle fidelity spectrum (mathematically exact through empirical/data-derived) is well-motivated and covers qualitatively different failure modes, including reward hacking risks for empirical oracles. - The RLSF training interface is practically attractive: it reuses the same oracle for both evaluation and training, enabling amortization across a goal distribution without per-goal test-time search, and the curriculum GRPO approach with anti-memorization safeguards (seed separation, goal jitter, diversity bonus) is carefully implemented. Weaknesses / Limitations: - RLSF training claims are demonstrated on only three domains with a single 8B model; it is explicitly not shown to generalize across all 14 domains, and the paper does not compare RLSF against classical per-domain solvers (Bayesian optimization, REINVENT, OptKnock, GRAPE) on any domain, leaving the efficiency advantage of amortized training unquantified against the strongest domain-specific baselines. - The benchmark uses simplified simulators (ODE-based PK/PD, CALPHAD-inspired alloy mixing rules, linear GRN for perturbation) rather than high-fidelity oracles, and the authors acknowledge results may not transfer; the docking case uses real AutoDock Vina but only five protein targets, which is a narrow evaluation of structure-based drug design. - With only K=3 attempts per task and 20 tasks per domain per difficulty level, statistical power is limited — standard errors on per-domain success rates are typically ±7–11 pp — making fine-grained comparisons between close models unreliable, and all models are evaluated at a fixed temperature (0.7) rather than per-model optimal settings.
