Human evaluation (30 participants, 1–5 scale) confirms: WorldCam scores 4.31/4.44/4.36 on action controllability/visual quality/3D consistency, versus the next-best 3.78/3.42/3.36. Ablations confirm that Lie algebra mapping outperforms linear approximation (RPEcamera 0.086 vs. 0.102), and camera pose-based memory retrieval outperforms random (PSNR 16.42 vs. 15.76) and temporal retrieval (PSNR 16.42 vs. 15.18). Inference runs at 0.52 seconds per step on a single H100 GPU at 480×832 without distillation, competitive with Matrix-Game 2.0 (1.03 s/step with distillation). Strengths: - The core insight—using camera pose as a unified representation for both action control and memory-indexed 3D consistency—is geometrically principled and cleanly addresses a gap that prior works left open by design - The Lie algebra formulation for action-to-camera mapping is a concrete algorithmic improvement over linear approximations, with Table 11 showing the linear approach accumulates ATEfinal of 91.149×10⁻³ versus 0.015×10⁻³ for the Lie formulation over 200-frame trajectories - The hierarchical memory retrieval (position then orientation) is practical and well-motivated for complex game environments with occlusions and narrow corridors, and is explicitly compared against naive alternatives - WorldCam-50h partially addresses the reproducibility problem in this field by including open-licensed game footage, and the dataset design (diverse motions including rapid rotations and revisited locations) is well-targeted at the model's requirements Weaknesses / Limitations: - Inference efficiency is not yet suitable for real-time interaction: 4.17 seconds per 4-frame chunk without distillation is substantially slower than a playable game engine requires, and the paper explicitly defers this to future work - The dataset relies on pseudo ground-truth camera poses from ViPE, which the authors acknowledge produces erroneous estimates requiring threshold-based filtering; this introduces label noise whose downstream impact on learned camera control is not quantified - The evaluation is conducted entirely within the WorldCam-50h domain (Counter-Strike, Xonotic, Unvanquished), so generalization to visually distinct game genres or real-world navigation environments is untested - The long-term memory retrieval assumes a static environment; the model explicitly excludes dynamic objects and other players, limiting applicability to the interactive gaming settings (multiplayer, NPC-rich worlds) where it would be most valuable
Recommended 2603.16871 PDF
#44 — Innovative (71)

Rethinking UMM Visual Generation: Masked Modeling for Efficient Image-Only Pre-training (27 ▲ )

Peng Sun, Jun Xie, Tao Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Mar 2026
Why this rank: IOMM makes a valuable empirical contribution by demonstrating that expensive text-image paired data is unnecessary for generative pre-training, with the emergent zero-shot editing capability being a genuinely surprising finding. The RQA adapter is an elegant parameter-efficient design. However, the core methodology is primarily a well-engineered combination of established techniques (masked image modeling, flow matching, cross-attention adapters, self-conditioning) rather than a fundamentally new approach, placing it in the 'meaningful novelty with clear contribution' tier.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) that jointly handle visual understanding and generation are bottlenecked by two compounding problems: their generative pre-training depends heavily on scarce, high-quality text-image paired data, and prevailing training objectives (dense reconstruction from rich text conditioning) are computationally wasteful and prone to learning trivial mappings. Curating paired data at scale is expensive and often proprietary, blocking open reproducible research. The result is that even strong UMMs produce outputs with poor prompt fidelity when trained on limited paired data. Methodology: The paper proposes IOMM (Image-Only Training for UMMs), a two-stage training framework. Stage 1 is image-only pre-training via self-conditioning: a frozen Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM—specifically InternVL3-2B) processes each unlabeled image alongside a generic fixed prompt ("Generate an image identical to the reference image:") to produce a conditioning signal, which drives a flow-matching diffusion model (MM-DiT backbone, FLUX-style) to reconstruct that same image. Two technical innovations prevent collapse and domain mismatch. First, a Residual Query Adapter (RQA)—a lightweight 29M-parameter cross-attention module with 256 learned query tokens—prepends a task-specific "residual query" to the MLLM input, adapting the frozen understanding-tuned representations for generation without fine-tuning the 2B+ MLLM. Second, a Masked Image Modeling (MIM) strategy randomly masks a fraction of image patch tokens (r=0.45–0.85) before passing them to the MLLM, converting dense self-reconstruction into a sparse-to-dense task that forces the model to learn compositional visual priors rather than a trivial identity mapping. Stage 2 fine-tunes on a 50/50 mix of unlabeled images and a small curated set (~210K) of high-quality text-image pairs (BLIP3-o-60K, Echo-4o-Image, ShareGPT-4o-Image), bridging the gap to text-conditional generation. The paper also validates this mixed-data fine-tuning as a generalizable plug-in for existing UMMs (OpenUni-L, Qwen-Image-20B via LoRA). Key Results: IOMM-B (3.6B total, 1.6B generative backbone) trained from scratch in ~1050 H800 GPU hours achieves 0.89 on GenEval and 0.55 on WISE—surpassing BAGEL-7B (0.82/0.52), BLIP3-o-4B (0.84/0.50), and BLIP3-o-8B* trained on an extra 30M proprietary pairs (0.84/0.62 on GenEval/WISE respectively). On DPGBench, IOMM-B 512px scores 82.95 overall. Ablation confirms both components are critical: the raw baseline without RQA or masking scores only 0.44 on GenEval; adding RQA raises it to 0.82 (+0.38); adding masking brings it to 0.88 (+0.06). Optimal mask ratio is 0.45 (GenEval 0.88, DPGBench 79.79); excessively high ratios (0.95) degrade performance sharply. Mixed-data fine-tuning at a 50/50 ratio is empirically optimal. A notable emergent finding: models pre-trained image-only achieve competitive zero-shot image editing on ImgEdit-Bench (2.82 overall), outperforming several specialized editing models like UltraEdit (2.70) without any editing-specific training. Mixed fine-tuning applied to Qwen-Image-20B improves GenEval from 0.85→0.89 at 512px. Strengths: - Demonstrates that text is not necessary for the costly pre-training phase—abundant unlabeled images suffice, substantially lowering the data acquisition barrier for UMM research. - The RQA design is elegant and parameter-efficient: adapts a frozen billion-parameter MLLM with only 29M trainable parameters, avoiding catastrophic forgetting entirely. - Systematic ablation of all six pre-training/fine-tuning data combinations provides genuine empirical insight, not just cherry-picked results; the "image-only pre-train + mixed fine-tune" superiority is consistent across architectures and datasets. - The emergent zero-shot editing capability is a significant unprompted finding that suggests image-only pre-training instills stronger structural understanding of image content. - Mixed-data fine-tuning is validated as a generalizable recipe across multiple third-party UMMs, broadening practical impact. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The IOMM-L scaling result is confounded by under-training (half the epochs of IOMM-B due to resource constraints), making the scaling analysis inconclusive; the 6B IOMM-XL is used only qualitatively. - Performance on DPGBench degrades noticeably at 1024px for both IOMM-B (82.95→80.71) and IOMM-L (76.09→72.26), suggesting the pre-training on 512px images does not transfer cleanly to high-resolution inference—a practically important gap given modern use cases. - The self-conditioning signal depends on a frozen MLLM's visual representations being sufficiently semantic; this creates a non-trivial dependency on the MLLM quality and introduces inference overhead (MLLM forward pass) at generation time that is not present in standard diffusion models. - The 50/50 image-text mix ratio is presented as a single sweet spot without exploring whether this generalizes to very different data regimes or fine-tuning dataset sizes.
Recommended 2603.16139 PDF
#45 — Innovative (71)

LLM as Graph Kernel: Rethinking Message Passing on Text-Rich Graphs

Ying Zhang, Hang Yu, Haipeng Zhang, Peng Di · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 71 for a conceptually fresh reframing of LLMs as native graph aggregation operators, with the 'Semantic Anchor' dual-representation scheme offering a principled solution to the semantic-structural decoupling problem in text-rich graphs. The unification of aggregate-update into a single decoder pass and the dual-reconstruction pre-training are technically creative. The scope is narrow (only homogeneous citation/e-commerce graphs, 2 MP rounds), and the modest GraphQA gain over vanilla LLM (0.36%) raises questions about when graph structure genuinely adds value versus when the LLM's parametric knowledge suffices.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph neural networks applied to text-rich graphs (where nodes carry long natural language content) suffer from a fundamental "Semantic-Structural Decoupling": raw text is compressed into a fixed-size embedding once during initialization, and all subsequent message-passing layers operate on these frozen vectors, never re-reading the original text. This creates an irreversible information bottleneck where fine-grained semantic details needed for downstream tasks (e.g., a specific phrase in an abstract) are permanently discarded before structural reasoning even begins. Existing LLM-hybrid methods (OFA, LLaGA, PromptGFM) still aggregate on intermediate compressed representations rather than raw text, leaving the bottleneck unaddressed. The paper argues that overcoming this requires recasting the LLM itself as the graph-native aggregation operator. Methodology: RAMP (Raw-text Anchored Message Passing) introduces an asymmetric dual-representation scheme. For each node, two representations are maintained simultaneously: (a) the raw text Xi as a persistent "Semantic Anchor" providing full-fidelity context at every layer, and (b) compact summary tokens S_i^(l) (of length ⌈ρ·L_i⌉, where ρ is a compression ratio and L_i is text length) that serve as the propagated structural message. At each message-passing layer, an LLM decoder takes as input the concatenation [neighbor summary tokens || target node raw text || target node self-summary tokens] and produces updated summary tokens for the next layer. This unifies GNN Aggregate and Update into a single parallelizable decoder inference step. After L layers, the final summary tokens' KV cache is materialized and used by the same decoder for answer generation, enabling both discriminative and generative tasks under one formulation. Training proceeds in two stages: pre-training on 80K+ subgraphs (MAPLE dataset, Economics/Math/Geology domains) using self-reconstruction and neighbor-reconstruction objectives; fine-tuning per task with LoRA (rank=64). The backbone is Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct. Only lightweight attention layers for summary tokens are updated during pre-training; all backbone parameters are frozen. Key Results: On six node classification benchmarks, RAMP-7B consistently outperforms traditional GNNs (GraphSAGE, GCN, GAT) and text-only LLM baselines (Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, BERT, RoBERTa) across all datasets. Against the strongest directly reproducible hybrid baselines (OFA, LLaGA), RAMP achieves leads of up to 6.77% (PubMed: 93.68% vs 86.91%) and 4.83% (Arxiv: 75.38% vs 70.55%). RAMP trails PromptGFM (which uses closed-source GPT-4o) by ~6.5% on average, but closes to within 0.97% on PubMed and 1.63% on History. On GraphQA (ExplaGraphs), RAMP-7B achieves 93.86% accuracy, surpassing all GNN-based baselines (best: Align-GRAG 89.92%) and matching its backbone (93.50%). Structural integrity is confirmed via permutation tests: neighbor-order shuffling causes negligible accuracy drop (~0.06%), while cross-node topology shuffling causes a consistent drop (~0.5%), confirming permutation invariance and genuine structure sensitivity. Scalability experiments show RAMP's inference time grows near-linearly (<5×) as subgraph size scales to ~100 nodes, versus 22× growth for a graph-to-text baseline of comparable size. Strengths: - The Semantic Anchor insight is technically principled: it directly maps the GAT query-key-value asymmetry to raw text (query) vs. neighbor summary tokens (keys/values), giving a clean theoretical grounding for why raw-text re-reading at every layer matters. - The dual-reconstruction pre-training (self-reconstruction for fidelity, neighbor-reconstruction for communicability) is well-motivated and empirically validated — the compact baseline without raw-text anchoring fails (PPL >25) while RAMP achieves PPL ~9, confirming the mechanism works as designed. - Scalability is non-trivial: the paper explicitly compares against a graph-to-text baseline and demonstrates near-linear scaling, addressing a real concern for LLM-based graph methods. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The compression ratio ρ is a heuristic hyperparameter that may require task-specific tuning, and the paper provides no systematic guidance for choosing it beyond the evaluated ρ=0.1 default. - The evaluation scope is narrow: only 2 message-passing rounds are tested on homogeneous citation and e-commerce graphs. Heterogeneous, noisy, or multilingual graphs are explicitly listed as future work, making it unclear how RAMP generalizes beyond the tested settings. - The GraphQA gain over the base LLM is modest (93.86% vs. 93.50%), suggesting the structural signal may be limited for tasks where the LLM's parametric knowledge already suffices, and the paper does not analyze when graph structure adds meaningful value versus when it is a distraction.
Citations 2603.14937 PDF
#46 — Innovative (71)

CacheSolidarity: Preventing Prefix Caching Side Channels in Multi-tenant LLM Serving Systems

Panagiotis Georgios Pennas, Konstantinos Papaioannou, Marco Guarnieri, Thaleia Dimitra Doudali · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: CacheSolidarity identifies a genuinely novel security problem — timing side channels in LLM prefix caching — that is practically relevant given widespread APC deployment by OpenAI, DeepSeek, and Google. The condition-adaptive defense via KDE overlap is a clean design insight, isolating prefixes rather than users, but the core technical machinery (KDE, flagging metadata, sliding windows) is relatively standard systems security engineering applied to a new context.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Automatic Prefix Caching (APC) is a widely deployed optimization in LLM serving systems (OpenAI, DeepSeek, Google Gemini, vLLM) that reuses previously computed KV tensors for shared prompt prefixes. While APC dramatically reduces latency for repeated or multi-turn queries, it introduces a timing side channel: cache hits are faster than misses, and this latency difference—observable via time-to-first-token (TTFT)—leaks whether another user's prompt shares a prefix with the current request. In multi-tenant deployments, adversaries can exploit this iteratively to reconstruct sensitive prompt content (names, medical conditions, private identifiers) word-by-word through "prompt-stealing attacks." The paper demonstrates through empirical TTFT analysis across 9 LLMs and 5 load levels that the channel's exploitability is conditional: it depends on prefix length, model size, and system load (RPS). Existing defenses—full user-level cache isolation, timing obfuscation, and LLM-based semantic classification—all introduce unnecessary overhead for scenarios where the side channel is not actually exploitable, penalizing benign users. Methodology: CacheSolidarity introduces a lightweight, condition-adaptive defense built on a key insight: it is sufficient to isolate only the specific prefixes that enable attacks, not entire users. The system extends vLLM's KV cache with two metadata fields per entry: OwnerID (immutable, set at cache allocation) and AttackFlag (a marker set when cross-user reuse is detected). Three components work together: (1) the KV Cache Extension tracks ownership and flags suspicious entries with only 32 bytes of overhead per entry; (2) the Detector runs per-request on cache hits—if a hit belongs to a different user's unflagged entry, it flags that entry and isolates subsequent reuse; if a hit belongs to a flagged entry owned by another user, reuse is blocked and a new cache path is created ("partial hit"); (3) the Activator uses Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) overlap between observed TTFT distributions of hits and misses over a sliding window to dynamically enable or disable selective isolation only when the timing side channel is statistically exploitable. When KDE overlap exceeds a configurable threshold θ (hits and misses are indistinguishable), the detector is deactivated and full prefix sharing resumes. This cooperative-efficiency design allows benign shared prefixes to remain accessible while blocking adversarial reuse paths. CacheSolidarity is implemented on top of vLLM 0.8.5 and will be open-sourced. Key Results: Evaluation across 5 multi-user workloads (10 users × 100 requests = 1,000 requests each) and 9 LLMs (0.5B–13B parameters) on an NVIDIA A100 40GB shows: (1) CacheSolidarity achieves up to 70% higher cache hit rate and 30% lower TTFT compared to User Cache Isolation (the strongest security baseline), while staying within 5–10% of unprotected Prefix Caching performance. (2) Across all 9 LLMs, CacheSolidarity closes the performance gap to within 6% of Prefix Caching while significantly outperforming User Cache Isolation. (3) In an attacker-perceived performance experiment with a 20-probe reconstruction attack, the unprotected system shows a clear TTFT and hit-rate spike at the 9th request (the correct secret guess), which the attacker can trivially identify; CacheSolidarity eliminates this spike entirely—hit rate and TTFT are uniform across all 20 attacker requests, empirically validating that the timing leak is closed. (4) System overhead is negligible: 0.004 ms per request for the Detector, 0.003 ms for the Activator, and 32 bytes per cache entry for metadata. Strengths: - The conditional activation via KDE overlap is well-motivated by a thorough empirical characterization of when the side channel is actually exploitable—avoiding the over-protection that plagues prior work. - The selective prefix isolation mechanism is semantically clean: it isolates paths of flagged prefixes rather than users, preserving intra-user reuse even past flagged entries, which is a meaningful improvement over blunt user isolation. - Overhead is genuinely negligible (sub-millisecond per request, 32 bytes per cache entry), and the implementation on production vLLM with open-source commitment enhances credibility and deployability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The security guarantees have two explicit gaps: CacheSolidarity does not protect against attacks targeting the very first prefix entry (no parent to flag), and it does not protect against an attacker who correctly guesses the secret on the first attempt—both acknowledged but potentially meaningful in low-entropy secret spaces. - The KDE threshold θ is left to the system administrator with limited guidance on how to set it safely; an adversary who can manipulate traffic patterns (burst requests to inflate KDE overlap) can potentially force the Activator to deactivate protection, though the paper argues sliding-window aggregation mitigates this. - Evaluation is limited to a single hardware platform (A100) with synthetic workloads derived from ShareGPT; real-world deployments with heterogeneous hardware, mixed model serving, or disaggregated prefill/decode architectures may exhibit different TTFT dynamics that could affect the KDE-based activation logic.
Search Results 2603.10726 PDF
#47 — Innovative (69)

MetaClaw: Just Talk -- An Agent That Meta-Learns and Evolves in the Wild (90 ▲ )

Peng Xia, Jianwen Chen, Xinyu Yang, Haoqin Tu, Jiaqi Liu, Kaiwen Xiong, Siwei Han, Shi Qiu, Haonian Ji, Yuyin Zhou, Zeyu Zheng, Cihang Xie, Huaxiu Yao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The skill generation versioning mechanism that enforces support-query separation is a precise and novel contribution — correctly identifying that RL updates become invalid after skill evolution is an insight overlooked by prior work. The dual-timescale design unifying gradient-free skill adaptation with opportunistic RL is architecturally ambitious. However, evaluation on a synthetic benchmark with narrow rule-based preferences makes the quantitative claims difficult to generalize, and the full pipeline is tested on only one backbone model.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents deployed in production are static artifacts: trained once, served indefinitely, and increasingly misaligned as the task distribution drifts with real user workloads. On platforms such as OpenClaw — a CLI agent connecting to 20+ messaging channels — a user's needs may shift from file system operations one week to multi-agent workflows the next. Existing approaches address only one dimension of adaptation in isolation: memory-based methods store raw trajectories without distilling transferable behavioral patterns; skill-based methods compress experience into reusable instructions but never coordinate with weight optimization; and RL-based methods update model weights but ignore a critical data validity problem — once skills evolve, trajectories collected under the old skill context carry stale reward signals that contaminate gradient updates. No prior system unifies fast and slow adaptation while maintaining the support-query separation necessary for a coherent meta-learning objective. Methodology: MetaClaw represents the agent as a meta-model M = (θ, S), where θ are the base LLM policy weights and S is an evolving library of reusable natural-language skill instructions. Two mechanisms operate at different timescales. Skill-driven fast adaptation is gradient-free: after failures accumulate, an LLM evolver analyzes failure trajectories and synthesizes new behavioral instructions (e.g., "always create .bak before modifying any file") that are immediately injected into the agent's system prompt via embedding-based top-k retrieval, with zero service downtime. Opportunistic policy optimization uses GRPO-based RL with a process reward model (PRM) to update θ via cloud LoRA fine-tuning, triggered only during user-inactive windows detected by the Opportunistic Meta-Learning Scheduler (OMLS), which monitors three idle signals: configured sleep hours, OS keyboard/mouse inactivity timer, and Google Calendar event occupancy. A skill generation versioning mechanism enforces strict support-query separation: each trajectory is stamped with its skill generation index g, support trajectories (failures that triggered skill evolution) are excluded from the RL buffer, and all buffer samples with version ≤ g are flushed when the skill generation advances. This prevents policy optimization from penalizing the model for failures that skill adaptation has already corrected. The two mechanisms are mutually reinforcing by design: a better θ produces more informative failures for skill synthesis, and richer skills yield higher-reward trajectories for policy optimization. Key Results: Experiments use two backbone LLMs (GPT-5.2, Kimi-K2.5) on MetaClaw-Bench — 934 questions across 44 simulated workdays of sequential CLI tasks — and AutoResearchClaw, a 23-stage autonomous research pipeline. On MetaClaw-Bench Part I (30 days, 346 questions), skill-driven adaptation alone improves Kimi-K2.5 accuracy from 21.4% to 28.3% (+32.2% relative); the full pipeline advances it further to 40.6%, nearly closing the gap with GPT-5.2's baseline of 41.1%. End-to-end file-check task completion improves 8.25× (2.0% → 16.5%) under MetaClaw (Full), while skills alone leave completion unchanged — confirming that weight-level optimization is necessary for reliable execution. For GPT-5.2, skills alone improve accuracy by +7.1% (Part I) and +9.4% (Part II), with file-check completion rising from 58.4% to 67.5%. On AutoResearchClaw, skill injection alone (no RL) reduces stage retry rate by 24.8%, cuts refine cycles 40%, advances pipeline completion from 18/19 to 19/19 stages, and improves composite robustness from 0.714 to 0.845 (+18.3%). Per-day analysis shows MetaClaw's advantage is most pronounced in mid-range days (11–22) requiring multi-step procedural compliance; all conditions converge to similarly low performance in late days (25–30) where accumulated skills are insufficient without stronger policy weights. Strengths: - The support-query separation via skill generation versioning is a precise and practically important contribution: it correctly identifies and addresses the stale reward contamination problem that would otherwise invalidate RL updates after skill evolution, a gap overlooked by prior work. - The opportunistic scheduling design (OMLS with three idle signals) is practically engineered for real deployment — training only during sleep windows, keyboard inactivity, or calendar-free periods eliminates service interruption without requiring a dedicated training cluster or local GPU. - Evaluation on two structurally different benchmarks (structured CLI tasks with automated checkers and open-ended 23-stage research pipelines) provides genuine evidence of cross-domain generalization for the skill injection mechanism. Weaknesses / Limitations: - MetaClaw-Bench is an authored simulation rather than a dataset of real user sessions; the authors acknowledge that absolute accuracy magnitudes may not transfer to production workloads, weakening the quantitative claims. The benchmark's implicit preference rules (P1–P5) are narrow and rule-based, making skill distillation unusually tractable compared to realistic open-ended user drift. - The skill library grows monotonically (S_{g+1} ⊇ S_g) with no pruning mechanism for outdated, redundant, or conflicting skills. As the library scales, retrieval quality and context overhead become potential failure modes that are unaddressed by the current design. - MetaClaw (Full) with RL is evaluated only on Kimi-K2.5, not GPT-5.2, due to the need for a cloud LoRA training endpoint configured per backbone. This limits the ability to assess whether the virtuous-cycle hypothesis — that better policy and richer skills mutually reinforce — holds across model capability levels, and precludes a clean ablation separating RL contribution from model-capacity effects.
Recommended 2603.17187 PDF
#48 — Innovative (69)

Look Before Acting: Enhancing Vision Foundation Representations for Vision-Language-Action Models (12 ▲ )

Yulin Luo, Hao Chen, Zhuangzhe Wu, Bowen Sui, Jiaming Liu, Chenyang Gu, Zhuoyang Liu, Qiuxuan Feng, Jiale Yu, Shuo Gu, Peng Jia, Pheng-Ann Heng, Shanghang Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The systematic identification of visual information attenuation across deep VLA layers — validated with layer-wise masking experiments across three distinct architectures — is a meaningful diagnostic contribution that reframes how the community should think about visual grounding in VLAs. The VL-MoT shared-QKV fusion and action-guided pruning are technically clean solutions that follow directly from the diagnosis. Results are strong but the added computational cost of the 0.8B Vision Expert is not characterized, limiting practical assessment.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation depend critically on accurate visual grounding to generate correct actions. Yet existing approaches treat the LLM backbone as a black box, offering little insight into how visual information propagates through it. This paper identifies a concrete failure mode: task-relevant visual information injected only at the first LLM layer progressively attenuates as it propagates through deeper Transformer layers, leaving the deepest layers — which produce the final action embeddings — poorly grounded in visual evidence. The gap is both qualitative (Grad-CAM attention maps become diffuse in deep layers) and quantitative (masking task-relevant ROI tokens in deep layers produces negligible MSE degradation, while masking the same tokens in shallow layers causes large accuracy drops). This phenomenon holds across three architecturally distinct VLA models (OpenVLA, π0, QwenVLA-OFT), suggesting it is a systematic structural problem rather than a model-specific artifact. Methodology: The paper proposes DeepVision-VLA, built on two complementary mechanisms. The first is a Vision-Language Mixture-of-Transformers (VL-MoT) framework that couples a dedicated Vision Expert (DINOv3-H, 0.8B parameters) to the deep layers of the VLA backbone via shared QKV attention. Rather than concatenating features or using simple cross-attention, VL-MoT directly exposes the intermediate QKV tensors from the last n layers of DINOv3 and merges them with the corresponding QKV tensors from the last n VLA LLM layers, computing joint attention over both token sets. The Vision Expert receives a higher-resolution image (512×512 vs. 256×256 for the VLA branch) to provide finer spatial detail where needed. DINOv3's last-16-layer features are specifically chosen because they encode high-level, object-centric, spatially rich representations — superior for manipulation compared to early-layer or uniformly sampled features, and better than SigLIP which prioritizes image-text alignment over spatial precision. The second mechanism is Action-Guided Visual Pruning (AGVP): before fusing DINOv3 features into the deep VLA layers, irrelevant background tokens are pruned. Shallow VLA layers maintain reliable action-conditioned visual grounding, so AGVP aggregates action-to-vision attention maps from layers 4–19, interpolates the resulting saliency map to the Vision Expert's token resolution, and retains only the top-K tokens (pruning ratio 0.5). This focuses the Vision Expert on task-critical regions and simultaneously reduces computational cost. The model is pretrained end-to-end on 400K+ cross-embodiment trajectories (Open X-Embodiment, DROID, RoboMIND) and fine-tuned per environment. The baseline is a custom QwenVLA-OFT (Qwen3-VL 4B, parallel action decoding with L1 regression). Key Results: On RLBench across 10 manipulation tasks, DeepVision-VLA achieves 83% mean success rate, outperforming the prior best HybridVLA (74%) by 9%, CoT-VLA (66%) by 17%, and the direct baseline QwenVLA-OFT (69%) by 14%. Gains are especially large on visually demanding tasks: Sweep to Dustpan improves from 15% to 95% (+80pp over baseline), and Wine at Rack from 65% to 85%. On real-world single-arm tasks (4 tasks on a Franka Research 3), DeepVision-VLA reaches 91.7% average success rate, surpassing π0.5 (84.2%), QwenVLA-OFT (74.2%), and OpenVLA-OFT (71.7%). It achieves 100% on the two-stage "pour coke to bottle" task where π0.5 drops to 70% on the second stage. Generalization experiments under unseen backgrounds and lighting show DeepVision-VLA degrades only 5% on pick-fruit tasks versus 18% for the baseline. Ablations confirm VL-MoT's superiority over early fusion (+15pp), feature alignment (+21pp), and that AGVP with multi-layer action-token guidance (88%) beats instruction-token guidance (84%) and CLS-token guidance (65.5%). Strengths: - The diagnostic methodology — layer-wise ROI token masking combined with Grad-CAM visualization across three distinct VLA architectures — provides principled, reproducible evidence for the visual attenuation phenomenon, grounding the design decisions directly in empirical analysis rather than intuition. - The VL-MoT shared-attention design is technically clean: by fusing at the QKV level rather than concatenating features or using output-level adapters, the Vision Expert and VLA backbone maintain separate processing pathways while exchanging information, avoiding the feature interference common to naive fusion approaches. - AGVP achieves double duty — it improves task-relevance of injected features while also enabling higher-resolution Vision Expert inputs at manageable compute cost — a genuinely efficient design choice rather than a brute-force scaling approach. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The model adds a substantial Vision Expert (DINOv3-H at 0.8B parameters) on top of an already 4B LLM backbone, significantly increasing inference cost. The paper does not report wall-clock latency, memory usage, or FLOPs compared to baselines, making it impossible to assess whether the performance gains justify the compute increase in time-critical manipulation settings. - Evaluation is limited to a single robot platform (Franka) with single RGB cameras and no depth. Real-world tests cover only 4 tasks with 20 rollouts each — a relatively narrow assessment given the diversity of environments and embodiments that generalizable VLA models must handle. The generalization experiments test only two environmental perturbation types on a subset of tasks. - The choice of DINOv3 as the Vision Expert is motivated empirically but not ablated against other spatially-rich models (e.g., SAM, ViT-based depth predictors). The claim that DINOv3's spatial representations are superior for manipulation lacks a controlled comparison to alternatives of similar scale.
Recommended 2603.15618 PDF
#49 — Innovative (69)

HCP-DCNet: A Hierarchical Causal Primitive Dynamic Composition Network for Self-Improving Causal Understanding

Ming Lei, Shufan Wu, Christophe Baehr · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: HCP-DCNet presents an ambitious four-layer causal primitive algebra with a formally type-safe composition framework and a meta-evolution mechanism that autonomously discovers new primitives — the 22% gain from 7 discovered primitives is a concrete and novel result. However, the reliance on hand-constructed knowledge graphs directly contradicts the autonomous discovery narrative, all evaluation is in simulation with no sim-to-real validation, and the convergence guarantees rest on assumptions (µ-strong convexity, finite meta-state space) that are unlikely to hold in practice, making the theoretical contributions more aspirational than operational.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep learning systems excel at statistical association but fail at Pearl's higher rungs of causation—intervention (do-calculus) and counterfactual reasoning. Existing causal representation learning methods assume fixed discrete variable sets and static causal graphs, ill-suited to continuous, open-ended physical and social dynamics. World models learn predictive representations but lack explicit causal semantics. The gap is the absence of a unified architecture that simultaneously handles continuous physical dynamics, compositional symbolic inference, and autonomous self-improvement without pre-specified task distributions. Methodology: HCP-DCNet decomposes causal understanding into four interlocking components. First, a Causal Primitive Algebra formalizes reusable, typed computational units—causal primitives—organized into four abstraction layers: Physical (PINNs/neural ODEs for rigid-body dynamics), Functional (FSMs for object states), Event (transformers for recurring event schemas), and Rule (differentiable logic networks for social norms). A formal type system prevents semantically invalid compositions, proven type-safe by structural induction. Second, a Dual-Channel Dynamic Routing Network computes a causal influence matrix W by fusing a symbolic channel (differentiable theorem proving + knowledge graph GNN via NeuroSAT/CompGCN) with a sub-symbolic channel (hierarchical intra/inter-cluster attention), reconciled via a Causal Flow Conservation Principle that minimizes conservation violations. Third, the resulting Causal Execution Graph (CEG) is a hybrid directed graph with disjoint data-flow and causal edges, executed via differentiable message passing and compilable into an interpretable Structural Causal Model. Fourth, a Causal-Intervention-Driven Meta-Evolution framework treats self-improvement as a Constrained MDP: the system intervenes on its own primitive library and routing parameters, learns a performance causal graph via NOTEARS, and optimizes a safe meta-policy via Constrained Policy Optimization (CPO). Key Results: On CausalWorld (robotic manipulation), HCP-DCNet achieves Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) of 0.4/1.1/1.9 on Push/Stack/Chain tasks, versus the best baseline CITRIS at 0.7/1.8/3.5. On Social Interaction Blocks, SHD drops to 1.4 vs. 1.9 (LTN). On CLEVRER-Hypothesis counterfactual accuracy (CF-Acc), the model reaches 0.81 vs. 0.72 (LTN), with consistency score 0.92 vs. 0.88. HCP-DCNet converges using ~30% fewer training samples than DreamerV2 or CITRIS on the Stack task. Over 500 meta-episodes, the system autonomously discovers 7 new primitives and achieves a 22% performance gain on held-out chain-reaction tasks, while HCP-NoMeta shows no improvement. Average inference time is 45ms on a V100 GPU, with hierarchical attention reducing routing time 65% versus full attention. Strengths: - The four-layer primitive hierarchy with a formal algebra and type-safety theorem provides a principled compositional substrate that prior neural causal models lack—the modular structure yields generalization error growing linearly rather than exponentially with composition depth. - The dual-channel routing, reconciled by the Causal Flow Conservation Principle, is a well-motivated mechanism for preventing logically invalid primitive combinations that pure neural routers would permit. - The meta-evolution formalism as a CMDP with safety constraints is a concrete and theoretically grounded approach to autonomous self-improvement, and the empirical primitive discovery results (7 new primitives, 22% gain) substantiate the concept beyond ablation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The symbolic channel depends on a hand-constructed knowledge graph (ConceptNet + domain axioms), directly undermining claims of autonomous causal discovery; the system cannot bootstrap symbolic knowledge from scratch, and automating this acquisition remains entirely open. - All experiments are conducted in simulation (CausalWorld, SI-Blocks, CLEVRER); the sim-to-real gap is acknowledged but unaddressed empirically, and it is unclear whether learned primitives would transfer to real sensor noise and unmodeled dynamics. - The convergence theorems rest on strong assumptions—µ-strong convexity for routing (unrealistic for deep nonlinear routing landscapes) and finite meta-state space for the CMDP—that are unlikely to hold in practice, making the guarantees primarily aspirational rather than operationally binding. - Training overhead is substantially higher than monolithic baselines due to multi-stage curriculum and meta-evolution, and the paper provides no wall-clock training time comparison, making practical deployment cost hard to assess.
Search Results 2603.12305 PDF
#50 — Innovative (69)

Beyond Final Answers: CRYSTAL Benchmark for Transparent Multimodal Reasoning Evaluation

Wayner Barrios, SouYoung Jin · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: CRYSTAL introduces a principled process-level evaluation framework for multimodal reasoning that moves beyond answer-only benchmarks. The Delphi-inspired multi-agent reference generation pipeline and the CPR multiplicative reward are methodologically sound contributions, and the empirical finding that all 20 models systematically cherry-pick reasoning steps is genuinely informative. However, the core idea of step-level evaluation is well-established in text-only reasoning, and the single-path reference limitation significantly constrains the benchmark's fairness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing multimodal vision-language benchmarks evaluate models solely on final answer correctness, making it impossible to distinguish genuine understanding from lucky guesses or shortcut reasoning. A model can answer correctly while internally contradicting itself (e.g., claiming an object is larger while selecting it as smallest). This answer-centric evaluation structurally incentivizes hallucination and rewards confident incorrect reasoning paths equally with correct ones. The gap is consequential: without step-level observability, systematic perceptual and logical failures go undetected across all model families and scales. Methodology: The paper introduces CRYSTAL (Clear Reasoning via Yielded Steps, Traceability and Logic), a diagnostic benchmark of 6,372 instances drawn from five existing benchmarks (MathVision 47.7%, ScienceQA-IMG 31.7%, RealWorldQA 12.0%, MMVP 4.7%, PLOTQA 3.9%). Each instance is annotated with an ordered sequence of verifiable intermediate reasoning steps averaging 11.6 steps per question (range 3–42). Reference steps are constructed via a Delphi-inspired four-phase pipeline: (1) four independent MLLMs from distinct architectures (Qwen2.5-VL-72B, InternVL3-76B, Gemma3-27B, Llama-4-Maverick) generate candidate steps from the (question, image, answer) triplet; (2) steps are embedded and clustered via connected components at cosine similarity τ=0.45, with medoids selected as representatives; (3) a fifth MLLM (Molmo-72B) validates logical soundness, visual grounding, and answer consistency; (4) a trained human annotator applies a final quality gate (fewer than 5% require re-iteration). Two evaluation metrics are proposed: Match F1, which computes step-level precision and recall via greedy 1:1 cosine-similarity matching using all-distilroberta-v1 (τ=0.35); and Ordered Match F1, which additionally penalizes disordered reasoning chains by multiplying Match F1 with an LIS (Longest Increasing Subsequence) ratio weighted by α=0.3. Beyond evaluation, the paper proposes the Causal Process Reward (CPR), a multiplicative reward that conditions the step-level bonus on answer correctness: correct answers receive the full answer bonus plus a step-quality bonus, while wrong answers receive only a heavily discounted (λ=0.3) step bonus. CPR-Curriculum extends this with a two-phase GRPO training schedule: phase 1 trains answer-only to establish stable generation, phase 2 introduces CPR with progressive difficulty ordering by reference step count, using PCGrad to resolve gradient conflicts between accuracy and reasoning objectives. Key Results: Evaluation of 20 MLLMs (16 open-source 1B–38B, 4 commercial) reveals four systematic findings. First, cherry-picking is universal: 19 of 20 models exhibit precision substantially exceeding recall (ratios 1.2× to 7.2×), including commercial frontier systems absent from benchmark construction—GPT-5 (P/R = 0.925/0.479), GPT-5-mini (0.978/0.669), GPT-5.2 Instant (0.974/0.416). Only Gemini 2.5 Flash inverts this by generating 17.1 steps on average (recall 0.765 > precision 0.701). Second, accuracy and reasoning fidelity diverge: GPT-5 leads accuracy (57.99%) but ranks 8th in F1 (0.612); GPT-5-mini leads F1 (0.773) at lower accuracy (55.59%); Gemma3-4B (F1=0.618) outperforms InternVL3.5-38B (F1=0.612) despite 9.5× fewer parameters. Third, scaling exhibits non-monotonic reasoning trade-offs: Qwen3-VL-32B achieves higher F1 (0.718) than Qwen3-VL-8B (0.659) but lower accuracy (49.22% vs 57.66%). Fourth, no competitive model preserves more than 60% of matched steps in correct order (LIS): GPT-5-mini achieves the highest Ordered F1 (0.670) with LIS=0.560. For training, CPR-Curriculum on Qwen2.5-VL-3B improves accuracy by +7.67pp and Match F1 by +0.153 (0.480→0.633) where additive Composite and Answer-Only strategies fail (F1 ≤ 0.43) and diverge to NaN gradients at step 1,500. On InternVL3.5-4B, CPR-Curriculum yields even larger gains (+8.15pp accuracy, +0.401 F1, recall nearly tripling from 0.325 to 0.811), confirming cross-architecture transfer with identical reward weights. Strengths: - The Delphi-inspired multi-agent reference generation pipeline is principled and produces high-quality step sequences without requiring manual annotation at scale, validated by 84% human agreement (κ=0.534) and 100% agreement below the matching threshold. - The CPR multiplicative reward design cleanly addresses the fundamental flaw in additive RL rewards—it prevents reward hacking by ensuring neither accuracy nor reasoning can be independently maximized, and produces stable training where additive strategies catastrophically diverge. - The benchmark spans five distinct source datasets covering diverse reasoning types (mathematical, scientific, spatial, chart, fine-grained perception), and commercial frontier models not included in reference construction still exhibit the same systematic failure patterns, providing an unbiased generalization test. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The fixed single-path reference sequences do not capture the full space of valid reasoning trajectories; a model using a correct but alternative approach will be penalized on recall regardless of its logical soundness, systematically underestimating models that reason differently from the four generator families. - The CPR training signal, while effective at improving step coverage, relies on word-overlap-based step matching that cannot verify numerical correctness within steps—models learn to articulate the right algorithmic structure while sometimes executing calculations incorrectly, as demonstrated by regressions on geometry and combinatorial tasks. - Training experiments are limited to one base model size (3B/4B), and the reward weight sensitivity analysis is conducted only on a held-out validation split rather than held-out benchmarks, leaving generalization of optimal hyperparameters to other task distributions unverified.
Search Results 2603.13099 PDF
#51 — Innovative (68)

Optimal Expert-Attention Allocation in Mixture-of-Experts: A Scalable Law for Dynamic Model Design

Junzhuo Li, Peijie Jiang, Changxin Tian, Jia Liu, Zhiqiang Zhang, Xuming Hu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper identifies and formalizes a previously ignored first-order architectural variable—the expert-to-attention FLOPs ratio—and demonstrates it has a smooth, predictable optimum that follows power laws in both compute and sparsity. The out-of-sample validation on a held-out sparsity level lends real credibility to the extended scaling law. However, the contribution is primarily empirical characterization rather than methodological innovation, and the narrow sweep range and small model scales (≤5B active parameters) leave extrapolation to frontier regimes unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformers introduce an architectural degree of freedom absent from dense models: how to distribute a fixed per-token compute budget between self-attention layers and expert (feed-forward) layers. In practice this allocation is inherited from dense Transformer recipes or tuned heuristically, and existing scaling laws (Chinchilla and its MoE extensions) treat the internal compute split as a fixed constant. This paper asks whether there is a principled, compute-aware optimum for the expert-to-attention FLOPs ratio r = C_E/C_A, and whether that optimum varies predictably with scale and sparsity. Methodology: The authors define sparsity S = (E − E_act)/E (fraction of inactive experts per token) and the FLOPs ratio r as the two key architectural variables, then sweep r ∈ [0.2, 1.5] while holding total per-token compute C fixed across GPT-style decoder-only MoE models (100M–5B active parameters, up to 1×10²¹ training FLOPs, E ∈ {17, 33, 65, 129} experts, sparsity levels S ∈ {82–98%}). For each (C, S) pair they identify r empirically as the FLOPs ratio that minimizes validation loss. They fit the relationship r(C, S) = α_r(S) · C^{β_r(S)} and further show that both α_r and β_r follow power-laws in the activation ratio (1−S). Building on this, they extend the Chinchilla loss formula with two extra terms—one penalizing expert misallocation via an efficiency saturation term c·e^R(1−S)^γ/N^λ and one penalizing excessive expert allocation d·r/(r+1)—yielding an extended scaling law (Eq. 2) with nine fitted coefficients estimated via L-BFGS. Experiments fix context length to 4096 tokens, vocabulary to 128k tokens, and activate 3 experts per token (1 shared + 2 routed) throughout. Key Results: The optimal FLOPs ratio r is a well-defined, smooth function of (C, S): across all compute budgets and sparsity levels the loss surface exhibits a clear valley along r rather than a flat plateau. r increases monotonically with C on log–log scales following r* = α_r · C^{β_r}, confirming scale-dependent allocation. Fitting the sparsity dependence gives α_r = 6.7×10⁻⁵(1−S)^{−1.23} and β_r = 0.24(1−S)^{0.21}: lower-sparsity models (more activated experts per token) require steeper increases in expert compute at scale, while high-sparsity models should retain relatively more attention capacity. The extended scaling law (Eq. 2) fits observed losses tightly both on training data and on a fully held-out sparsity level (S=97.67%), demonstrating out-of-sample generalization. Estimated leading coefficients are α=0.629, β=0.045, λ=0.423, γ=0.043, with the loss baseline τ=13.74. Comparison against Wang et al. (2024) and Abnar et al. (2025) baselines shows those formulations fail to capture high-sparsity or small-model loss trends, whereas the proposed law tracks the full training trajectory accurately (Figure 4). Strengths: - Identifies and formalizes a previously ignored first-order architectural variable (expert–attention FLOPs ratio) and shows it has a quantifiable, predictable optimum—directly actionable for model design under fixed budgets. - The empirical coverage is broad: four sparsity regimes, six model scales spanning two orders of magnitude in active parameters, and training runs up to 10²¹ FLOPs, lending credibility to the power-law fits. - Out-of-sample validation on a held-out sparsity level (S=97.67% excluded from fitting) demonstrates the extended scaling law generalizes rather than merely interpolating. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study is restricted to autoregressive language modeling on a curated multilingual corpus; whether the discovered r*(C,S) law transfers to multimodal architectures, encoder-decoder models, or different routing mechanisms (e.g., adaptive or learned sparsity) is unexamined. - The experimental setup fixes several important hyperparameters—3 activated experts per token, fixed routing policy, no hardware-aware communication costs—that materially affect the compute decomposition in real deployments. The practical r* for production MoE systems trained across heterogeneous clusters may differ substantially from what this idealized FLOP accounting predicts. - The r sweep range [0.2, 1.5] is relatively narrow and the models are small by current standards (≤5B active parameters); whether the power-law extrapolation holds into the frontier 100B+ active parameter regime seen in DeepSeek or Mixtral remains unvalidated.
Recommended 2603.10379 PDF
#52 — Innovative (68)

Understanding by Reconstruction: Reversing the Software Development Process for LLM Pretraining

Zhiyuan Zeng, Yichi Zhang, Yong Shan, Kai Hua, Siyuan Fang, Zhaiyu Liu, Jiaheng Liu, Haozhe Wang, Yining Zheng, Ming Ding, Ke Shen, Ge Zhang, Wenhao Huang, Xipeng Qiu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 68 for a creative reframing of pretraining data curation: reverse-engineering the latent development process from static repositories is an insightful problem formulation that addresses a genuine root cause of LLM weakness on repository-scale tasks. The perplexity-driven CoT optimization is a practical alternative to RL that elegantly operationalizes 'better reasoning leads to easier code prediction.' However, modest absolute gains and single-model evaluation temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs trained on static code repositories struggle with the deep, long-horizon reasoning required for complex software engineering tasks. The root cause is a mismatch between the training signal and the generative process: a repository in its final form is a compressed artifact that has abstracted away the intermediate planning, debugging, and iterative refinement steps that produced it. Training on this static code teaches models to mimic surface-level structural patterns rather than the causal reasoning required to derive them. This explains why models that excel at short snippet generation fail on repository-scale tasks. The paper proposes bridging this gap by reverse-engineering the latent agentic trajectories hidden behind static repositories to provide a richer supervision signal. Methodology: The framework, called Repo2Agent, consists of two stages. First, Multi-Agent Trajectory Curation simulates the software development process using an LLM-driven multi-agent workflow. A Main Agent generates high-level project requirements and a dependency-aware implementation plan from a source repository, then delegates file-level implementation to Sub-Agents. Each Sub-Agent plans its file, uses a Read tool to gather inter-file context, and writes the final code. To prevent hallucination drift, the simulation is grounded with factual information extracted directly from the repository: the file structure tree, an inter-file dependency graph (from import analysis), and intra-file structure (from AST parsing). Tool call responses and final Write outputs are replaced with ground-truth content from the actual repository. Second, LongCoT Optimization refines the quality of the synthesized Chain-of-Thought reasoning via a perplexity-driven search. Each CoT step is iteratively resampled; a candidate replacement is accepted only if it lowers the perplexity of the ground-truth code (i.e., maximizes log p(x|z)). This avoids the instability of RL while still optimizing reasoning quality. The resulting trajectories are flattened into sequential documents and used for continuous pre-training of Llama-3-8B on 20B tokens with a 64k context window. Loss masking is applied to Observation tokens so the model learns only to predict Think and Action tokens. Key Results: Experiments compare four variants: the Prolong baseline (external), Raw-Repos (raw code), Repo2Agent (unoptimized trajectories), and Repo2Agent-Search (search-optimized trajectories). On long-context understanding, Repo2Agent-Search achieves 61.80 average on Ruler at 64k tokens versus 61.00 for Raw-Repos and 57.10 for Prolong, demonstrating that reconstructed trajectories are more robust at extreme context lengths. On the Helmet benchmark at 32k, Repo2Agent-Search scores 62.65 versus 60.98 for Raw-Repos. Coding improvements are clear: HumanEval scores rise from 34.76 (Raw-Repos) to 37.20 (Repo2Agent-Search), and LongCodeBench-32k improves from 34.16 to 36.46. General reasoning also benefits: MATH improves from 2.18 to 3.76 and BBH from 66.27 to 67.03. On APTBench, which measures agentic potential of base models, Repo2Agent achieves 30.10 overall versus 29.02 for Raw-Repos, with particular gains in planning-centric sub-tasks. The CoT optimization analysis confirms the mechanism: as search iterations increase, CoT length grows and code perplexity monotonically decreases, validating that more explicit reasoning directly eases the code generation task. Trajectories expand average per-repository token count from 4,865 (raw code) to 12,083 (Repo2Agent-Search). Strengths: - The grounding mechanism — replacing simulated tool outputs with actual repository content (file contents, AST-derived structure, dependency graphs) — is a practical and effective solution to the hallucination problem in purely LLM-simulated trajectories, ensuring the terminal state of every trajectory is factually correct. - The search-based CoT optimization is a computationally lightweight alternative to RL that directly operationalizes a clear objective (minimize code perplexity given reasoning), and the case studies demonstrate it produces qualitatively richer, more technically precise reasoning rather than mere paraphrasing. - The controlled experimental design — fixing total token budget at 12% of 20B tokens across all variants — cleanly isolates the effect of data structure (trajectory vs. raw code) from data volume, making the performance gains attributable to information density rather than scale. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is restricted to a single base model (Llama-3-8B), and it is unclear whether the gains generalize to larger models or different architectures; it is possible that larger models already implicitly recover more of the latent process from static code. - The method uses a powerful LLM (Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct) to generate trajectories for a much smaller target model, creating a significant teacher-student quality gap and raising questions about scalability of the data generation pipeline in terms of computational cost per repository.
Recommended 2603.11103 PDF
#53 — Innovative (68)

DenoiseFlow: Uncertainty-Aware Denoising for Reliable LLM Agentic Workflows

Yandong Yan, Junwei Peng, Shijie Li, Chenxi Li, Yifei Shang, Can Deng, Ruiting Dai, Yongqiang Zhao, Jiaqi Zhu, Yu Huang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: DenoiseFlow provides a clean theoretical grounding via the Noisy MDP formulation that principally motivates its three-stage architecture rather than engineering it ad hoc, and the label-free online calibration via temperature scaling is practically important. The adaptive branching achieves 40-56% cost reduction while matching fixed-branching accuracy, addressing a real efficiency-robustness tradeoff. However, the absolute accuracy gains are modest (+1.3% over JudgeFlow), and the individual components — uncertainty estimation, adaptive branching, root-cause tracing — are individually well-studied; the contribution lies in their integration under a unified framework rather than fundamentally new ideas.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-horizon LLM agentic workflows decompose complex tasks into multi-step reasoning chains, but reliability degrades as sequence length grows. The core failure mode—termed accumulated semantic ambiguity—occurs when minor interpretation errors in natural language instructions compound silently across steps, progressively drifting execution from the intended specification. Existing approaches are fundamentally reactive: static workflow optimizers (AFlow, MermaidFlow) fix execution structure offline with no runtime adaptivity; error-recovery methods (Reflexion, Self-Refine) detect failures only post-hoc via explicit signals; uniform branching strategies (CoT-SC, Tree-of-Thoughts) allocate exploration budget wastefully regardless of per-step uncertainty; and single-path methods ignore uncertainty entirely. No existing method integrates all five capabilities needed for robust long-horizon execution: uncertainty sensing, adaptive branching, root-cause tracing, online calibration, and targeted correction. Methodology: DenoiseFlow formalizes multi-step agent interaction as a Noisy MDP, where state transitions are perturbed by a latent semantic noise process ξ representing LLM-induced ambiguity. Accumulated Semantic Divergence Δ_t grows with graph depth and width via a recurrence over predecessor couplings, motivating a closed-loop policy that maximizes task reliability under a fixed inference budget. The framework operates through three coordinated stages. Stage 1 (Sensing) estimates per-step uncertainty u_t via Monte Carlo sampling: N=5 parallel LLM calls are clustered by cosine-similarity into semantic interpretations C_t, and Normalized Semantic Entropy over the cluster distribution yields a bounded uncertainty proxy ∈ [0,1]. Uncertainty propagates through a Probabilistic Dependency Graph G via a dual-channel recurrence combining a Bottleneck channel (λ=0.5, capturing single high-risk predecessors) and an Aggregation channel (β=0.3, capturing cumulative mild degradation). Online self-calibration via temperature scaling continuously corrects overconfidence/underconfidence using verifier feedback—without ground-truth labels. Stage 2 (Regulating) synthesizes a unified risk metric combining propagated systemic risk and local slot-level uncertainty, then routes each step to one of three execution modes: Direct (high confidence, single greedy path), Branch (medium confidence, K_t parallel paths with consensus scoring by validity + cohesion + cluster size), or Refine/Escalate (low confidence, trigger Stage 3). Thresholds θ_high and θ_low are set adaptively from the running confidence distribution (Q3 and Q1–0.5×IQR). Stage 3 (Correcting) performs influence-based root-cause localization over G by backpropagating failure influence to identify k = argmax_k I_k, applies asymmetric calibration (boosting uncertainty at k to 1.0), and re-executes from the corrected state—preserving valid intermediate results rather than performing global restarts. Key Results: Evaluated on six benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning (GSM8K: 1,319 problems; MATH: 500 competition problems), code generation (MBPP: 500 problems; HumanEval: 164 problems), and multi-hop QA (HotpotQA: 7,405; DROP: 9,536), all using GPT-4o-mini as the backbone LLM under a unified evaluation protocol. DenoiseFlow achieves 83.3% average accuracy, outperforming the strongest baseline JudgeFlow (82.0%) by +1.3% and AFlow (78.1%) by +5.2%—the best on all six benchmarks simultaneously. On competitive MATH, the gain is +2.9% over JudgeFlow (+8.6% over AFlow), where multiple valid solution strategies create genuine interpretation ambiguity that adaptive branching resolves. On code generation (MBPP 84.9%, HumanEval 93.9%), closed-loop refinement with binary test execution feedback enables rapid confidence calibration. On multi-hop QA, root-cause tracing outperforms full restart by +2.64 F1 and local retry by +3.11 F1 on DROP while reducing cost by 13%. Critically, adaptive branching reduces computational cost by 40–56% compared to fixed K=5 or K=7 branching (average cost $0.194 on GSM8K vs. $0.32 for fixed K=5; $1.012 on MATH vs. $1.69). Cross-model experiments confirm generalization: on GPT-4o, DenoiseFlow achieves 99.5% GSM8K and 68.5% MATH; on DeepSeek-V2.5, 88.8% GSM8K and 55.5% MATH—with the largest relative gains on weaker models (+12.5% MATH on DeepSeek-V2.5). Ablation identifies Adaptive Branching as the most critical component (−3.87% average when removed, −5.81% on MBPP, −5.79 F1 on HotpotQA), with Online Calibration second (−2.20% average). The uncertainty estimates are rank-consistent with actual difficulty (binned Spearman ρ = −0.782 on GSM8K and MATH), validating the routing signal. Stage 3 refinement recovers 33–44% of triggered failures. Strengths: - Clean theoretical grounding: the Noisy MDP formulation with a formal propagation bound (Proposition M.1) motivates the architecture rather than engineering it ad hoc; errors at early steps compound exponentially, justifying front-loaded uncertainty sensing. - Label-free online calibration via temperature scaling with verifier feedback is practically important—most calibration methods require held-out labeled data, making them inapplicable during autonomous execution. - Adaptive routing achieves cost reduction (40–56%) while matching or exceeding fixed-branching accuracy, addressing the core efficiency-robustness tradeoff; branching is concentrated where it is genuinely needed. - Comprehensive experimental design: 13 baselines reproduced under a unified protocol with the same backbone LLM, three random seeds, and sensitivity analysis across all key hyperparameters. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are primarily conducted with GPT-4o-mini as backbone; validation on fully open-source models (Llama, Mistral) is absent, and different models may exhibit different uncertainty characteristics requiring threshold recalibration. - Online calibration requires approximately 20 warm-up observations to stabilize—during cold start, uncertainty estimates are unreliable, and temperature may not transfer across task domains when task distribution shifts. - Stage 3 refinement effectiveness is coupled to verification signal quality: for tasks without clear success criteria (open-ended generation, creative writing), the refinement loop degrades to blind re-execution; the 33–44% recovery rate is modest even on well-defined tasks. - The Monte Carlo sampling overhead in Stage 1 (N=5 concurrent LLM calls per step) adds non-trivial cost for uniformly simple problems, and the proposed mitigation (a meta-controller to skip uncertainty estimation) is left as future work.
Recommended 2603.00532 PDF
#54 — Innovative (68)

CAMD: Coverage-Aware Multimodal Decoding for Efficient Reasoning of Multimodal Large Language Models

Huijie Guo, Jingyao Wang, Lingyu Si, Jiahuan Zhou, Changwen Zheng, Wenwen Qiang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: CAMD earns this score for its theoretically grounded approach to adaptive inference-time compute allocation, providing formal convergence results connecting difficulty distribution tails to sampling budgets — a principled foundation rarely seen in decoding strategy papers. The Bayesian adaptive sampling with posterior coverage estimation is a creative mechanism that simultaneously improves accuracy and efficiency. However, the practical gains over the strongest baseline (FarSight) are modest (+0.6 on MMBench), and the reliance on an LLM for semantic clustering at each iteration undermines the efficiency narrative.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing decoding strategies for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)—such as Best-of-N and self-consistency voting—apply fixed sampling budgets uniformly across all inference instances regardless of their difficulty. This causes a "compute-difficulty mismatch": easy instances are over-sampled (wasting tokens), while genuinely hard ones receive insufficient computation. The paper addresses this by asking how inference-time computation should be adaptively allocated across instances of heterogeneous difficulty in multimodal settings, where cross-modal inconsistency and visual ambiguity compound the challenge. Methodology: The paper first develops a theoretical framework modeling per-trial success probability s as a random variable drawn from an instance-level difficulty distribution G(s). It proves that when G(s) is heavy-tailed (polynomial decay near s=0), residual failure probability Δ(K) decays only as K^{-α}—confirming that fixed sampling is fundamentally suboptimal. This motivates Coverage-Aware Multimodal Decoding (CAMD), a plug-and-play, training-free adaptive decoding wrapper with three components: (1) Evidence-weighted scoring integrates generation log-likelihood, token-level cross-modal alignment (cosine similarity between visual and textual encoder embeddings), and reasoning coherence (consecutive hidden-state similarity) into a per-candidate score S(y|x); (2) Posterior coverage estimation clusters semantically similar candidates using an LLM, computes softmax-normalized cluster weights, and halts sampling if the maximum cluster weight p̂* ≥ 1-δ (default δ=0.05); (3) Bayesian adaptive sampling maintains a Dirichlet posterior over cluster success probabilities, updates via soft evidence counts, and reweights next-round token generation as a cluster-weighted mixture distribution, directing computation toward the most uncertain reasoning directions. Key Results: On image benchmarks, CAMD applied to LLaVA-1.5 improves MMBench by +2.3%, MM-Vet by +2.6%, VizWiz by +2.7%, and POPE-A (hallucination) by +4.6% over the base model, consistently outperforming the best prior decoding method FarSight (e.g., 66.6 vs. 66.0 on MMBench). Hallucination scores (CHAIRS) drop from 48.0 to 40.6 (-7.4). On zero-shot video QA across MSVD-QA, ActivityNet-QA, and a text generation benchmark, CAMD yields consistent +2% accuracy gains and improvements across all five evaluation dimensions (correctness, consistency, detail, contextual, temporal). On the efficiency front, CAMD reaches accuracy comparable to fixed N=8 sampling using only N≈2-3 on easy instances and N=32-64 on hard ones, matching near-N=256 oracle accuracy at substantially lower token budgets. GPT-4o-assisted evaluation confirms highest scores for accuracy, correctness, and detailedness among all compared methods. Strengths: - Theoretically grounded: provides formal convergence results (Theorem 4.2) connecting difficulty tail behavior to required sampling budget, giving the adaptive stopping criterion a principled basis. - Training-free and modular: CAMD wraps any MLLM without fine-tuning, requiring only candidate outputs at decoding checkpoints, enabling broad applicability. - Simultaneously improves both accuracy and efficiency, with hallucination metrics improving in tandem rather than trading off against throughput. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The posterior coverage estimation step (Eq. 13) invokes an LLM for semantic clustering of candidate answers at each iteration, introducing non-trivial overhead that is not directly accounted for in the token-budget efficiency comparisons—making the efficiency gains potentially overstated. - Improvements over the strongest baseline (FarSight) are modest in several settings (e.g., +0.6 on MMBench for LLaVA-1.5), and the ablation study (Figure 6) shows a very narrow performance range (~0.7%) over λg and λc, raising questions about whether the three scoring components provide meaningfully distinct signal. - The cross-modal consistency score requires access to intermediate visual and textual encoder embeddings at token level; this may not be straightforward in all MLLM architectures and is not discussed as a limitation. - The theoretical framework characterizes the difficulty distribution's tail type qualitatively but offers no method to empirically estimate which regime a given dataset/model pair falls into, leaving the adaptive threshold δ as a manually tuned hyperparameter.
Citations 2603.14745 PDF
#55 — Innovative (68)

GTM: AGeneral TIME-SERIES MODEL FOR EN-HANCED REPRESENTATION LEARNING OF TIME-SERIES DATA

cse.cuhk.edu.hk · karypis - new results
Why this rank: GTM introduces a genuinely novel Fourier attention module with granularity-aware low-rank frequency learning matrices, backed by rigorous KDE analysis showing that temporal granularity creates distinct frequency distributions. The hybrid masking pre-training strategy that unifies masked reconstruction and autoregressive forecasting into a single task-agnostic framework is a meaningful architectural contribution. However, the work is ultimately another entry in a crowded time-series foundation model space, with incremental gains over strong baselines (some of which outperform it on specific benchmarks), and the claimed SOTA status is overstated given buried comparisons in the appendix.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing time-series foundation models (TSFMs) suffer from two core limitations: (1) they operate exclusively in the temporal domain, ignoring frequency-domain structure, and (2) they require task-specific architectural modifications (at the token, pre-training, or projection-head level) to handle different downstream tasks such as forecasting, imputation, anomaly detection, and classification. The authors demonstrate empirically via FFT and 2D Kernel Density Estimation that amplitude-frequency and phase-frequency joint distributions differ significantly across temporal granularities (4-second vs. 5-minute vs. hourly vs. daily data), a critical but previously ignored axis of variation. No prior TSFM could adapt to all generative tasks in a truly task-agnostic manner. Methodology: GTM is a decoder-only Transformer pre-trained from scratch on the UTSD-12G dataset (~1B time points across 7 domains). Its central architectural innovation is a Fourier attention module inserted after each temporal self-attention layer. The module (1) applies column-wise FFT to the temporal attention output, (2) uses time granularity encoded as a quintuple (day, hour, minute, second, millisecond) to form a query vector, (3) computes soft attention weights over five low-rank granularity-specific frequency learning matrices (each parameterized by A∈R^{385×1}, B∈R^{1×385}) plus a global full-rank module, and (4) applies iFFT to return to the time domain. This design lets the model selectively emphasize frequency patterns appropriate to a dataset's temporal resolution. The pre-training framework combines two masking strategies: random span masking (sampled spans are shuffled and the model reconstructs them autoregressively — adapted from GLM) and controlled consecutive tail masking (applied with probability pred_ratio=0.3, masking the last 30% of the sequence to simulate forecasting). A 2D positional encoding informs the model of span lengths, and full attention is used for reconstruction targets while causal attention governs autoregressive generation. The unified output projection (a single linear layer) generates patch-level predictions for all generative tasks without any task-specific heads. Classification requires only swapping the output projection to a label head. Key Results: On long-term forecasting across ETTh1, ETTm1, Weather, Traffic, and Electricity datasets, GTM achieves the best or second-best result in 43 of 50 individual tests (prediction lengths 96–720), outperforming PatchTST, GPT4TS, UniTS-PMT, TimesNet, and DLinear. Representative averages: ETTh1 MSE 0.404 vs. 0.413 (PatchTST); Weather MSE 0.225 vs. 0.225 (tied with PatchTST); Electricity MSE 0.161 vs. 0.158 (TTME best). On imputation, GTM reduces MSE by 23.1% and 25.0% over the second-best on ETTh1 and ETTm1 respectively. On anomaly detection, GTM achieves an average F1 of 87.01% across five datasets, beating GPT4TS (86.72%) and UP2ME. On zero-shot forecasting, GTM ranks first in average MSE (0.380) across 5 datasets, beating TIMER-1B (0.392), MOIRAI-S (0.405), MOMENT (0.727). On classification, GTM wins 5 out of 10 benchmark datasets, the most of any model. Ablation confirms both the Fourier module and the granularity-aware sub-modules contribute materially (removing frequency entirely degrades ETTh1 average MSE from 0.404 to 0.419). GTM follows scaling laws: accuracy monotonically improves with depth, width, and pre-training data size. The model (35.7M parameters, 12 layers, dim 768) achieves sub-second inference (0.043s/item on A100) with FFT/iFFT adding negligible overhead (0.0007s). Strengths: - The frequency-domain Fourier attention with granularity-aware low-rank modules is a principled and empirically motivated design; the KDE analysis rigorously establishes that temporal granularity creates distinct frequency distributions, justifying the architectural choice. - The hybrid masking pre-training strategy elegantly unifies masked reconstruction and autoregressive forecasting into a single objective, making GTM the first TSFM that adapts to all generative tasks without any inference-time architectural changes. - Comprehensive empirical validation: 4 task types, 20+ datasets, zero-shot/few-shot settings, ablations, scalability studies, and efficiency benchmarks are all reported; error bar analysis over 10 independent trials confirms stability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Comparison fairness is inconsistent: results for some baselines (e.g., TTM_E on forecasting, UP2ME on anomaly detection) are missing for several datasets, and baselines were not uniformly re-run under identical pre-training conditions, making it difficult to attribute gains cleanly to GTM's architecture vs. its pre-training data or compute budget. - The model underperforms Time-MOE-Base on ETTh1 (avg MSE 0.404 vs. 0.379) and on ETTm1 at longer horizons, and is beaten by Sundial-Small on some ETTh1 settings — the paper buries these comparisons in the appendix rather than the main body, and the claimed SOTA status is overstated in the abstract. - The architecture's robustness to low signal-to-noise ratio or highly irregular (non-periodic) time series is explicitly unaddressed; the Fourier attention design is structurally biased toward periodic signals, which limits applicability to financial or event-driven data.
Search Results
#56 — Innovative (68)

SGP4SR: Seperated-Modality Guided User Perference Learning for Multimodal Sequential Reconmmendation

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SGP4SR introduces a principled decomposition of multimodal noise into cross-modal and internal-modal types with architecturally distinct solutions for each — a genuinely novel framing in multimodal sequential recommendation. The Co-occurrence Guided Graph Construction module creatively grounds semantic similarity in behavioral co-occurrence to prune noisy edges without supervision. The controlled noise injection experiments provide unusually rigorous evidence of robustness. However, the approach is evolutionary rather than paradigm-shifting, building on established SASRec and graph convolution foundations, and the highly uneven gains across datasets raise questions about generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal sequential recommendation systems (SRS) incorporate image and text features to combat interaction sparsity and improve next-item prediction. However, existing methods naively concatenate or fuse modal embeddings, ignoring two distinct sources of noise: (1) cross-modal noise, arising from mismatches or misalignments between modalities (e.g., an item image of a jackfruit labeled as 'Durian'), and (2) internal-modal noise, arising from irrelevant content within a single modality (e.g., background objects in product images or task-irrelevant adjectives in text descriptions). The paper argues these noise sources systematically degrade representation quality and that addressing them jointly requires architecturally distinct treatments. Methodology: SGP4SR introduces a two-stage framework that handles both noise types. To combat cross-modal noise, the model adopts a separated-modality architecture: image, text, and ID-based representations are learned independently through separate Transformer encoders (SASRec-style) and only combined at prediction time via weighted fusion. This prevents noisy cross-modal interference during representation learning. To combat internal-modal noise, two modules are proposed: (1) the Co-occurrence Guided Graph Construction module (CGC) replaces raw modal features with graph-propagated ID embeddings. It builds a modal relation graph by intersecting modal semantic neighbors (cosine similarity) with behavioral co-occurrence neighbors, discarding noisy edges that lack behavioral grounding. Graph convolution then propagates this structure into ID embeddings (Ê = ÂE^id), providing a denoised structural proxy for modal content. (2) The Clustering Interest Perception module (CIP) initializes k interest centers via K-means on modal features, then iteratively refines them through cross-attention with the evolving Transformer sequence representations.
