At inference, a Gumbel-Softmax operator selects the most salient center, which is added (with weight α) to the last-position sequence embedding to form the per-modality user representation. Prediction scores from multimodal fusion, image-only, and text-only branches are combined via a weighted sum with cross-entropy training loss over all branches. Key Results: SGP4SR is evaluated on four Amazon benchmark datasets (Pantry, Baby, Clothing, Office) using Recall@{10,50} and NDCG@{10,50}. Against the strongest baseline (MissRec), SGP4SR achieves an average Recall@10 improvement of 8.84% across all datasets, with a peak gain of ~29.85% NDCG@10 on Clothing. On the Baby dataset, R@10 improves by 11.61% and N@10 by 14.57% over MissRec. Notably, SGP4SR underperforms MissRec on R@50 for Pantry (-2.29%), suggesting less robustness at wider recall cutoffs on dense datasets. In a controlled noise injection experiment (replacing up to 50% of image features with mismatched ones on Baby), baselines degrade by 11–29% on R@10, while SGP4SR degrades by only ~6%, demonstrating superior noise robustness. Ablation studies confirm that removing CGC or CIP independently hurts performance significantly, and removing both (w/o C2C) yields the largest drop, indicating synergistic denoising effects. Strengths: - Principled decomposition of modal noise into two mechanistically distinct types with tailored architectural solutions for each, rather than a single generic denoising strategy. - The CGC's use of behavioral co-occurrence signals to validate and prune modal graph edges is a practical, label-free approach to grounding noisy visual/textual similarities in actual user behavior. - The noise robustness experiment with controlled image replacement ratios provides compelling, reproducible evidence of robustness beyond standard held-out accuracy metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The CGC graph construction has O(|X|²d) complexity in the item-item co-occurrence matrix computation, which may become prohibitive for catalogs with millions of items, even though it can be done offline. - Performance gains are highly uneven across datasets (negligible on Office: +0.11% N@10; large on Clothing: +29.85% N@10), and the paper does not analyze which dataset properties (sparsity, item count, modality quality) drive this variance, limiting transferability of insights. - The separated-modality framework requires maintaining and training multiple independent Transformer encoders plus separate prediction heads, substantially increasing model size and tuning complexity (multiple hyperparameters: α^m, ρ^m, H^m, H^o, k), which is not adequately addressed in the complexity analysis.
Search Results
#57 — Innovative (68)

Enhancing the Knowledge Tracing via a Plug-In Guided Diffusion Model

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 68 for introducing a genuinely architecture-agnostic diffusion-based plug-in module for knowledge tracing, which is a creative reframing of the prediction pipeline. The person-wise noise scheduling is a non-obvious, well-motivated inductive bias specific to the educational domain. However, the 'generation' operates over continuous embeddings rather than actual responses, weakening the cognitive-plausibility narrative, and the core contribution is applying an established generative modeling technique (conditional diffusion) to a new application domain rather than introducing a fundamentally new mechanism.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge tracing (KT) — predicting whether a student will answer a future question correctly given their interaction history — has been dominated by a learn-to-predict paradigm: models learn latent knowledge states and directly map them to binary predictions. The authors argue this paradigm oversimplifies the actual student problem-solving process, where students first generate a response based on their understanding before a correctness judgment can be made. Existing attempts to improve KT have followed tailored, architecture-specific refinements (graph structures, contrastive learning, auxiliary tasks), leading to increasing complexity and reduced generalizability. The paper asks whether explicitly modeling the response generation step — as a teacher would, reasoning from student understanding to expected answer — can systematically boost any KT backbone. Methodology: The paper proposes GOOD (Guided diffusiOn mODule), a plug-in diffusion module that reframes KT as a learn-generate-to-predict paradigm. The pipeline has three stages. First, any existing KT backbone (e.g., DKT, AKT, DisKT, FoLiBiKT) encodes the student's interaction history into latent knowledge state vectors h_1:n. Second, GOOD runs a conditional diffusion process over the target response representation: a forward process corrupts the response embedding with Gaussian noise, and a reverse process (denoising) regenerates it conditioned on h_1:n. Two KT-specific design choices are central: (1) a person-wise noise scheduling strategy that applies the same noise level uniformly across all timesteps in a student's sequence (rather than the conventional token-wise varying noise), motivated by temporal consistency in learning behavior; (2) a Transformer-Modulated Denoising Network (TMDN) that uses multi-head cross-attention with knowledge states as keys/values and the noisy response as query, combined with a knowledge-aware adaptive layer normalization (AdaLN) mechanism driven by causal temporal convolutions over h_1:n. At inference, DDIM sampling with 50 steps accelerates generation. The training objective combines binary cross-entropy on correctness predictions with an L2 reconstruction loss on the generated response representation, balanced by a scalar λ. Final prediction concatenates h_n and the generated response representation x̂_n^0 into an MLP. Key Results: Experiments span four benchmark datasets (Assist09, Assist12, Ednet, Spanish) with five backbone models (DKT, LPKT, AKT, DisKT, FoLiBiKT) evaluated via 5-fold cross-validation using AUC, ACC, and RMSE. GOOD consistently improves all five backbones. The strongest gains appear on larger datasets: FoLiBiKT-GOOD achieves +3.1% AUC on Assist12 (0.7954 vs. 0.7640) and +3.2% AUC on Ednet (0.7313 vs. 0.6995), while gains on Spanish are more modest (~1.2% AUC). GOOD uniformly outperforms HD-KT (the competing plug-in baseline based on anomalous interaction denoising) across all five backbone+dataset combinations, despite using fewer parameters — e.g., AKT-GOOD achieves 0.7844 AUC vs. HD-AKT's 0.7710 on Assist12. Prediction quality analysis confirms GOOD reduces both KL divergence and L2 distance between predictions and ground truth (e.g., FoLiBiKT-GOOD reduces KL from 0.5695 to 0.5275 on Assist09). Ablation studies confirm both person-wise noise scheduling and TMDN's TemporalConv are individually necessary; removing both causes the largest performance drop. The λ hyperparameter shows broad stability (AUC insensitive across λ ∈ [0.1, 8] for most settings), with optimal values around λ ∈ {0.5, 1.0}. Training+inference time per epoch for GOOD-enhanced models (e.g., 2.44–2.82s) is competitive with HD-enhanced models (1.68–2.49s) at similar or lower parameter counts. Strengths: - Plug-in design is genuinely architecture-agnostic: demonstrated across RNN-based (DKT, LPKT) and Transformer-based (AKT, DisKT, FoLiBiKT) backbones with consistent gains, which is a strong empirical claim about generalizability. - The person-wise noise scheduling is a well-motivated and non-obvious design choice that reflects a real inductive bias (sequence-level coherence), supported by ablation showing it matters independently of the TMDN. - Practical efficiency: GOOD achieves better AUC than HD-KT with fewer parameters and comparable per-epoch wall-clock time, making the performance-efficiency trade-off favorable. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The generated response x̂_n^0 is a continuous embedding, not an actual response — the "generation" is of a representation that then feeds a prediction head, so the cognitive plausibility argument ("like a teacher reasoning from understanding to answer") is somewhat figurative rather than a mechanistic model of response generation. - Gains on small datasets (Spanish: 182 students, ~579K interactions) are noticeably smaller (~1% AUC), and the authors attribute this to diffusion models needing sufficient data — this creates a practical caveat for real educational deployments where student history is sparse. - DDIM with 50 inference steps adds latency compared to direct-prediction baselines; the per-epoch timing comparison conflates training and inference and may obscure inference-time overhead in deployment scenarios where batching or real-time prediction is required.
Search Results
#58 — Innovative (68)

MLLMRec-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in Large Language Models for Multimodal Sequential Recommendation

Yu Wang, Yonghui Yang, Le Wu, Jiancan Wu, Hefei Xu, Hui Lin · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: MLLMRec-R1 introduces a genuinely creative solution to two real obstacles in multimodal GRPO-based recommendation: the visual token inflation problem is elegantly sidestepped via offline textualization, and the reward inflation diagnosis — where CoT supervision paradoxically hurts test performance through shortcut exploitation — is a concrete, well-characterized finding. The mixed-grained data augmentation with confidence-aware filtering is a principled fix. However, the core building blocks (GRPO, CoT distillation, collaborative filtering via LLMs) are all established, and evaluation on small datasets with 10-100 candidate sets limits the impact claim.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal sequential recommendation (MSR) requires modeling users' dynamic preferences over items described by both text and images (e.g., movie posters, video covers). Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has shown strong promise for LLM-based recommenders, but extending it to multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) faces two fundamental obstacles. First, visual encoding inflates token counts — a single image becomes ~196 tokens — and these costs scale linearly with history length and candidate set size, making GRPO's group rollout prohibitively expensive. Second, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) supervision for recommendation introduces reward inflation: naively constructed CoT data can boost training rewards without improving — and sometimes hurting — test-time ranking performance, because the model exploits shortcut signals inadvertently embedded in the reasoning traces. Methodology: MLLMRec-R1 addresses both obstacles through a two-stage framework. In the offline stage, visual signals are textualized to eliminate visual tokens during training and inference: (1) an MLLM (Qwen3-VL-8B) generates fine-grained captions for each item image; (2) given only the textualized interaction history (no target item), the MLLM generates a structured pseudo-CoT exposing multimodal preference cues; (3) the pseudo-CoT and captions are fed to DeepSeek-R1 for CoT refinement, which denoises and strengthens the reasoning trace while enforcing a target-free constraint to prevent label leakage. In the online stage, a mixed-grained data augmentation (MDA) strategy filters low-confidence CoT samples using two signals: modality consistency (cosine similarity between visual and textual embeddings of title–image pairs) and prediction consistency (alignment between the CoT's predicted next-item profile and the actual target). Only top-p% samples (p=0.1 for SFT, p=0.05 for GRPO) are injected as CoT-augmented training instances; the remainder use standard no-CoT prompts. This selectively exposes the model to high-quality reasoning while preventing over-reliance on CoT shortcuts. GRPO post-training uses lightweight scalar rewards: a format check (+0.3 or -1.0) and a binary hit check (+1.0 if the predicted item ID matches the ground truth), avoiding a learned reward model entirely. Key Results: On three benchmark datasets (Movielens-1M, Microlens, Netflix) with a 10-candidate evaluation setting, MLLMRec-R1 consistently outperforms all baselines. Against the strongest prior MLLM-based method (MLLM-MSR, AAAI'25), gains are substantial: HR@3 improves by 21.9% on Movielens, 21.6% on Microlens, and 20.9% on Netflix. Against the strongest LLM-based method using GRPO (RecZero, NeurIPS'25), which lacks multimodal CoT, MLLMRec-R1 achieves relative gains of 15.82% NDCG@3 on Movielens, 13.14% on Netflix, and 13.61% on Microlens. At the more realistic 100-candidate scale (zero-shot transfer from the 10-candidate trained model), MLLMRec-R1 achieves 26.1% gain in NDCG@5 and 28.7% in NDCG@10 over RecZero on Movielens. Ablation studies confirm that all three components of the MCoT pipeline (caption, pseudo-CoT, CoT refinement) contribute independently, with pseudo-CoT providing the largest gain and CoT refinement alone increasing NDCG@3 by roughly 3–5 points across datasets. Removing MDA causes performance to drop below the no-MCoT baseline in some settings, confirming that unfiltered CoT data actively hurts generalization. Strengths: - The offline textualization of visual signals is an effective and practical engineering choice: it eliminates visual token overhead at both training and inference time while preserving multimodal semantics, making GRPO rollout computationally tractable. - The reward inflation diagnosis is concrete and well-supported: the paper identifies the mechanism (shortcut signals in CoT leaking target information), quantifies its effect (training reward rises while test HR/NDCG fall), and proposes a targeted fix via confidence-aware filtering and mixed-grained augmentation. - Lightweight rule-based rewards (format + hit) avoid the cost and instability of a learned reward model, and the design rationale — not rewarding free-form reasoning to prevent reward hacking — is principled and supported by the broader GRPO literature. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The offline CoT construction pipeline is expensive and depends on two large external models (Qwen3-VL-8B and DeepSeek-R1); the one-time construction cost is not quantified, making total compute requirements difficult to assess. - Evaluation uses small, relatively clean datasets (Microlens: 25K users; Netflix: 13K users; Movielens-1M: 6K users) with candidate sets of only 10 or 100 items, which is far from the millions-of-item retrieval scale of production recommender systems — the results may not generalize to realistic deployment settings. - Performance is non-monotonic with history length (peaks at t=10, degrades at t=20 on Movielens), suggesting that the CoT reasoning approach does not gracefully handle longer interaction sequences — a significant limitation given that real-world users often have hundreds of interactions.
Recommended 2603.06243 PDF
#59 — Innovative (68)

Well-Connected Community Detection at Extreme Scale: Shared-and Distributed-Memory Parallel Algorithms

researchsquare.com · 7 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper fills a genuine gap by delivering the first distributed-memory community detection pipeline that enforces user-defined connectivity guarantees — a property no prior distributed method provides. The architectural insight of integrating subgraph materialization into the Leiden clustering step is elegant systems-level thinking that eliminates 86% of baseline runtime. However, the core algorithmic components (parallel union-find, round-robin distribution, mmap I/O) are standard, and the unresolved VieCut bug limits reliability on arbitrary graphs.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Community detection algorithms such as Leiden and Louvain frequently produce clusters that are internally disconnected or only weakly linked, undermining the interpretability and robustness of discovered communities. Post-processing frameworks — Well-Connected Clusters (WCC) and Connectivity Modifier (CM) — address this by recursively refining any input clustering via global minimum cut bisection to enforce a user-defined edge connectivity criterion λ(G[C]) > f(|C|). Prior shared-memory Chapel implementations demonstrated feasibility on graphs up to 2B edges on a single 128-core node, but profiling revealed that graph loading and subgraph construction together consumed over 86% of total runtime (43.5% and 42.9% respectively on Open-Alex at 64 cores), while the actual minimum cut computation accounted for only 10%. Single-node memory capacity (>250 GB required for billion-edge graphs) is a hard ceiling that prevents scaling to graphs with tens of billions of edges. Methodology: The paper's central architectural contribution is integrating subgraph file generation directly into the Leiden clustering step. Rather than loading the full graph and constructing cluster subgraphs at WCC/CM runtime, the modified pipeline writes each cluster's induced subgraph (and its connected components) to disk as standalone files during Leiden execution. The WCC/CM pipeline then begins immediately with pre-materialized subgraph files — no node ever loads the full graph. Subgraph files are distributed across compute nodes via round-robin assignment; each node executes a fully independent WCC or CM pipeline with no inter-node communication during the recursive refinement phase. Final cluster assignments are aggregated via a single collective operation. Three engineering optimizations are applied uniformly: (1) memory-mapped I/O via mmap replacing buffered sequential reads, yielding 3–5× file loading speedup; (2) two-level parallel connected component computation using parallel union-find within each node; and (3) decoupled subgraph loading that reduces subgraph construction from up to 60 minutes to seconds. The paper delivers two independent distributed implementations: C++ with MPI (two variants — distributed recursive WCC/CM-DR and distributed queue WCC/CM-DQ) and Chapel with multi-locale execution (recursive variant only, integrated into the Arachne/Arkouda framework). Experiments cover 10 real-world networks spanning up to 2.1 billion edges (Open-Alex), using Leiden-CPM at resolution parameters γ ∈ {0.001, 0.01} and the well-connectedness criterion f(n) = log₁₀(n) on a 4-node cluster of dual-socket AMD EPYC 7713 nodes (128 cores/node, 512 GB RAM each). Key Results: On shared-memory baselines, WCC-Chapel achieves up to 12× speedup over the C++ baseline on small-to-medium networks and up to 15× on CEN (the largest network where the C++ baseline runs); the C++ baseline fails entirely on Open-Alex, Open-Citations-v2, Open-Citations, and Wikipedia-Links due to OOM. The C++ distributed implementation (WCC-DQ) achieves up to 65× speedup over the C++ baseline on CEN at CPM 0.001, reducing >4 hours to under 70 seconds. On Open-Citations-v2 (∼2B edges), WCC-DQ completes in 72 seconds at CPM 0.01 — a graph on which both the C++ baseline and shared-memory Chapel fail entirely. On small-to-medium networks, the distributed C++ variants achieve up to 8× speedup over the baseline (Bitcoin, CPM 0.001). Chapel distributed delivers consistent 1.2×–2.1× speedups over Chapel shared-memory across all graphs and resolution parameters, with gains of up to 2.13× for CM on Wikipedia-Links at CPM 0.01. The Chapel distributed implementation is the only configuration that processes all 10 benchmark graphs successfully, including Open-Alex (2.1B edges), Open-Citations (1.4B edges), and Wikipedia-Links — graphs on which C++ distributed fails due to a known internal limitation in the VieCut minimum cut library triggered by specific cluster subgraph structural properties. Strengths: - The architectural insight of integrating subgraph generation into Leiden is elegant and practically decisive: it eliminates 86% of baseline runtime not through algorithmic improvement but through pipeline restructuring, a textbook example of systems-level thinking applied to graph analytics. - Chapel distributed is the first distributed-memory implementation to enforce user-defined connectivity standards in a community detection pipeline, filling a genuine gap — all prior distributed community detection methods (distributed Louvain variants) provide no connectivity guarantees whatsoever. - The empirical evaluation is thorough: 10 diverse real-world networks spanning five orders of magnitude in edge count, two resolution parameters, multiple implementation variants, and phase-level profiling that precisely identifies the bottleneck and validates the fix. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The VieCut minimum cut library has a structural bug triggered by specific cluster subgraph properties, causing failures on Open-Alex, Open-Citations, and Wikipedia-Links for all C++ implementations and for Chapel WCC on Wikipedia-Links at CPM 0.001. This limitation is acknowledged but unresolved in this paper, and the authors do not provide a workaround or characterize which graph properties trigger the failure, leaving the reliability of the system on arbitrary large graphs uncertain. - The round-robin subgraph assignment strategy ignores cluster size distribution, producing load imbalance when cluster sizes are heavy-tailed (as is typical in real networks). The paper acknowledges this caps distributed speedups at 1.2×–2.1× over the already-optimized Chapel shared-memory baseline, but provides no analysis of imbalance severity or a preliminary work-stealing prototype — the fix is deferred entirely to future work.
Citations
#60 — Innovative (67)

TACTIC for Navigating the Unknown: Tabular Anomaly deteCTion via In-Context inference

Patryk Marszałek, Tomasz Kuśmierczyk, Marek Śmieja · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: TACTIC's core contribution — pretraining a tabular transformer with an anomaly-centric mixture prior that yields calibrated probabilities in a single forward pass — is a well-motivated departure from repurposing classification-oriented PFNs. The explicit training on contaminated contexts addresses a real practical gap, and the discriminative output eliminating threshold selection is a genuine advantage. However, the ADBench-derived GMM prior introduces potential circularity in evaluation, and the feature dimensionality cap (~50) limits applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Unsupervised anomaly detection in tabular data is a persistent challenge: classical methods lack expressivity for complex patterns, deep learning methods require dataset-specific tuning and produce uncalibrated anomaly scores requiring post-hoc thresholding, and existing in-context models like TabPFN were designed for classification and suffer unstable behavior when repurposed for anomaly detection—especially in realistic settings where the reference set is contaminated with unlabeled anomalies. The paper identifies a concrete gap: no in-context foundation model has been specifically pretrained for anomaly detection with an anomaly-centric prior, and the naive unsupervised extension of TabPFN (uTabPFN) degrades badly under noisy contexts while also being computationally expensive. Methodology: TACTIC frames anomaly detection as an in-context prediction problem. Given an unlabeled context set D_ctx (representing training data) and query points D_q, the model directly predicts calibrated probabilities p(y=anomaly | x, D_ctx) for each query point in a single forward pass—no per-dataset optimization is needed at inference. The architecture is a 12-layer tabular transformer (512-dimensional embeddings, 4 attention heads, GeLU), where context tokens attend to each other and query tokens attend to context tokens only; each query token is decoded by a 2048-hidden-unit MLP to a binary output. The key innovation is the pretraining prior: a mixture of (1) TabPFN-style classification priors using Structural Causal Models and Bayesian Neural Networks (weight 0.7), and (2) GMM-based priors that explicitly generate local, cluster, and global anomalies using ADBench mechanisms (weight 0.3). Dimensionality is randomized (d ∈ {2,...,50}) and both clean and contaminated contexts (5–30% anomaly rate) are seen during training, explicitly building robustness to realistic conditions. From a Bayesian perspective, this is amortized posterior predictive inference under an anomaly-centric learned prior. Key Results: On 38 ADBench real-world datasets, TACTIC achieves the best average AUCROC of 90.4% (vs. 88.9% for uTabPFN, 88.4% for KNN) and the best average F1 of 65.5% (vs. 58.2% KNN, 49.0% LOF) in the clean-context setting. In the noisy-context setting TACTIC-N achieves 80.6% AUCROC vs. 67.6% for uTabPFN (which degrades dramatically) while remaining top-ranked. Robustness experiments varying anomaly contamination from 0–20% show TACTIC-N is consistently the most stable method across all noise levels; at 20% context contamination, TACTIC-N achieves 71.5% AUCROC and 48.1% F1 vs. uTabPFN's 67.4% and (no F1 since it lacks a threshold). On synthetic Gaussian mixture datasets with diverse anomaly types, TACTIC achieves 99.2% AUCROC / 89.4% F1 on local anomalies, 99.9% / 89.5% on global anomalies, and 78.5% / 34.4% on cluster anomalies, consistently outperforming baselines. Ablation confirms both prior components are necessary: removing the PFN-style prior drops clean AUCROC from 90.4% to 76.0% and noisy F1 from 28.7% to 11.7%; removing GMM priors causes smaller but still significant drops. Crucially, TACTIC's inference time is comparable to fast shallow methods, unlike uTabPFN which is slow in high-dimensional settings. Strengths: - Task-specific pretraining with a carefully designed mixture prior directly addresses the mismatch between classification-oriented PFNs and anomaly detection, yielding a model that generalizes across anomaly types without dataset-specific adaptation - Discriminative calibrated output (direct probability of anomaly) eliminates the need for threshold selection or score post-processing, which is a significant practical advantage over all score-based baselines - Explicit robustness to contaminated contexts through noisy pretraining is well-motivated and empirically validated; the model remains competitive even at 20% context contamination where most methods degrade substantially Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance on cluster anomalies (78.5% AUCROC) lags behind local/global anomalies (>99%) substantially, and the gap between TACTIC and baselines narrows considerably in this hardest regime—suggesting the prior may underrepresent this anomaly type - The method is limited to datasets with up to ~50 features (by pretraining design), with no evaluation on truly high-dimensional tabular data (e.g., hundreds of features), which limits applicability to some real-world settings - Despite strong average performance, the paper evaluates only on ADBench datasets which are themselves used to design the GMM prior (via ADBench anomaly mechanisms), introducing a potential circularity in evaluating generalization
Citations 2603.14171 PDF
#61 — Innovative (67)

Video-CoE: Reinforcing Video Event Prediction via Chain of Events (24 ▲ )

Qile Su, Jing Tang, Rui Chen, Lei Sun, Xiangxiang Chu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The diagnostic finding that vanilla SFT actively harms visual token attention is counterintuitive and important, elevating this beyond a standard RL-for-video paper. The CoE-GRPO reward design with timestamp-based video cropping for cross-modal similarity is technically clever and prevents reward hacking. Data efficiency is striking—beating 72B commercial models with 2K samples on a 7B model—though evaluation is limited to a single model family.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Video event prediction (VEP) — forecasting what will happen after a video ends — is an underexplored capability in multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Unlike standard video understanding tasks that reason about observable content, VEP requires inferring causally plausible future events from observed evidence, a qualitatively harder problem. Prior evaluations show that even the best models (Qwen3-VL achieves only 66.9% average accuracy on FutureBench) fail substantially. This paper diagnoses why: (1) MLLMs rely on textual option analysis rather than video-grounded reasoning, and (2) attention distributions show models allocate far less weight to visual tokens than to textual tokens during inference. Both prompt-based and attention-bias heuristics previously proposed to fix these issues are empirically shown to degrade performance. Methodology: The paper proposes the Chain of Events (CoE) paradigm, which reformulates VEP as a two-stage structured reasoning process. First, the model performs fine-grained temporal segmentation of the input video into an explicit event chain EC = [E1, E2, ..., En], where each event is a structured pair of timestamps and a textual description. Second, the model reasons jointly over the video and the event chain to predict the future event, enforcing logical causal connections rather than surface-level option matching. Two training protocols implement this paradigm: (1) CoE-SFT uses a small dataset (~2,000 samples) distilled from Qwen2.5-VL-72B, where the teacher is prompted with the video, question, and correct answer to produce logically grounded reasoning chains (avoiding option analysis). (2) CoE-GRPO applies reinforcement learning with three reward components: an accuracy reward ra (verifiable answer correctness), a CoE format and length reward re that incentivizes correct event tag structure and regulates chain length via a penalty on deviation from target length L, and a cross-modal similarity reward rs computed by cropping video segments at predicted timestamps and measuring cosine similarity between clip and description embeddings using VideoCLIP-XL. The combined reward drives the model to construct visually faithful event chains without requiring additional human annotation. Training uses Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B as base models on up to 16 H20 GPUs for 150 steps. Key Results: On FutureBench, CoE-GRPO with the 7B model achieves 75.00% average accuracy, outperforming all open-source and commercial baselines including GPT-4o (59.04%), GPT-5 (57.92%), Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B (66.86%), and Qwen2.5-VL-72B (58.33%) — using a model 10× smaller than the latter. Compared to the strongest fine-tuned baseline (NEP-GRPO at 67.28% for 7B), CoE-GRPO improves by +7.7 percentage points. Gains on 3-Hop and Interp. tasks (not seen during training) demonstrate generalization of causal reasoning. On AVEP, CoE-GRPO-7B achieves Action F1 of 9.88/9.88 (test/val) versus 6.48/7.26 for vanilla GRPO, with Verb accuracy improving from 9.64 to 12.24. Attention analysis confirms CoE-GRPO raises visual token attention winning rate from 59% (vanilla GRPO) to 77%, while CoE-SFT achieves 93% — compared to vanilla SFT which actually reduces visual attention (WR=32%). Against RL-based competitors VideoChat-R1 (46.59%) and Video-R1 (67.47%), CoE achieves 75.00% on FutureBench. The data scaling curve shows performance is far from saturated at 2,000 training samples. Strengths: - Rigorous diagnostic analysis using attention distribution visualization across six MLLMs motivates the method rather than proposing CoE ad hoc; the finding that vanilla SFT actively harms visual attention is a counterintuitive and important result. - The CoE-GRPO reward design is technically sound: the similarity reward using video segment cropping at predicted timestamps directly incentivizes temporal localization accuracy and prevents reward hacking, distinguishing it from format-only RL approaches. - Data efficiency is impressive: 2,000 CoE-SFT samples and 2,000 RL samples suffice to beat 72B-parameter commercial models, with the scaling curve showing strong potential for further gains. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is evaluated only on Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B base models; it is not demonstrated on other MLLM families (LLaMA-based, Gemini, etc.), so generality of the training approach is assumed but unvalidated. - The event chain representation is intentionally simple (linear timestamp-description pairs); the paper acknowledges that richer structures (relation-aware chains, event graphs) could improve performance but leaves this unexplored. - The judge-based open-set evaluation, while well-motivated for real-world applicability, uses Qwen2.5-VL-72B as judge — a model architecturally similar to the SFT training teacher — potentially introducing bias that disadvantages CoE-GRPO's different output style.
Recommended 2603.14935 PDF
#62 — Innovative (67)

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via Reinforcement Learning

Xiaofeng Lin, Seungbae Kim, Zhuoya Li, Zachary DeSoto, Charles Fleming, Guang Cheng · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: ReTabSyn introduces a genuinely clever oracle-free preference construction for DPO fine-tuning of tabular generators, grounded in a formal utility decomposition that directly targets the conditional P(y|X) rather than the full joint. The schema-native perturbation strategies (target and feature perturbation) are well-motivated and eliminate the oracle classifier bias that plagued prior RL-guided approaches like PTA and SynRL. However, the core technique is an adaptation of DPO — already well-established in the LLM alignment literature — to a new domain, and the regression task limitations suggest the conditional alignment strategy has meaningful blind spots.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard deep generative models (DGMs) for tabular data optimize the full joint distribution P(X, y), which spreads limited statistical budget equally across all feature marginals and the decision boundary. In low-data, class-imbalanced, or distribution-shifted regimes, this leads to synthetic data that hallucinates unrealistic feature co-occurrences and degrades downstream ML utility. The authors ground this observation in a formal utility decomposition theorem (Xu et al., 2023) showing that the utility gap is bounded by two terms: feature mismatch and regression mismatch (i.e., error in the conditional P(y|X)). This motivates prioritizing the conditional distribution over generic joint fidelity — a gap that existing methods, including RL-guided approaches like PTA and SynRL that rely on external oracle classifiers, do not cleanly address. Methodology: ReTabSyn (Reinforced Tabular Synthesis) fine-tunes a pre-trained LM-based tabular generator (GREAT/GPT-2 backbone) using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) without any external reward model or human labels. The pipeline has two stages: (1) supervised pre-training on SMOTE-augmented tabular data serialized as natural language strings, with adaptive augmentation ratios M(N) ranging from 30x for N≤128 to 1x for N>1000; (2) DPO fine-tuning on oracle-free preference pairs. Preference pairs (chosen C, rejected R) are constructed via two tabular-native perturbation strategies: Target Perturbation — keeping features X fixed and replacing label y with a marginal sample ỹ, directly targeting the regression mismatch term; and Feature Perturbation — perturbing one feature in a strongly correlated pair (identified by Pearson's r or Cramér's V) while keeping its correlated partner fixed, penalizing unrealistic feature co-occurrences. A 70/30 mix of target vs. feature perturbations is used. The DPO-Positive objective enlarges the log-likelihood margin between chosen and rejected completions while a regularization term (weight λ=0.1) prevents drifting from the pre-trained feature manifold. The entire preference construction is schema-validated and label-free, eliminating oracle classifier bias. Key Results: Evaluated across 10 benchmark tabular classification datasets under three challenging regimes — small data (N∈{32,64,128,256,512}), imbalanced targets (1% minority prevalence), and demographic distribution shift — ReTabSyn consistently outperforms all baselines including GReaT, TVAE, TabSyn, SMOTE, and the classifier-guided RL methods PTA and SynRL. In the small data regime (32–128 rows), ReTabSyn achieves the highest synthetic AUROC across datasets and in augmentation settings outperforms real-data baselines at very low N. On imbalanced data (PR-AUC at 1% prevalence), it reaches 0.906 on Adult vs. 0.891 for the next-best baseline (SMOTE). Under distribution shift, ReTabSyn leads by 1–4 AUC points over all synthetic baselines on Adult, Churn, Liver, and Titanic. On statistical fidelity, ReTabSyn achieves the highest or tied-highest β-Recall (0.33), Shape (0.83), and Corr. (0.83) among all generators. Privacy analysis using 13 membership inference attacks shows ReTabSyn leakage (AUC≈0.62) is substantially lower than interpolation-based SMOTE (0.83) and comparable to deep generator baselines. On domain constraint satisfaction (Adult), ReTabSyn achieves 0.00% violation rate vs. 1–3% for all other deep generators. Ablations confirm both training stages are necessary and that oracle-based preference labeling underperforms the schema-native perturbation approach. Strengths: - Theoretically grounded: the method directly minimizes the utility gap decomposition rather than heuristically applying RL signals, providing a principled justification for targeting P(y|X) - Oracle-free and label-free design eliminates guidance-model bias, reduces compute cost, and avoids privacy leakage from external classifiers — a genuine practical advantage over PTA/SynRL - Strong empirical breadth: 10 datasets, 3 evaluation regimes, 10 random seeds, comprehensive fidelity + privacy + utility metrics, and thorough ablations covering training stages, perturbation types, augmentation scale, and DPO budget allocation Weaknesses / Limitations: - The base generator is exclusively GPT-2/GREAT (autoregressive LM); generalization to diffusion-based or GAN/VAE backbones is acknowledged as future work, limiting conclusions about architectural independence - Regression task performance lags SMOTE on two of three regression datasets (Abalone, Shoppers), suggesting the conditional alignment strategy is less effective when the decision boundary is continuous and noisy — a non-trivial limitation given many real-world tabular tasks are regression - The DPO fine-tuning and augmentation pipeline adds meaningful implementation complexity and an additional ~3 minutes of fine-tuning per dataset (on an H100 GPU), which may be non-trivial for practitioners without access to high-memory accelerators despite the lightweight framing
Citations 2603.10823 PDF
#63 — Innovative (67)

When Do LLM Preferences Predict Downstream Behavior?

Katarina Slama, Alexandra Souly, Dishank Bansal, Henry Davidson, Christopher Summerfield, Lennart Luettgau · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Addresses an important missing link in AI safety by empirically testing whether LLM preferences spontaneously drive behavior rather than only when explicitly instructed. The superadditivity finding for refusal behavior and the methodological separation of preference measurement from behavioral testing are meaningful contributions. Weakened by anonymized models preventing replication and correlational-only evidence.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: AI alignment research has raised concern about "sandbagging" — models strategically underperforming on evaluations to hide capabilities. A necessary precondition for such behavior is that model preferences actually influence spontaneous actions. Prior work has separately established (1) that LLMs hold consistent preferences as a side effect of training, and (2) that LLMs can sandbag when explicitly instructed to do so. What has not been established is whether these two phenomena are linked: do LLMs act on their preferences without being told to? If they do not, sandbagging through preference-driven action cannot emerge. If they do, users may receive systematically biased assistance and evaluation benchmarks may reflect model preferences rather than pure capabilities. This paper tests that missing link directly. Methodology: The study employs a two-stage design across five frontier LLMs (Models A–E from two undisclosed providers) and 72 entities spanning diverse domains. Stage 1 measures model preferences using two independent methods: exhaustive pairwise comparisons (2,556 pairs × 5 repetitions, yielding Elo ratings) and direct overall rankings (36 entities, 5 repetitions). High cross-method correlation establishes that preferences are stable. Stage 2 tests whether these preferences predict three behavioral outcomes in entirely separate queries: (a) donation advice — pairwise choices and lump-sum allocation of $100,000 across entities; (b) refusal behavior — retry counts needed to obtain a valid response, both in donation and task contexts, categorized by refusal type using an LLM grader; and (c) task performance — accuracy on BoolQ (12,697 questions across train and validation splits) framed as helping users win donations for specified entities, and two agentic benchmarks (53 GAIA level-1 tasks, 16 Cybench tasks) using a ReAct agent. Batch Elo is used for order-independence; Spearman correlations test preference-behavior associations; linear regression models test superadditivity of refusal effects; logistic regression tests whether preference effects differ between BoolQ and agentic settings. Key Results: Preference consistency is strong and uniform: all five models achieve Spearman ρ = .91–.92 between Elo-derived and direct rankings (p < .001), confirmed as non-arbitrary by an alphabetical control (ρ ≈ .999 for alphabetical task; ρ ≈ .07 between alphabetical and preference rankings). Donation advice is strongly preference-driven across all five models: pairwise donation Elo correlates with preference Elo at ρ = .94–.98, and lump-sum allocations at ρ = .80–.91 (all p < .001, passing Bonferroni correction). Refusal behavior in the donation context is preference-correlated for all five models (ρ = .57–.83), with refusal effects superadditive: when both entities in a pair are low-preference, retries increase more than the sum of individual effects (positive Li × Lj interaction, all p < .001). The composition of stated refusal reasons also shifts with preference: for highly preferred entities, 96–99% of refusals cite "personal decision" vs. 51–61% for least-preferred entities. Task performance results are mixed: two models (B: ρ = .55; C: ρ = .64) show significant positive preference-accuracy correlations on BoolQ; one model (D: ρ = −.24 to −.29) shows a significant negative correlation; two show no effect. Effect sizes are small — less than 1 percentage point accuracy difference between top and bottom preferred entities for Model C (88.9% to 89.5%, ~64 additional correct answers out of 9,427). On GAIA and Cybench agentic tasks, no model shows a significant preference effect (all p > .15). Strengths: - The separation of preference measurement from behavioral testing into independent queries is a critical methodological advance over prior work that measured both in the same session (avoiding priming confounds). - The refusal analysis is unusually thorough: retry counts provide a continuous behavioral signal, robustness checks exclude timeouts, an LLM grader categorizes refusal reasons, and a confound analysis rules out question difficulty as the primary driver. - The superadditivity finding — that pairs of low-preference entities trigger disproportionately more refusals than either entity alone — is a novel and practically important result not previously reported. - The pre-registration of key analyses and use of Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons strengthens causal inference relative to most behavioral LLM studies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Model and provider identities are withheld, making it impossible to verify whether findings generalize to specific publicly known models or to replicate the study; this undermines one of the most basic requirements of empirical science. - The agentic task evaluation is severely underpowered: only 10 entities (5 most vs. 5 least preferred) with 53 GAIA and 16 Cybench tasks per model, making null results uninformative — the study explicitly acknowledges it cannot detect small effects that may be present. - Preference measurement uses a single entity type (72 entities of an unspecified but apparently charity/organization type), so generalization to other entity classes, political figures, or technical domains is entirely unknown. - The study establishes correlations but cannot establish causation: whether preferences mechanistically drive behavior or whether both are driven by a common latent factor (e.g., training data co-occurrence) cannot be distinguished without intervention experiments such as activation steering or controlled fine-tuning.
Recommended 2602.18971 PDF
#64 — Innovative (67)

Distilling LLM Semantic Priors into Encoder-Only Multi-Talker ASR with Talker-Count Routing

Hao Shi, Yusuke Fujita, Roman Koshkin, Mengjie Zhao, Yuan Gao, Lianbo Liu, Yui Sudo · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The core insight — distilling LLM semantic priors into an encoder-only model during training, then discarding the LLM entirely at inference — is a well-motivated and cleanly executed idea that yields a compelling 26× speedup with competitive or superior accuracy, especially on the challenging 3-talker case. The ablation convincingly shows LLM distillation is the critical enabler, not architecture depth alone. Evaluation on only the synthetic LibriMix benchmark and the rigid binary talker-count routing limit the demonstrated generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-talker automatic speech recognition (MT-ASR) — transcribing all speakers from overlapping speech — has made progress through serialized output training (SOT) with large language model (LLM) decoders, but this approach has two compounding problems. First, autoregressive LLM decoding is slow (high real-time factor). Second, LLM decoders fail to reliably improve performance on challenging three-talker mixtures because the encoder remains talker-agnostic and becomes the bottleneck: the LLM must disentangle heavily overlapped representations over long sequences. Encoder-only CTC approaches are faster but unstable under heavy overlap without strong semantic regularization, and they require the number of talkers to be known in advance. The paper addresses the question of whether LLMs can provide semantic regularization during training without being present at inference. Methodology: The paper proposes a two-phase training framework built on WavLM-Large (24 Transformer blocks) as the backbone. A shared encoder (layers 1–12) feeds into two specialized Transformer branches — one for two-talker and one for three-talker conditions — each initialized from WavLM layers 13–24. Each branch is followed by an LSTM-based separator that produces talker-ordered streams, which are decoded via serialized CTC heads (one per talker in onset-time order). In Phase 1, a LLaMA-3.2-1B decoder is attached and jointly fine-tuned using the SOT cross-entropy objective while only LoRA adapters and token embeddings are updated, distilling semantic guidance into the encoder via backpropagation. In Phase 2, the adapted LLaMA is frozen and continues to provide an SOT teacher signal (weighted by α) while the separator and CTC heads are trained, yielding a hybrid CTC–attention objective. At inference, LLaMA is completely absent. To handle variable talker counts without a priori knowledge, a Talker-Count Head (TCH) is introduced: an attentive-statistics pooling module over shared-encoder features that predicts binary logits (two vs. three talkers) and dynamically routes to the appropriate branch. Key Results: On LibriMix (clean condition, evaluation set), the proposed encoder-only model (ID-8, with 6 TCH Transformer layers) achieves WER of 4.1% on Libri2Mix and 14.3% on Libri3Mix. For comparison, the LLaMA-1B SOT baseline achieves 4.6%/21.6%, and the best LLM-based system (SOP-LLaMA-3B) achieves 3.6%/16.5%. In the noisy condition, the encoder-only model reaches 9.7%/24.5% vs. 11.3%/39.1% for LLaMA-1B SOT — a dramatic improvement on 3-talker. Real-time factor is 0.0043 vs. 0.1150 for the LLM baseline on Libri2Mix (26× faster). Training without LLM distillation (ID-5) completely fails, with WER exceeding 66–81% across conditions, confirming that semantic regularization is essential for CTC convergence under heavy overlap. Talker count accuracy reaches 97–100% for two-talker conditions but degrades to 88–94% for three-talker in the best TCH configuration (12 layers). Strengths: - The train-time distillation idea is well-motivated and cleanly executed: LLM cost is amortized into training only, yielding a 26× inference speedup with minimal accuracy loss on 2-talker and actual accuracy gains on 3-talker relative to the LLM-based baseline. - The ablation study is thorough — testing CTC-only training, varying encoder depths, and varying TCH depths — providing clear evidence that LLM distillation is the critical enabling component, not merely depth or architecture. - The three-talker scenario, where prior LLM-decoder approaches degrade badly (39.1% WER), is handled significantly better (14.3% WER), addressing the key failure mode of decoder-centric methods. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to a single, synthetic benchmark (LibriMix), which is constructed from clean audiobook recordings. Generalization to real conversational scenarios with natural reverberation, acoustic variety, and more than three talkers is not demonstrated. - The Talker-Count Head accuracy for three-talker mixtures remains substantially lower than for two-talker (88–94% vs. 97–100%), and TCH errors directly propagate to recognition errors — the gap between oracle talker-count (ID-6, WER 12.9%) and predicted count (ID-8, WER 14.3%) represents headroom that the paper acknowledges but does not close. - The framework is constrained to two discrete branches (2-talker and 3-talker) and does not generalize gracefully to arbitrary or unknown talker counts; extending beyond this binary routing is left as future work without a concrete design.
Search Results 2603.10587 PDF
#65 — Innovative (67)

GigaWorld-Policy: An Efficient Action-Centered World--Action Model (18 ▲ )

Angen Ye, Boyuan Wang, Chaojun Ni, Guan Huang, Guosheng Zhao, Hao Li, Hengtao Li, Jie Li, Jindi Lv, Jingyu Liu, Min Cao, Peng Li, Qiuping Deng, Wenjun Mei, Xiaofeng Wang, Xinze Chen, Xinyu Zhou, Yang Wang, Yifan Chang, Yifan Li, Yukun Zhou, Yun Ye, Zhichao Liu, Zheng Zhu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: GigaWorld-Policy's core insight — using future video prediction as dense training supervision while making it optional at inference via causal attention masking — is a clean and effective architectural contribution that directly solves the latency problem of prior world-action models. The 9x inference speedup with comparable performance and 10x data efficiency are strong empirical results. However, the idea is relatively straightforward once stated, the real-world evaluation is narrow (four tabletop tasks on one arm), and simulation performance slightly trails the slower Motus baseline.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation suffer from supervision sparsity: action labels are sparse and low-diversity relative to the rich visual observation space, causing models to collapse onto a small set of action prototypes rather than learning physically consistent policies. Prior World–Action Models (WAMs) address this by jointly predicting future video and actions, providing denser temporal supervision, but they require iterative diffusion sampling at inference time (causing high latency, e.g., 3231ms for Motus), and pixel-level prediction errors compound over long horizons, degrading action quality. Two-stage designs (predict video first, then decode actions via an inverse dynamics model) inherit these error propagation and latency problems. The paper asks: can future visual dynamics be used as a dense training signal without incurring video generation cost at inference? Methodology: GigaWorld-Policy is a causal sequence model built on a 5B-parameter diffusion Transformer backbone (Wan 2.2) that jointly models action tokens and future visual tokens under a blockwise causal attention mask. The key architectural insight is a strict causal ordering: observation tokens (T_o) and state tokens (T_s) are computed first; action tokens (T_a) attend only to T_o and T_s (no lookahead); future video tokens (T_f) attend to all of {T_o, T_s, T_a}, enabling action-conditioned dynamics prediction. This masking prevents information leakage from future frames into action generation and makes future-video prediction optional at inference — the model can run in action-only mode, decoding only T_a via flow matching in ~360ms. Multi-view camera inputs (left, front, right) are spatially composed into a single composite image to enable cross-view reasoning without modifying the backbone. Sparse future frame prediction (one frame every Δ=12 steps over a p=48 action horizon) reduces redundancy while preserving temporal supervision. Training follows a three-stage curriculum: (1) initialization from a web-video generative model (Wan), (2) embodied data pre-training on ~10,000 hours of robot and egocentric human video across 9 datasets, and (3) task-specific post-training on robot trajectory data with combined flow-matching objectives weighted λ_action=5, λ_video=1. Key Results: On the RoboTwin 2.0 simulation benchmark (50 manipulation tasks, clean and randomized scenes), GigaWorld-Policy achieves average success rates of 0.86/0.85 (clean/randomized), comparable to Motus (0.89/0.87) while delivering 9× faster inference (360ms vs. 3231ms on A100). It improves over π0.5 by 44 percentage points. In four real-world tasks on an AgileX PiPER 6-DoF arm (Clean Desk, Scan QR Code, Sweep Trash, Stack Bowls), GigaWorld-Policy achieves 0.83 average success rate vs. Motus 0.76 (+7%) and π0.5 0.69 (+14%), while being 9× faster than Motus. Data efficiency experiments show GigaWorld-Policy matches π0.5's peak performance using only 10% of training demonstrations. Ablations confirm that future video prediction adds +0.18 SR over no future prediction (0.83 vs. 0.65), the optimal stride is Δ=12 predicting 4 frames, causal masking improves video generation quality (PSNR 28.41 vs. 27.87, SSIM 0.901 vs. 0.892), and the full pre-training curriculum (web video init + embodied pre-training) yields 0.83 SR vs. 0.45 from scratch. Strengths: - Elegant decoupling of training-time and inference-time compute: future video prediction provides rich dense supervision during training but is entirely optional at inference, solving the core latency problem of prior WAMs without architectural complexity. - The causal attention mask is a principled design that enforces the correct factorization (actions depend on observations, future frames depend on actions), preventing information leakage and yielding measurably better video prediction quality. - Strong empirical efficiency: 10× data efficiency over a competitive VLA baseline and consistent improvement with embodied pre-training scale, suggesting the approach transfers knowledge effectively from diverse video sources. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation scope is limited: only four real-world tasks on a single 6-DoF arm platform (AgileX PiPER), all involving relatively structured tabletop manipulation. Generalization to unstructured environments, dexterous hands, or bimanual systems is not demonstrated. - The paper does not analyze failure modes or robustness to out-of-distribution scenarios beyond the domain randomization in RoboTwin 2.0 simulation. The mechanism by which future video supervision improves actions (beyond the ablation showing it helps) is not analyzed or interpreted. - GigaWorld-Policy's simulation average (0.86) is slightly below Motus (0.89), and on several fine-grained tasks (Hanging Mug, Blocks Ranking Size, Stack Blocks Three) performance drops substantially below Motus, suggesting the method does not uniformly dominate and may trade some precision tasks for speed.
Recommended 2603.17240 PDF
#66 — Innovative (66)

3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model (39 ▲ )

Hyun-kyu Ko, Jihyeon Park, Younghyun Kim, Dongheok Park, Eunbyung Park · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The 1-frame optimization paradigm is a clean and practical insight — decoupling spatial identity from temporal dynamics to avoid temporal overfitting without multi-view video data is well-motivated and effective. The asymmetrical conditioning strategy in 3Dapter is a nice engineering contribution that meaningfully reduces fine-tuning cost. However, the reliance on a self-constructed benchmark limits evaluation confidence, and the overall framework is more of a careful recombination of existing techniques (DreamBooth-style fine-tuning, joint attention, selective routing) than a fundamentally new approach.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing subject-driven video generation methods treat subjects as 2D entities, relying on single-view visual features or text prompts to encode identity. Because real-world subjects are inherently 3D, these 2D-centric approaches lack the spatial priors needed to reconstruct geometry, forcing models to hallucinate plausible but arbitrary details for unseen viewpoints. The core challenge is achieving genuine 3D-aware video customization without large-scale multi-view video datasets, which remain scarce. Fine-tuning on limited video sequences tends to cause temporal overfitting, degrading motion quality. Methodology: The paper introduces a two-component framework. The first component, 3DreamBooth, decouples spatial geometry from temporal motion via a 1-frame optimization paradigm: rather than fine-tuning on video sequences, it restricts weight updates to spatial (frame-level) representations while leaving temporal modules frozen. This "bakes" a 3D prior into the base model (HunyuanVideo) using only multi-view still images, avoiding temporal overfitting entirely. A learnable [v] identifier token captures subject identity. The second component, 3Dapter, is a visual conditioning module that provides fine-grained texture guidance and accelerates convergence. It uses multi-view joint attention with shared weights and an asymmetrical conditioning strategy: pre-trained on single-view conditioning, then jointly optimized with the main generation branch across multi-view inputs. At inference, 3Dapter acts as a dynamic selective router, querying view-specific geometric hints from a minimal multi-view reference set (as few as 4 views). The combined system trains 3DreamBooth at only 400 steps with 3Dapter initialization rather than 1600 steps standalone. Key Results: Evaluation is conducted on the authors' 3D-CustomBench benchmark. Against VACE (strongest baseline), 3DreamBooth+3Dapter achieves: CLIP-I 0.8871 (vs. 0.8964), DINO-I 0.7420 (vs. 0.7395), GPT-4o holistic rating 4.57±0.04 (vs. 3.95±0.11), and shape accuracy rating 4.80 vs. 4.39. For 3D geometric fidelity (Chamfer Distance), the full system achieves CD=0.0177 (accuracy 0.0182, completeness 0.0172), versus 3DreamBooth alone at CD=0.0239 — a 26% improvement. Video quality metrics include motion smoothness 0.9918, aesthetic quality 0.5920, and imaging quality 73.34. Ablation confirms that 3Dapter at 400 steps outperforms 3DreamBooth alone at 1600 steps. The framework generalizes to WanVideo 2.1 (720p), demonstrating model agnosticity. Strengths: - The 1-frame optimization paradigm is a principled and practical solution to temporal overfitting: by keeping temporal modules frozen, it sidesteps the need for multi-view video data entirely while still producing temporally coherent outputs. - 3Dapter's asymmetrical conditioning strategy is clever — single-view pre-training provides a strong initialization that transfers cleanly to multi-view joint optimization, dramatically reducing required fine-tuning steps (400 vs. 1600). - The GPT-4o holistic rating gain (+0.62 over VACE) is large and meaningful; the framework is also shown to generalize across video generation backbones (HunyuanVideo and WanVideo 2.1). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark (3D-CustomBench) appears to be introduced by the authors themselves, raising questions about evaluation objectivity and generalizability; comparison against established video generation benchmarks (e.g., EvalCrafter, VBench) is absent. - Despite beating VACE on most metrics, CLIP-I is slightly lower (0.8871 vs. 0.8964), suggesting some trade-off in 2D appearance fidelity for 3D consistency; the paper does not deeply analyze failure cases or the regime where 2D-centric baselines win.
Recommended 2603.18524 PDF
#67 — Innovative (66)

BenchPreS: A Benchmark for Context-Aware Personalized Preference Selectivity of Persistent-Memory LLMs (15 ▲ )

Sangyeon Yoon, Sunkyoung Kim, Hyesoo Hong, Wonje Jeung, Yongil Kim, Wooseok Seo, Heuiyeen Yeen, Albert No · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: BenchPreS identifies a genuinely novel and practically important evaluation dimension — preference selectivity — that is orthogonal to all prior personalization benchmarks. The core finding that AAR and MR move together across all frontier models, meaning no model has learned true contextual selectivity, is non-obvious and actionable. The benchmark design is rigorous with unanimous three-way annotation. Limited by small scale and the absence of any proposed solution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs increasingly maintain persistent user preferences (tone, style, role, markers, nickname) to personalize responses across interactions. As these models are deployed as agents for third-party communication — automated email replies, legal filings, institutional correspondence — a critical question arises: should all stored preferences always be applied? A user who prefers jokes and emojis in casual chat should not have those preferences reflected in a letter to an IRS agent. Existing personalization benchmarks (LAMP, PrefEval, FollowBench) implicitly assume preferences should always be applied and measure adherence accordingly. No benchmark evaluates the complementary and arguably more practically important capability: knowing when to suppress preferences. BenchPreS fills this gap. Methodology: BenchPreS is structured around 10 user profiles × 39 communication contexts (recipient–task pairs), spanning five formal domains (finance/legal, health, education, employment, housing). Each user profile contains ~152 memory attributes, of which 5 are preference attributes (role, style, tone, markers, nickname), drawn from ChatGPT's personality customization taxonomy. Gold labels g(t,a) ∈ {0,1} indicating whether each preference should be applied in each context were assigned by human annotators requiring unanimous three-way agreement; ambiguous or culturally variable cases were excluded. This yields 1,950 attribute-level evaluation instances with an average of 1.7 appropriate and 3.3 inappropriate preferences per context. Evaluation uses an LLM-as-Judge framework (DeepSeek-R1) with 92–95% agreement against human and GPT-4o-mini annotators. Two metrics are defined: Misapplication Rate (MR) — the fraction of should-be-suppressed preferences that are incorrectly applied — and Appropriate Application Rate (AAR) — the fraction of should-be-applied preferences that are correctly applied. Ideal behavior is low MR and high AAR. Ten frontier models are evaluated including Gemini 3 Pro, GPT-5.2, Claude-4.5 Sonnet, DeepSeek V3.2, Qwen3 235B (reasoning and non-reasoning), K-EXAONE-236B, Llama-3.3 70B, and Mistral 7B, accessed via OpenRouter at temperature 1.0 with three samples per instance. Key Results: No model achieves the desirable combination of high AAR and low MR. The dominant pattern across all models is that AAR and MR move together — stronger preference application correlates with more misapplication, not with better selectivity. Gemini 3 Pro achieves the highest AAR (88.69%) but also the highest MR (86.48%), indicating near-indiscriminate application. Mistral 7B achieves the lowest MR (38.49%) but also the lowest AAR (49.77%), indicating its low misapplication stems from weak preference following rather than selectivity. GPT-5.2 achieves the best AAR–MR gap (46.38 pp: 87.33% AAR, 40.95% MR), yet still misapplies suppressed preferences in over 40% of cases. Qwen3 235B Thinking yields a negative gap (−1.77), applying inappropriate preferences more often than appropriate ones. Enabling reasoning mode increases both AAR and MR in parallel (no selectivity gain). Prompt-based mitigation (explicit instructions to suppress inappropriate preferences) reduces MR substantially — by 19–74 pp across frontier models — at the cost of only 0.78–3.82 pp AAR reduction, but residual misapplication remains (12–41% MR post-mitigation), and effectiveness varies widely across models. Surface-level preferences (markers/emojis, nicknames) show consistently higher MR across all models compared to role and style preferences. Task completeness analysis shows GPT-5.2 maintains or improves task quality with memory active (4.957 vs. 4.925), while Gemini 3 Pro degrades notably (3.746 vs. 4.855). Strengths: - Identifies a concrete, practically important failure mode (preference over-application in formal contexts) that is orthogonal to what all prior personalization benchmarks measure, and operationalizes it with clean, well-motivated metrics (MR and AAR). - Rigorous gold labeling through unanimous three-way human annotation with deliberate exclusion of culturally ambiguous cases, validated against LLM judge with high agreement (92–95%), lending credibility to the evaluation signal. - Comprehensive model coverage (10 frontier models spanning reasoning/non-reasoning, proprietary/open, multiple scales) with controlled ablations on reasoning mode and prompt-based mitigation that yield actionable and non-obvious findings. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Small benchmark scale (10 profiles × 39 contexts × 5 preferences = 1,950 instances) concentrated entirely in formal communication domains; informal, social, or culturally nuanced settings — where selectivity failures may be equally prevalent — are explicitly excluded, limiting generalization claims. - The benchmark evaluates only the final generation stage and does not address settings where preferences are retrieved selectively rather than prepended wholesale; retrieval-based memory systems, which dominate production deployments, are out of scope. - The paper diagnoses the problem and identifies a promising training-data direction (context-aware reasoning traces in post-training data) but does not propose, implement, or validate any method that actually improves preference selectivity — leaving the core challenge unresolved.
Recommended 2603.16557 PDF
#68 — Innovative (66)

TaxBreak: Unmasking the Hidden Costs of LLM Inference Through Overhead Decomposition

Prabhu Vellaisamy, Shreesh Tripathi, Vignesh Natarajan, Surya Santhan Thenarasu, Shawn Blanton, John P. Shen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: TaxBreak introduces a well-principled three-component decomposition of host-side orchestration overhead that is formally grounded and collectively exhaustive, turning a vague 'framework tax' into mechanism-level attribution. The cross-platform CPU experiment produces a genuinely non-obvious finding — that CPU single-core speed dominates GPU clock for MoE latency — which has practical implications for hardware procurement. The methodology is sound but ultimately a measurement tool rather than a new optimization, and the CUDA-only scope limits generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference latency in interactive and agentic deployments is increasingly dominated by host-side (CPU) orchestration overhead rather than GPU compute. Existing profiling approaches expose this cost only as either a coarse aggregate residual ("framework tax") or as a single launch/queue metric (TKLQT), neither of which identifies which layer of the software stack is the bottleneck. This attribution gap is particularly acute for mixture-of-experts (MoE) models, where sparse routing fragments execution into thousands of small, dependency-chained kernels per token, making the host-dispatch path a first-order performance constraint even on high-end GPUs. Methodology: TaxBreak is a trace-driven profiling methodology that decomposes host-visible orchestration overhead per kernel invocation into three mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive components: (1) ΔFT — framework translation time, comprising Python dispatch overhead (TPy) plus irreducible ATen dispatch cost; (2) ΔCT — CUDA library translation time, the additional front-end overhead (heuristic selection, descriptor setup) incurred by vendor-library-mediated kernels (cuBLAS, cuDNN), gated to zero for framework-native kernels; and (3) ΔKT — kernel launch-path floor, the hardware-level cost from cudaLaunchKernel call to GPU kernel start, measured via null-kernel profiling. The measurement pipeline has two phases: a full-model trace under PyTorch Profiler to capture kernel sequences and Python-layer timings, followed by isolation replay of each unique kernel under Nsight Systems to cleanly separate dispatch and launch-floor costs without queue interference. From these components the authors derive the Host–Device Balance Index (HDBI = TDeviceActive / (TDeviceActive + TOrchestration)), a scalar ratio in (0,1) that summarizes whether a workload is host- or device-bound. Experiments span Llama-3.2-1B/3B (dense) and OLMoE-1B/7B, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B (MoE) on NVIDIA H100 and H200 systems in both prefill and decode phases. Key Results: MoE models dispatch 8–11× more GPU kernels per output token than dense models of comparable activated parameter count (9,305 vs. 848 for OLMoE vs. Llama-3.2-1B at BS=4/SL=2048), with OLMoE-1B/7B issuing 93,053 total kernel launches vs. 8,475 for Llama-3.2-1B in a 10-token decode run. Despite 11× more launches, MoE kernel diversity ratios are lower (0.002 vs. 0.009), confirming fragmentation arises from repeated invocations of a small routing/GEMM kernel set, not architectural heterogeneity. MoE GPU utilization consequently falls to 15.5% vs. 58.9% for dense. The null-kernel hardware floor is stable at ~4.7 µs on H100 and ~4.5 µs on H200; GEMM families (cuBLAS) deviate most at 36–40% above floor. HDBI analysis shows dense models transition from host-bound at small decode (HDBI≈0.23) to device-dominant at large batch/context (HDBI≈0.97), while MoE models remain persistently host-bound (HDBI≈0.10–0.15) across all tested configurations. A cross-platform CPU comparison (H100 paired with Intel Xeon 8480C vs. H200 paired with Intel Xeon Gold 6538Y+, with H200 GPU running 9.9% slower clock) shows that faster single-thread CPU performance reduces TOrchestration by 10–29% and improves end-to-end latency by 11–14% for MoE workloads, even as TDeviceActive is 8% worse — demonstrating that for host-bound workloads the CPU is a first-order design parameter. FlashAttention-2 reduces end-to-end latency by 68.6% at BS=8/SL=2048 primarily through device-side work reduction, not host overhead elimination, a distinction TaxBreak makes explicit. Strengths: - The three-component decomposition (ΔFT, ΔCT, ΔKT) is formally grounded, mutually exclusive, and collectively exhaustive, turning a coarse host-vs-device diagnosis into actionable mechanism-level attribution that directly suggests the correct optimization target (runtime compilation, kernel fusion, or CUDA Graphs). - The cross-platform CPU experiment is well-controlled and produces a genuinely non-obvious, practically important finding: for MoE inference, CPU single-core speed dominates GPU clock rate as a latency lever, with quantitative evidence that a slower GPU paired with a faster CPU wins end-to-end. - Empirical breadth is solid — prefill and decode, dense and MoE, two Hopper-generation GPU platforms, multiple batch sizes and sequence lengths — and the HDBI diagnostic is shown to correctly predict where hardware improvements will and will not transfer to latency gains. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The methodology is strictly NVIDIA/CUDA-specific (relies on NVTX, CUPTI, nsys), with no path to AMD ROCm or other accelerator ecosystems; the authors acknowledge future work on MI300X but the current tool has no portability. - Isolation replay attribution can be imperfect for autotuned or synchronization-heavy kernels (acknowledged), and the kernel matching fallback hierarchy introduces a potential for misattribution when autotuning selects a different variant than the original trace — the magnitude of this error is not quantified. - The study targets eager-mode PyTorch exclusively; compiled-mode (torch.compile, CUDA Graphs) execution, which is increasingly the production default for static-shape workloads, is discussed but not profiled, leaving open the question of how TaxBreak's decomposition applies to captured/replayed graph execution where the host dispatch structure changes fundamentally.
Search Results 2603.12465 PDF
#69 — Innovative (66)

Persistent Story World Simulation with Continuous Character Customization

Jinlu Zhang, Qiyun Wang, Baoxiang Du, Jiayi Ji, Jing He, Rongsheng Zhang, Tangjie Lv, Xiaoshuai Sun, Rongrong Ji · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The orthogonal subspace decomposition for interference-free continual LoRA learning is mathematically principled and elegantly eliminates the soft regularization that causes identity drift in prior continual learning methods. The MLLM-as-Judge quality gating is a pragmatic and novel training controller. However, the system is primarily a well-engineered integration of existing components (LoRA, region-based sampling, VLM scoring) rather than a fundamentally new approach, and evaluation on only 14 stylized cartoon characters leaves scalability to realistic settings undemonstrated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Story visualization—synthesizing coherent image sequences depicting user-specified characters faithful to a narrative—requires simultaneously maintaining inter-frame character identity and text-semantic alignment across an expanding cast. Existing approaches split into two camps, each with a fundamental gap: open-ended (training-free) methods lack the mechanism to extract identity from user-provided reference images, while closed-world tuning-based methods cannot generalize to unseen characters without full retraining. Recent continual learning (CL) approaches for concept customization partially address expansion but rely on soft regularization that allows gradual identity drift as the character pool grows—a critical failure mode for storytelling that demands unwavering consistency. Methodology: EverTale proposes three tightly integrated components built on top of a Stable Diffusion 1.5 backbone. First, the All-in-One-World Character Integrator (CI) encodes all characters into a single shared LoRA module by decomposing each character's A-matrix as A_i = D_i L_i, where D_i matrices are mutually orthogonal columns drawn via QR decomposition of a random Gaussian pool. During inference, character i's input is projected through P_i = D_i D_i^T, ensuring that updates for character j (j ≠ i) produce zero interference on character i's output branch. This eliminates the need for per-character LoRA modules or post-hoc gradient fusion, which are the dominant sources of identity blending in prior work. Second, the Character Quality Gate (CQG) employs Qwen-VL 2.5 as an MLLM-as-Judge: at the end of each character's training iteration, the MLLM generates an out-of-distribution prompt, EverTale synthesizes an image, and the MLLM performs chain-of-thought scoring on three criteria (character attendance, visual consistency, style alignment). If the mean score across five evaluations falls below threshold τ=3, learning rate and steps are reduced exponentially and the character is retrained, capping at five attempts. Third, Character-Aware Region-Focus Sampling (CA-RF) decomposes multi-character generation into two stages: global context synthesis (LoRA branch disabled) establishes scene layout, followed by local character-specific enhancement in designated bounding-box regions. Early denoising timesteps confine self-attention KV context to each character's region to prevent identity blending; later timesteps expand to global context for natural inter-character composition. Key Results: On the TBC-Bench benchmark (Pororo: 9 characters; Frozen: 5 characters), EverTale achieves best single-character D-I of 64.65 on Pororo (+6.35% vs. FLUX.1 Kontext, the next best) and 53.02 on Frozen (+2.60% vs. StoryWeaver), while simultaneously achieving the highest CLIP text-image alignment (C-T: 34.42/34.21) on both datasets—demonstrating that the identity–semantics tradeoff is substantially mitigated. In multi-character evaluation, EverTale achieves the best CLIP-image alignment (C-I: 77.40/78.46) and highest user study scores (C-A: 4.06, S-A: 4.54, V-Q: 4.06 out of 5) at a competitive 15-second inference time, versus Mix-of-Show's 15-minute LoRA merge plus 11-second sampling. Ablation confirms each component is necessary: removing CI drops D-I from 58.84 to 34.30; fixed training without CQG leads to either underfitting (300 steps) or overfitting (1000 steps) that depresses C-T by 6 points; removing CA-RF collapses multi-character D-I from 48.57 to 27.24. Strengths: - The orthogonal subspace decomposition for interference-free continual LoRA learning is mathematically principled and eliminates the need for any auxiliary regularization loss, which prior CL methods depend on and which empirically fails as character count grows. - MLLM-as-Judge quality gating is a practical and adaptive training controller that handles character difficulty heterogeneity without manual tuning; the CQG converges within 3 attempts for all characters, demonstrating reliability at scale. - CA-RF achieves competitive multi-character synthesis using only coarse bounding-box inputs, avoiding the dense spatial constraints (keypose maps, segmentation masks) required by Mix-of-Show and LoRA-Composer, substantially reducing annotation burden. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation dataset (TBC-Bench) is small—14 characters total from two animated properties—and characters are stylized (cartoon/CGI), leaving open whether the orthogonal projection mechanism scales without degradation to photorealistic humans or very large character pools where r1 approaches d. - Despite competitive SD1.5 results, the paper acknowledges computational constraints prevented full evaluation on DiT-based backbones (FLUX, SD3), and the mathematical orthogonality argument assumes r1 ≪ d; behavior when this assumption breaks is addressed only with heuristic fallback strategies in the appendix, not empirically validated.
Search Results 2603.16285 PDF
#70 — Innovative (65)

GPrune-LLM: Generalization-Aware Structured Pruning for Large Language Models

Xiaoyun Liu, Divya Saxena, Jiannong Cao, Yuqing Zhao, Yiying Dong, Penghui Ruan · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
