Why this rank: GPrune-LLM contributes a well-motivated mechanistic insight — that neurons differ in distribution sensitivity, invalidating the implicit uniformity assumption of shared-space importance ranking — and translates it into a principled two-stage framework with consistent gains across models and metrics. The plug-and-play nature and strong high-sparsity recoveries are practically valuable, but the ~1 hour preprocessing overhead, ~30% module instability across calibration pairs, and exclusion of attention heads from modularization temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Structured pruning of LLMs relies on activation-based importance metrics computed over a small calibration dataset to identify and remove FFN neurons and attention heads. This creates calibration bias: importance rankings are implicitly conditioned on calibration distribution, and when that distribution diverges from downstream tasks, generalization degrades. Prior remedies focused on better calibration data selection. This paper instead targets the pruning mechanism itself, asking whether the ranking procedure can be made intrinsically more robust to calibration bias. The authors identify two structural failure modes: (1) cross-behavior ranking interference — distribution-sensitive neurons that strongly activate on calibration inputs dominate the shared ranking space, crowding out distribution-robust neurons that are broadly important across tasks; and (2) local ranking distortion — distribution-sensitive neurons that activate infrequently on calibration data receive too little signal for reliable local ranking even within their group. Methodology: GPrune-LLM is a two-stage plug-and-play framework applied on top of existing importance metrics (e.g., FLAP, Wanda-sp). Stage 1, Behavior-Consistent Modularization (BCM), partitions FFN neurons into modules whose members share similar cross-distribution behavior. It jointly models two signals: data-free parameter cosine similarity (a proxy for co-activation affinity independent of calibration data) and cross-dataset rank drift — the normalized absolute rank shift of each neuron between two calibration datasets. Module initialization uses KMeans clustering on parameter similarity with silhouette-based K selection, followed by drift-heterogeneity splits. A differentiable refinement stage then jointly optimizes compactness (Linner), worst-module penalty (Lpair), rank-drift consistency (Lconsis), and centroid repulsion (Lrep) via soft membership probabilities and Adam. Stage 2, Reliability-Aware Modular Pruning (RMP), evaluates per-module metric reliability using module mean rank drift and mean activation score. Only modules with high drift AND low activation score switch from the activation-based metric to an activation-independent one (weight-norm). Module-wise pruning thresholds are then learned via straight-through estimators under an augmented Lagrangian sparsity constraint, enabling adaptive per-module sparsity allocation within a global budget. Key Results: Experiments cover LLaMA1/2-7B, LLaMA2-13B, Vicuna1.5-7B, and LLaVA1.5-7B (multimodal), without any post-pruning fine-tuning. GPrune-LLM consistently improves both WikiText2/PTB perplexity and 7-task commonsense accuracy over its base metric at all retention ratios. Gains are most dramatic at high sparsity: on LLaMA2-7B at 50% retention, GPrune-LLM (Wanda-sp) reduces WikiText2 PPL from 262.32 to 50.65 (>80% reduction) and lifts average accuracy by 6.64%. On LLaMA2-13B at 30% retention, FLAP collapses to PPL 2676.82 while GPrune-LLM (FLAP) recovers it to 113.96, raising accuracy by 5.60%. On LLaVA1.5-7B at 80% retention, overall capability retention improves from ~80% (both base metrics) to 90.44% (Wanda-sp) and 86.75% (FLAP). Inference throughput at 50% retention exceeds 50% speedup over the dense baseline and is nearly identical to base metrics (~165 vs. 167 tokens/s for Wanda-sp variants on LLaMA2-7B). A notable secondary effect is that GPrune-LLM substantially narrows the performance gap between different importance metrics: on Vicuna1.5-7B at 70% retention, the accuracy gap between Wanda-sp and FLAP shrinks from 2.80% to 0.30% after applying GPrune-LLM. Ablations confirm all four components contribute, with adaptive sparsity learning being particularly critical (removing it raises WikiText2 PPL from 50.65 to 174.63 on LLaMA2-7B at 50%). Strengths: - The core insight — that neurons differ in distribution sensitivity and that this heterogeneity invalidates the implicit uniformity assumption of shared-space ranking — is mechanistically well-motivated and empirically validated with Kendall rank correlation analysis. - The framework is plug-and-play: it wraps any existing activation-based importance metric and produces consistent gains without requiring fine-tuning, making it practically deployable on top of FLAP, Wanda-sp, or future metrics. - The use of two complementary calibration datasets to measure rank drift is a lightweight but effective way to expose distribution sensitivity without requiring multiple target-task datasets; the cross-dataset module overlap analysis (Table 13, ~70% matched accuracy) shows the learned modules reflect genuine model structure rather than calibration artifacts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method requires two calibration datasets and additional preprocessing (BCM takes ~1 hour on LLaMA2-7B on an A6000); this overhead is non-trivial in practice and the sensitivity to the choice of auxiliary dataset (Table 6 shows absolute results vary with calibration pair) is not fully resolved. - Module boundaries show ~30% instability across dataset pairs (matched IoU ~0.48), and gains are noticeably weaker on LLaMA1-7B due to less structured neuron modularity — the method's effectiveness thus depends on model-level properties the practitioner cannot control, and there is no lightweight diagnostic to predict in advance whether BCM will be effective. - Attention heads are not modularized; a single per-layer threshold is used for heads, meaning the cross-behavior heterogeneity analysis and reliability-aware metric switching apply only to FFN neurons, leaving a significant architectural component outside the framework's core contribution.
Recommended 2603.13418 PDF
#71 — Innovative (65)

ARES: Adaptive Reasoning Effort Selection for Efficient LLM Agents

Jingbo Yang, Bairu Hou, Wei Wei, Yujia Bao, Shiyu Chang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: ARES presents a meaningful contribution by formulating per-step reasoning effort selection as a sequential decision problem within multi-turn agent settings, which is a genuinely underexplored angle compared to single-turn adaptive compute work. The decoupled data pipeline and RL refinement via GRPO are well-engineered, and the cross-scale generalization result is encouraging. However, the core idea of routing inputs to different compute tiers based on predicted difficulty is well-established in the broader efficiency literature, and the evaluation is confined to a single model family with only three benchmarks, limiting claims of generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern LLM agents powered by chain-of-thought reasoning incur substantial inference costs because they accumulate large numbers of reasoning tokens across multi-step trajectories. Many state-of-the-art LLMs (e.g., GPT-5, Gemini-3) expose configurable reasoning levels (high/medium/low), but static strategies are ineffective: always using low effort causes ~20% accuracy drops, while random selection provides neither accuracy nor meaningful cost savings. The key insight is that not all steps in an agentic task are equally hard — navigating a complex web structure demands intensive reasoning, while opening a target URL does not. Prior work on adaptive reasoning targets single-turn settings (math, code), leaving the multi-turn, error-propagation-sensitive agent setting largely unaddressed. Cross-model routing approaches also suffer from non-monotonic cost-performance tradeoffs and lack KV cache reuse. Methodology: ARES introduces a lightweight router model (Qwen3-1.7B) that runs alongside the main LLM agent and predicts, at each step, the minimum sufficient reasoning effort level (low/medium/high) given the full interaction history and current observation. The router is trained via a three-phase data pipeline: (1) Trajectory Collection — successful, minimal-step trajectories are collected under high-effort mode to serve as reference paths; (2) Reasoning Effort Annotation — for each step, the agent is sampled K=3 times at each effort level and the lowest level achieving majority-correct actions is labeled as the supervision target, with domain-specific equivalence checks for tool-use, web, and deep-research agents; (3) Rationale Generation — a powerful teacher LLM generates concise (3–5 sentence) chain-of-thought justifications for each label, which the router learns to produce before predicting the effort level. The router is first fine-tuned with cross-entropy loss (SFT), then further optimized with GRPO reinforcement learning using a composite reward: outcome reward (+5.0 for task success), a normalized per-step cost penalty (−0.2/−0.5/−1.0 for low/medium/high), and a format penalty. RL data is filtered to exclude unsolvable tasks and to focus on high-reward-variance prompts where effort selection actually matters. Because ARES operates within a single model's thinking levels rather than routing across different models, KV cache can be reused across effort levels, avoiding redundant context re-encoding. Key Results: ARES is evaluated on TAU-Bench (tool-use, retail and airline domains), BrowseComp-Plus (deep research), and WebArena (web navigation), all using gpt-oss-20b as the backbone. After SFT: on TAU-Bench Retail, ARES matches the high-effort baseline at 54.8% accuracy while reducing total reasoning tokens by 35.2% (652k vs. 1007k); on BrowseComp-Plus, ARES achieves 41.3% vs. 42.7% for high-effort while cutting tokens by 41.8% (1071k vs. 1841k); on WebArena, ARES surpasses the high-effort baseline (46.5% vs. 45.0%) while using 45.3% fewer tokens. The headline claim of 52.7% token reduction refers to the RL-trained variant on TAU-Bench. After RL: on TAU-Bench Retail, ARES-RL improves accuracy from 54.8% (SFT) to 58.5% while further reducing tokens from 652k to 476k; on Airline, accuracy rises from 36.0% to 42.0% while tokens drop from 678k to 133k (an ~80% reduction). Cross-scale generalization experiments show that a router trained on gpt-oss-20b transfers to gpt-oss-120b, achieving 65.2% accuracy (vs. 67.8% high-effort ceiling) at 23% lower token cost. Ablations confirm that both SFT (+13.1% accuracy vs. zero-shot router) and rationale generation (+3.5% vs. direct label prediction) are important components. The RL training also corrects an "overthinking" phenomenon in the Airline domain, where high effort actually hurts performance, by shifting selection from >50% high-effort (post-SFT) to ~70% low-effort. Strengths: - Cleanly formulates per-step reasoning effort selection as a sequential decision problem with a well-defined, monotonic cost-performance frontier, avoiding the non-monotonic pitfalls of cross-model routing. - The decoupled data generation pipeline — collecting successful trajectories first, then independently labeling minimum effort per step — is practically elegant and avoids the exponential |E|^T search space. - RL training with GRPO and a normalized cost reward demonstrably pushes the Pareto frontier beyond what SFT alone achieves, simultaneously improving accuracy and reducing cost in multiple domains, including a counterintuitive correction of overthinking behavior. - Plug-and-play design: the router is model-agnostic and can leverage external trajectory datasets (e.g., APIGen-MT) without expensive self-sampling, and generalizes across model scales. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The router itself adds latency and token overhead, though smaller than proprietary router models; this overhead is not fully quantified as a fraction of end-to-end wall-clock latency, making real deployment cost savings hard to assess. - Evaluation is limited to three benchmarks and a single backbone family (gpt-oss); generalization to open-weight models with different thinking-level APIs (e.g., DeepSeek-R1's budget tokens, Qwen's thinking mode) is untested and may require retraining. - The effort annotation pipeline assumes discrete, fixed levels (low/medium/high) rather than continuous budget control, which may leave efficiency gains on the table compared to fine-grained token-budget approaches. - RL evaluation is only reported on TAU-Bench (two domains); it is unclear whether the RL gains extend to the more complex BrowseComp-Plus and WebArena environments.
Citations 2603.07915 PDF
#72 — Innovative (65)

Beyond Local Patterns: Multiscale Inconsistency Learning for Graph Anomaly Detection

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: MI-GAD's core insight — that GNN neighborhood smoothing is architecturally misaligned with anomaly detection — is well-articulated and empirically validated, and the resulting GNN-free framework is refreshingly simple and scalable. However, the individual components (MLP encoding, hypersphere normalization, one-class center deviation) are well-established techniques recombined rather than fundamentally new, placing this as a solid integrative contribution rather than a methodological breakthrough.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Unsupervised graph anomaly detection (UGAD) aims to identify nodes that deviate significantly from normal structural or attribute patterns, with applications in fraud detection, spam mitigation, and financial security. Existing methods uncritically transplant graph representation learning paradigms—GNN backbones, reconstruction losses, and contrastive pretext tasks—into the anomaly detection setting, despite a fundamental mismatch: GNNs are designed for neighborhood smoothing, which dilutes anomaly signals rather than amplifying them. A second gap is the reliance on purely local inconsistency signals, which fail on "community camouflage" anomalies—anomalous nodes that mimic the local similarity patterns of normal nodes and are therefore indistinguishable from their neighbors. The authors demonstrate empirically that on the T-Finance dataset, abnormal node pairs actually exhibit higher pairwise similarity than normal node pairs, making local affinity useless in isolation. Methodology: The paper proposes MI-GAD (Multiscale Inconsistency learning for Graph Anomaly Detection), a GNN-free framework built on four components. (1) Individual Node Encoding: a plain MLP encodes each node's attributes independently, deliberately avoiding the neighborhood aggregation that corrupts anomaly signals in GNNs. A theoretical result (Theorem 1) formalizes that GNN message passing implicitly minimizes a local affinity objective but does so with reduced flexibility and signal quality compared to an explicit, decoupled formulation. (2) Hypersphere Space Mapping: all node embeddings are L2-normalized onto a unit hypersphere to prevent representation collapse, equalize norms, and provide a uniform geometric space for both local and global scoring. (3) Local Affinity Discrimination (LAD): inspired by group discrimination, a binary cross-entropy loss trains the model to assign high similarity to neighbor pairs (positive) and low similarity to non-neighbor pairs (negative), sampled to match neighbor count for scalability. This produces a local anomaly score equal to the mean neighbor cosine similarity. (4) Global Inconsistency Modeling (GIM): a learnable representation center c is defined as the mean of all hypersphere embeddings; node deviation JND = ||h̃_i − c||² measures each node's distance from the global normal distribution centroid, analogous to one-class classification (SVDD/DeepSVDD). Minimizing the mean deviation pulls normal nodes toward c while anomalies remain distant. Final anomaly scores additively combine the local affinity score and the node deviation, weighted by a scalar λ. Total time complexity is O(ND² + ND + ED), linear in graph size for fixed embedding dimension. Key Results: MI-GAD is evaluated on seven real-world datasets (Facebook, Reddit, Amazon, Yelp, T-Finance, Elliptic, T-Social—the last two with millions of nodes) against eight baselines including DOMINANT, CoLA, PREM, VGOD, TAM, GADAM, HUGE, and SmoothGNN. MI-GAD achieves the best AUROC on all seven datasets: 98.37% on Facebook (+1.94% over HUGE), 61.07% on Reddit, 90.99% on Amazon (+2.34% over HUGE), 63.84% on Yelp (+6.36% over SmoothGNN), 94.65% on T-Finance (+18.80% over SmoothGNN, the largest margin), 78.38% on Elliptic (+12.30% over SmoothGNN), and 69.52% on T-Social (+3.65% over SmoothGNN). AUPRC improvements are equally strong (51.79% on Facebook vs. 30.10% for GADAM; 63.45% on T-Finance vs. 14.09% for SmoothGNN). Several baselines run out of memory (24 GB GPU) on large graphs while MI-GAD scales to 5M-node T-Social. Ablation confirms all three non-trivial components (MLP backbone, hypersphere mapping, both LAD and GIM) are necessary; removing GIM causes catastrophic failure on T-Finance (21.32% AUROC), while removing LAD causes collapse on Facebook (33.91%). Strengths: - The core insight—that GNN smoothing is architecturally misaligned with anomaly detection—is both theoretically grounded (Theorem 1 connecting graph convolution to implicit local affinity optimization) and empirically validated with a clean GNN-vs-MLP ablation before the main method is introduced. - The global inconsistency module (node deviation) directly addresses community camouflage, a well-documented failure mode of local-only methods, and the combination of local and global signals produces complementary gains confirmed by ablation. - The method is simple, scalable (linear complexity in nodes and edges), and memory-efficient; it is one of the few that successfully handles the 5M-node T-Social graph without OOM errors, making it practical for real-world deployment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The balance parameter λ requires dataset-specific tuning (values range from 0.2 to 0.5 across datasets with no principled selection criterion), and the paper offers no automatic or adaptive strategy for setting it, which limits out-of-the-box usability. - The global inconsistency module assumes a unimodal normal distribution (single centroid c), which may fail when normal nodes form multiple distinct clusters; the paper does not analyze or discuss this structural assumption or its breakdown conditions. - Evaluation is limited to node-level anomaly detection on static graphs; the framework's behavior on dynamic graphs, edge anomalies, or heterogeneous graphs is unexplored, constraining the scope of the claimed generality.
Search Results
#73 — Innovative (65)

Advancing Multimodal Agent Reasoning with Long-Term Neuro- Symbolic Memory

Rongjie Jiang, Jianwei Wang, Gengda Zhao, Chengyang Luo, Kai Wang, Wenjie Zhang · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The SK-Gen pipeline for automatically constructing procedural DAGs from episodic memory without manual ontology engineering is a meaningful contribution, and the ablation demonstrating genuine neural-symbolic synergy (combined gain exceeding the sum of individual components) is compelling. Evaluation on a single benchmark from the same research lineage and significant hyperparameter sensitivity (10+ point accuracy swings) limit confidence in generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal agents operating in open-world environments need memory systems that support both associative recall and deterministic constraint-aware reasoning. Existing approaches rely on vector-centric, embedding-based retrieval (RAG over episodic and semantic nodes) which handles System 1 intuitive reasoning well but fundamentally fails at System 2 deductive reasoning — e.g., recognizing that a prerequisite step is blocked by a constraint discovered elsewhere in memory. This gap is critical for real-world decision-making involving step dependencies, constraint satisfaction, and abductive inference from partial observations. Methodology: NS-Mem is a three-layer neuro-symbolic memory framework. The episodic layer stores timestamped multimodal observations as text descriptions with embeddings, anchored to persistent entity identities extracted via ArcFace (face) and ERes2Net (voice) clustering. The semantic layer maintains entity-centric attribute summaries with a reinforcement-based consolidation policy that increments edge weights on corroborating observations rather than inserting duplicate nodes. The logic layer is the core novelty: each Logic Node pairs dual-level index vectors (goal-level and step-averaged embeddings) with a Procedural DAG (directed acyclic graph) encoding step-by-step dependencies, transition probabilities, and attribute constraints. The SK-Gen mechanism constructs logic nodes automatically: it extracts action sequences from episodic memory, mines recurring procedural patterns using PrefixSpan sequential pattern mining, filters candidates via LLM-based coherence verification, constructs DAGs, and computes index vectors. Incremental updates use EMA for neural refinement (β=0.9 default) and counting-based transition statistics for symbolic refinement, avoiding full reconstruction. A Knowledge Fusion phase merges single-path DAGs from multiple observations via bipartite node alignment, edge union, and Bayesian parameter pooling. Hybrid retrieval classifies queries as factual, constraint, or character-type, applies two-stage neural retrieval (similarity search + type-aware re-ranking), then executes deterministic symbolic functions (getProcedureWithEvidence, queryStepSequence with constraint filtering, aggregateCharacterBehaviors) directly on retrieved DAGs. Three theorems with proofs establish posterior consistency of transition statistics, soundness of DAG fusion, and determinism of symbolic query functions. Key Results: On M3-Bench (long-video QA benchmark with robot and web video subsets), NS-Mem achieves 53.6% accuracy on M3-Bench-web and 34.7% on M3-Bench-robot, representing +4.7 and +4.0 absolute point gains over the strongest baseline M3-Agent (48.9% / 30.7%). The gains are heavily concentrated on structured reasoning: +12.5% on constrained queries, +11.9% on procedural queries, but only +1.8% on factual queries — validating that symbolic structures add value specifically where deterministic reasoning is needed. NS-Mem reduces average retrieval rounds from 4.01 to 3.38 on Robot (-15.8%) and from 3.14 to 2.84 on Web (-9.6%), with corresponding wall-clock reductions of 7.4% and 4.1%. Ablation confirms synergy: the full model (+4.7 on Web) exceeds the sum of the neural-only (+1.1) and symbolic-only (+2.8) variants. Dynamic incremental maintenance modestly but consistently outperforms static graphs (+0.9% accuracy on Robot, -0.14 avg rounds). Strengths: - The dual-index design (goal-level + step-level embeddings per Logic Node) is a practical and well-motivated solution to the multi-granularity retrieval problem, mirroring document title/content indexing paradigms. - The SK-Gen pipeline is end-to-end automatic — no manual ontology engineering — and the incremental maintenance via EMA + transition counting avoids costly retraining while providing theoretical convergence guarantees. - The ablation demonstrates genuine synergy between the neural and symbolic components: the combined system outperforms the sum of individual contributions, validating the architectural coupling. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to a single benchmark (M3-Bench) introduced by the same line of prior work (M3-Agent); broader evaluation across diverse agent benchmarks (e.g., embodied tasks, open-domain dialogue) would substantially strengthen generalizability claims. - The construction pipeline's LLM-based verification step (Step 3 in SK-Gen) introduces a non-trivial computational bottleneck and an additional source of noise; error propagation from this step into the symbolic structures is not analyzed. - The system requires reliable face and voice recognition (ArcFace, ERes2Net) to establish entity anchors — a significant brittleness point for videos with poor visual quality, occlusion, or large casts, where entity identity tracking commonly degrades. - Multiple hyperparameters (τ, δ, α, β, σ) exhibit meaningful sensitivity (Figure 4 shows accuracy swings of 10+ points across τ values), yet no automated tuning strategy is provided, limiting out-of-the-box applicability to new domains.

#74 — Innovative (65)

Thinking in Uncertainty: Mitigating Hallucinations in MLRMs with Latent Entropy-Aware Decoding (81 ▲ )

Zhongxing Xu, Zhonghua Wang, Zhe Qian, Dachuan Shi, Feilong Tang, Ming Hu, Shiyan Su, Xiaocheng Zou, Wei Feng, Dwarikanath Mahapatra, Yifan Peng, Mingquan Lin, Zongyuan Ge · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 65 for the well-grounded empirical observation linking transition words, high-entropy states, and hallucination onset in multimodal reasoning models, which provides a concrete mechanistic basis for the decoding intervention. The dual-component LEAD design — soft embeddings for preserving semantic diversity plus visual anchor injection for grounding — is well-motivated and validated across five base models. However, the approach is an inference-time decoding modification in a crowded space of similar methods, and the uncharacterized computational overhead of vocabulary-wide embedding computation is a practical concern.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal large reasoning models (MLRMs) trained via reinforcement learning generate extended chain-of-thought reasoning chains before answering, but remain highly prone to hallucinations. The paper identifies a specific, previously underexplored mechanism: transition words (e.g., "however," "because," "wait") that restructure reasoning chains correlate strongly with hallucinations — in 200 sampled outputs from four MLRMs, the majority of hallucinations co-occur within 10 tokens following such transition words. The authors show via token entropy analysis that these transition words are high-entropy states, meaning the model is maximally uncertain at these junctures. Crucially, standard discrete decoding collapses the full predictive distribution to a single sampled token at each step, discarding distributional information precisely when it is most needed. A secondary finding is that high-entropy tokens linked to hallucinations exhibit lower visual attention ratios, meaning the model also neglects visual grounding under uncertainty. Methodology: LEAD (Latent Entropy-Aware Decoding) is a training-free, plug-and-play decoding modification with two components. First, entropy-aware reasoning mode switching: at each reasoning step, token-level Shannon entropy H_t is computed from the output distribution and compared to a dynamic reference threshold Ĥ. When entropy rises above Ĥ (high-uncertainty, exploratory phase), the model replaces the standard one-hot discrete token embedding with a probability-weighted combination of all token embeddings — effectively a soft, continuous representation that preserves multiple competing hypotheses simultaneously. When entropy falls (convergent phase), the model reverts to standard discrete token sampling. A persistence window prevents rapid mode oscillation: transitions from discrete to latent mode require a minimum number of consecutive high-entropy steps (W_{D→L}), while transitions back to discrete occur immediately upon confidence recovery. A global switch counter (C_max = 5) prevents overthinking by halting further mode switching once the budget is exhausted. Second, entropy-aware visual anchor injection: at the onset of each high-entropy phase, a visual guidance vector (averaged embedding of pretrained visual special tokens) is blended into the probability-weighted embedding at strength λ = 0.4. This one-time injection per latent phase reinjects visual grounding without continuously interfering with subsequent reasoning steps. The method operates entirely at inference time and requires no retraining. Key Results: Evaluated across five 7B-scale MLRMs (R1-Onevision, Vision-R1, VL-Rethinker, VL-Cogito, OpenVLThinker) on 19 benchmarks spanning general understanding, hallucination assessment, mathematical reasoning, and scientific reasoning. On R1-Onevision-7B, LEAD yields +4.7% on VStar, +3.9% on RealWorldQA, +4.5% on MMEval-Pro, +4.7% on MMHalu, +3.8% on Bingo, and +2.5% on MathVision. On Vision-R1-7B, POPE accuracy improves by +3.4–3.7% across all three POPE variants. Domain-specific reasoning gains average +2.0% on mathematics benchmarks and +3.2% on scientific benchmarks. LEAD also generates shorter reasoning chains than baselines (fewer tokens on MathVision) while achieving higher accuracy, and reaches peak Pass@k accuracy at smaller k values than VCD, MemVR, and SID — indicating both sample efficiency and reasoning diversity. GPT-5-assisted evaluation confirms that fluency, grammar, and naturalness are preserved. Strengths: - The core empirical observation — transition words coincide with high-entropy states and precede hallucinations — is well-supported by both token masking ablations and attention analysis, giving the method a concrete mechanistic grounding rather than purely heuristic motivation. - LEAD is entirely training-free and modular: it integrates as a drop-in decoding strategy across multiple base models without any fine-tuning, making it immediately practical. - The dual-component design (soft embeddings for semantic diversity + visual anchor for grounding) is justified by two distinct but complementary empirical findings, and the ablation studies systematically validate each hyperparameter (threshold, window size, injection strength, switch count). Weaknesses / Limitations: - All evaluated models are 7B parameters; scalability of the approach to larger (e.g., 70B) or smaller (3B) models is not demonstrated in the main paper, and the appendix results for different scales are not fully analyzed. - The vocabulary-wide probability-weighted embedding computation (ẽ_t = E_{v~p_t}[e(v)]) requires a full matrix multiplication over the entire vocabulary at each high-entropy step, introducing non-trivial inference overhead that is not measured or reported — particularly relevant for models with large vocabularies (e.g., 128K tokens). - The threshold for "high entropy" is determined dynamically relative to a tracked reference Ĥ, but the initialization and update rule for Ĥ are not fully specified for multi-turn or batched settings, leaving open questions about consistency across diverse generation lengths and sampling temperatures.
Recommended 2603.13366 PDF
#75 — Innovative (64)

inference -fleet-sim: A Queueing-Theory-Grounded Fleet Capacity Planner for LLM Inference

Huamin Chen, Xunzhuo Liu, Yuhan Liu, Junchen Jiang, Bowei He, Xue Liu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: inference-fleet-sim fills a clear practical gap as the only tool jointly optimizing pool topology, routing policy, GPU type, and fleet count against cost with SLO constraints. The two-phase analytical+DES design is efficient and the eight case studies surface genuinely counterintuitive results with operational value—particularly the agent fleet failing at 30% utilization and the A100-prefill/H100-decode assignment. The methodology itself (queueing theory + simulation) is well-established rather than novel, and the linear roofline GPU model limits accuracy for non-calibration configurations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Sizing a GPU fleet for LLM inference requires answering interdependent questions—how many GPUs, which type, how to split requests across pools, which routing policy—that have no closed-form answers. Existing tools (Vidur, AIConfigurator, Mélange) address per-engine configuration or GPU type selection for a fixed cluster size, but none answer the upstream provisioning question: given a token-length distribution, arrival rate, SLO, and GPU catalog, what is the minimum-cost fleet configuration that meets a P99 TTFT target? The problem is hard because KV-cache slot counts create an 8× capacity ratio between short and long requests, queueing dynamics under heavy-tailed workloads are not well captured by standard analytical models, and the joint space of pool topology, routing policy, and GPU type is too large for exhaustive simulation. Methodology: inference-fleet-sim combines analytical M/G/c queueing with discrete-event simulation (DES) in a two-phase optimizer. Phase 1 sweeps candidate configurations (number of GPUs per pool, split threshold B_short, GPU type) using the Kimura two-moment M/G/c approximation, which adjusts for high-variance workloads via a (1 + C²_s)/2 correction term, and evaluates both queue wait and prefill time against the SLO. This sweep runs in milliseconds and produces a ranked Pareto frontier. Phase 2 runs DES on the top-k candidates to verify actual queueing dynamics, including head-of-line blocking effects invisible to the analytical model. The GPU performance model uses a linear roofline parameterized by (W, H, n_max)—baseline compute latency, memory-bandwidth cost per concurrent sequence, and maximum KV-slot count—calibrated from published hardware data for A10G, A100-80GB, and H100-80GB without requiring hardware access. The tool also supports reliability-aware sizing via a node availability parameter derived from published GPU failure-rate and MTTR data, and a grid demand-response analysis via a logistic GPU power curve fitted to ML.ENERGY Benchmark data. Key Results: The paper presents eight case studies, each demonstrating a result that naive analysis or intuition gets wrong. (1) On LMSYS at λ=100 req/s, B_short=4096 routes 98.4% of traffic short and cuts fleet size from 14 to 8 A100s (−43% cost, $155K/yr); B_short=512 costs 7% more than a homogeneous fleet. (2) A 24-GPU H100 agent fleet at 30% utilization fails its 1000 ms SLO (measured P99=1,052 ms) because high-C²_s service times cause head-of-line blocking that Erlang-C cannot predict; adding a two-pool split (25 GPUs, +4% cost) drops short-request P99 to 17 ms. (3) On the Azure workload at λ=100, A10G in a two-pool layout costs $168K/yr vs. $211K for 6 H100s—slower but cheaper cards win because the slot multiplier from B_short compensates. (4) GPU provisioning scales sub-linearly: a 16× traffic increase (25→400 req/s) requires only 5.75× more GPUs (4→23). (5) CompressAndRoute, designed for sizing, causes SLO violations in production by overwhelming the small short pool; LengthRouter is the correct production policy. (6) On LMSYS at 65K context, A10G+H100 saves 9% vs. all-A100 and is the only pairing that meets the SLO; A10G+A100 fails due to 700–2,800 ms prefill times. (7) In disaggregated serving, the optimal counterintuitive assignment is A100 prefill + H100 decode ($125K/yr, −46% vs. aggregated A100), because H100's 2.5× faster per-token iteration reduces the decode pool from 6 to 3 GPUs. (8) A 40-GPU H100 fleet can sustain 30% power curtailment (saving 6.8 kW) or commit 40% curtailment for short events (<2 min, saving 9.3 kW from 23.3 kW baseline) without SLO breach. Strengths: - The two-phase analytical+DES design is practically efficient: the analytical sweep prunes the enormous configuration space in milliseconds, and DES verifies only the top candidates, making the tool usable without hardware access. - The case studies are grounded in real public workload traces (LMSYS, Azure) and surface genuinely non-obvious results—particularly the agent fleet failure at 30% utilization and the counterintuitive GPU assignment in disaggregated serving—that have direct operational value. - The tool is open-source, reproducible, and fills a clear gap in the existing ecosystem: it is the only tool that jointly optimizes pool topology, routing policy, GPU type, and fleet count against a cost objective with SLO constraints. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The DES operates at request granularity (two events per request), not at the decode-iteration level, so it cannot model preemption, batching reorder, or speculative decoding. Results for scheduler-sensitive comparisons require a higher-fidelity tool like Vidur. - The GPU performance model uses a linear (W, H) roofline and hand-calibrated constants for Llama-3-70B on single-node TP configurations; nonlinear effects (FlashAttention speedup, quantization, multi-node NVLink/InfiniBand overhead) are absorbed into constants rather than explicitly modeled, limiting accuracy for configurations that deviate from the calibration point. - The agent-heavy workload trace is synthetic, not derived from a public production log, so the agent-workload case studies (Puzzles 2, 5) should be interpreted cautiously as illustrative rather than empirically validated. - The Poisson sub-stream approximation—splitting a Poisson arrival stream by token-length threshold—is a standard engineering approximation, not exact; when prompt length correlates with time-of-arrival (e.g., long requests arriving in bursts), queue-length estimates from the analytical phase may be materially off.
Search Results 2603.16054 PDF
#76 — Innovative (64)

Pancake: Hierarchical Memory System for Multi-Agent LLM Serving

Zhengding Hu, Zaifeng Pan, Prabhleen Kaur, Vibha Murthy, Zhongkai Yu, Yue Guan, Zhen Wang, Steven Swanson, Yufei Ding · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Pancake addresses a well-characterized and increasingly important systems bottleneck with a practical three-pronged design, and the FSM-based modeling of agent memory access patterns to drive prefetching and early termination is a clever insight specific to agentic workloads. The 4.29x+ average speedup and near-linear multi-agent scaling are strong empirical results. The work is primarily systems optimization — applying locality-aware caching and index coordination to a new workload domain — rather than a conceptual departure.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLM agents increasingly rely on external memory stores to maintain action histories, user profiles, and knowledge bases, approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search over these stores has become a critical performance bottleneck. Existing memory frameworks like Mem-GPT and A-Mem suffer from memory operations consuming over 82% of total execution time at scale. The root causes are threefold: (1) single-agent workloads exhibit fine-grained interleaved search-insert patterns that scatter inserted vectors across many IVF clusters due to the high-dimensional shell effect, degrading both search efficiency and recall; (2) multi-agent scenarios compound this by requiring each agent to traverse the full coarse index of every other agent, causing coarse search overhead to reach 80% of latency at just 20 agents; and (3) GPU-CPU hybrid platforms are underutilized because existing caching strategies assume static indexes, while agentic workloads require frequent updates that break GPU-resident consistency. Methodology: Pancake introduces a three-pronged multi-tier ANN memory system. First, a pattern-driven multi-level index cache handles single-agent memory: a three-level hierarchy (L0 of Np recently accessed vectors, L1 of intermediate clusters caching top-k' neighbors, L2 of stable coarse clusters) is organized to reflect the agent's intrinsic locality. Crucially, each agent's memory access sequence is modeled as a Finite State Machine (FSM) over semantic cluster states, capturing both intra-request locality and inter-request step-wise locality. These FSMs drive workload-aware search reordering, early termination (skipping lower levels when top-k candidates satisfy a distance threshold α_et · d_agent), and prefetching of likely-next clusters during parallel LLM generation. Second, multi-agent index coordination is achieved via a hybrid graph that unifies all agents' coarse indexes into a single navigable structure with sparse inter-graph connections (probability 1/ef_connect) enabling cross-scope coarse search in a single BFS-like traversal rather than per-agent traversals. Agent-specific profiles associated with static clusters track each agent's local access vectors to improve fine-search recall without modifying the global index. Third, dynamic GPU-CPU index coordination caches hotspot clusters on GPU based on access frequency, maintains a CPU-side per-cluster insertion buffer (size B_insert = 128) to absorb fine-grained updates without synchronous transfers, and uses fully asynchronous GPU-CPU migration with proactive cluster-space allocation and GPU-based K-means for on-GPU cluster splitting. Key Results: Pancake is evaluated on 8× NVIDIA H100 GPUs against agent-framework baselines (A-Mem, MemGPT, LlamaIndex, LangMem) and standalone vector-database baselines (Quake, SpFresh, DiskANN) across six agent datasets (UltraChat, Prm800k, Gsm8k, APIGen, AgentGym, UltraFDBK) and four workload patterns. In single-agent end-to-end throughput, Pancake achieves 1.12× to 26.18× improvements over existing libraries, averaging more than 4.29× speedup, reducing memory operation time to an average of 3.2% of total execution. Memory-only operation speedup exceeds 6.81×. For vector-database throughput under agent workloads, Pancake achieves 1.9× to 4.2× speedup over Quake, SpFresh, and DiskANN baselines; GPU acceleration yields an additional 2.2× gain. In two-agent mixed-workload scenarios, existing frameworks degrade 29.9%–55.9% while Pancake holds degradation to under 9.8%. With up to 20 concurrent agents, end-to-end performance degradation stays below 10.2%, demonstrating near-linear scalability. The multi-level cache reduces scanned vectors by up to 2.23× in latency over long-serving workloads, and the hybrid-graph agent-profile mechanism reduces total vector similarity computations by 11.6% (structure) plus an additional 21.8% (agent profiles) compared to independent per-agent indexes. Strengths: - Thorough empirical motivation grounded in real agent workload traces: the scattered-cluster, step-wise locality, and GPU-CPU cost characterizations are concrete and drive each design choice directly. - Practical design that integrates with existing frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, MemGPT) through a simple Python API, lowering adoption friction and enabling real deployment. - The hybrid-graph approach elegantly eliminates O(N_agents) coarse-index traversals through a single BFS traversal, with inter-graph connection density adaptively calibrated to relative index densities. - Comprehensive ablation study isolates the contribution of each component (multi-level cache, hybrid graph, agent profiles, GPU caching) with clear attribution of speedup sources. Weaknesses / Limitations: - FSM convergence requires a warm-up period: the paper acknowledges that IVF-Split shows a "long pre-convergence phase," and while Pancake's multi-level cache adapts faster, cold-start behavior for new agents or rapidly shifting workloads is not directly quantified. - The hybrid-graph connection density parameter α_ic (empirically set to 4–8) and early-termination threshold α_et (0.6–0.8) require per-deployment tuning and the sensitivity of these choices across memory scales beyond the MS MARCO 8M-passage corpus is not analyzed. - GPU memory competition with LLM inference weights and KV caches is acknowledged but not deeply studied; the 5–15 GB hotspot cache assumed for Pancake may be difficult to sustain on heavily loaded inference servers, and the degradation curve beyond that budget is not characterized.
Recommended 2602.21477 PDF
#77 — Innovative (64)

Online Experiential Learning for Language Models (35 ▲ )

Tianzhu Ye, Li Dong, Qingxiu Dong, Xun Wu, Shaohan Huang, Furu Wei · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The reward-free, environment-free formulation is elegant and practically significant — extracting transferable knowledge from raw text trajectories via self-distillation without requiring scalar rewards or simulator access addresses a real deployment bottleneck. The on-policy vs. off-policy comparison is cleanly controlled and directly validates the design. But evaluation on two tiny grid games (3×3, 6×6) severely limits confidence that the approach scales to realistic open-ended tasks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models are trained offline using human annotations or simulated environments with verifiable rewards, then deployed as static artifacts. This creates a fundamental bottleneck: the model gains nothing from the rich stream of real-world interactions accumulated during deployment. Realizing online learning from deployment is non-trivial because (1) the server side typically cannot access user-side environments, and (2) real-world feedback is purely textual — no scalar rewards exist — making standard RL inapplicable. The paper addresses how to extract useful training signal from raw textual experience alone, without environment access or reward supervision on the server side. Methodology: Online Experiential Learning (OEL) operates in two alternating stages. In Stage 1 (Extraction), the deployed model acts as its own knowledge extractor: it processes collected interaction trajectories sequentially, accumulating transferable experiential knowledge in an incremental fashion — each extraction step conditions on previously accumulated knowledge. This process is repeated K=10 times with different random seeds to produce a diverse set of knowledge summaries C. Knowledge can be structured (bulleted "EXPERIENCE ITEM" lists) or unstructured free-form text. In Stage 2 (Consolidation), this knowledge is internalized into model weights via on-policy context distillation: partial rollout prefixes from deployment trajectories form training inputs; the student model generates responses conditioned only on the prefix (no knowledge context), while a frozen teacher model — augmented with experiential knowledge — provides token-level supervision through reverse KL divergence. Because the student generates its own responses, training is fully on-policy and requires no environment access. The two stages iterate: the improved model redeploys, collects better trajectories, extracts richer knowledge, and consolidates again. Evaluated on Frozen Lake (3×3 grid navigation) and Sokoban (6×6 box-pushing puzzle) from the TextArena framework, using Qwen3 models at 1.7B, 4B, and 8B scales, both thinking and non-thinking variants. Key Results: OEL achieves consistent pass rate improvements across successive iterations on both environments. On Frozen Lake with Qwen3-1.7B, pass rate grows from ~10% (initial) to over 40% after three OEL rounds. On Sokoban with Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, pass rate rises from ~10% to ~28%. Response length simultaneously decreases to roughly 70% of initial length by Round 3, demonstrating improved token efficiency alongside accuracy gains. On the on-policy vs. off-policy comparison (Figure 6), on-policy consolidation achieves higher in-distribution pass rate (~48% vs. ~40% at 2500 training samples) while preserving OOD performance on IF-Eval (~66.5% vs. ~65%), whereas off-policy distillation causes clear OOD degradation. Ablations confirm that extracted experiential knowledge substantially outperforms raw trajectories: knowledge in-context yields 18.2% vs. 10.9% pass rate, and after consolidation 21.4% vs. 7.8% on Sokoban. On-policy consistency matters: Qwen3-1.7B using its own trajectories achieves 31.1% (consolidated) vs. 22.7% using Qwen3-4B trajectories, showing self-generated experience transfers better than stronger-model experience. Strengths: - Reward-free and environment-free on the server side: the framework requires no scalar rewards, no reward models, and no simulated environments — only pre-collected text trajectories — which is practically significant for real-world deployment scenarios. - Clean theoretical grounding: the on-policy reverse KL distillation objective is well-motivated, and the on-policy/off-policy comparison is carefully controlled and directly supports the design choice. - Simultaneous gains in accuracy and inference efficiency (response length reduction), with demonstrated preservation of out-of-distribution generalization — a property that is empirically validated rather than assumed. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to two small, structurally simple text-based grid games (3×3 Frozen Lake, 6×6 Sokoban). It is unclear whether OEL scales to open-ended, high-dimensional real-world tasks where the action space is not discrete and the environment feedback is far noisier and less structured. - The extraction stage is itself a potentially expensive LLM call that accumulates over many trajectories, and the accumulation is sequential — the paper does not discuss computational cost or wall-clock overhead of the extraction loop relative to standard RL baselines, making practical deployment cost comparisons difficult. - The framework assumes the model can self-extract meaningful knowledge from its own trajectories, but for domains where initial model performance is very low (nearly random behavior), the extracted knowledge may be systematically misleading — there is no mechanism to detect or filter low-quality or incorrect experiential knowledge items.
Recommended 2603.16856 PDF
#78 — Innovative (64)

SVLL: Staged Vision-Language Learning for Physically Grounded Embodied Task Planning

Yuyuan Yang, Junkun Hong, Hongrong Wang, Honghao Cai, Xunpeng Ren, Ge Wang, Mingcong Lei, Shenhao Yan, Jiahao Yang, Chengsi Yao, Xi Li, Yiming Zhao, Yatong Han, Jinke Ren · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SVLL's primary contribution is precise diagnostic framing of two failure modes (premature temporal binding and causal misalignment) with targeted, independently validated fixes. The B-DPO modification addressing likelihood displacement in constrained action spaces is a meaningful refinement. However, staged training curricula and DPO variants are well-trodden territory, and the overall framework is an engineering composition of known techniques rather than a conceptual departure.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Embodied task planning requires vision-language models (VLMs) to generate action sequences that are both visually grounded and causally coherent over time. Existing training paradigms suffer from two fundamental failure modes. First, joint end-to-end supervised fine-tuning causes premature temporal binding: models learn to shortcut reasoning by over-relying on textual action history rather than grounding decisions in current visual observations, producing repetitive or stale actions when environment state changes. Second, applying standard Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to embodied planning causes causal misalignment: DPO's purely relative ranking objective ignores absolute likelihood constraints on expert trajectories, causing the policy to drift away from the expert distribution (likelihood displacement) and produce confident but physically invalid actions. These problems are especially acute in embodied domains where actions must satisfy strict physical affordance constraints. Methodology: The paper proposes SVLL, a unified three-stage training framework built on Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Stage 1 (Spatial Grounding) trains the model on history-free data by explicitly blocking the action history channel (h_t = ∅), forcing the model to resolve actions purely from current visual observations. The vision encoder is frozen throughout to preserve pretrained visual features. Stage 2 (Temporal Reasoning) initializes from the Stage 1 checkpoint and unlocks the full action history, fine-tuning on sequential trajectory data so the model learns causal temporal dependencies without becoming over-reliant on them. Stage 3 introduces Bias-DPO (B-DPO), a novel alignment objective that modifies standard DPO in three ways: (1) an amplified positive weight (w > 1) on chosen trajectories to reinforce alignment with expert behavior; (2) an auxiliary SFT loss L_SFT that anchors the policy to the expert manifold, explicitly preventing likelihood displacement; and (3) a threshold-triggered unlikelihood penalty L_UL that activates only when the rejected trajectory's likelihood exceeds a safety threshold τ, surgically suppressing overconfident hallucinations without destabilizing the policy. The total loss is L_total = a·L_DPO + b·L_SFT + L_UL. Negative preference samples for Stage 3 are constructed from four predefined physical constraint violation categories (invalid navigation, repetitive search, erroneous navigation, failure to terminate). Key Results: On the AI2-THOR interactive embodied planning benchmark, SVLL-Stage 3 achieves 78.35% Success Rate (SR), 68.53 Success weighted by Path Length (SPL), 89.64% Valid Action Rate (VAR), 7.22% Loop Rate (LR), and 26.34% Constraint Violation Rate (CVR). This outperforms closed-source models—GPT-4o (48.45% SR, 40.32% CVR) and Gemini-2.0-flash (58.76% SR, 31.55% CVR)—as well as the open-source embodied-specific RoboBrain2.0-32B (27.84% SR, 52.61% CVR), despite SVLL using only 7B parameters. Ablations confirm the contribution of each stage: skipping Stage 1 (Direct Stage 2 baseline) raises LR to 10.31% and CVR to 30.65%, demonstrating premature temporal binding; adding Stage 3 B-DPO over Stage 2 reduces CVR from 29.18% to 26.34% while raising VAR from 84.16% to 89.64%. In real-world robot deployment across 9 tasks on a 6-DoF manipulator, SVLL achieves 55.56% SR and 4.35% CVR versus RoboBrain2.0-32B's 22.22% SR and 10.94% CVR, demonstrating strong zero-shot sim-to-real transfer with a 4.6× smaller model. Strengths: - Precise diagnostic framing: the paper cleanly identifies and independently validates two distinct failure modes (premature temporal binding and causal misalignment) with targeted ablations for each stage, making the contribution scientifically rigorous rather than purely empirical. - B-DPO is a principled and well-motivated fix to likelihood displacement—a known but underaddressed flaw of standard DPO in structured, constraint-governed action spaces—with a clean theoretical justification. - Outperforms significantly larger models (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0-flash, RoboBrain2.0-32B at 32B parameters) with only 7B parameters, demonstrating that architectural alignment quality can substitute for brute-force parameter scaling. Weaknesses / Limitations: - B-DPO's strong anchoring to expert trajectories is also its Achilles' heel: it risks overfitting to suboptimal or noisy demonstrations, potentially missing more efficient or novel strategies that deviate from the training distribution. - Sim-to-real transfer relies on pixel-level visual alignment without domain randomization or perception augmentation, limiting robustness to unstructured lighting, occlusion, and sensor noise in diverse real-world deployments. - The three-stage pipeline incurs substantial training overhead (~29 total GPU-hours on 8× A100s) and requires careful hyperparameter tuning across multiple objectives (a, b, w, τ, ε), which may limit accessibility and reproducibility outside well-resourced settings.
Search Results 2603.11563 PDF
#79 — Innovative (64)

TabGeoFlow: A Geometric Flow Matching Model for Tabular Data Synthesis

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The geometric inductive bias of decomposing the flow field into tangent and normal components to directly penalize off-manifold trajectories is a creative and principled framing for the privacy-utility tradeoff, more targeted than generic regularization or DP noise. The adaptive dynamic threshold via the uncertainty proxy head is a nice mechanism. However, the fidelity and utility results are at parity with existing baselines rather than advancing the quality frontier, the theoretical privacy argument remains informal with no formal guarantees, and the fixed k=30 tangent dimension undermines the geometric interpretation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: High-fidelity tabular generative models (GANs, VAEs, diffusion models) achieve strong statistical and utility performance, but their capacity to accurately fit complex distributions also causes them to memorize training instances, making them vulnerable to Membership Inference Attacks (MIA). Existing mitigations either apply indirect regularization (L1/L2 parameter norms, adversarial objectives) or rely on Differential Privacy, which incurs severe utility degradation. This paper hypothesizes that memorization stems from generative trajectories deviating off the intrinsic data manifold to "reach for" specific training points, and proposes addressing this by directly regularizing the geometry of the flow matching vector field. Methodology: TabGeoFlow extends Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) with a Geometric Structure-Aware Module. A shared Transformer backbone encodes the current state and feeds four parallel heads: (1) a Principal Flow Head predicting the tangent-aligned component v‖, (2) a Residual Flow Head predicting the normal component v⊥, (3) a Tangent Basis Head outputting an orthonormal k-dimensional basis Q_t (k=30 fixed) approximating the local tangent space, and (4) an Uncertainty Proxy (Entropy) Head outputting ρ_t ∈ [0,1] to drive a dynamic regularization threshold τ_dyn. The decomposition uses projection: v‖ = Q_t Q_t^T v_θ, v⊥ = v_θ − v‖. The total loss is L_Total = L_Flow + λ_SFR · L_SFR, where the Structural Flow Regularization (SFR) loss penalizes the ratio |v⊥|² / (|v‖|² + ε) exceeding a dynamic threshold τ_dyn = τ_end + (τ_start − τ_end) · ρ_t. This schedule applies weaker regularization near t≈0 (noisy prior region) and stronger regularization near t≈1 (data manifold), analogous to annealing. The Tangent Basis Head is trained implicitly end-to-end with no direct supervision on tangent accuracy; it is incentivized to align its basis with the dominant flow directions to minimize total loss. Key Results: Evaluated on seven public datasets (Adult, Default, Shoppers, Magic, Beijing, News, Diabetes) against eight baselines (CTGAN, TVAE, GOGGLE, GReaT, STaSy, CoDi, TabDDPM, TabSyn, TabDiff). On Trend Similarity Error Rate (lower is better), TabGeoFlow achieves 2.34% average vs. TabDiff's 2.29% and TabSyn's 2.94% — essentially on par with the best. On Shape Similarity Error Rate, TabGeoFlow averages 1.34% vs. TabDiff's 1.24% and TabSyn's 1.29%, again competitive. On TSTR downstream ML utility (average gap from real data), TabGeoFlow achieves 6.75 vs. TabDiff's 6.02 and TabSyn's 6.82 — marginally behind TabDiff but comparable. The primary advantage is in privacy: TabGeoFlow achieves average DCR of 50.45% (ideal = 50%), compared to TabDiff's 52.06% and TabSyn's 51.12%, with near-random MIA accuracy across all datasets. TabDDPM shows severe DCR deviation (59.37% average) indicating significant memorization. Ablation confirms SFR is the key driver: removing it degrades DCR to 51.11% / 48.46% on Adult/Default while utility drops marginally (AUC from .913 to .898 / .761 to .745). Replacing the dynamic threshold with a fixed one also degrades performance, validating the adaptive annealing schedule. Strengths: - The geometric inductive bias (manifold-constrained vector field decomposition) is a principled and causally direct approach to privacy-utility trade-off, more targeted than parameter regularization or DP noise injection. - The Uncertainty Proxy Head enabling a dynamic regularization threshold is a clever adaptive mechanism that handles the inhomogeneous density along generative trajectories (noisy early, structured late). - Strong empirical validation across 7 datasets and 8 baselines, with privacy metrics (DCR ≈ 50%, near-random MIA) substantially better than high-fidelity models like TabDiff and TabSyn without meaningful utility loss. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper claims to reduce memorization via off-manifold penalization, but the theoretical connection between suppressing v⊥ and preventing sample memorization is informal — there is no formal privacy guarantee (e.g., DP-style (ε,δ) bound), and the evaluation relies solely on empirical DCR and shadow-model MIA, which have known limitations as privacy metrics (cited but not fully addressed). - The tangent basis dimension k=30 is fixed as a universal hyperparameter across all datasets regardless of actual intrinsic dimensionality, and the tangent basis is learned implicitly without supervision — it is unclear whether it genuinely approximates the data manifold's tangent space or merely learns a convenient decomposition for loss minimization, making the geometric interpretation partly aspirational. - Fidelity and utility gains over the best baselines (TabDiff, TabSyn) are marginal and within noise on several datasets; TabGeoFlow does not decisively outperform on quality metrics, meaning the primary contribution is privacy protection at parity, not a new quality frontier.
Search Results
#80 — Innovative (63)

ConFu: Contemplate the Future for Better Speculative Sampling

Zongyue Qin, Raghavv Goel, Mukul Gagrani, Risheek Garrepalli, Mingu Lee, Yizhou Sun · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: ConFu's insight that draft models should condition on the target model's forward-looking latent intent via contemplate tokens is conceptually clean and addresses the well-characterized error accumulation problem in speculative decoding. The surgery-free design (frozen target model) is practically valuable. However, the 10–15% improvement over an already strong baseline (EAGLE-3) is modest, evaluation is limited to two Llama-3 sizes, and the MoE component contributes minimally (+0.05 accept length).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by having a lightweight draft model propose candidate tokens that a larger target model verifies in a single parallel pass. The critical bottleneck is draft quality: existing draft models, including the state-of-the-art EAGLE series, condition token generation solely on the current prefix. This causes error accumulation — the draft model's hidden representations progressively drift from the target model's, lowering token acceptance rates as the sequence grows. The paper argues that draft models need forward-looking signals: knowledge of the target model's current high-level "thought" or semantic trajectory before committing to specific token proposals. Methodology: ConFu introduces three interlocking mechanisms. First, it injects contemplate tokens (pause tokens) and soft prompt tokens into the target model's KV cache at inference time. The contemplate token is appended after each input position, causing the target model to compute additional intermediate representations that encode its forward-looking intent. Critically, the target model is frozen — only the soft prompt embeddings and contemplate token embeddings are learned — so the output distribution is not altered. The hidden state of the contemplate token (a future vector f) is passed as an auxiliary input token to the draft model, which conditions all subsequent token proposals on this fixed signal. During verification, one contemplate token is inserted per draft tree node and processed in parallel via a modified tree attention mask, so the overhead scales as 2T tokens (T draft nodes) rather than requiring additional sequential passes. Second, because a single static contemplate embedding is insufficient across diverse generation contexts (math vs. prose), ConFu replaces it with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture: a router conditions on the hidden state of the last accepted token and produces a weighted combination of K expert embeddings, yielding context-adaptive future signals. Third, the training framework addresses two efficiency problems: anchor token sampling selects a sparse subset of positions for contemplate token insertion, growing sequence length from N to N+K_train instead of 2N; future prediction replication trains the draft model to predict tokens at nearby positions using the same future vector f, implicitly encouraging the contemplate signal to be smooth and robust across local context windows. Key Results: ConFu is evaluated on SpecBench across 11 task categories (writing, QA, summarization, translation, coding, math/reasoning, etc.) using Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as target models, with temperatures {0, 0.7, 1.0} and draft budgets of 30 and 60 nodes. Against EAGLE-3 (the strongest published baseline), ConFu consistently improves: for the 3B model under greedy decoding with 30 nodes, average accept length increases from 4.00 to 4.41 (+10.3%) and speed-up ratio improves from 1.83× to 2.11× (+15.3%). For the 8B model under the same setting, accept length rises from 4.59 to 5.01 (+9.2%) and speed-up from 2.36× to 2.69× (+14.0%). Gains are consistent across all tasks and settings, though most pronounced at lower temperatures where the target distribution is sharper. Ablations show that future prediction replication contributes ~+0.17 accept length and MoE contributes ~+0.05, confirming both components add independently. Strengths: - The core insight — that draft models should be conditioned on the target model's latent forward intent, not just its past hidden states — is conceptually clean and addresses a real, well-characterized failure mode (error accumulation) in a principled way. - The implementation is surgery-free with respect to the target model: no fine-tuning of target weights, no change to output distribution, making ConFu drop-in compatible with any frozen LLM that EAGLE supports. - The overhead analysis is rigorous: processing 2T tokens during verification vs. T is modest at typical draft tree sizes (T=30-60), and the anchor token sampling trick limits training memory growth to N+K rather than 2N. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are restricted to two Llama-3 model sizes (3B and 8B) on a single benchmark suite (SpecBench). Generalization to other model families (Mistral, Qwen, Gemma) and larger target models (70B+) is undemonstrated, which is the regime where inference acceleration matters most commercially. - The 8-11% headline improvement is measured relative to an already highly optimized baseline (EAGLE-3), but absolute wall-clock throughput numbers on real serving hardware are not reported — it is unclear how the 2T-token verification overhead interacts with batched inference or different hardware memory bandwidths beyond a single H100 with batch size 1. - The MoE contribution (+0.05 accept length) is small relative to replication (+0.17), raising the question of whether the additional architectural complexity of MoE is justified in practice versus simpler context-conditioned mechanisms.
Recommended 2603.08899 PDF
#81 — Innovative (63)

MoEless: Efficient MoE LLM Serving via Serverless Computing

Hanfei Yu, Bei Ouyang, Shwai He, Ang Li, Hao Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: MoEless introduces a clean architectural separation by decoupling experts into serverless functions, and the insight of reusing the model's own gate networks as lightweight load predictors (≤80K parameters, fine-tuned in under 5 minutes) is both practical and elegant. The 92.68% cost reduction over Megatron-LM is striking. However, applying serverless computing patterns to MoE serving is a relatively expected systems direction, and the evaluation on a single 8-GPU NVLink testbed leaves multi-node scalability—where the approach's benefits would be most impactful—unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs are widely adopted to scale model capacity without proportional compute costs, but their sparse activation mechanism creates severe expert load imbalance during serving under expert parallelism (EP). A small subset of "hot" experts receive disproportionately high token loads while others sit idle, causing the straggler problem: overloaded GPUs delay the all-to-all gather synchronization that all other GPUs must wait on, inflating inference latency and serving cost. Existing load balancing solutions (e.g., EPLB from DeepSeek) rely on static resource configurations on serverful infrastructure, limiting scalability and elasticity, and must choose between costly real-time expert swapping or lossy expert re-routing that degrades generation quality. Methodology: MoEless is the first serverless MoE serving framework that decouples experts from the MoE model and packages them as independent serverless functions, enabling fine-grained elastic scaling without touching the non-expert modules in data parallelism. The system has three main components: (1) Expert Load Predictor: lightweight neural networks that reuse and fine-tune the MoE model's own gate networks to speculate expert load distributions d layers ahead, leveraging the high cosine similarity between residual-connected hidden states across layers. A layer-aware fine-tuning strategy selectively fine-tunes only low-accuracy (early) layers, keeping overhead minimal (≤80K parameters per predictor, full fine-tuning in under 5 minutes on one GPU). Prediction runs asynchronously on a dedicated CUDA stream with zero latency impact. (2) Expert Scaler: a greedy heuristic that iteratively identifies straggler experts using a max-heap, adds replicas, and splits their load until the coefficient of variation (CV) of loads drops below a threshold (CV ≤ 0.2) or a per-layer memory cap is reached. The resulting scaling plan targets per-layer balanced load distributions within a memory budget. (3) Expert Placer: assigns replicas to GPUs using a Join-the-Shortest-Queue strategy that reuses warm replica instances to avoid serverless cold-start overhead, while balancing aggregate GPU loads to minimize straggler effects and all-to-all communication overhead. All three components operate asynchronously with a configurable prediction distance (default d=1 layer) to fully overlap with ongoing inference. Key Results: MoEless is evaluated on three MoE models — Mixtral-8×7B, Phi-3.5-MoE, and Llama-4-Scout — across two real-world LLM serving traces (LMSYS-Chat-1M and ShareGPT) on an 8-GPU NVIDIA A6000 testbed. Against Megatron-LM and EPLB as serverful baselines: MoEless reduces average MoE layer forward latency by 43.19% vs. Megatron-LM and 21.89% vs. EPLB across all models and datasets. On total inference cost (GPU memory × latency), MoEless reduces cost by 92.68% vs. Megatron-LM, 84.06% vs. Oracle, and 95.11% vs. EPLB. Expert load prediction accuracy improves by up to 18% over Mixtral-offloading and 15% over ProMoE baselines. MoEless's performance tracks closely to the Oracle upper bound (which achieves perfect load balance but at the cost of generation quality), demonstrating near-optimal load balancing without sacrificing output fidelity. Predictor memory footprint is ≤2% of ProMoE's (e.g., 1.92 MB vs. 128.32 MB for Mixtral-8×7B). Ablation confirms all three components are essential — removing any one degrades latency. Strengths: - Elegant co-design insight: reusing the model's own gate networks as load predictors (with lightweight fine-tuning) avoids large external predictor overhead while achieving superior accuracy, making the approach both practical and deployable - The serverless expert decoupling design cleanly separates scaling concerns — non-expert modules stay in static data parallelism while only experts scale elastically — yielding dramatic cost reductions without generation quality loss - Layer-aware fine-tuning is well-motivated by empirical observation that early layers have lower input similarity and need more tuning, whereas later layers are naturally more predictable; this targeted approach is both efficient and principled Weaknesses / Limitations: - System parameters (prediction distance, CV threshold, memory cap) are determined via offline profiling and remain fixed at runtime; no adaptive or learned mechanism is provided, which may limit generalization to highly non-stationary workloads - The evaluation is confined to a single 8-GPU NVLink-connected testbed; scalability to larger clusters with multi-node all-to-all communication — where communication costs dominate differently — is not validated - Standard serverless management schemes (pre-warming, fixed keep-alive periods) are borrowed wholesale to handle cold starts; the paper explicitly defers more advanced runtime optimizations, leaving open whether the approach remains effective under extreme cold-start or rapid workload shifts
Recommended 2603.06350 PDF
#82 — Innovative (63)

Mitigating Low-Quality Reasoning in MLLMs: Self-Driven Refined Multimodal CoT with Selective Thinking and Step-wise Visual Enhancement

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The relative attention mechanism — exploiting the attention dichotomy between multiple-choice and open-ended prompting to cancel edge artifacts — is a technically elegant and genuinely novel insight. Achieving 60%+ token savings while improving accuracy in a fully training-free, architecture-agnostic manner is a meaningful practical contribution. The selective thinking module via entropy thresholding is simpler but effective. Limited to classification/VQA tasks and reliance on discrete answer options for the reference attention pattern constrain generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Chain-of-Thought (MCoT) methods for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from two interrelated problems: (1) overthinking, where models apply expensive step-by-step reasoning to trivially easy queries, wasting tokens without accuracy gain; and (2) inefficient visual grounding, where reasoning chains fail to attend to task-relevant image regions at each step. Existing approaches either ignore visual content during reasoning (prompt-based), inject coarse visual supplements at the input stage incurring large token overhead (visual supplementation), or insert cropped/generated image patches at each step requiring external tools or specialized training (interleaved-modal). None simultaneously address both problems in a training-free, architecture-agnostic way. Methodology: The paper proposes Self-Driven Refined Multimodal CoT (SDR-MCoT), a training-free framework with two modules. The selective thinking module uses entropy of the first generated token's logit distribution as a confidence proxy: low entropy (confident) queries are answered directly; high entropy queries trigger full chain-of-thought reasoning. The threshold θ is set adaptively by averaging entropy over a small sample subset, requiring no labeled calibration data. The step-wise visual enhancement module operates during each CoT reasoning step without inserting new tokens. It computes a "relative attention" map by dividing attention weights from open-ended prompting (question-focused but edge-noisy) by attention from multiple-choice prompting (dispersed but edge-clean), effectively isolating semantically relevant visual regions. At each reasoning step, this relative attention is multiplied element-wise with the current step's attention map (Region Amplification), morphological erosion is applied to suppress attention sink artifacts, adaptive token selection determines how many visual tokens to boost based on remaining high-attention patch count, and finally selected tokens' post-softmax attention weights are scaled by factor α and renormalized. The entire pipeline adds only a single extra forward pass (for confidence estimation) and in-place attention manipulation during decoding. Key Results: Experiments span 8 benchmarks (M³CoT, CoMT, ScienceQA, MMStar, A-OKVQA, V*, HallusionBench, MathVista) across 4 base models (Qwen2-VL-7B, InternVL3-8B, LLaVA-1.5-7B/13B). On Qwen2-VL-7B, SDR-MCoT achieves 63.69% average accuracy vs. 61.75% for the best baseline (MMCoT), while using only 67.94 tokens on average vs. 172-920 tokens for competing MCoT methods — approximately 60% reduction relative to zero-shot CoT (172 tokens). On InternVL3-8B, SDR-MCoT reaches 72.87% average, outperforming MMCoT (70.89%) with 100 tokens vs. 407. On LLaVA-1.5-7B/13B, gains are more modest but consistent. Ablation confirms both modules contribute: selective thinking alone cuts tokens by ~65% (212→74) with moderate accuracy gain; visual enhancement adds consistent accuracy improvement with negligible token cost; combined performance is best across all three ablation benchmarks. Strengths: - Genuinely training-free and plug-and-play across architecturally diverse MLLMs (decoder-only Qwen2-VL, InternVL, LLaVA), requiring no fine-tuning, RL, or external tools. - The relative attention mechanism is technically elegant — exploiting an existing attention dichotomy (multiple-choice vs. open-ended prompting) to cancel edge-attention artifacts, computed once per sample with negligible cost. - Addresses both efficiency and effectiveness simultaneously, unlike prior methods that trade one for the other; token savings are substantial (60%+) while accuracy consistently improves. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The entropy threshold θ is set by sampling a small subset at inference time, introducing a dataset-level calibration dependency; it is unclear how sensitive results are to subset size or distribution shift. - The relative attention trick requires the question to have discrete answer options for the "dispersed" attention reference pattern; open-ended questions fall back to using raw active attention, losing the edge-cancellation benefit. - Evaluation is limited to classification/VQA-style tasks; applicability to generation-heavy tasks (captioning, visual dialogue, grounded generation) where correct answer entropy is harder to define is not examined. - The enhancement factor α and morphological erosion parameters are not ablated, leaving open questions about robustness to hyperparameter choice across different image resolutions and model architectures.
Search Results
#83 — Innovative (63)

Inference Scaling Law for Retrieval Augmented Generation

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper fills a genuine gap by systematically characterizing RAG inference scaling across four dimensions, producing actionable deployment guidance (prioritize generator, cap documents at 50-70, avoid oversized context windows). However, the methodology is standard empirical curve-fitting applied to a new setting rather than a fundamentally new technique, and reliance on older model families (BERT, T5, OPT) without held-out predictive validation limits both novelty and practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has become a dominant framework for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks, yet how its inference performance scales with computational resources remains poorly understood. Prior scaling law research focuses on pure LLM training (Kaplan et al. 2020; Hoffmann et al. 2022) and does not transfer directly to RAG systems, which introduce a dual-component architecture with additional degrees of freedom: retriever model size (Sr), generator model size (Sg), number of retrieved documents (Nd), and context window size (Cw). Without a quantitative understanding of these interactions, deploying RAG systems under real-world latency and resource budgets is largely guesswork.
