Methodology: The authors formulate RAG performance as P = f(Sr, Sg, Nd, Cw) and empirically characterize each dimension independently using a zero-shot RAG pipeline. The retriever is a dual-encoder (BERT-base to GTR-XXL, 110M–4.8B parameters) with FAISS for top-k retrieval; the generator uses T5 or OPT models (60M–30B parameters). Evaluation is on NQ, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and FEVER using Wikipedia as the document corpus, measured by F1 score. Each dimension is swept while others are held fixed, with 50 independent runs per configuration. Four empirical scaling laws are fit via least squares: (1) retriever size follows a power-law saturation P(Sr) = A(1 − Sr^{−α}) + δη; (2) generator size follows P(Sg) = C − B·Sg^{−α}; (3) number of retrieved documents follows a sigmoid P(Nd) = A/(1+e^{−α(Nd−B)}) + δη; (4) context window follows a sigmoid multiplied by an exponential decay P(Cw) = [A/(1+e^{−α(Cw−B)})]·e^{−βCw} + δη, capturing the non-monotonic degradation at very large windows. These are then combined into a joint scaling law over all four factors simultaneously, with a Theorem 1 providing a first-order Taylor approximation argument showing the joint form recovers each marginal law when other factors are held fixed. The scaling law is applied to a latency-constrained resource allocation problem, framed as maximizing performance subject to Tr + Tg ≤ T. Key Results: All marginal scaling laws achieve R² > 0.93 across datasets, indicating strong fit. Retriever scaling saturates early: moving from GTR-XL (1.24B) to GTR-XXL (4.8B) yields minimal gains on most datasets. Generator scaling provides much larger and more consistent F1 improvements across all sizes tested (1.3B–30B OPT), with TriviaQA F1 rising from ~0.26 to ~0.40+. The number of retrieved documents shows sigmoid saturation typically around 50–70 documents, beyond which gains are negligible. Context window size exhibits non-monotonic behavior — performance peaks near 1000–1500 tokens and can degrade at 2048 tokens due to noise injection. For resource allocation under tight latency budgets (5s–50s), prioritizing the generator over the retriever consistently yields better performance. A mid-sized retriever is found to be optimal; an oversized retriever can become a bottleneck without proportional benefit. Joint scaling law R² ranges from 0.938 to 0.975 across datasets. Strengths: - First systematic empirical characterization of RAG inference scaling across four key dimensions, filling a meaningful gap between LLM-only scaling work and RAG practice. - High-quality empirical fits (R² > 0.93 throughout) with a mathematically justified joint formulation, lending credibility beyond pure curve-fitting. - Produces actionable, non-obvious deployment recommendations: prioritize generator budget over retriever, cap document count around 50–70, avoid excessively large context windows. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is exclusively zero-shot with older model families (BERT, T5, OPT); scaling behavior may differ substantially for instruction-tuned or RLHF-trained models (e.g., Llama 3, Mistral, GPT-4), limiting immediate applicability. - The latency models (Equations 15–16) assume linear relationships between latency and model size, document count, and context length — oversimplifications that ignore attention's quadratic complexity in context length and memory bandwidth bottlenecks. - The joint scaling law is acknowledged to be potentially unreliable due to high dimensionality and limited joint experimental coverage; the theoretical justification relies on a first-order approximation that only holds asymptotically for large model sizes. - No held-out predictive validation: the paper never demonstrates that the fitted laws accurately predict performance on configurations not used for fitting, which is the primary utility of a scaling law.
Search Results
#84 — Innovative (63)

Explain the Why: A Dual-Layer Interpretable Framework for Mental Health Detection

openreview.net · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The dual-level interpretable framework addresses a genuine clinical need by jointly exposing etiological causes and diagnostic reasoning paths, which is a meaningful conceptual contribution beyond single-level explainability approaches. The neural-symbolic design combining PANAS constructs with FOL rule extraction is creative, and outperforming much larger black-box LLMs is notable. However, the reliance on DeepSeek-V3-generated pseudo-labels for recognition-level ground truth introduces circularity that undermines the clinical validity claims, and all evaluation uses Kaggle-sourced social media data with no DSM-5 clinical validation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mental health disorders affect over 450 million people globally, and AI/deep learning systems have achieved strong predictive accuracy for conditions like depression and anxiety. However, clinical deployment is blocked by two key gaps: (1) decision-level opacity — black-box models provide no transparent reasoning path from symptoms to diagnosis, undermining clinician trust; and (2) recognition-level opacity — models fail to identify the underlying biopsychosocial causes (e.g., employment stress, interpersonal loss, discrimination) needed for personalized treatment. Existing interpretable approaches address at most one of these dimensions, not both simultaneously. Methodology: The paper proposes Dual-level logical Rule Learning (DRL), a neural-symbolic framework that jointly learns recognition-level and decision-level logical rules. The pipeline proceeds in three stages. First, BERT embeddings of input text are matched against the 20 PANAS (Positive and Negative Affect Schedule) psychological constructs via element-wise dot products and sigmoid gating, yielding binary first-order logic (FOL) predicates P1(Xi, Mj) indicating affective concept relevance. Second, a knowledge-augmented representation vi is formed by weighting PANAS concept embeddings by their activation scores; a recognition module then classifies vi into one of six etiological categories C0–C5 (no cause, bias/discrimination, career/education, illness, interpersonal relations, existential/meaning) using cross-entropy loss supervised by DeepSeek-V3-generated pseudo-labels. This yields recognition-level FOL rules: conjunctions of P1 predicates implying P2(Xi, Ck) cause assignments. Third, the predicted cause probabilities q = [P(c^(k)|vi)] serve as features for the final decision classifier, producing decision-level rules mapping cause patterns to diagnosis labels Pd(Xi, Dy). Training uses a weighted combination L = 0.2·Lc + 0.8·Ld. The LLM outputs serve only as optimization targets, not direct explanations, preserving controllability against hallucination. Key Results: DRL is evaluated on three text classification benchmarks — MHR (10K samples), RER (23.5K Reddit posts), and DAR (7K Facebook/social media posts) — with macro-averaged precision, recall, and F1. DRL achieves F1 scores of 89.22%, 95.38%, and 96.35% on MHR, RER, and DAR respectively, outperforming all baselines. Against the best LLM baseline (DeepSeek-V3): +2.38% on MHR, +10.60% on RER, +16.26% on DAR. Against the best white-box baseline (Explainable Boosting Machine): +17.39% on MHR, +12.25% on RER, +39.93% on DAR. A case study demonstrates that for a suicidal ideation text, DRL correctly identifies negative PANAS concepts (ashamed M13, guilty M14, upset M16, scared M17, jittery M19) and attributes the cause to C5 (existential meaninglessness), producing a fully traceable logical derivation chain. Strengths: - The dual-level explainability design is principled and clinically motivated — recognition-level rules expose etiology while decision-level rules expose symptom-to-diagnosis mapping, addressing two distinct clinical needs that prior work conflates or ignores. - Using LLM outputs as soft training targets rather than direct explanations is a pragmatic design choice that mitigates hallucination risk while still leveraging LLM world knowledge for etiological labeling. - Empirical results are strong and consistent across three heterogeneous datasets, with DRL outperforming both much larger black-box LLMs (GPT-4, DeepSeek-V3) and all white-box baselines simultaneously. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The recognition-level ground truth is generated by DeepSeek-V3 with a simple prompt, introducing a circular dependency: the quality and clinical validity of the etiological annotations — the foundation of recognition-level explainability — are never independently verified against expert psychiatric assessment. - The etiological taxonomy (C0–C5) is coarse and borrowed from a single 2022 annotation paper (CAMS corpus); there is no ablation or sensitivity analysis showing whether this specific categorization meaningfully impacts classification performance or clinical utility. - All datasets are Kaggle-sourced social media text with automated or crowd-sourced labels; no clinical validation (e.g., against DSM-5 diagnostic records) is performed, making it unclear whether the framework generalizes to real clinical deployment settings.
Search Results
#85 — Innovative (63)

FS_GPlib: Breaking the Web-Scale Barrier-A Unified Acceleration Framework for Graph Propagation Models

Chang Guo, Juyuan Zhang, Chang Su, Tianlong Fan, Linyuan Lü · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: FS_GPlib delivers a meaningful engineering contribution by unifying 29 propagation models under a batched tensor framework and demonstrating the only known Monte Carlo simulation system on billion-edge graphs. The dual-acceleration design (micro-level message-passing vectorization + macro-level simulation batching) is a clean formulation, and the target-node partitioning with O(|V|) communication is a practical distributed computing insight. However, the core techniques — batched tensor operations, synchronous message passing, LPT load balancing — are well-established individually; the contribution is their systematic combination for propagation models rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Propagation models (epidemic spreading, information diffusion, opinion dynamics) are fundamental tools for analyzing dynamic processes on web-scale graphs with billions of edges. Existing libraries — NDlib, EoN, CyNetDiff — provide convenient interfaces but are bottlenecked by four nested loops (simulations × time steps × nodes × neighbors), making them computationally prohibitive at scale. GPU-parallel methods exist for specific models (e.g., GP-IC) but lack breadth and scalability. General-purpose graph systems (Pregel, GraphLab) scale but lack propagation-specific optimizations. No existing tool combines broad model coverage, GPU acceleration, distributed execution, and theoretical scalability guarantees. Methodology: FS_GPlib introduces a dual-acceleration framework applied to a unified formal model of mechanism-based propagation. At the micro level, it replaces sequential node-by-node traversal with synchronous message-passing updates formalized as message, aggregate, and update functions (matching GNN forward propagation), executed as vectorized tensor operations over all nodes simultaneously. At the macro level, it stacks B independent Monte Carlo simulation states into a B×|V| tensor and runs all simulations in a single batched forward pass, inspired by deep learning mini-batch processing. For distributed execution on web-scale graphs, FS_GPlib partitions by target node using a Longest Processing Time heuristic (nodes sorted by in-degree, assigned via cumulative-weight bucketing), guaranteeing each target node belongs to exactly one partition — enabling local aggregation without cross-partition communication. After local updates, an all_reduce synchronizes states with O(|V|) communication per step, independent of |E|. The framework proves O(1) amortized runtime per simulation step under idealized parallelism assumptions (p = Θ(m) processors, B = Θ(S) batch capacity). It ships 29 built-in models across epidemic, opinion dynamics, and dynamic graph categories, with a PyTorch/PyG-based Python API. Key Results: On 1,000 Monte Carlo IC simulations, FS_GPlib(GPU) achieves up to 35,000× speedup over NDlib on the Yelp graph (716K nodes, 14M edges), completing in 10.75s vs. NDlib's estimated 376,977s. FS_GPlib(GPU) consistently outperforms CND-IC and GP-IC on large graphs, where GP-IC runs out of memory on Yelp. For SIR on Yelp, FS_GPlib(GPU) runs in 99.21s vs. NDlib's estimated 271,245s (~2,734× speedup), outperforming GT-SIR (1,546s) and EoN-SIR (28,765s extrapolated). On the Webbase graph (118M nodes, 993M edges), distributed simulation across 6 GPUs completes 1,000 Monte Carlo SIR runs in ~11,500s total — approximately 11.5s per simulation. Accuracy validation on IC and SIR across Cora, PubMed, Flickr, and Yelp shows simulation outcomes match NDlib and NetworkX closely. Batch-size experiments confirm diminishing returns beyond an optimal B_max due to memory bandwidth saturation, consistent with the theoretical bound. Strengths: - Practical software contribution: open-source Python library with 29 models, PyTorch/PyG integration, clean API, and cross-platform validation (Linux/macOS, CPU/GPU), making it immediately deployable. - Strong scalability: the only demonstrated system capable of full Monte Carlo simulation on a billion-edge graph; speedups grow with graph size, the correct behavior for a scalable system. - Novel target-node partitioning reduces inter-process communication to O(|V|) per step regardless of |E|, with a provable load-balance guarantee bounded by max in-degree. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The O(1) theoretical result rests on idealizations (p = Θ(m) processors, B = Θ(S) batch capacity, negligible overhead) that do not hold in practice; the paper acknowledges hardware bottlenecks cause diminishing batch returns, and large graphs like Pokec already exceed single-GPU memory at batch size > 10. - Evaluation is limited to IC and SIR for efficiency comparisons; the 27 remaining models are validated only for correctness on small graphs (Cora, PubMed) and often with fewer than 1,000 simulations, leaving scalability of opinion and dynamic-network models uncharacterized. - The distributed simulation interface is incomplete — no built-in multi-process orchestration is provided, only a reference implementation — limiting accessibility for users targeting billion-edge graphs without systems expertise.
Search Results 2603.14895 PDF
#86 — Innovative (63)

Temporal Gains, Spatial Costs: Revisiting Video Fine-Tuning in Multimodal Large Language Models (16 ▲ )

Linghao Zhang, Jungang Li, Yonghua Hei, Sicheng Tao, Song Dai, Yibo Yan, Zihao Dongfang, Weiting Liu, Chenxi Qin, Hanqian Li, Xin Zou, Jiahao Zhang, Shuhang Xun, Haiyun Jiang, Xuming Hu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 63 for systematically documenting and formalizing the 'temporal trap' — that Video-SFT reliably degrades image performance across architectures and scales. The controlled ablation ruling out domain shift (via replicated static frames) and the theoretical gradient alignment analysis provide genuine mechanistic insight. The Hybrid-Frame Strategy is a practical mitigation. However, the core finding that multi-task gradient interference causes performance degradation is broadly expected from the multi-task learning literature, limiting the conceptual novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly trained with video-based supervised fine-tuning (Video-SFT) as a post-training step to improve temporal understanding. A tacit assumption underlying this practice is that Video-SFT benefits unified visual learning broadly — that improvements in video understanding should at least preserve, if not enhance, static image capability. This assumption has not been systematically examined. Given that images and videos share visual encoders and alignment mechanisms in modern MLLMs, it is far from obvious that gradient updates optimized for temporal tasks will be neutral or beneficial to spatial reasoning. Methodology: The authors conduct a controlled empirical study of Video-SFT across three dimensions: model architecture (LLaVA-1.5, LLaVA-NeXT-Video, Qwen2.5-VL), model scale (3B, 7B, 32B, 72B parameters of Qwen2.5-VL), and temporal budget (8, 16, 32, 64 frames). A curated training set of 20,000 videos from 10 sources (derived from LLaVA-Next-Video-178K) is used under a unified fine-tuning pipeline. Evaluation spans four image benchmarks (MME, MMStar, MMBench, POPE) and four video benchmarks (Video-MME, MVBench, TempCompass, Video-MMMU). The authors complement empirical results with a conservative first-order theoretical analysis using gradient alignment between image and video objectives under shared-parameter optimization. They derive three propositions: (1) a sufficient condition under which a Video-SFT gradient step increases image loss (negative gradient alignment); (2) a discrete temporal-budget threshold m* beyond which expected transfer flips from cooperative to conflicting; and (3) a formal justification for sample-adaptive frame allocation via a smoothness-based upper bound. Motivated by this theory, they implement a Hybrid-Frame Strategy that uses a VLM predictor (Qwen3-VL-8B or Qwen2.5-VL-3B) to assign per-sample frame budgets (8/16/32/64) based on five dimensions: event duration, motion continuity, causal relations, object interaction, and fine-grained visual attributes. Key Results: The consistent finding across all architectures and scales is what the authors term the "temporal trap": Video-SFT reliably improves video benchmark performance but frequently degrades static image benchmarks. LLaVA-1.5 suffers the largest drops (e.g., celebrity recognition on MME drops 80.59 points); Qwen2.5-VL is more resilient but not immune. Larger models are more robust — the 72B Qwen2.5-VL largely recovers image performance post-SFT — but scaling to 72B is often impractical. Increasing frame count from 8 to 64 consistently improves video performance (Video-MME improves ~15 points for Qwen2.5-VL-7B), but hurts or provides diminishing returns on image tasks. A controlled ablation confirms the degradation stems from gradient interference, not input format mismatch: when the base model is evaluated on replicated static frames matching training frame counts, image scores also decline with more frames, ruling out domain shift as the sole cause. The Hybrid-Frame Strategy (Qwen3-VL-8B predictor, average ~11 frames per sample) achieves the best MMStar (62.33%) and POPE (88.20%) scores among all Qwen2.5-VL-7B configurations — outperforming fixed 64-frame Video-SFT on image tasks — while maintaining competitive video performance. The strategy is effective across both Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-1.5 architectures, demonstrating generality. Strengths: - The study is genuinely systematic: three architecture families, four model scales, four frame-count settings, and eight benchmarks, all under a controlled unified pipeline — producing a reliable and reproducible finding rather than an anecdotal one. - The theoretical analysis, while conservative and local, is honest about its assumptions and provides actionable insight: it formalizes exactly when and why frame budget amplifies the image-video conflict, directly motivating the Hybrid-Frame intervention. - The Hybrid-Frame ablation is thorough, comparing DINOv2-based, Qwen2.5-VL-3B-based, and Qwen3-VL-8B-based predictors, and validating across architectures — providing clear implementation guidance rather than just a proof of concept. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Hybrid-Frame predictor operates solely on textual instructions, ignoring actual video visual content — as the authors acknowledge, a temporally complex query paired with a nearly static video would be misclassified. The predictor is also a large VLM itself (8B parameters), adding non-trivial inference overhead not discussed in terms of wall-clock cost. - The theoretical analysis derives only local sufficient conditions under stylized decompositions (e.g., Eq. 15 separating shared vs. temporal gradient components), with no empirical validation that these assumptions hold in practice. The gap between the population-level propositions and actual minibatch training dynamics remains uncharacterized. - The study covers only standard offline benchmark evaluation; streaming, interactive, or long-video settings are excluded, and the temporal trap may manifest differently under online or continual training regimes that are increasingly common in deployed MLLMs.
Recommended 2603.17541 PDF
#87 — Innovative (62)

SAMA: Factorized Semantic Anchoring and Motion Alignment for Instruction-Guided Video Editing (56 ▲ )

Xinyao Zhang, Wenkai Dong, Yuxin Song, Bo Fang, Qi Zhang, Jing Wang, Fan Chen, Hui Zhang, Haocheng Feng, Yu Lu, Hang Zhou, Chun Yuan, Jingdong Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The factorization of video editing into temporally sparse semantic anchoring and physically grounded motion alignment is a clean conceptual contribution, and the zero-shot editing capability from Stage 0 pretraining provides meaningful validation of the hypothesis. However, the approach is fundamentally an engineering refinement atop an existing DiT backbone, and reliance on VLM judges without human evaluation weakens the empirical claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Instruction-guided video editing requires two competing objectives: applying precise semantic changes per the instruction while preserving temporally coherent motion from the source video. Existing approaches inject explicit external priors — VLM-extracted semantic features or structural signals like depth maps and skeletons — to mediate this tension. The authors argue this external-prior dependency bottlenecks generalization and robustness, and that the real gap is a lack of factorization: models conflate semantic planning (sparse, temporally stable) with motion modeling (dense, physically grounded), preventing the diffusion backbone from learning inherent representations for each. Methodology: SAMA (factorized Semantic Anchoring and Motion Alignment) is built on top of Wan2.1-T2V-14B, a video diffusion transformer trained via flow matching. The framework decomposes video editing into two complementary tasks. Semantic Anchoring (SA) samples N sparse anchor frames from the target video, encodes each via SigLIP to produce M=64 patch-level tokens plus one global token, projects them via a two-layer MLP into the latent space, and prepends them to the noisy target latent sequence. The DiT jointly denoises target latents and predicts these semantic tokens under an L1 loss, adding a semantic alignment objective (λ=0.1) to the flow matching loss. This accelerates DiT convergence and stabilizes training. Motion Alignment (MA) applies three motion-centric restoration pretext tasks exclusively to the source video during pre-training: Cube Inpainting (30% temporal masking), Speed Perturbation (2× temporal acceleration), and Tube Shuffle (2×2×2 spatiotemporal tube permutation). These pretext tasks are prompted with task tokens prepended to the editing instruction, forcing the model to internalize motion recovery from raw video without paired editing data. Training proceeds in two stages: Stage 0 pre-trains on ~4M image editing pairs and ~1.6M text-to-video clips using both SA and MA; Stage 1 fine-tunes on ~1M paired video editing samples (Ditto-1M, OpenVE-3M, ReCo-Data) with SA retained. Learned type embeddings (ids 0/1/2 for source/semantic/target tokens) replace shifted RoPE for role disambiguation, yielding faster and more stable convergence. Key Results: SAMA is evaluated on three benchmarks: VIE-Bench (GPT-4o judge), OpenVE-Bench (Gemini-2.5-Pro judge), and ReCo-Bench (Gemini-2.5-Flash-Thinking judge). On VIE-Bench, SAMA achieves average scores of 8.711 (Add), 9.340 (Swap/Change), 9.144 (Remove), and 9.259 (Style) — outperforming all open-source baselines and matching or exceeding Kling-Omni on three of four categories (Kling-Omni scores: 9.181 Add, 9.194 Swap, 9.133 Remove, 9.341 Style). On OpenVE-Bench, SAMA scores 4.05 (Global Style), 3.93 (Local Change), 3.32 (Local Remove), and 3.63 (Subtitle Edit), topping all open-source methods. On ReCo-Bench, SAMA achieves overall scores of 8.92 (Add), 9.10 (Replace), 8.09 (Remove), and 9.25 (Style), best-in-class across most tasks. Ablations confirm that SA alone improves overall score from 6.312 to 6.696 (+0.384), MA alone to 6.711 (+0.399), and the combination to 7.095 (+0.783), demonstrating complementarity. Stage 0 pre-training alone (zero-shot) already enables usable video editing across Replace/Add/Remove/Style tasks without any paired editing data. Strengths: - The factorization insight is clean and well-motivated: semantic editing is temporally sparse and stable while motion coherence is physically grounded, making them naturally separable. This framing eliminates the fragile external-prior pipeline that prior work relied on. - The zero-shot capability from Stage 0 alone is a strong empirical validation of the factorization hypothesis — useful editing behavior emerges before any paired editing supervision, which is a non-trivial and theoretically meaningful finding. - Motion Alignment pretext tasks (cube inpainting, speed perturbation, tube shuffle) are lightweight, require no additional labels, and leverage existing large-scale raw video data at no annotation cost — a practical and scalable design choice. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation relies entirely on VLM judges (GPT-4o, Gemini variants), which are known to have biases and can be gamed or inconsistent; no human evaluation or perceptual user studies are reported to validate judge reliability. - The zero-shot setting (Stage 0 only) still shows notable failure modes — temporal color inconsistency in attribute edits, blurry object additions, and ghosting artifacts in removals — indicating the factorization is not yet sufficient without paired supervision for fine-grained edits. - With N=1 anchor frame used throughout all experiments for efficiency, the Semantic Anchoring is underexplored: the sensitivity of edit quality to N is not quantified, and it is unclear how the method scales to long videos where a single anchor frame may be insufficient to capture temporal semantic evolution.
Recommended 2603.19228 PDF
#88 — Innovative (62)

Stepwise Contrastive Reasoning for Retrieval-Augmented Generation over Knowledge Graphs

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SCR's stepwise reasoning with dynamic question context updates is an elegant solution to the 'lost in the middle' problem, and the efficiency gains (1.2s retrieval, 240 tokens) while enabling tiny LLMs to match frontier models demonstrate genuine practical value. The contrastive training of joint GNN+Transformer representations builds meaningfully on prior work but the core techniques (GAT message passing, InfoNCE loss, beam search) are well-established, and evaluation is limited to Freebase-based benchmarks only.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) requires accurately retrieving multi-hop reasoning paths from large KGs to ground LLM responses in structured facts. Existing LLM-based methods represent KG facts as flat text (e.g., triples), losing graph structure and failing at multi-hop reasoning. GNN-based methods capture structure but lack semantic alignment between question text and entity descriptions. Joint GNN+LLM methods (e.g., GNN-RAG) improve recall but flood the LLM with many candidate paths, exacerbating the "lost in the middle" problem. Fine-tuning LLMs on KG-specific data is expensive and brittle to KG updates. The gap is a lightweight, no-fine-tuning framework that jointly leverages graph structure and textual semantics for interpretable, efficient KG retrieval. Methodology: SCR (Stepwise Contrastive Reasoning) trains a relational GAT-based GNN and a BERT Transformer encoder jointly via InfoNCE contrastive loss, totaling ~112M trainable parameters. A frozen pretrained BERT encodes all entity and relation texts into embeddings, which the GNN propagates via relation-aware attention message passing (incorporating both entity and relation text embeddings) to produce structurally-enriched entity representations. Reasoning is iterative: starting from a topic entity e0, a trainable BERT encodes the current question context qi; cosine similarity selects the most aligned neighbor entity as the next step; the question is updated by appending the selected entity's description and the traversed relation. This repeats until the topic entity stabilizes (convergence = answer found). The process is extended to beam search (top-K paths in parallel), producing K hypothesis answer-path pairs that are formatted into a prompt for any downstream LLM. Training supervision uses shortest paths between topic and answer entities as positive examples; the InfoNCE loss pushes the question embedding closer to the correct next-hop entity versus all candidate neighbors. Key Results: On WebQSP, SCR (ChatGPT) achieves 90.9 Hit / 73.4 F1 and SCR (GPT-4o-mini) achieves 90.8 Hit / 74.8 F1, competitive with or exceeding all baselines except SubgraphRAG (GPT-4o-mini) at 90.1 / 77.5 F1. On CWQ, SCR (ChatGPT) achieves 70.4 Hit / 60.2 F1, the best F1 across all methods. Crucially, SCR with tiny LLMs (Qwen3-0.6B: 86.5 / 51.0 on WebQSP; Qwen3-8B: 89.1 / 68.6) matches much larger standalone LLMs. For multi-hop breakdown on CWQ ≥3 hops, SCR's F1 of 64.2 far exceeds GNN-RAG (51.8) and RoG (40.0). On efficiency, SCR achieves 1.2s retrieval time on WebQSP vs. GNN-RAG's 68s and RoG's 948s, with only 240 LLM input tokens vs. 414–1229 for competitors. Ablation shows removing stepwise reasoning drops Hit/F1 by 20.7/29.4 on WebQSP; removing beam search drops 16.1/25.7; replacing the custom GNN with R-GCN drops 6.9/8.0. Strengths: - Strong efficiency-accuracy tradeoff: fastest retrieval (1.2s vs. next-best 2.5s for GCR, which performs poorly) with fewest LLM tokens, while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art performance on two standard benchmarks. - Generalizes across LLM backends without any LLM fine-tuning — even a 0.6B model with SCR matches frontier LLMs used standalone, demonstrating genuine modularity. - Stepwise reasoning with dynamic question context update elegantly addresses the "lost in the middle" problem by progressively filtering the KG rather than dumping all candidates at once. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluated only on Freebase-based benchmarks (WebQSP and CWQ); generalization to other KG schemas or open-domain KGs with heterogeneous entity descriptions is not demonstrated. - The termination criterion (topic entity stops changing) can cause premature stopping if the correct answer is not a neighbor of any visited entity, or if the graph has misleading high-similarity neighbors; the authors acknowledge filtering erroneous paths as future work. - F1 on WebQSP (74.8) is still 2.7 points below SubgraphRAG (GPT-4o-mini, 77.5), which uses more tokens via in-context learning — suggesting the retrieval precision of SCR is not uniformly dominant, especially for simpler questions where richer LLM context helps.
Search Results
#89 — Innovative (62)

A Neuro- Symbolic Framework Combining Inductive and Deductive Reasoning for Autonomous Driving Planning

Hongyan Wei, Wael AbdAlmageed · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The dual-path decision conditioning — injecting ASP decisions as both a semantic query offset and a KBM velocity bias — is a technically precise integration that goes beyond typical post-hoc symbolic filtering, and the honest ablation revealing that physics constraints alone can hurt safety makes a clear case for deductive conditioning. The hand-crafted 9×6×3 output vocabulary constrains the LLM's reasoning to a narrow action space, undermining claims of open semantic reasoning, and the absence of closed-loop evaluation leaves the safety claims unvalidated in interactive settings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: End-to-end autonomous driving planners (UniAD, VAD, SparseDrive, MomAD) rely entirely on inductive, data-driven reasoning: neural networks statistically map sensor inputs to trajectories. This creates two fundamental gaps. First, purely black-box planners cannot provide logical justification for their decisions, making safety certification and error attribution difficult. Second, their performance is bounded by training data coverage, leaving them brittle on long-tail, out-of-distribution scenarios. Existing mitigations—post-hoc collision re-scoring, data augmentation—do not change the inductive nature of the planner. Pure symbolic/rule-based systems are the logical alternative but cannot handle real-world semantic richness, and their discrete outputs cannot be trivially converted into kinematically feasible continuous trajectories. Methodology: The paper proposes a three-module neuro-symbolic framework that runs an inductive stream and a deductive stream in parallel. (1) Perception Fact Extraction: a SparseDrive-based sparse perception backbone (ResNet-50) produces structured ASP predicates encoding nearby objects (id, category, distance, speed, heading, relative position, TTC) and ego state. (2) Dynamic Deductive Reasoning Engine: an LLM (Qwen3) ingests the structured scene facts via a carefully designed system prompt and generates 3–6 scene-specific ASP logic suggestions, each typed as suggestion(Action, TargetSpeed, Type). The output vocabulary is strictly bounded to 9 actions × 6 speed levels × 3 navigation modes. These suggestions, combined with a static safety axiom library, are fed to a Clingo ASP solver that applies a five-tier priority ordering (emergency avoidance > safety precautions > legal compliance > efficiency) to produce a unique, deterministic final_decision(Action, Speed). (3) Decision-Conditioned Physical Residual Planner: the discrete decision is embedded into the neural decoder via a dual path—Path 1 injects a learned embedding as a semantic offset to the planning query Q′_plan = Q_plan ⊕ d (shaping trajectory topology); Path 2 biases the initial velocity of a differentiable Kinematic Bicycle Model (KBM), v′_0 = v_0 + b_v (shaping speed profile). The KBM integrates a (acceleration, steering angle) sequence via second-order Runge-Kutta to produce a physically feasible baseline trajectory τ_physics. A neural residual τ_residual (bounded by tanh and a scale λ) is added: τ_final = τ_physics + λ·tanh(τ_residual). An anisotropic residual loss suppresses lateral sideslip with 10× the penalty of longitudinal residuals. Training is two-stage: Stage 1 trains the KBM-constrained planner to establish physical priors; Stage 2 fine-tunes with ASP conditioning for 10 epochs. ASP decisions are pre-computed offline and indexed by sample token to avoid LLM latency during training. Key Results: On the nuScenes validation set, the full neuro-symbolic model achieves L2 mean = 0.57 m, collision rate = 0.075%, and TPC = 0.47 m, outperforming MomAD (the primary baseline) by 5.0%, 16.7%, and 13.0% respectively. Ablation isolates contributions: KBM alone reduces L2 from 0.60→0.58 m and TPC from 0.54→0.47 m but paradoxically raises collision rate from 0.09%→0.11%, confirming that physics-constrained trajectory shaping without logical guidance can hurt safety in edge cases. Adding ASP conditioning then drops the collision rate to 0.075% (a 31.8% reduction over KBM-only). Short-horizon improvements are pronounced (1 s L2: −12.9%, 2 s: −5.3%) while long-horizon improvements are modest (3 s: −1.1%), consistent with the design intent: ASP constrains the immediate action intent, while 3 s trajectory shape remains inductive. Inference runs at 10 FPS on H200, marginally faster than SparseDrive (9.0) and MomAD (7.8) on RTX4090; the lightweight Clingo solver executes in under 5 ms per frame with the LLM running asynchronously offline. Strengths: - The dual-path decision conditioning (query offset + velocity bias into KBM) is a technically precise and non-trivial integration: ASP decisions directly modulate physically meaningful control quantities rather than acting as a post-hoc filter, which is the key design advance over prior neuro-symbolic work. - The anisotropic residual loss is a well-motivated inductive bias that enforces the physical constraint that cars do not sideslip, bridging model assumptions and learned corrections cleanly. - The ablation is honest about the failure mode of KBM alone (collision rate increases), lending credibility to the full system's results and making the necessity of deductive conditioning empirically clear. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is entirely open-loop on nuScenes, which the paper itself acknowledges underestimates closed-loop collision risk; it is unknown whether the safety gains hold in interactive closed-loop simulation (CARLA), and such experiments are deferred to future work. - The LLM rule extraction runs at ~1 s/frame offline (too slow for real-time), and the asynchronous low-frequency operation in deployment introduces logical latency during rapid scene changes—this is acknowledged but not empirically characterized, leaving the practical deployment gap unresolved. - The symbolic vocabulary (9 actions × 6 speeds) is hand-crafted and may not generalize beyond urban structured scenarios; the claim that the LLM generates 'open semantic reasoning beyond a fixed rule base' is weakened by this constrained output space, since the LLM cannot produce actions outside the vocabulary even for genuinely novel situations.
Search Results 2603.12421 PDF
#90 — Innovative (62)

The constructionalization of the Chinese continuous simile construction “Ru A Si B”: research conducted from the novel standpoint of conceptual integration

degruyterbrill.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Scores 62 for applying Conceptual Integration Theory diachronically to constructionalization — a genuinely novel theoretical combination — and for proposing three language-specific mechanisms (prosodic rhythm, structural symmetry, lexical adhesion) beyond the standard reanalysis/analogy toolkit. The multi-pathway poly-genetic analysis with intertextual derivational evidence is methodologically rigorous. However, the cognitive claims have limited falsifiability, the phonetic experiment relies on a single speaker, and the contribution is domain-specific to Chinese historical linguistics with limited cross-disciplinary impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: This paper addresses the formation history of the Chinese continuous simile construction "ru A si B" (如A似B, e.g., "rú láng sì hǔ" — like wolves and tigers), a compact, fixed dual-simile frame that has received no dedicated scholarly investigation despite being a distinctive and productive phenomenon in Chinese. While single-vehicle simile constructions have been extensively studied, continuous simile constructions across languages (Korean "gati…gati", Vietnamese "như…như", Japanese "no goto…ni nite") remain underexplored. The paper also addresses broader theoretical gaps in constructionalization research: the poly-genetic origins of constructions, the need for more visible diachronic evidence, and the insufficiency of reanalysis and analogy as the only recognized mechanisms of constructional change. Methodology: The study combines Constructionalization Theory (Traugott and Trousdale 2013) with Conceptual Integration Theory (Fauconnier and Turner 1998, 2002) to trace the diachronic formation of "ru A si B". The empirical base is the Peking University CCL Corpus (~700 million characters spanning the 11th century BCE to the present) and the BCC Corpus (~9.5 billion characters), queried with character-span and Boolean operators. The authors identify four source structures that historically fed into the target construction: (1) "X ru A + Y si B" (two separate simile clauses sharing a topic), (2) "ru AB xiangsi/si" (a single simile with a compound vehicle), (3) "rusi + AB" (a compound simile marker followed by a disyllabic noun), and (4) "ru + si A si B" (a nested major-minor simile). Each pathway is analyzed through the Conceptual Integration framework — specifying input spaces, generic space, blending space, and emergent meaning — and substantiated with intertextual derivational evidence showing the "before" and "after" forms in historically related texts. Corpus frequency data across dynasties (Five Dynasties: 2 cases; Song: 18; Yuan: 66; Ming–Qing: 398) chart the construction's diachronic trajectory. The study also conducts a phonetic experiment using Praat acoustic analysis to compare the prosodic properties of "ru si jiao qi" vs. "ru jiao si qi", measuring F0 trajectories across 324 data points. Key Results: The construction first emerged in the Five Dynasties period (907–960 CE) and reached maturation by the Ming–Qing era, accounting for 82.23% of attested cases. All four source structures show statistically significant pre-Song usage: "X ru A + Y si B" appears 4,808 times, "ru AB xiangsi/si" 1,997 times, "rusi + AB" 714 times, and "ru + si A si B" 534 times, establishing frequency conditions for constructionalization. For specific idioms like "如胶似漆" (ru jiao si qi) and "如龙似虎" (ru long si hu), at least two to three distinct source pathways are identified, demonstrating poly-genesis. The BCC corpus shows a 705-to-53 ratio of written vs. spoken usage, confirming register asymmetry. The phonetic experiment confirms that "ru jiao si qi" produces significantly superior rhythmic cadence over "ru si jiao qi": the F0 of jiāo in the target form maintains a high-pitch plateau (mean 53) with standard deviation 0.235, whereas in the source form F0 drops erratically (mean 33, SD 0.193). Beyond reanalysis and analogy, the paper proposes three China-specific mechanisms: (a) prosodic rhythm — the 2+2 disyllabic beat template and tonal alternation (平仄) between ru (level tone) and si (oblique tone) drove structural compression; (b) structural symmetry — co-occurring constructions like "bu…bu…" exerted formal and semantic pressure on the developing "ru…si…" frame; (c) lexical adhesion — the cognitive bond of disyllabic compounds (e.g., 胶漆, 形影) persists after morpheme separation and drives structural compaction by eliminating redundant elements. Strengths: - Rigorous empirical grounding: the multi-pathway analysis is supported by direct intertextual derivational evidence (paraphrases and cross-volume borrowings within the same text) rather than inferred frequency alone, providing unusually concrete diachronic traces. - Theoretical innovation: applying Conceptual Integration Theory diachronically to explain form-meaning co-evolution is genuinely novel within constructionalization research, and the four conceptual integration diagrams (input/generic/blending spaces) clarify the cognitive process at each pathway. - Language-specific mechanisms: prosodic rhythm, structural symmetry, and lexical adhesion are proposed and argued as distinct from the conventional reanalysis/analogy toolkit, with the phonetic experiment providing instrumental validation for the prosodic claim. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single-speaker phonetic experiment: the prosodic analysis relies on one 25-year-old male Mandarin speaker with controlled conditions, making acoustic conclusions about historical tonal motivation difficult to generalize; cross-dialectal or multi-speaker validation is absent. - Corpus finiteness and potential pathway incompleteness: the authors acknowledge that the four identified pathways may not be exhaustive, and Chinese textual heritage is too vast to be fully covered by any current corpus, leaving open the possibility of unrecovered source structures or counter-examples. - Limited falsifiability of cognitive claims: the Conceptual Integration framework is applied post-hoc to historical data; alternative cognitive or structural explanations for the same formal changes are not systematically considered, and the claim that "blending" rather than simpler mechanisms (haplology, ellipsis) drove integration is not decisively tested.
Search Results
#91 — Innovative (61)

MonoArt: Progressive Structural Reasoning for Monocular Articulated 3D Reconstruction (26 ▲ )

Haitian Li, Haozhe Xie, Junxiang Xu, Beichen Wen, Fangzhou Hong, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: MonoArt demonstrates strong systems-level engineering with its progressive decomposition — geometry, then parts, then motion — which is a sensible inductive bias for articulated reconstruction. The comprehensive ablation study and competitive inference speed are commendable. However, the individual components (triplane projection, contrastive learning, dual-query transformers, Hungarian matching) are well-established building blocks assembled in a competent but expected manner, and the method's tight coupling to PartNet-Mobility limits its demonstrated generalization beyond household objects.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reconstructing articulated 3D objects — geometry, part decomposition, and joint kinematics — from a single image is a fundamental challenge in computer vision with direct applications in robotics and scene synthesis. The core difficulty is the entanglement between motion cues and object structure, which makes direct regression of articulation parameters from image features unstable. Prior single-image approaches fall into three problematic camps: retrieval-based methods (SINGAPO, Articulate-Anything) that assemble parts from asset libraries, causing texture misalignment and geometric inaccuracies; VLM-based methods (PhysX-Anything) that rely on handcrafted priors limiting generalization; and video-generation-based methods (DreamArt) that are computationally expensive (>200 seconds per instance). None of these develop intrinsic 3D structural understanding sufficient to directly infer part composition and kinematics from a single observation. Methodology: MonoArt is an end-to-end framework that progressively transforms a single RGB image into a fully articulated 3D representation through four sequentially composed components. (1) A frozen TRELLIS backbone generates a canonical 3D mesh and sparse voxel latent Z ∈ R^(Nz×Nz×Nz×d1) from the input image, providing a geometry-stable foundation independent of articulation state. (2) The Part-Aware Semantic Reasoner lifts geometry-aligned point features (obtained via tri-linear interpolation of Z at M=100K surface samples) into globally contextualized embeddings H through triplane projection followed by a Part Contrast Transformer. These embeddings are supervised with triplet contrastive loss to enforce part-level feature separation, producing discriminative, motion-consistent representations. (3) The Dual-Query Motion Decoder maintains two complementary query types — content queries Qc encoding part semantics and position queries Qp representing spatial motion anchors — initialized from global object context and iteratively refined over L=6 layers via self-attention (inter-part interactions) and cross-attention (evidence retrieval from H). Per-layer part class logits retrieve CLIP semantic prototypes to enhance semantic consistency. A confidence scoring mechanism based on Hungarian matching to ground truth determines which queries correspond to valid parts. (4) The Kinematic Estimator regresses full articulation parameters per part — mask (via query-point affinity Qc·H^T), joint type, axis, pivot, and motion limits — and predicts the kinematic tree structure via a learned bilinear compatibility matrix over part category distributions. Training uses a four-stage curriculum: warmup of the semantic reasoner with triplet loss only, initialization of dual queries with category supervision, joint optimization of the full network, and isolated training of the kinematic tree predictor. Key Results: On PartNet-Mobility, MonoArt achieves state-of-the-art on all seven geometry and kinematic metrics in both evaluation settings. On 7 categories: F-Score 0.728 vs. SINGAPO 0.572 (+27%), joint type accuracy 88.26% vs. SINGAPO 77.12% (+11pp), axis error 0.209 rad vs. SINGAPO 0.493 rad (57% reduction), pivot error 0.085 vs. SINGAPO 0.201 (58% reduction). On all 46 categories: F-Score 0.670 vs. PhysXAnything 0.531 (+26%), joint type accuracy 67.47% vs. 63.35%, pivot error 0.108 vs. 0.173 (38% reduction). Inference runs in 20.5 seconds per instance on an A6000 GPU — comparable to SINGAPO (19.6s) and 11× faster than Articulate-Anything (229.9s) and PhysXAnything (256.8s). In a user study with 20 participants evaluating real-world generalization on 100 in-the-wild images, MonoArt achieves scores of 4.63/4.37 (geometry/kinematics on 1–5 scale), substantially outperforming the next best, PhysXAnything at 3.34/3.12. Ablations confirm that all three novel components contribute materially: removing the Part-Aware Semantic Reasoner drops type accuracy from 67.47% to 24.72%; replacing triplet supervision with cross-entropy reduces it to 57.74%; removing dual-query initialization drops type accuracy to 44.06%. Strengths: - The progressive structural decomposition — geometry first, then parts, then motion — directly addresses the instability of flat articulation regression and produces interpretable intermediate representations that can be supervised independently. - Competitive inference speed relative to methods with similar generality (46 categories), achieved without video generation or retrieval, which are the main cost drivers of comparable systems. - Comprehensive ablation study isolates the contribution of each architectural decision (reasoner enabled/disabled, loss type, query initialization, residual update branches, number of decoder layers), giving clear evidence for design choices rather than relying solely on end-to-end comparison. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method samples points uniformly over the full surface, which creates sparse coverage for small components (e.g., buttons on a printer). This is a structural limitation of the representation, not just a training artifact, and would require either adaptive or hierarchical sampling to address. - MonoArt relies entirely on PartNet-Mobility for training, which covers a specific distribution of household objects with relatively conventional articulation patterns. The model degrades on objects with uncommon topologies or kinematic structures (e.g., bicycles) due to domain gap, and there is no mechanism for out-of-distribution detection or graceful degradation beyond confidence thresholding. - The four-stage training curriculum with frozen modules at each transition introduces non-trivial engineering complexity and approximately six days of training on four A100s, which limits accessibility for iterating on the design and raises questions about whether joint end-to-end training from initialization might achieve similar or better results.
Recommended 2603.19231 PDF
#92 — Innovative (61)

TOP-RL: Task- Optimized Progressive Token Pruning with Reinforcement Learning for Vision Language Models

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The MDP formulation for progressive token pruning is a creative departure from heuristic-based methods, and the dense step-wise reward with temporal scaling is well-motivated. Results are strong across six LVLMs and nine benchmarks. However, the token pruning space is crowded, the task-specificity that drives its accuracy advantage is also its main practical liability (requiring per-task agents), and the method does not improve GPU memory or achieve the best efficiency on the Pareto frontier.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) incur heavy inference costs because a single image maps to hundreds or thousands of visual tokens. Existing token pruning methods either compress all tokens once before the LLM (input-stage pruning) or drop a fixed subset at one shallow layer using heuristic thresholds, both ignoring how visual redundancy evolves across layers and across tasks. The paper empirically demonstrates two key phenomena: (1) the "Information Cliff" — the layer beyond which visual tokens are no longer attended to — occurs much earlier for reasoning tasks (GQA, ScienceQA) than for perception tasks (POPE, TextVQA); and (2) pruning sensitivity varies substantially across both layers and tasks. These observations motivate a pruning strategy that is simultaneously progressive across layers and adaptive to the downstream task objective — a combination absent from prior work. Methodology: TOP-RL formulates visual token pruning as a multi-stage Markov Decision Process (MDP). A lightweight MLP-based actor network is trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to produce differentiable binary masks at each pruning stage (implemented via Gumbel-Softmax during training, deterministic top-k at inference). The state at each stage is the current set of visual token embeddings; the action is a binary keep/drop mask; transitions advance through Transformer decoder blocks. The reward is dense and step-wise: r_t = r_Q − α_t · r_L_t, where r_Q is the task metric ratio (BLEU, accuracy, etc.) after full generation and r_L_t is the token retention ratio at stage t. A temporal scaling factor α_t = η^(T−t) (η = 0.95) encourages earlier and more aggressive pruning. To maintain compatibility with FlashAttention and stable Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE) across variable-length sequences, the method uses an attention masking strategy that zeros out pruned tokens' contributions while keeping the attention map at full N×N size. Key Results: On LLaVA-1.5-7B with 192 retained tokens (66.7% reduction), TOP-RL achieves 97.6% average performance retention across 9 benchmarks spanning perception (POPE, VizWiz, Flickr30k, RefCOCO, TextVQA) and reasoning (GQA, AI2D, MathVista, ScienceQA), outperforming VisionZip (94.3%), DivPrune (93.9%), SparseVLM (91.3%), and PyramidDrop (91.2%). Under extreme compression (64 tokens, 88.9% reduction), TOP-RL retains 91.0% performance versus FastV's 66.5% and SparseVLM's 78.0%. On LLaVA-NeXT-7B with 320 average visual tokens, TOP-RL achieves a 1.91× inference speedup and 9.32× FLOPs reduction while achieving F1=87.2, the best among all baselines at the same compression level. On video QA (Video-LLaVA), compressing 2048 tokens to 194, TOP-RL achieves 93.9% average retention versus 93.4% for VisionZip and 90.7% for SparseVLM. Cross-task transfer experiments show that within-domain transfer (e.g., POPE→TextVQA) is largely graceful, while cross-cognitive-level transfer (e.g., TextVQA→SQA) degrades noticeably. Strengths: - Principled MDP formulation that enables end-to-end RL optimization of pruning decisions across all decoder layers, directly optimizing downstream task metrics rather than proxy signals like attention entropy. - Dense, step-wise reward with temporal scaling is a concrete and well-motivated design choice that avoids the sparse-reward problem common in token-level RL, and the temporal factor specifically addresses the desire for front-loaded pruning. - Strong empirical coverage: experiments span six LVLMs (LLaVA-1.5, LLaVA-NeXT, Video-LLaVA, LLaVA-OV, Qwen2.5-VL, InternVL-3) and nine benchmarks at two compression rates, with consistent improvement over five baselines. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Task-specificity is a double-edged sword: cross-task transfer experiments reveal meaningful degradation when transferring across cognitive levels (e.g., a perception-trained agent on reasoning tasks), implying that deployment requires either a separate trained agent per task type or a task-identification mechanism — a practical burden not fully addressed. - The RL training loop requires running full inference through the LVLM to compute rewards, which is significantly more expensive than the supervised or attention-heuristic training of competing methods; training cost and sample efficiency are not discussed. - GPU memory usage (15.6 GB) is not improved over simpler baselines like FastV/PDrop, and VisionZip achieves a higher FLOPs reduction (13.09×) at a better speedup (2.11×), revealing that TOP-RL's accuracy advantage comes at a real efficiency cost on the Pareto frontier.
Search Results
#93 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Memento-Skills: Let Agents Design Agents (23 ▲ )

Huichi Zhou, Siyuan Guo, Anjie Liu, Zhongwei Yu, Ziqin Gong, Bowen Zhao, Zhixun Chen, Menglong Zhang, Yihang Chen, Jinsong Li, Runyu Yang, Qiangbin Liu, Xinlei Yu, Jianmin Zhou, Na Wang, Chunyang Sun, Jun Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The parameter-free continual learning paradigm and behavior-aligned skill routing via offline RL are solid contributions with principled theoretical backing from the SRDP framework. However, the sharp degradation of cross-task transfer on unstructured benchmarks undermines the system's generality claim, absolute performance remains modest, and the evaluation lacks comparisons against published SOTA agent systems, making it difficult to contextualize the empirical contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents are typically deployed as frozen models whose parameters remain fixed after pretraining. This makes them stateless: they cannot learn from deployment experience without expensive gradient-based fine-tuning. Existing workarounds — few-shot prompting, RAG over raw trajectory logs, or text-only prompt optimization — either overfit to small datasets or fail to generalize across tasks. The gap this paper fills is enabling continual, parameter-free improvement of a frozen LLM agent by treating reusable, executable skills as the unit of external memory. Methodology: Memento-Skills instantiates the Stateful Reflective Decision Process (SRDP) framework from prior work (Memento 2) with a concrete deployable system. The core mechanism is a Read–Write Reflective Learning loop: (1) Observe a task; (2) Read — a behavior-aligned skill router retrieves the most relevant skill from a growing library of structured skill folders (each folder contains a declarative SKILL.md spec, helper scripts, and prompts); (3) Execute the skill through the frozen LLM via a ReAct-style loop; (4) Collect feedback via an LLM judge; (5) Write — on failure, a failure attribution selector identifies the responsible skill, a skill rewriter patches it in place, or if the skill's utility rate drops below a threshold, a skill discovery module synthesizes an entirely new skill. All mutations are validated by an auto-generated unit-test gate before being committed to the library. The skill router is trained with single-step offline RL using a multi-positive InfoNCE contrastive loss over ~8k curated skills and synthetically generated positive/hard-negative query pairs. This yields "Memento-Qwen," a fine-tuned Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B model that optimizes for behavioral execution success rather than semantic surface similarity. The theoretical guarantee from Memento 2 ensures convergence of the KL-regularized soft policy iteration over the Reflected MDP, with the asymptotic performance gap decomposing into LLM quality, memory coverage radius, and retrieval error — three independently improvable components. Key Results: On the General AI Assistants (GAIA) benchmark (165 questions, 65 held-out test examples), Memento-Skills achieves 66.0% overall test accuracy vs. 52.3% for a Read-Write ablation that disables skill evolution (+13.7 percentage points). Training accuracy rises from 65.1% to 91.6% across three reflective retry rounds. On Humanity's Last Exam (HLE, 342 test examples across 8 academic domains), Memento-Skills achieves 38.7% vs. 17.9% baseline — more than doubling performance (+116.2% relative). Per-domain training gains range from +14.5pp (Engineering) to +30.4pp (Biology). The skill library grows from 5 atomic seeds to 41 skills after GAIA learning and 235 skills after HLE learning, organized into semantically coherent clusters (Search/Web, Quantum/Physics, Math/Chemistry, Code/Text, etc.). For the behavior-aligned router, Memento-Qwen achieves Recall@1 of 0.60 vs. 0.54 for Qwen3 embedding and 0.32 for BM25 on 140 synthetic queries, and lifts end-to-end judge success rate from 0.79 (Qwen3) to 0.80. A key finding is that cross-task transfer is strong when the benchmark has structured domain taxonomy (HLE) but weak when tasks are highly diverse with little pattern overlap (GAIA), where most optimized skills were never triggered at test time. Strengths: - No parameter updates required: all adaptation occurs in the external skill library, making deployment-time learning feasible at zero retraining cost with any frozen LLM - Principled theoretical foundation: the SRDP framework provides convergence guarantees for the retrieval policy, and the performance gap bound gives three interpretable, independent levers for improvement (stronger LLM, more episodes, better embedding) - Behavior-aligned routing via offline RL demonstrably outperforms both lexical (BM25) and semantic (dense embedding) retrieval, validating the claim that execution success and semantic similarity diverge in large skill libraries Weaknesses / Limitations: - Cross-task transfer degrades sharply on benchmarks with low domain structure (GAIA): most learned skills are never reused on held-out questions, limiting the system's benefit to domains with natural taxonomic overlap - Absolute performance remains modest even after learning (38.7% on HLE test, 66.0% on GAIA test), and the evaluation sets are small (65 and 342 examples respectively), making it difficult to assess statistical significance of improvements - Safety and sandboxing are explicitly deferred as future work; the system executes LLM-generated code in a uv-isolated Python environment but no formal security analysis is provided, which is a practical concern for production deployment - The paper's unusual narrative format (interleaved fictional dialogues, multi-track sections) obscures experimental rigor — key details such as the choice of Gemini-3.1-Flash as the base LLM, the synthetic router evaluation on only 140 queries, and the absence of comparisons against published state-of-the-art agent systems make the empirical contribution harder to assess

#94 — Moderately Innovative (59)

FASTER: Rethinking Real-Time Flow VLAs (40 ▲ )

Yuxiang Lu, Zhe Liu, Xianzhe Fan, Zhenya Yang, Jinghua Hou, Junyi Li, Kaixin Ding, Hengshuang Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: FASTER identifies a real and underappreciated bottleneck — reaction time vs. trajectory smoothness in flow-based VLAs — and exploits a clean empirical insight that near-term actions require fewer denoising steps. The horizon-aware schedule and streaming dispatch are practical systems contributions. However, the core idea is fundamentally a scheduling optimization rather than a methodological advance, and the approach still requires fine-tuning despite framing as lightweight.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic control face a critical latency bottleneck when deployed in real-world dynamic environments. Existing asynchronous inference methods for action chunking policies focus on trajectory smoothness but neglect reaction time — the delay between an environmental change and the robot's response. The standard practice of applying a constant timestep schedule in flow-based VLAs forces the system to complete all denoising steps (typically 10) before any action can be dispatched, creating an unnecessarily large Time to First Action (TTFA). This makes flow-based VLAs like π0.5 and X-VLA too slow for highly dynamic tasks such as table tennis. Methodology: FASTER (Fast Action Sampling for ImmediaTE Reaction) introduces a Horizon-Aware Schedule (HAS) that decouples the denoising timestep across the action chunk. The key insight, validated through a pilot study on flow straightness metrics, is that near-term actions follow straighter interpolation paths and converge to accurate estimates within far fewer sampling steps than distant-horizon actions. HAS assigns each action index a distinct 'hit time' u_i = (1 - i/(H-1))^α · u_0, where u_0 is set to (N-1)/N so the first action completes in a single denoising step. A mixed scheduling strategy (probability p for HAS, 1-p for constant) is used during fine-tuning to bridge the gap from pretraining. FASTER also introduces a streaming client-server interface where finalized actions are dispatched immediately while later actions continue denoising, plus an early-stopping mechanism that skips remaining steps once all actions within the execution horizon are ready. This reduces the minimum execution horizon s_min, thereby increasing inference frequency and further tightening reaction time bounds. Key Results: On π0.5 with RTX 4090, FASTER reduces TTFA from 80.0ms to 62.1ms (1.29× speedup) and expected reaction time from 130.0ms to 112.1ms. On X-VLA with RTX 4060, TTFA drops from 399.5ms to 129.2ms (3.09× speedup) and expected reaction time from 599.5ms to 229.2ms (2.62× improvement). For X-VLA on both GPUs, FASTER achieves deterministic dominance — its worst-case reaction time is lower than the baselines' best case. In real-world table tennis experiments, synchronous inference completely fails while FASTER achieves scores of 0.80 (4090) and 0.47 (4060) compared to 0.53/0.30 for Training-time RTC. On LIBERO and CALVIN simulation benchmarks, FASTER maintains competitive performance with only marginal degradation (e.g., π0.5 average 97.8 vs 98.0 on LIBERO-10, CALVIN Avg. Len 4.292 vs 4.313). Strengths: - Elegant and well-motivated approach grounded in a rigorous analysis of reaction time as a uniform random variable, with the TTFA metric providing a clear optimization target analogous to TTFT in LLMs - Plug-and-play design requiring no architectural modifications — only a schedule change during fine-tuning and inference, making it applicable to any flow-based VLA (demonstrated on both π0.5 and X-VLA) - The streaming client-server interface with early stopping is a practical systems contribution that compounds the algorithmic gains, especially on consumer-grade GPUs where the benefits are most pronounced (up to 3× TTFA speedup on RTX 4060) Weaknesses / Limitations: - Simulation benchmark results show marginal performance degradation (X-VLA+FASTER drops from 4.151 to 4.058 avg chain length on CALVIN, and from 70.2 to 63.7 on the hardest 5-task chain), suggesting the aggressive sampling of near-term actions does sacrifice some long-horizon accuracy - The method still requires fine-tuning with the mixed schedule strategy — it is not purely training-free despite claims of no additional training cost; the mixing probability p and schedule parameter α introduce hyperparameters that need tuning per model/task - Real-world evaluation is limited to a single robot platform (Piper 6-DoF arm) with only three tasks and relatively short demonstration data (~14 minutes for table tennis), leaving open questions about generalization to more complex manipulation scenarios or different robot morphologies

#95 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation of Language Models

Woogyeol Jin, Taywon Min, Yongjin Yang, Swanand Ravindra Kadhe, Yi Zhou, Dennis Wei, Nathalie Baracaldo, Kimin Lee · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 58 because the core diagnosis of diversity collapse at high-entropy tokens is well-executed and the entropy-gated forward KL fix is clean and practical, but the idea itself is a relatively straightforward combination of existing components — forward KL, entropy thresholding, and top-k truncation are all well-known techniques. The contribution is primarily in the careful empirical analysis showing where and why reverse KL fails rather than in methodological novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) of language models trains a student by matching a teacher's token-level distributions over the student's own generated trajectories, using reverse KL divergence as the objective. While effective at transferring dominant behaviors, reverse KL is mode-seeking: it concentrates the student's probability mass on the teacher's most likely tokens and ignores low-probability modes. This causes diversity collapse at positions where the teacher assigns significant probability to multiple plausible continuations — precisely the high-entropy tokens that represent ambiguous decision points in complex reasoning tasks. Empirically, distilled students retain only 6.8% of high-entropy tokens (entropy ≥ 1.0) compared to 18.5% in the teacher, and reverse KL produces unstable gradient signals (top-1 predictions fluctuate with a change count of 84.0 ± 16.7) when the teacher distribution is broad. The gap matters most for mathematical reasoning, where multiple valid solution paths exist and preserving the teacher's uncertainty is critical for exploration at test time. Methodology: The paper proposes Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation (EOPD), which augments the standard clipped reverse KL objective (OPD/PPO-style) with a forward KL term that is selectively activated at token positions where the teacher's entropy exceeds a threshold τ. The per-token loss is: L_EOPD(θ; c_t) = L_OPD(θ; c_t) + 𝟙[H_t^te > τ] · L_FKL(θ; c_t), where the forward KL is approximated using only the teacher's top-k tokens (k=16) to avoid the memory cost of computing over the full vocabulary. Forward KL is mode-covering: it penalizes the student for assigning low probability to any token the teacher deems plausible, directly preserving multimodal structure. By restricting forward KL to high-entropy positions (roughly 15–20% of tokens at convergence with τ=0.8), EOPD avoids the efficiency cost and over-diffusion pathologies of naively applying forward KL everywhere. The full training loop is standard on-policy rollout with PPO-style clipping; the only addition is querying the teacher for its entropy at each token position and conditionally adding the top-k forward KL term. Key Results: Evaluated on six math reasoning benchmarks (MATH500, AMC23, Minerva, OlympiadBench, AIME24, AIME25) across three Qwen3 student sizes (0.6B, 1.7B, 4B), EOPD consistently outperforms OPD. For Qwen3-0.6B-Base, EOPD improves Avg@8 by +1.16 and Pass@8 by +1.37 averaged across benchmarks. For 1.7B: +0.99 Avg@8 and +2.39 Pass@8. For 4B: +1.80 Avg@8 and +5.05 Pass@8. Pass@k curves on AIME show the EOPD advantage widens as k increases (from 8 to 128), indicating more diverse exploration. On out-of-domain benchmarks (GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, AlpacaEval 2.0), EOPD achieves higher average accuracy than OPD on general reasoning tasks, suggesting the diversity benefit transfers beyond the math training distribution. Token-level entropy histograms confirm EOPD preserves substantially more probability mass in the high-entropy region (≥1.0 nats) compared to OPD, staying closer to the teacher. EOPD also outperforms entropy-promoting baselines (OPD + Entropy Bonus, OPD + Advantage Shaping) on forward KL at high-entropy positions, showing that generic exploration incentives are insufficient — teacher-guided KL selection is key. Results generalize to Llama-3.2-3B / Llama-3.1-8B pairs. Strengths: - The core diagnosis is sharp and well-supported: the paper isolates the diversity collapse problem with both a controlled toy experiment and a careful token-level entropy analysis on real AIME prompts, making the motivation concrete rather than hand-wavy. - The intervention is minimal and practical — a single indicator-gated forward KL term with top-k truncation adds negligible computational overhead while addressing the root cause, requiring no architectural changes or additional teacher rollouts. - Ablations are thorough: the paper tests sensitivity to τ (stable over 0.6–1.6), compares full vs. random vs. entropy-gated forward KL placement (entropy-gating wins), and benchmarks against two entropy-promotion baselines, isolating the contribution of teacher-guided selection. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to mathematical reasoning; it is unclear whether the same diversity-collapse problem and EOPD's remedy generalize to domains like code generation or instruction following, where the teacher's high-entropy tokens may carry different semantic significance. - The method introduces a hyperparameter τ that requires tuning, and while the paper shows stability across a range, the optimal τ=0.8 was selected empirically; no principled criterion for setting τ given a new teacher/student pair is provided. - Gains on the hardest benchmark (AIME24/25) are modest in absolute terms for smaller models (e.g., Pass@8 +0 for 0.6B on AIME25), and the student-teacher gap remains large overall, leaving open the question of whether entropy-awareness is sufficient or whether architectural capacity is the binding constraint at these difficulty levels.
Recommended 2603.07079 PDF
#96 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Complementary Reinforcement Learning (28 ▲ )

Dilxat Muhtar, Jiashun Liu, Wei Gao, Weixun Wang, Shaopan Xiong, Ju Huang, Siran Yang, Wenbo Su, Jiamang Wang, Ling Pan, Bo Zheng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 58 for a well-motivated neuroscience-inspired framework where actor and experience extractor co-evolve, with a clean ablation demonstrating that static experience banks degrade. The subgroup-split advantage estimation is technically sound. However, the core ideas — experience replay, retrieval-augmented RL, co-training — are individually well-established, and the empirical gains on small models are modest.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning for LLM-based agents suffers from poor sample efficiency because policy updates rely exclusively on sparse binary outcome rewards, leaving rich procedural information embedded in interaction trajectories—successful strategies, recoverable failure patterns, critical decision points—largely unexploited. While augmenting agents with historical experience is a natural remedy, prior approaches treat experience as a static resource: offline experience banks become stale as the actor improves, and online extractors that are not co-trained with the actor suffer progressive distributional misalignment, producing increasingly irrelevant guidance as training advances. The paper establishes through a motivating pilot study that neither offline nor static-online experience provides sustained benefit, identifying distributional drift as the root cause. Methodology: Inspired by Complementary Learning Systems (CLS) in neuroscience—where the hippocampus manages fast episodic memories that are consolidated via cortical feedback—the authors propose Complementary RL, a framework that jointly optimizes a policy actor πθ and an experience extractor πϕ within a single RL loop. The actor is trained with a modified GRPO objective (J_GRPO^split) that partitions rollouts into experience-guided and experience-free subgroups with separate within-subgroup advantage normalization; this prevents reward-scale bias from collapsing training and forces the actor to internalize experience rather than become dependent on it. The extractor is optimized via the CISPO objective using binary rewards indicating whether its distilled experience contributed to actor success, creating a closed co-evolutionary feedback loop. Experience management is handled by a centralized asynchronous ExperienceManager (H) that decouples rollout collection from distillation via a producer-consumer queue, uses embedding-based semantic retrieval with query batching and parallel search workers (under reader locks), and performs periodic chunk-wise Merge operations to eliminate redundant or conflicting entries. A Search-and-Ask mechanism lets the actor issue context-aware mid-episode queries to the experience bank, increasing utilization and diversifying extractor training signal. Additional stabilization techniques include retrieval diversification (penalizing frequently retrieved entries) and training-count-aware advantage reweighting (cooldown windows and count-based decay) to prevent overfitting of the extractor. Key Results: On single-task evaluations across MiniHack Room, WebShop, ALFWorld, and SWE-Bench, Complementary RL consistently outperforms experience-free agentic RL baselines: ~1.3× performance margin on MiniHack and ALFWorld, +3.0% on SWE-Bench. Action efficiency also improves substantially—1.5× fewer actions on MiniHack Room and 2× fewer on ALFWorld—demonstrating that distilled experience guides more purposeful decision-making. In multi-task training (3 tasks jointly), Complementary RL achieves average reward 0.82 vs. 0.75 for baseline (+7% with experience at test time, +2% without), while Static Online Exp. fails to reach baseline performance (0.59 avg), confirming that extractor co-evolution is essential rather than merely online collection. A stronger extractor (Qwen3-30B-A3B vs. Qwen3-4B) yields an additional +5% average gain. Task scaling experiments show consistent improvement at both 3-task (+6.6%) and 6-task (+8.1%) mixtures. The asynchronous framework introduces negligible rollout latency (<1 second average search time across all batch sizes tested up to 1024 parallel environments). Strengths: - The core insight—that experience and actor must co-evolve or the experience becomes stale—is well-motivated by a clean ablation (offline vs. static-online vs. co-evolved) and the distributional misalignment argument is crisp and empirically validated. - The subgroup-split advantage estimation (J_GRPO^split) is a technically sound contribution that prevents both over-reliance on experience at inference time and reward-scale bias from mixing conditioned and unconditioned rollouts. - The asynchronous dual-loop infrastructure design is practically important and the paper shows it introduces essentially zero added latency, making Complementary RL a viable drop-in replacement for standard agentic RL training. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluations use relatively small actor models (Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-4B), and it remains unclear whether the co-evolutionary benefit persists or diminishes as actor capacity scales to frontier-scale models where self-reflection is already reliable. - The self-distillation integration collapses during later training stages, and the failure is attributed to hyperparameter sensitivity without a resolved fix, leaving the most natural path to internalizing experience gains underexplored. - The reward signal for the extractor (binary task success of guided trajectories) is noisy and delayed—a single distilled experience may guide many diverse episodes, making the credit assignment from episode outcome to extractor action inherently ambiguous, an issue acknowledged but not fully resolved.
Recommended 2603.17621 PDF
#97 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Beyond Translation: A Decomposed Collaborative Reasoning Framework Harnessing LLMs and Symbolic Solvers

openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The empirical demonstration that FOL translation accuracy degrades sharply with premise count is a useful diagnostic insight, and building a recursive decomposition-verification loop around it is a sensible architectural response.
