However, the framework remains squarely within the established neuro-symbolic paradigm — replacing single-shot translation with iterative decomposed translation — and the evaluation lacks statistical rigor (no variance reporting, single-instance benchmarks), placing this as a solid but largely incremental advance over prior work like CLOVER and Logic-LM.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic frameworks for logical reasoning treat LLMs primarily as text-to-symbol translators, delegating all inference to symbolic solvers (e.g., Z3, Prover9). This underutilizes LLMs' strengths in contextual exploration and problem structuring, while also producing suboptimal translations because the LLM must map complex multi-premise problems to formal logic without understanding each premise's role in the deduction chain. Pure LLM methods (e.g., SymbCoT) avoid this but suffer from hallucinations in complex multi-step reasoning. The paper targets this gap: how to integrate LLMs more deeply into the deduction process while still using symbolic solvers for rigorous verification. Methodology: The proposed Decomposed Collaborative Reasoning Framework (DCRF) replaces single-shot translation with a recursive decomposition-verification loop. The pipeline has three interacting components: (1) A deduction LLM (F) generates a chain-of-thought solution, from which an extractor LLM (G) parses it into a structured deduction graph of substeps S_i = (conclusion, proofs). (2) A translation LLM (H) converts each atomic substep into First-Order Logic (FOL), and the Z3 SMT solver verifies each substep as True/False/Uncertain. If a substep is Uncertain (i.e., requires a reasoning leap the solver cannot verify), it is recursively disassembled further until all steps are atomic and verifiable. Only verified True subconclusions are used as premises for subsequent steps, blocking error propagation. (3) Solver feedback (correct/incorrect substeps) is passed back to the LLM as structured guidance for iterative refinement over a maximum of T trials. The key empirical motivation is shown via a controlled experiment: translation accuracy drops sharply as the number of premises grows (from ~0.7 at 2 premises to ~0.1 at 10 premises for GPT-4), confirming that decomposition is the key bottleneck. Key Results: Evaluated on five benchmarks (FOLIO, AR-LSAT, Zebralogic, Puzzle, PrOntoQA) across three backbone LLMs (GPT-4, GPT-3.5-turbo, DeepSeek-V3). On GPT-4, DCRF achieves 0.804 on FOLIO, 0.632 on AR-LSAT, 0.735 on Zebralogic, 0.820 on Puzzle, and 0.965 on PrOntoQA, versus the prior best (CLOVER) at 0.784/0.641/0.715/0.825/0.925. DCRF achieves a 32.7% average accuracy improvement over standard prompting and a 3.1% gain over CLOVER, which was the strongest baseline. Crucially, on the two hardest benchmarks (AR-LSAT and Zebralogic), where baselines collapse to ~40% (near random), DCRF reaches 63.1% and 69.8% respectively. DCRF also reduces inference time versus CLOVER: 76.2s vs. 99.2s per question. Ablation confirms that decomposition depth is the primary driver (+12.4% accuracy from depth 0 to 1 on GPT-4), with outlining/iteration providing complementary but smaller gains (+2.6–5.3%). Results are consistent across GPT-3.5 and DeepSeek backbones, with 15–21% gains over Logic-LM across all models. Strengths: - The core insight — that translation quality degrades with premise count and that recursive decomposition into atomic steps directly addresses this — is empirically validated and mechanistically sound, not merely post-hoc. - The framework is model-agnostic and demonstrates consistent gains across three heterogeneous LLM backends (GPT-4, GPT-3.5, DeepSeek), indicating the improvements reflect architectural choices rather than model-specific tuning. - The ablation study cleanly disentangles the contributions of decomposition depth and outline/iteration, and reveals a positive interaction effect between them, lending credibility to both design decisions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation uses only accuracy as a metric and does not report statistical significance or variance across runs, which is important given the stochastic nature of LLM outputs and the small dataset sizes (e.g., PrOntoQA uses a single instance — this is a glaring omission that makes results on that benchmark essentially anecdotal). - The framework involves multiple sequential LLM calls per problem (deduction, extraction, translation, feedback) plus recursive decomposition, yielding 76.2s average per question. While faster than CLOVER, this latency is prohibitive for real-time applications, and the paper does not analyze how cost scales with problem complexity or decomposition depth. - The scope is limited to First-Order Logic problems with a Z3 backend. It is unclear how the framework would handle domains requiring other formalisms (e.g., temporal logic, probabilistic reasoning) or how it degrades when the LLM's decomposition produces sub-problems that are still not atomically verifiable after the maximum depth.
Citations
#98 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Joint Prediction of Adjuvant Therapy Response and Time-to-Response for Cancer Patients Using the Personalized-DrugRank Method

medrxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Personalized-DrugRank offers a meaningful contribution by jointly predicting therapy response and time-to-response in an unsupervised framework that functions on extremely small cohorts, which is a practical advantage over supervised alternatives. However, it is largely an extension of the authors' prior DrugMerge framework with added Cox regression and pathway-overlap scoring, and the individual components (enrichment analysis, co-expression networks, Cox models) are standard tools assembled in a pipeline rather than a novel methodology.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Predicting how individual cancer patients will respond to a specific pharmacological regimen — and how quickly — remains a central unsolved problem in personalized oncology. Most existing computational methods either predict categorical therapy outcome (responder vs. non-responder) using supervised ML trained on large cohorts, or stratify populations rather than making patient-level predictions. No widely-adopted method jointly predicts both the binary therapy response and the continuous time-to-response (i.e., time-to-event). The paper also addresses the fundamental data integration challenge: patient tumor transcriptomic profiles and drug perturbation data from cell line assays are heterogeneous, independently derived data sources, and merging them in a principled, unbiased manner is non-trivial. Existing approaches relying on known drug mechanisms of action (MOA) can introduce biases, while purely supervised deep learning methods require large training cohorts unavailable in homogeneous treatment subgroups. Methodology: The authors propose Personalized-DrugRank (P-DR), a two-phase pipeline that extends their prior DrugMerge drug repositioning framework. In Phase 1, patient-specific differentially expressed genes (DEGs) are computed by comparing each patient's tumor RNA-seq data against a reference normal tissue profile from TCGA. These DEGs are mapped onto a pre-computed modular gene co-expression network (built via the Core&Peel community detection algorithm), identifying a patient-specific disease active subnetwork. Drug perturbation signatures from GEO cell line assays are then cross-referenced against these modules using the enrichR enrichment tool. Eight distinct scoring functions (including -log10 p-value, combined score, deltadeg, odds ratio log, and their products) generate eight candidate drug rankings per patient. The ranking with the highest Reciprocal Hit-Rank (RHR) score — where RHR sums 1/position for each known disease drug found in the ranking — is selected, yielding four patient-specific indices: RHR score, global rank, local rank, and a binary DrugMerge prediction. For therapy response prediction, a parallel pipeline computes six additional derived indices capturing the overlap between the drug's effect and the patient's top-20 relevant biological processes (from GO-BP or Reactome), using inner products over shared modules. Response prediction uses unsupervised thresholding (no supervised ML) on these indices to assign high-risk vs. low-risk labels. For time-to-event prediction, Phase 2 feeds the P-DR indices together with standard clinical variables (TNM staging, disease grade, molecular subtype) into Cox Proportional Hazard regression models, optimized across 128+ feature configurations and multiple spline/piecewise/breslow parameterizations via repeated stratified k-fold cross-validation. Key Results: The method was validated on TCGA data across three cancer types (breast — BRCA, stomach — STAD, colorectal — CRC), 10 pharmacological regimens, and 13 homogeneous treatment cohorts with 7–32 patients each. For therapy response prediction, P-DR achieves average AUC 0.749 (range: 0.625–0.875), average accuracy 0.809, average PPV 0.921, and average NPV 0.769, with statistically significant AUC in all but one cohort (average p-value 0.030). The best single cohort (CRC-Irinotecan using Reactome) reaches AUC 0.875 and accuracy 0.95. For time-to-event prediction, the mixed clinical+P-DR Cox models achieve average concordance index 0.782 (geometric mean; max 0.904, min 0.651) versus 0.678 for clinical-only models, with improvement in 9 of 13 cohorts and average log-likelihood p-value 0.004. Within BRCA, the best subpopulation (Doxorubicin) achieves CI = 0.904; for STAD, the method improves CI from 0.666 (clinical-only) to 0.819. Molecular subtype features improve STAD predictions but are not critical for BRCA or unavailable for CRC. Strengths: - Jointly predicts both response category and time-to-response in a unified framework, a capability absent from most existing personalized drug response methods. - Operates without supervised learning in the response-prediction phase: mechanistic indices derived entirely from first principles allow statistically significant predictions even in cohorts of 7–32 patients, far below what supervised ML typically requires. - The approach is MOA-agnostic, integrating global transcriptional perturbation effects that implicitly capture both on-target and off-target drug activity, reducing bias from incomplete or disputed MOA knowledge. - P-DR indices demonstrate independent predictive value on top of standard clinical parameters (significant improvement in 9/13 cohorts), confirming they capture biologically meaningful signal not encoded in TNM staging or grade alone. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Cohorts are extremely small (7–32 patients per treatment-homogeneous group), which limits statistical power, raises overfitting risk despite the unsupervised response prediction design, and makes the survival analysis results difficult to generalize without independent external validation on held-out datasets. - The method fails to extend to longer-term endpoints: initial tests with progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) produced unsatisfactory results, likely because short-duration cell line perturbation assays (≤72h) miss longer-term tumor dynamics and do not capture tumor microenvironment effects. - Drug toxicity is not modeled — the indices reflect predicted efficacy against tumor cells but provide no information about patient-level tolerability, a critical factor in clinical decision-making. - Patient-level DEG computation uses a population mean normal reference profile rather than matched individual normal tissue, which is an approximation that may introduce systematic confounds for patients whose normal tissue expression diverges from the population mean.
Search Results
#99 — Moderately Innovative (58)

EmbTracker: Traceable Black-box Watermarking for Federated Language Models

Haodong Zhao, Jinming Hu, Yijie Bai, Tian Dong, Wei Du, Zhuosheng Zhang, Yanjiao Chen, Haojin Zhu, Gongshen Liu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 58 for a well-engineered solution combining black-box verifiability with client-level traceability in federated LM watermarking. The embedding-layer carrier is a clever technical choice naturally compatible with PEFT, and the evaluation is impressively thorough across 11 datasets, multiple architectures, and attack types. However, the core technique builds on established embedding-poisoning ideas, the trusted-server assumption is standard, and the threat model excludes colluding adversaries.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Federated Language Model (FedLM) training enables collaborative fine-tuning of large language models without sharing raw data, but introduces a critical IP vulnerability: any participating client can obtain the global model snapshot during training and redistribute it without authorization. Existing watermarking schemes for federated learning fail to address this threat adequately — they either embed a single shared watermark that proves only group-level ownership (not which client leaked), require white-box access to model parameters for verification, or demand client-side cooperation to embed watermarks (which adversarial clients can refuse). Furthermore, existing schemes are largely designed for vision models and are incompatible with PEFT methods like LoRA and prefix tuning that dominate FedLM deployments. The core gap is the absence of a server-side, black-box verifiable, client-traceable watermarking framework for federated language models. Methodology: EmbTracker addresses both challenges — black-box verifiability and client-level traceability — through an embedding-poisoning framework that operates entirely on the server side. The key insight is that word embedding matrices in LLMs provide a high-capacity, low-interference carrier: modifying individual token embeddings affects only sentences containing those tokens, leaving unrelated vocabulary untouched, and the embedding layer is typically frozen during PEFT-based client local training, making injected signals durable. The pipeline has three phases. First, trigger generation: each client $c_k$ generates a digital signature using RSA public-key cryptography; the signature is hashed (SHA256) to obtain a token index, which maps to a client-specific trigger word $Tr_k$ from the model vocabulary. Second, watermark injection: the server trains a universal watermark embedding $W_w$ for a universal trigger $Tr_u$ using embedding poisoning on an auxiliary dataset — only the embedding vector of $Tr_u$ is updated, a one-time cost. Before distributing the global model to each client, the server replaces $Tr_k$'s embedding with $W_w$, linking $Tr_k$ to the same backdoor behavior as $Tr_u$. This substitution is performed per-client with no additional training per client and no client-side modifications. Third, watermark verification: when a suspicious model is found, a Certification Authority queries it with each client's trigger set; a client is identified as the leaker if their trigger achieves verification rate (VR) ≥ γ while all other clients' triggers yield VR < γ. Watermark enhancement training is run server-side after each aggregation round to reinforce the signal. The scheme extends naturally to Vision-Language Models by targeting the LLM component's word embedding layer. Key Results: Evaluated across binary classification (SST-2, Enron, Twitter), multi-class classification (AGNews, DBpedia, Yahoo), question answering (FreebaseQA, CoQA, NQ), and visual QA (OK-VQA, OCR-VQA) tasks under both IID and Non-IID settings with BERT and Llama-2-7B: - EmbTracker achieves VR near 100% on almost all tasks and datasets under both IID and Non-IID conditions, meeting the ≥90% satisfactory threshold. The closest traceable black-box baseline, TraMark, fails or is inapplicable on NLP tasks. - Task accuracy (ACC) drops are typically within 1–2% relative to unwatermarked FedAvg, with some datasets showing slight ACC improvements due to regularization effects from watermark training. - Robustness to fine-tuning attacks: VR remains above or near 90% after adversarial fine-tuning on held-out data across most configurations. - Robustness to pruning: VR stays high with up to 30% parameter pruning; beyond this the model degrades to the point of being functionally invalid regardless. - Robustness to quantization: VR remains above 95% under INT8 and FP16 quantization even when ACC has degraded. - Robustness to overwriting attacks: original watermark VR stays at 98.43% after an adversarial client embeds a competing watermark, with timestamp-based mitigation resolving ambiguity. - Generalization across PEFT methods (LoRA, Prefix Tuning), FL algorithms (FedAvg, FedAvgM, FedProx, SCAFFOLD), and model architectures (Llama-3.2-3B, Vicuna-7B, Mistral-7B) consistently exceeds 95% VR. - Only 5 watermark training samples suffice to achieve >90% VR; a 5% poison ratio achieves 96.43% VR on NQ. - Time overhead is marginal: EmbTracker's per-round cost is a lightweight embedding replacement, substantially lower than TraMark (which retrains per-client models each round) and competitive with or better than WAFFLE and FedTracker. Strengths: - The embedding-space carrier is a genuinely clever technical choice: it is naturally compatible with PEFT (embedding layers are frozen on clients), provides high capacity for distinct per-client signals in large vocabularies (30,533 tokens for BERT; 32,000 for Llama-2-7B), and modifying a single token embedding has negligible parameter footprint (~6×10⁻⁷ of total model parameters for Llama-2-7B). - The scheme achieves both black-box verifiability and client-level traceability simultaneously from the server side, without any client-side cooperation — a combination no prior FL watermarking method achieved for LMs. - Thorough empirical evaluation spans 11 datasets, 3 base models, 4 FL algorithms, 2 PEFT methods, and 4 attack types, providing strong evidence of practical deployability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The trigger words are derived deterministically from client signatures via a hash-to-vocabulary-index mapping, which could allow a sophisticated adversary who reverse-engineers the scheme to predict their own trigger and selectively remove or corrupt only that embedding vector before leaking the model — a targeted attack not evaluated. - The scheme assumes the server is fully trusted and that clients faithfully follow the FL protocol (they are only modeled as passive leakers, not active watermark-removal adversaries during local training beyond fine-tuning and pruning). The threat model excludes colluding clients who pool their models to average out individual watermarks, a realistic concern in practice. - Verification confidence degrades somewhat as client count grows to 50 (Fig. 6 shows VR declining on NQ from ~97% at 10 clients to ~90% at 50), suggesting scalability limits in large federations that are acknowledged but not fully resolved.
Recommended 2603.12089 PDF
#100 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Spattack: Subgroup Poisoning Attacks on Federated Recommender Systems

shichuan.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Spattack introduces a practically motivated and previously unstudied threat model — subgroup-targeted poisoning in federated recommender systems — which is a meaningful contribution. The multi-component attack design is thorough and well-ablated. However, the methodology builds incrementally on established poisoning attack frameworks (approximate-and-promote), and the evaluation is limited to NCF on small-to-medium datasets, leaving generalization unclear.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Federated recommender systems (FedRec) allow personalized recommendations without centralizing user data, but are vulnerable to poisoning attacks where malicious clients inject crafted gradients to promote target items. Existing attacks promote items to all users indiscriminately (full-group attacks), which is both suboptimal for adversaries with demographic targeting goals and more detectable. The paper identifies a practically important but unstudied threat model: subgroup poisoning, where an attacker wants to promote items only to a specific user subgroup (e.g., health supplements to older users) while leaving the non-target group unaffected. Existing full-group attacks cannot achieve this — as the malicious client ratio increases, both target and non-target attack performance grow together, precluding a favorable trade-off. Methodology: Spattack follows an approximate-and-promote paradigm executed entirely on malicious clients using only public model parameters (no access to benign user data). In the approximation phase, each malicious client synthesizes separate user embeddings for the target and non-target groups: target embeddings are trained with the attacker's chosen "interested items" (semantically related to the target demographic) as positives and target items as negatives (hard negative simulation); non-target embeddings use a randomly sampled disjoint item set. Two approximation enhancements are added: (1) contrastive inter-group repulsion via a margin-based L2 distance loss to explicitly push the two synthesized embeddings apart, and (2) clustering-based relevant item construction that expands the interested item set by selecting the top-k most cosine-similar items (computed efficiently via average embedding, O(|V|) complexity) to improve group discriminability. In the promotion phase, target item embeddings are pulled toward the target group and pushed away from the non-target group. Two promotion enhancements address conflicting gradient dynamics: (1) target–relevant item embedding alignment via cosine similarity loss, which indirectly guides target items toward the target user region; and (2) adaptive coefficient tuning that reweights the target vs. non-target promotion losses dynamically based on the current normalized rank of target items within each approximated group, scaled by a quadratic warmup factor to discount unreliable early-round rankings. Only item embedding gradients and model parameter gradients are uploaded to the server, keeping the attack within standard FedRec protocol. Key Results: Experiments are conducted on ML-100K, ML-1M, and Steam across 7 attack baselines (PipAttack, FedRecAttack, A-hum, PSMU, PoisonFRS, PIECKUEA, PIECKIPE) and 7 defense methods, with malicious ratios as low as 0.1% (ML-1M) and 0.2% (smaller datasets). Spattack achieves 100% ER@5 on the target group across all three datasets while holding non-target ER@5 to 6.31%, 3.82%, and 6.28% respectively — substantially lower than competing attacks that achieve target ER@5 only by also heavily impacting non-target users (e.g., A-hum achieves 100% target but 9.59–100% non-target). On the combined 0.5-GER@5 metric, Spattack scores 96.85%, 98.09%, and 96.86% vs. next-best baselines at 93.05%, 84.02%, and 95.21%. Spattack also preserves recommendation quality (HR@10, NDCG@10 comparable to or better than no-attack baseline on ML-1M). Against defenses, Spattack remains effective against NORMBOUND, KRUM, MULTIKRUM, and BULYAN; MEDIAN is the only defense that substantially weakens it, but MEDIAN degrades recommendation quality significantly making it impractical. Training overhead is minimal: 1.1s/round vs. 1.0s baseline on ML-100K. Ablation confirms all four enhancement modules contribute meaningfully — removing all enhancements collapses attack performance to zero. Strengths: - Identifies a novel and practically realistic threat model (subgroup poisoning) that prior work ignores, with clear real-world motivation and formal problem definition including a new evaluation metric (γ-GER@K). - The multi-component design (contrastive repulsion + clustering expansion + embedding alignment + adaptive weighting) is well-motivated and each component is validated through thorough ablation to contribute distinctly. - Operates under strict black-box FedRec constraints (only public parameters visible), achieves effectiveness at extremely low malicious ratios (0.1%), and introduces negligible computational overhead compared to vanilla FedRec. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to NCF (MLP-based collaborative filtering) with a brief GNN extension; scalability and effectiveness on transformer-based or LLM-augmented recommenders is untested. - The target user group definition requires all group members to have interacted with every interested item, which can yield very small target groups (as few as 2 users at 15 interested items on ML-100K) — raising questions about statistical meaningfulness and generalization to finer-grained subgroup definitions. - MEDIAN aggregation, despite being impractical due to recommendation quality degradation, can break the attack at low malicious ratios; the paper does not propose a defense-robust variant or analyze how the attack scales to defeat it, leaving an open vulnerability.
Search Results
#101 — Moderately Innovative (58)

When AI Navigates the Fog of War (21 ▲ )

Ming Li, Xirui Li, Tianyi Zhou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The leakage-resistant evaluation design using a genuinely post-cutoff geopolitical crisis is methodologically clever and addresses a real confound in LLM forecasting benchmarks. However, the single-run protocol, absence of human expert baselines, and subjective qualitative analysis limit the rigor, making this more of a well-executed case study than a generalizable evaluation framework.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Evaluating LLM geopolitical reasoning is fundamentally confounded by training-data leakage: major historical events are extensively documented in pretraining corpora, meaning models may recall outcomes rather than reason from evidence. Existing forecasting benchmarks (MIRAI, ForecastBench, EvolveCast) either use retrospective historical events or rely on the flawed strategy of prompting models to "pretend not to know" pre-cutoff outcomes — a technique shown to fail as a reliable proxy for genuine ignorance. This paper asks a harder question: can LLMs reason coherently about a crisis as it unfolds, armed only with partial, noisy information available at each moment? Methodology: The authors use the early stages of the 2026 Middle East conflict — which unfolded entirely after the training cutoff of all evaluated frontier models — as a temporally grounded case study. They construct 11 critical temporal nodes (T0–T10, spanning Feb 27 – Mar 6, 2026) representing key strategic inflection points: initial strikes, retaliatory actions, nuclear facility damage, leadership succession, economic shockwaves (Qatar halting LNG production, Strait of Hormuz threats), and political signaling. For each node, a context corpus is built from 12 international news outlets (Reuters, Al Jazeera, BBC, Bloomberg, Financial Times, etc.), growing from 158 articles at T0 to 1,787 at T10 (≈889K words total), capped at 480K characters (~120K tokens). No editorial curation is applied — redundancy, conflicting reports, and noise are preserved to simulate a real-world forecasting environment. Five frontier models are evaluated: claude-sonnet-4.6, gpt-5.4, gemini-3.1-flash, qwen3.5-35b-a3b, and kimi-k2.5, each queried at temperature 0.3 with identical context. Two question types are used: 42 node-specific verifiable questions (anchored to outcomes observable within one week) and 5 general exploratory questions asked at every node to track narrative evolution. Calibration is measured as 1 − MAE between model probability estimates (manually extracted by two researchers) and binary ground truth outcomes. Key Results: The cross-model average calibration score is 0.72, indicating that models produce probabilistic estimates broadly aligned with plausible event trajectories despite operating under strict temporal constraints. Domain variation is more informative than model variation: Theme III (Economic Shockwaves/Macroeconomic Contagion) achieves the highest average of 0.79, reflecting that causal chains from military disruption to energy market effects are structurally legible. Theme II (Threshold Crossings & Internationalization) and Theme IV (Political Signaling & Regime Dynamics) both average 0.67, indicating that alliance entanglement, escalation thresholds, and leadership dynamics — defined by strategic ambiguity and multi-actor interactions — pose a more fundamental challenge. Cross-model scores range narrowly from 0.63 (gpt-5.4) to 0.75 (gemini-3.1-flash and qwen3.5-35b-a3b), with stylistic differences (assertive vs. conservative probability estimates) confounding direct comparison. Qualitatively, models exhibit three consistent patterns: (1) structural strategic reasoning — attending to sunk costs, deterrence pressures, and material constraints rather than surface political rhetoric; (2) domain-uneven reliability — more consistent on economic causal chains than on multi-actor political ambiguity; and (3) narrative evolution — shifting from early expectations of rapid containment (coerced negotiation within 2–8 weeks) toward systemic accounts of attritional de-escalation and frozen conflict as the crisis deepens. Strengths: - The leakage-resistant experimental design is the paper's most significant methodological contribution: anchoring evaluation to a genuinely post-cutoff event eliminates both verbatim and paraphrased-or-cross-lingual contamination, making this among the most credible evaluations of LLM reasoning under real-world uncertainty currently feasible. - The longitudinal framework — tracking model narratives across 11 sequential temporal nodes — is a substantive advance over static snapshot evaluations; it enables observation of belief revision, narrative coherence, and how models update analyses as new information arrives. - Human-informed node selection (interviews with individuals with lived experience of the conflict) improves the ecological validity of the timeline and grounds the reasoning probes in real-world salience, not just formal geopolitical milestones. - The qualitative analysis is detailed and specific, with representative model response snippets illustrating concrete inferential patterns ("credibility trap" reasoning, disentangling rhetoric from doctrine, institutional realism on NATO) rather than vague capability claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The single-run protocol (each question posed exactly once per model) provides no estimate of output variance; stylistic differences in probability expression (e.g., models using 0.95 vs. 0.70 for equivalent underlying assessments) confound calibration comparison across models, and the authors acknowledge this but do not correct for it. - Ground truth is operationalized with an arbitrary one-week observation window, and the authors explicitly note that longer windows could change binary labels for many evolving geopolitical questions — yet the quantitative results are presented as if the labels are stable anchors, which overstates their reliability for scoring purposes. - The qualitative analysis, while rich, is inherently subjective and not reproducible: the categorization of inferential trajectories and narrative shifts was performed by manual reading without an inter-rater agreement protocol for the thematic coding itself, only for probability extraction. - The study evaluates only five models and provides no baseline comparison against human expert forecasters or structured analytical frameworks (e.g., prediction markets, professional intelligence assessments), making it difficult to contextualize whether a 0.72 average calibration represents strong or weak reasoning in absolute terms.
Recommended 2603.16642 PDF
#102 — Moderately Innovative (58)

wjEy9yovBP

wjEy9yovBP
Why this rank: The paper's contribution lies in synthesizing three previously siloed areas — neuromorphic computing, runtime safety assurance, and SNN-adapted XAI — into a single end-to-end system validated on real hardware with a rigorous human study. However, the individual components rely on established techniques (LIF/Izhikevich neurons, FSM monitors, LRP attribution), the cislunar motivation is aspirational given non-radiation-hardened hardware, and the proprietary self-cited dataset and frameworks from the same single author undermine independent validation of the core claims.
Recommended
#103 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Detecting advanced persistent threats via heterogeneous graph learning from homophily and heterogeneity views

link.springer.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: APT-HERA's dual-view representation combining homophily and heterogeneity encoders addresses a genuine gap in provenance graph analysis, with the mutual information justification providing theoretical grounding. The Woodbury identity optimization for the self-expression matrix is a clever engineering contribution. However, the overall pipeline architecture (graph construction → representation learning → classifier → community detection) follows established patterns in graph-based APT detection, making this a well-executed refinement rather than a paradigm shift.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are stealthy, prolonged cyberattacks targeting enterprises and governments. Existing provenance graph-based detection methods suffer from three key weaknesses: (1) heavy reliance on predefined expert rules that fail against novel or randomized attacks (e.g., SolarWinds); (2) dependence on rich, high-quality semantic data that may be unavailable in resource-constrained environments; and (3) inability to capture complete graph structural information, since meta-path or random-walk methods only examine subsets of the graph. No prior method simultaneously exploits both homophily (similar-node connectivity patterns) and heterogeneity (cross-type node interactions) of provenance graphs. Methodology: APT-HERA is a four-stage pipeline operating on system audit logs. Stage 1 constructs a heterogeneous provenance graph (nodes = processes, files, memory, network sockets/IPs; edges = system call interactions) and partitions it into fixed-size subgraphs via BFS-based staged expansion. Stage 2 is the graph representation module with two complementary encoders: (a) a homophily encoder that uses an MLP to project node features and then applies a self-expression matrix S (solved in closed form via Woodbury identity to reduce complexity from cubic to O(Nd² + d³ + kd)) to linearly represent each node as a weighted combination of same-type neighbors; (b) a heterogeneity encoder that aggregates one-hop neighbors per edge type and averages across all edge types. The two representations are mapped to shared and private latent spaces, trained jointly with a consistency loss (maximize alignment) and a specificity loss (preserve unique information), then concatenated to form the final embedding Ẑ — proven via mutual information arguments to contain strictly more attack-relevant information than either representation alone. Stage 3 classifies nodes as benign or malicious using XGBoost on Ẑ. Stage 4 applies the Louvain community detection algorithm to provenance graph communities, maps detected malicious nodes to their communities, aggregates temporally correlated subgraphs, and reconstructs the attack execution trace. Key Results: Evaluated on four public datasets — DARPA Engagement 3 (Trace, Theia, Cadets subsets; 51.6 GB of logs), StreamSpot, Unicorn Wget, and DARPA 1998. At node-level detection, APT-HERA achieves average precision of 99.08%, recall of 97.68%, AUC >99.9%, and FPR of 0.16% across DARPA E3 and DARPA 1998. On the information-sparse DARPA 1998 dataset (only IP/port/protocol, no semantic logs), APT-HERA achieves 98% precision and 0.26% FPR, outperforming Flash (84% precision, 6.80% FPR), ThreaTrace (89%, 0.78%), and MAGIC (95%, 0.57%). Inference speed is 5–8× faster than MAGIC on E3 datasets, processing a day's worth of audit logs (~1.27 GB) in 1–2 minutes post-preprocessing. Ablation confirms that combining homophily and heterogeneity representations yields consistently higher and more stable performance than either representation alone, especially on the harder Cadets and Theia subsets where heterogeneity-only drops recall below 80%. Strengths: - The dual-view representation (homophily + heterogeneity) is theoretically grounded via mutual information inequalities and empirically validated, addressing a genuine blind spot in prior work that used only one view. - The Woodbury matrix identity transformation reduces the self-expression matrix computation from O(N³) to O(Nd² + d³ + kd), making the approach tractable on provenance graphs with millions of edges. - Strong generalization to information-scarce scenarios (DARPA 1998 network-only data) where semantic-feature-dependent methods like Flash collapse, demonstrating practical value for realistic deployments with heterogeneous data quality. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The self-expression matrix requires O(|R|·Nd) space and O(n²) pairwise feature distance computation per subgraph, resulting in peak memory exceeding 2 GB for subgraphs of 1,000 nodes — a significant constraint for edge deployment without subgraph size tuning. - The tracing module uses community detection to map malicious nodes to subgraphs but does not reconstruct causal chains between malicious entities, leaving the full attack narrative incomplete and requiring analyst effort to interpret fragmented subgraph clusters. - The model assumes log integrity (attackers cannot tamper with audit logs), an idealized assumption that breaks in sophisticated rootkit or log-poisoning scenarios, and the paper offers no concrete mitigation beyond flagging a GAE-based detection approach as future work. - Passive entities (malicious files and shared libraries) generate systematic false negatives because their interaction patterns closely resemble benign counterparts; the model relies on downstream traceability from correctly-detected active processes to recover them indirectly.
Citations
#104 — Moderately Innovative (58)

FinToolBench: Evaluating LLM Agents for Real-World Financial Tool Use (32 ▲ )

Jiaxuan Lu, Kong Wang, Yemin Wang, Qingmei Tang, Hongwei Zeng, Xiang Chen, Jiahao Pi, Shujian Deng, Lingzhi Chen, Yi Fu, Kehua Yang, Xiao Sun · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Mar 2026
Why this rank: FinToolBench addresses a genuine gap by creating an executable financial tool-use benchmark with finance-specific compliance dimensions (timeliness, intent, domain) that go beyond standard tool-calling accuracy. The separation of capability from compliance is a useful conceptual contribution. However, the modest scale (295 questions, 760 tools), reliance on unstable free-tier APIs, and very low execution success rates across all models limit its practical utility as a lasting benchmark.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents are increasingly deployed as interfaces to financial data systems, translating natural-language queries into sequences of API calls. Yet existing benchmarks fail to support rigorous evaluation of this use case. General tool benchmarks (API-Bank, StableToolBench) lack finance-domain specificity. Existing finance benchmarks (FinanceBench, FinQA, TAT-QA) rely on static QA over documents and involve virtually no executable tools. Neither class of benchmark measures three failure modes that are critical in practice: (1) timeliness violations, where an agent retrieves stale data for a query requiring real-time information; (2) intent escalation, where an agent invokes transactional tools when only informational access is authorized; and (3) domain misalignment, where an agent applies equity-market tools to a cryptocurrency query. The gap is both practical and safety-relevant: a syntactically correct but domain-mismatched tool call can produce confident-looking wrong answers with real financial consequences. Methodology: FinToolBench constructs an end-to-end executable benchmark through an eight-stage pipeline. The tool inventory is assembled from two free-tier sources — RapidAPI (diverse real-time APIs) and AkShare (stable Python financial data library) — starting from 5,470 candidates and filtering to 760 verified-executable tools via interface validity checks, deduplication, rate-limit enforcement, and runtime invocation testing. Each tool is annotated along three finance-specific dimensions using LLM-based majority voting (3 samples): update_frequency (realtime/daily/as_filed/periodic/static), intent_type (informational/advisory/transactional), and regulatory_domain (equity, bond, fund, forex, derivatives, macro, crypto, etc.). The question set (295 items: 166 single-tool, 129 multi-tool) is sourced from FinanceBench and OpenFinData, filtered to retain only queries requiring tool calls, and aligned to tools via BGE-M3 dense retrieval (Top-20) followed by Qwen3-8B majority-vote verification. Human experts spot-check annotations and tool-question alignment. Evaluation separates capability from compliance. Capability metrics are: TIR (Tool Invocation Rate), TESR (Tool Execution Success Rate), CER = TESR/TIR (conditional execution success), Soft Score (LLM-judged answer correctness averaged over 3 GPT-5.1 runs), and CSS (Soft Score conditioned on successful execution). Compliance metrics are question-level mismatch rates TMR, IMR, DMR — each flagging any trace where at least one tool call violates the corresponding finance constraint as assessed by GPT-5.1. The paper also presents FATR (Finance-Aware Tool Routing), a model-agnostic baseline that injects finance attribute tags into tool cards presented to the planner, runs a ReAct loop (max 5 steps), and adds execution stability utilities (caching, retries, output compression via LLM extraction). FATR is evaluated with four LLM planners: Doubao-Seed-1.6, Qwen3-8B, GLM-4.7-Flash, and GPT-4o. Key Results: No single model dominates across all dimensions, revealing a fundamental coverage-precision tradeoff. Qwen3-8B achieves the highest tool invocation rate (TIR=0.87) and best Soft Score (0.42) but has the lowest conditional execution success (CER=0.34), indicating frequent argument construction failures. Doubao-Seed-1.6 is the most balanced, achieving the highest TESR (0.33) and second-highest CER (0.50). GPT-4o is the most conservative (TIR=0.23, TESR=0.14) but most precise when it does invoke tools (CER=0.62, CSS=0.67) with the lowest intent and domain mismatch rates. GLM-4.7-Flash underperforms across all metrics with the highest mismatch rates (IMR=0.72). Intent mismatch rates (IMR) are uniformly high across all models (0.50–0.69), indicating that distinguishing informational from advisory/transactional intent is a persistent failure mode. Finance attribute injection in FATR improves CER (39.4%→46.3%) and reduces all three mismatch rates (TMR, IMR, DMR) at a modest cost to TIR, confirming that making constraints explicit in tool cards improves selection quality. Category-level analysis shows substantial heterogeneity: macro interpretation and fund analysis categories achieve stronger execution, while value extraction and security compliance categories see near-zero tool invocation success. Strengths: - The benchmark is genuinely executable — all 760 tools are runtime-verified under free-tier constraints, enabling real execution traces rather than mock environments. This is a significant advance over prior finance benchmarks. - The finance attribute schema (timeliness, intent, domain) is operationally grounded and decoupled from the agent under test, enabling auditable call-level compliance measurement rather than final-answer scoring alone. - The evaluation framework cleanly separates capability from compliance, exposing failure modes (e.g., high IMR even for correct answers) that aggregate metrics would mask entirely. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark scale is modest: 295 questions and 760 tools. Compared to general tool benchmarks (ToolLLM covers 16,000+ APIs), coverage of the financial tool space is narrow, and the question set may be too small to yield statistically robust per-category conclusions (several categories have fewer than 10 questions). - End-to-end execution success rates are very low across all models (TESR peaks at 0.33), partly due to real API instability (timeouts, rate limits, endpoint drift). This conflates agent capability failures with infrastructure failures, and the benchmark's reliability will degrade as free-tier APIs change over time — a sustainability problem the paper acknowledges but does not resolve. - The compliance metrics (TMR, IMR, DMR) rely on GPT-5.1 as judge for per-call alignment assessment, introducing a dependence on a proprietary model and inheriting known LLM-judge instability. The paper uses only single-run judgments for compliance (vs. 3-run averaging for Soft Score), which may be noisy for a binary mismatch label.
Recommended 2603.08262 PDF
#105 — Moderately Innovative (57)

PromptTuner: SLO-Aware Elastic System for LLM Prompt Tuning

Wei Gao, Peng Sun, Dmitrii Ustiugov, Tianwei Zhang, Yonggang Wen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: PromptTuner addresses a real but narrow problem in prompt-tuning-as-a-service with solid engineering: the two-layer Prompt Bank achieving 40x lookup reduction and the warm/cold GPU pool scheduling are well-designed. The characterization study clearly motivates why existing training and inference systems each fail for LPT workloads. However, the core techniques—K-medoid clustering for nearest-neighbor lookup, SLO-deadline-ordered scheduling with elastic pools—are competent combinations of known building blocks rather than fundamentally new ideas.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM Prompt Tuning-as-a-Service (LPT) has emerged as a widely adopted commercial offering, handling tens of thousands of daily requests that require GPU clusters to optimize soft prompts for downstream tasks. The core challenge is satisfying users' Service Level Objectives (SLOs)—latency deadlines for job completion—while minimizing GPU resource costs for providers. Prior systems designed for DL training (e.g., ElasticFlow) or inference (e.g., INFless) are ill-suited for LPT workloads for two reasons: training systems statically provision fixed-size GPU clusters with ~56% utilization, nearly doubling costs; inference systems incur 37–41% GPU allocation overhead from per-instance initialization, and are limited to single-GPU-per-instance designs that cannot support the synchronous cross-GPU communication LPT requires. A third problem unique to LPT is the high sensitivity of convergence speed to the choice of initial prompt—a well-chosen initial prompt can reduce the required tuning iterations by 1.7–4.5×, dramatically affecting SLO attainment and cost. Methodology: PromptTuner introduces two coordinated innovations built atop Knative and deployed on physical GPU clusters. First, the Prompt Bank is a query engine that automatically identifies effective initial prompts for incoming LPT jobs via prompt reusing. It assembles thousands of public prompt candidates and organizes them using a two-layer K-medoid clustering data structure indexed by LLM activation features. Lookup finds the best-matching cluster representative in the first layer, then scores candidates in that cluster using a metric score(p) = average loss on a small evaluation set (16 samples) without any tuning, reducing metric computations from C to K + C/K (optimally 2√C). The two-layer structure reduces lookup overhead by up to 40× while preserving near-ideal ITA (Iterations-to-Accuracy) performance. Second, the Workload Scheduler implements runtime reusing by maintaining per-LLM warm GPU pools with pre-loaded CUDA/DL runtimes and model weights, plus a shared cold GPU pool for elastic scaling. Two algorithms govern allocation: (1) a warm-pool allocator that progressively assigns GPUs to jobs in SLO-deadline order, and (2) a cold-pool allocator that adds/removes GPUs from warm pools using a DelaySchedulable function that delays execution of deferrable jobs to maximize warm-GPU reuse. A latency budget caps Prompt Bank overhead at 20% of each job's SLO. The system is implemented in ~2000 lines of Python (scheduler) and ~1000 lines (Prompt Bank), deployed on Knative with Memcached for cross-GPU communication. Key Results: Evaluated on a 32-node cluster of NVIDIA A100-80GB GPUs with GPT2-Base, GPT2-Large, and Vicuna-7B across 12 downstream tasks, PromptTuner reduces SLO violations by 4.0× vs. INFless and 7.9× vs. ElasticFlow, while lowering resource costs by 1.6× vs. INFless and 4.5× vs. ElasticFlow. Under varying SLO emergence (S=0.5 to 1.5), cost savings over INFless reach 38%, 23%, and 17%, and savings over ElasticFlow reach up to 70%. For heavy workloads with LLaMA-30B, PromptTuner reduces SLO violations by 1.36–2.90× and costs by 1.20–1.79× over baselines. For Qwen7B-R1 (32,768-token sequences, 4-GPU tensor parallelism), SLO violations drop by 1.56–3.24× and costs by 1.39–2.28×. The Prompt Bank achieves 90% of ideal-candidate ITA performance and outperforms induction initialization by 1.81×, 1.38×, and 1.28× ITA speedup for GPT2-Base, GPT2-Large, and Vicuna-7B, respectively. Prompt reusing alone reduces SLO violations by 13–23% and costs by 30–40%; runtime reusing contributes additional SLO reduction. Scheduling overhead is 13–67 ms, negligible relative to minute-level SLOs. Strengths: - The two-layer Prompt Bank data structure is a principled engineering contribution: it achieves near-ideal prompt selection quality with a 40× reduction in evaluation overhead and sub-10-second lookup latency, which is critical for meeting short SLOs. - The characterization study (§2–3) is thorough and well-motivated, quantitatively exposing why existing training and inference systems each fail LPT workloads along distinct axes (static provisioning vs. initialization overhead vs. prompt sensitivity). - Evaluation is comprehensive: physical cluster deployment (not simulation), multiple LLMs including large models (LLaMA-30B, Qwen7B-R1), ablations of all key components, and scalability tests up to 96 GPUs strengthen the validity of claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Prompt Bank relies on a static offline corpus of public prompts; its effectiveness degrades for novel or highly domain-specific LPT tasks where no similar public prompts exist, a scenario not stress-tested in evaluation. - The system assumes SLOs are specified as a fixed multiple of minimum GPU-time, which may not capture real user intent (e.g., hard wall-clock deadlines independent of GPU allocation); the sensitivity of results to this SLO formulation is not thoroughly analyzed. - Evaluation uses only gradient-based soft prompt tuning; compatibility with other prompt optimization paradigms (reinforcement learning, zero-order methods) is asserted but not demonstrated, limiting claims of generality.
Recommended 2603.05087 PDF
#106 — Moderately Innovative (57)

ReFORM: Review-aggregated Profile Generation via LLM with Multi-Factor Attention for Restaurant Recommendation

Moonsoo Park, Seulbeen Je, Donghyeon Park · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: ReFORM's contribution lies in the multi-factor cross-attention mechanism that adaptively weights domain-specific review factors per user-item pair, a meaningful step beyond methods that treat all semantic signals equally. The factor ablation and noise injection diagnostics are well-designed and yield actionable insights. However, the manually defined factor taxonomy limits generalizability, and the core architectural ideas (cross-attention, LightGCN backbone, BPR loss) are individually well-established.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing LLM-enhanced recommender systems predominantly leverage LLM internal knowledge about item titles and metadata (e.g., movie genres, restaurant names) while neglecting the rich decision-making signals embedded in user reviews. Graph Convolution Network (GCN)-based collaborative filtering methods capture high-order interaction signals but are blind to textual semantics. Methods like RLMRec integrate review-derived semantic representations into graph structures but treat all semantic factors equally, potentially introducing noise by failing to differentiate which factors actually drive individual user decisions. The gap: no prior work jointly extracts structured, domain-specific factor profiles from reviews and then adaptively weights those factors per user. Methodology: ReFORM combines three components. First, Review-aggregated Profile Generation (RPG) uses GPT-4o mini to distill user and item reviews into structured factor-specific profiles covering seven restaurant-domain factors: cuisine type, flavor, atmosphere, price, time, waiting, and companion. For users, reviews are sampled randomly (N=100); for items, longer reviews are prioritized to maximize lexical coverage. Second, Multi-Factor Attention (MFA) encodes each factor's profile text separately via BERT into matrices of shape M×d, then applies cross-attention where user profiles serve as queries and values while item profiles serve as keys (and vice versa for item-centric modeling). Attention weights from n randomly sampled historical items are aggregated via max pooling (inspired by squeeze-and-excitation networks) to emphasize dominant factor preferences while suppressing noise. Third, the MFA-weighted profile embeddings are concatenated with standard LightGCN graph embeddings and trained end-to-end using BPR pairwise ranking loss. The key design choice is Q=V in the attention formulation, meaning user representations refine themselves based on relevance to item keys, isolating which factors most influence that user's decisions. Key Results: On two restaurant datasets (Yelp with 10-core filtering, Google Restaurants with 5-core), ReFORM consistently outperforms all baselines. Against its LightGCN backbone: +51.3% Recall@20 on Yelp, +16.7% on Google Restaurants. Against the strongest LLM-enhanced baseline RLMRec: +9.15% Recall@20 and +8.41% NDCG@20 on Yelp; +11.82% Recall@20 and +11.91% NDCG@20 on Google Restaurants (all improvements statistically significant, p < 0.005). Factor ablation reveals cuisine type and flavor as most critical (removing cuisine type drops R@20 by 14.34% on GR), while time and waiting contribute minimally or negatively. Noise injection experiments confirm authenticity of reviews is critical: replacing 50% of reviews with random others degrades R@20 by 11.58%; full replacement by 15.9%. Strengths: - The dual-perspective MFA (user-to-item and item-to-user cross-attention) is a principled way to capture asymmetric factor salience, and the ablation convincingly shows MFA is essential — removing it drops GR performance below even the LightGCN baseline, demonstrating that unweighted factor profiles can actively hurt. - Factor-level ablation and noise injection experiments provide genuine diagnostic insight rather than just aggregate numbers, revealing actionable knowledge about which review signals matter in the restaurant domain. - Evaluation is methodologically sound: five random seeds, paired t-tests, all-ranking strategy, two datasets of different scales and densities. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The seven restaurant-domain factors were selected manually based on prior literature rather than learned from data; the framework's generalizability to other domains requires re-engineering the factor taxonomy, and the paper only evaluates on restaurant data despite claiming broad applicability. - The approach depends on GPT-4o mini API calls for offline profile generation, adding significant preprocessing cost and an external dependency; scalability to web-scale catalogs with millions of items/users is not analyzed. - The optimal number of attention keys n differs sharply between datasets (n=3 for Yelp, n=1 for GR), suggesting sensitivity to data sparsity that requires per-deployment tuning with no principled selection criterion provided.
Search Results 2603.16236 PDF
#107 — Moderately Innovative (57)

RECOMMENDER SYSTEMS AND DEMOCRACY: LINGUISTIC DIVERSITY, EPISTEMIC AGENCY, AND CIVIC PARTICIPATION

tara.tcd.ie · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The 'cultural technologies of omission' framing is theoretically productive and the cross-jurisdictional sock puppet design is creative, but the contribution is primarily diagnostic and qualitative. The work synthesizes existing methods (TTR, VADER sentiment, CDA) rather than introducing new ones, and stops short of prescriptive technical interventions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Recommender systems (RS) on major video-hosting platforms curate the vast majority of content citizens encounter online — 70% of watched content on YouTube is algorithmically recommended — yet their effects on linguistic diversity, epistemic agency, and democratic participation remain underexamined. Prior work has focused on overt political radicalization (e.g., the "YouTube rabbit hole"), but subtler shifts in vocabulary, framing, and sentiment may reshape civic discourse without being visible to users or regulators. This thesis addresses three interlinked gaps: how RS reshape the language and perspectives users encounter on platforms; how policymakers in contrasting political systems conceptualize and regulate RS; and how academic research itself frames RS in ways that marginalize social and linguistic concerns. Methodology: The thesis employs a three-strand qualitative and comparative design grounded in Frank Webster's four-component theory of the information society (technology, economy, culture, quality). Strand 1 uses 60 demographically differentiated sock puppet (SP) accounts — new accounts with no interaction history to exploit cold-start conditions — divided across four political orientations (US Democratic/Republican, Russia anti-war/pro-war) on YouTube and Dzen. Each SP followed recommendation chains (top-10 next videos) every three days from December 2023 to March 2024, yielding 17,964+ videos. Recommendation outputs were analyzed via Type-Token Ratio (TTR), vocabulary size, lexical density, and VADER sentiment analysis, stratified by gender and five age groups (18–64). Strand 2 applies critical discourse analysis and corpus linguistics to AI policy documents from the EU, US, and Russia, using keyword frequency, co-occurrence analysis, and normalized per-million growth rates to compare how terms like "recommendation," "bias," "transparency," and "market" are deployed. Strand 3 analyzes a corpus of RS research publications (2010–2024) across four thematic frames: governance/accountability, agency/autonomy, technical instrumentalism, and risk/mitigation, mapping how social and linguistic concerns are treated relative to technical solutions. Key Results: The case studies show that RS narrow lexical diversity by amplifying formulaic, repetitive discourse: both US partisan camps and Russian pro-/anti-war accounts exhibit low TTR despite large raw vocabularies, indicating high repetition of dominant frames rather than exposure to varied perspectives. Sentiment analysis reveals demographically differentiated affective environments — younger users are consistently exposed to more negatively valenced content, suggesting age-targeted engagement optimization. The Russian case demonstrates that RS operate through similar omission patterns regardless of political system: Dzen amplifies pro-war material while YouTube amplifies anti-war content, yet both platforms narrow the linguistic range of political expression in structurally equivalent ways. Policy analysis reveals three divergent governance paradigms: the EU foregrounds rights and risk (AI Act framing); the US prioritizes market efficiency and innovation; Russia centers state control and information management. None operationalizes linguistic diversity or epistemic agency as regulatory objectives. The research discourse analysis finds that technical instrumentalism dominates the literature — loss functions, accuracy metrics, and algorithmic efficiency are central, while social, linguistic, and democratic dimensions are treated as secondary or externally imposed constraints. The thesis introduces the concept of RS as "cultural technologies of omission," situating them historically alongside other filtering technologies (indexes, search engines, editorial gatekeepers) that exercise power through what they exclude rather than what they promote. Strengths: - Innovative multi-strand design that bridges computational linguistics, policy analysis, and science and technology studies, connecting platform behavior to regulatory discourse to academic framing in a unified argument - The sock puppet methodology exploits cold-start conditions to isolate algorithmic influence from user history, providing cleaner evidence of RS-driven exposure patterns than studies using organic users - The cross-jurisdictional and cross-platform comparison (democratic vs. authoritarian context, US vs. Russian platforms) surfaces a key finding: omission operates as a structural property of RS regardless of political system, undermining exceptionalist narratives about any one regime - The "cultural technologies of omission" framing is theoretically productive, situating RS within a longer history of information-filtering technologies and providing a vocabulary for regulation that goes beyond bias or misinformation Weaknesses / Limitations: - The four-month observation window captures a politically heightened period (Super Tuesday 2024, Russian elections) that may not generalize to routine recommendation behavior; longitudinal replication is needed - The sock puppet accounts follow only the top recommendation chain without interaction (watching, liking, commenting), meaning the data reflects cold-start and passive-browsing RS behavior rather than the fully personalized RS that most active users encounter - Lexical diversity metrics (TTR, vocabulary size) are corpus-size sensitive and can conflate structural repetition with dataset artifacts; while the study applies close-reading diagnostics to mitigate this, causal attribution from TTR patterns to RS design choices remains inferential - The thesis does not produce prescriptive technical interventions — it diagnoses the problem and argues for reframing governance, but concrete RS design modifications (alternative loss functions, diversity-aware evaluation metrics) are left largely to future work
Citations
#108 — Moderately Innovative (57)

MONET: Modeling and Optimization of neural NEtwork Training from Edge to Data Centers IEEE Publication

hal.science · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: MONET addresses a real gap by extending dataflow accelerator simulation from inference to training, and the finding that activation checkpointing costs are non-additive under layer fusion — invalidating standard MILP formulations — is a non-obvious theoretical insight. However, the work is validated only on small models (ResNet-18, small GPT-2) against simulated architectures with no real hardware measurements, and the layer-fusion heuristics are engineering approximations without optimality guarantees.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Hardware-software co-design for neural network accelerators has focused almost exclusively on inference workloads, leaving training — a far more resource-intensive and memory-demanding phase — largely unaddressed by existing simulation frameworks. Training imposes fundamentally different constraints: activations must be retained for backpropagation (dramatically increasing memory vs. inference), optimizer states add further overhead, and the backward pass introduces operators (ConvTranspose, gradient accumulation, transpositions) absent from inference graphs. The paper demonstrates empirically that hardware configurations Pareto-optimal for inference are not so for training (energy scales ~15-20x higher, latency ~3-10x higher for ResNet-18), motivating a dedicated training-aware modeling tool. Methodology: MONET extends Stream, an experimentally validated inference-focused framework for heterogeneous dataflow accelerators (HDAs), to support full training workloads. The core technical contribution is a set of ONNX transformation passes that automatically generate a complete training computation graph — forward pass, backward pass (decomposed from composite ONNX gradient operators like ConvGrad into fine-grained primitives), and optimizer steps (SGD, ADAM) — fully compatible with Stream's scheduler. Activation checkpointing is implemented as an additional ONNX pass that replaces selected activations with recomputation subgraphs. On top of this modeling capability, the paper introduces two optimization algorithms: (1) a constraint-based layer-fusion solver using Integer Programming over a BFS-enumerated candidate subgraph space, with memory, intra-core tiling compatibility, and operator-type constraints to prune the exponential search space; and (2) a multi-objective genetic algorithm (NSGA-II) for activation checkpointing that generates Pareto-optimal solutions trading off latency, energy, and memory — motivated by showing that the standard MILP formulation is invalid in the presence of layer fusion because recomputation costs are non-additive. Key Results: Design space exploration of ResNet-18 on Edge TPU (up to 49,152 total compute units across 6 parameters) confirms that inference-optimal and training-optimal hardware configurations are structurally distinct: larger PEs appear on the latency Pareto front for inference but not training, while their energy efficiency relationship inverts across workloads. For GPT-2 on FuseMax, the more homogeneous architecture shows less sensitivity to hardware variation. For layer fusion, the IP-based solver consistently outperforms both the baseline layer-by-layer schedule and manually designed fusion configurations on ResNet-18 inference on Edge TPU, with subgraph limit 6 achieving optimal performance. For activation checkpointing on ResNet-18 training (ADAM, batch 1, 224×224), the genetic algorithm finds solutions saving up to 13 MB of activation memory at a cost of only ~4% in latency and energy overhead within 3-4 generations of NSGA-II. Crucially, the paper demonstrates that the standard MILP model is incorrect in fused-layer settings: the joint recomputation cost of two activations is not the sum of individual costs, due to changed fusion opportunities and data locality effects. Strengths: - Fills a genuine gap: MONET is the only framework combining fine-grained layer-fusion modeling with full training workload support on heterogeneous (non-GPU) dataflow accelerators, as shown by a clear taxonomy table comparing against Timeloop, ZigZag, Dace-AD, Stream, and NVArchSim. - The modular ONNX-pass architecture is reusable beyond Stream, and the insight that activation checkpointing introduces non-linearity that invalidates MILP formulations in fused-layer settings is a non-obvious and practically important theoretical contribution. - The genetic algorithm approach for activation checkpointing is pragmatically justified and produces meaningful Pareto fronts within a small number of generations, making it tractable for real networks. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Validation is limited to two relatively small models (ResNet-18, small GPT-2) on simulated Edge TPU and FuseMax architectures; there is no experimental validation against real hardware measurements for training workloads, unlike Stream's validated inference models. Scaling to modern LLM-scale training (billions of parameters, multi-GPU) remains undemonstrated. - The layer-fusion solver's complexity constraints (≤3 convolutions, ≤2 GEMMs per subgraph) are engineering heuristics that may miss globally optimal fusion configurations, and the IP objective (minimize subgraph count) is a proxy that may not directly minimize latency or energy. The paper does not quantify how often the heuristic optimum matches the true optimum.
Search Results
#109 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Predicting Preferences for Unknown Products Toward Building Recommendation Systems Based on Collective Brain Activity

sensors.myu-group.co.jp · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The conceptual leap of treating NIRS brain activity vectors as entries in a collaborative filtering user-item matrix is genuinely novel — no prior work has bridged neural signal estimation and CF for unseen items. The 9.2% relative degradation versus actual brain measurements is a credible feasibility result. However, ~37% accuracy on a 5-class task with 20 subjects and NIRS headsets renders this a distant proof-of-concept with major scalability barriers.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Conventional collaborative filtering recommendation systems rely on consciously expressed user ratings, which may not capture subconscious preferences and emotional responses. Brain activity, measurable via near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS), can reflect subconscious mental states such as emotions and preferences. However, brain signals can only be recorded for products a user has actually viewed — there is no direct brain measurement for unknown/unviewed items. This creates a fundamental gap: how to leverage neural signals for recommendation when the target item has never been seen by the user. Prior neuromarketing and BCI research focused on individuals, leaving collective brain-based recommendation systems unexplored. Methodology: The authors propose a two-stage pipeline called BACF-SVM (Brain Activity Collaborative Filtering + SVM). In the data collection phase, 20 university students viewed images of 100 food and beverage products while wearing a 16-channel NIRS headset measuring oxyhemoglobin concentration in the prefrontal cortex; participants also provided explicit 1–5 preference ratings. Brain activity features were extracted by: (1) applying bandpass filters across six upper-cutoff frequencies (0.05–1.00 Hz, all starting at 0.01 Hz), (2) computing the integrated oxyhemoglobin signal over the 15-second viewing window per channel, and (3) optionally applying z-score normalization to reduce inter-subject variability. Two channel configurations were tested: all 16 channels and only the 4 medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) channels (7–9–10), targeting the brain's reward system. The key innovation is treating each subject's brain activity feature vector per product as an entry in a user-item matrix, then applying standard collaborative filtering to estimate brain activity features for products a given user never viewed. The estimated features are then fed into a Gaussian-kernel SVM (C and γ tuned via grid search over 10⁻⁸–10⁸ with 10-fold CV) to predict the 5-class preference rating. This is compared against: (1) a random-distribution baseline (RD), (2) standard CF on explicit ratings, and (3) BA-SVM — SVM using actual measured brain activity (the oracle upper bound). Key Results: Across all 20 participants and all 12 feature configurations (6 filter bands × 2 normalization choices), BACF-SVM achieved an average accuracy of approximately 37% compared to BA-SVM's ~40%, representing only a 3.7 percentage-point absolute drop (9.2% relative degradation). Both methods substantially outperformed random chance (~26%) and generally outperformed standard CF on ratings (~33%), except for 4 subjects (D, F, M, R) where CF was competitive. Individual participant accuracy ranged from ~28% to ~57% for BA-SVM and ~27% to ~56% for BACF-SVM. z-score normalization was beneficial specifically for BACF-SVM with mPFC channels (13 of 20 subjects improved), consistent with the need to reduce inter-subject variability when pooling brain signals. Restricting to mPFC channels improved BA-SVM performance (11 of 20 subjects benefited) but had mixed effects on BACF-SVM, likely because fewer channels reduce information available for CF estimation. Confusion matrix analysis confirmed that the system functions as a recommender: products with true user ratings of 4–5 were recommended (predicted ≥4) at markedly higher rates than low-rated products. Strengths: - Conceptually novel: the paper is the first to treat NIRS brain activity vectors as a user-item matrix amenable to collaborative filtering, creating a path to subconscious-preference-based recommendation for unseen items. - Empirically grounded comparison: the 9.2% relative accuracy loss versus actual brain measurements is an honest, quantified bound on the cost of the CF estimation step, making the feasibility claim credible. - Systematic preprocessing ablation: testing 6 bandpass filters × 2 normalization strategies provides useful engineering guidance (cutoff ≤0.5 Hz, z-score for collective settings) for future NIRS-based systems. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Extremely small and homogeneous sample: 20 university students aged ~21 is insufficient to establish generalizability; the paper itself acknowledges results need verification across broader age groups and populations. - Absolute accuracy is low: ~37% on a 5-class task (chance ~26%) leaves large headroom before practical deployment; the system is presented as a proof-of-concept rather than a production-ready recommender. - Unspecified collaborative filtering algorithm: the paper does not detail which CF variant (user-based, item-based, matrix factorization) was used for brain feature estimation, making reproducibility and fair comparison with rating-based CF ambiguous. - Scalability and practicality concerns: NIRS headsets require physical setup and controlled viewing conditions, making large-scale data collection for real-world recommendation scenarios highly impractical with current technology.
Search Results
#110 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Investigation of Bcl-2 3'UTR-driven Plant Stress Tolerance Through MicroRNA and RNA Interference

eprints.qut.edu.au · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Fills a genuine 20-year mechanistic gap by demonstrating cross-kingdom biogenesis of human Bcl-2 3'UTR-derived small RNAs in plants and linking them to a coherent pro-survival transcriptomic signature (ERQC/UPR/autophagy). However, the core phenomenon was already known; this thesis elucidates mechanism rather than discovering a new effect. The absence of direct causal validation (no target knockdown or CRISPR confirmation) leaves the sRNA-to-phenotype link correlational, and restriction to a model species with no agricultural value limits translational impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Abiotic stresses such as drought and salinity are accelerating threats to global food security, with climate change projected to require a 56–110% increase in food production by 2050. Genetic engineering of stress-resilience traits offers a faster route than conventional breeding. Earlier observations established that transgenic plants expressing the human Bcl-2 3' Untranslated Region (3'UTR) — a non-protein-coding sequence — exhibit enhanced tolerance to a range of abiotic and biotic stresses, yet the underlying molecular mechanism has remained entirely unknown for over two decades. Because UTRs lack coding potential, the regulatory effect must arise via RNA-based mechanisms such as miRNA or RNAi pathways. This thesis investigates whether the Bcl-2 3'UTR generates small RNAs (sRNAs) in transgenic plants and how these modulate endogenous gene expression to confer stress tolerance, filling a critical mechanistic gap in cross-kingdom non-coding RNA biology. Methodology: The first 713 bp of the human Bcl-2 3'UTR was cloned downstream of the CaMV35S constitutive promoter in the binary vector pOPT-EBX and introduced into Nicotiana benthamiana by Agrobacterium-mediated leaf disc transformation. Thirteen T0 transgenic lines were confirmed by PCR; Southern blot analysis identified four single-copy events, and two of these (lines #23 and #26) were selected for detailed study. Transgenic expression was verified by RT-PCR. Stress phenotyping used (1) a floating leaf disc assay at 150 mM NaCl with chlorophyll quantification, and (2) a whole-plant water deficit protocol in which soil VWC was held at 10% for two weeks, with measurements of leaf relative water content (RWC), CO2 assimilation, electrolyte leakage rate, and Evans Blue dye uptake. For mechanistic studies, a custom in silico miRNA prediction pipeline was developed: the UTR sequence was fragmented, putative stem-loops identified via the Vienna RNA Package (MFE algorithm), candidates aligned to miRBase v22.1, and targets predicted using psRNATarget against the N. benthamiana transcriptome. This was cross-validated by NGS (Illumina NovaSeq 6000 sRNA and mRNA libraries) from well-watered and water-deficit-stressed transgenic and non-transgenic plants. Differential gene expression (DESeq2), GO enrichment (GOEnrichment), and pathway analysis (MapMan/Mercator4) were performed using Galaxy Australia and QUT HPC. Additionally, Chapter 6 tested biotechnology applications: Bcl-2 3'UTR as a transcriptional terminator and as a co-infiltration agent to boost transient GFP expression via Agrobacterium leaf infiltration. Key Results: All four transgenic lines showed significantly greater chlorophyll retention than wild-type under 150 mM NaCl (P<0.05), with transgenic lines retaining ~56–58 µg total chlorophyll/well versus ~54 µg in WT. Under whole-plant water deficit, line #26 maintained leaf RWC of approximately 83% versus ~68% in WT (P<0.05). Membrane integrity was significantly better in transgenic plants: line #26 exhibited electrolyte leakage rates approximately half that of WT (P<0.05), and Evans Blue dye uptake of 2.65 nmol/g dry weight versus 3.00 nmol/g in WT (P<0.05). Photosynthetic rate (CO2 assimilation) showed no significant difference between transgenic and WT under stress. NGS confirmed 197 distinct sRNAs with 100% sequence identity to the Bcl-2 3'UTR in transgenic plants (with ≤25 reads mapping in non-transgenics), demonstrating in planta processing of the human sequence. The in silico pipeline predicted 34 candidate miRNAs; 25 of these were directly confirmed by NGS (16.5% coverage of the total NGS-derived sRNA set). Bcl-2 3'UTR transcripts did not show canonical endogenous target mimicry (eTM) activity. Transcriptomic analysis (DESeq2/Mapman) revealed that transgenic line #26 under water deficit upregulated 44 ERQC genes (chaperones, calnexin, calreticulin, BiP), 36 UPR genes (IRE1, bZIP60, GAAP, PDI), and core autophagy genes (Atg1, Atg5, Atg7, Atg8, Atg9, Atg13, Vps34), while pro-death executors (VPE vacuolar processing enzymes, metacaspases MCP1/2) were expressed at lower levels than in non-transgenics. In biotechnology applications, Bcl-2 3'UTR functioned as a weak transcriptional terminator in isolation; co-infiltration with Bcl-2 3'UTR-expressing constructs significantly elevated transient GFP expression (P<0.05), likely by attenuating Agrobacterium-induced cell death stress.
