Strengths: - First demonstration of cross-kingdom in planta biogenesis of human Bcl-2 3'UTR-derived sRNAs, validated by both custom in silico prediction (34 candidates, 25 confirmed) and NGS with 100% sequence identity filtering. - Multi-endpoint phenotypic characterization (chlorophyll retention, RWC, electrolyte leakage, Evans Blue) with rigorous Latin Square experimental design for water deficit trials, using well-characterized single-copy insertion lines. - Pathway-level transcriptomic analysis linking Bcl-2 3'UTR expression to a mechanistically coherent pro-survival signature (ERQC → UPR → autophagy upregulation, pro-death gene suppression), advancing beyond phenomenological observations in prior studies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Only two single-copy lines (#23 and #26) used for full molecular characterization and NGS, limiting statistical power; line #23 showed weaker or non-significant phenotypes in several assays, raising questions about penetrance and reproducibility. - The causal link between the 197 Bcl-2-derived sRNAs and the stress-tolerance phenotype is not directly established — no target knockdown validation (e.g., RACE, reporter assays, or CRISPR-based target confirmation) was performed, leaving the miRNA-mRNA connection at the level of prediction. - CO2 assimilation was not improved in transgenics under water deficit (both WT and transgenics reduced equally), meaning enhanced membrane integrity and water retention do not translate to maintained photosynthetic productivity — a critical limitation for agricultural relevance. - The study is conducted exclusively in N. benthamiana, a model laboratory species with no direct agricultural value; generalizability to crop plants (wheat, rice, maize) under field conditions remains entirely untested.
Citations
#111 — Moderately Innovative (56)

An Efficient Memoization Engine for Concurrent Graph Query Processing (complete version)

gaosen.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: KGraph's partition-based memoization with LLC-fit constraints is a clever systems insight that elegantly recovers small-graph memoization benefits at scale, and the Grab case study validates real production impact. However, this is fundamentally an optimization of existing concurrent graph processing rather than a new computational paradigm — memoization and graph partitioning are individually well-studied, and the contribution lies in their effective combination and automatic tuning.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Concurrent Graph Query (CGQ) processing—executing many homogeneous graph queries simultaneously—is a fundamental bottleneck in applications ranging from DeepWalk and Personalized PageRank to real-time taxi-hailing services (Grab processes 1.5K SSSP queries/minute at peak). Prior work on CGQ optimization (e.g., ForkGraph) focused on cache sharing via graph partitioning, but ignored computation sharing: the authors' profiling reveals that ~90% of edges appearing in one query's result also appear in another's, and >10% of edges appear in at least 90 queries' results. Naive memoization fails on large graphs because the operation reduction ratio is low while the overhead of scanning memoized results is high—producing 20% slowdowns on 100M-edge graphs even while delivering 2.3× speedups on 10K-edge graphs. Methodology: KGraph is a CGQ memoization engine built on two principal innovations. First, partition-based memoization with adaptation: rather than memoizing query results over the entire graph, KGraph partitions the graph and memoizes results only within each partition. This localizes memory accesses, raises the operation reduction ratio (emulating small-graph behavior), and fits data within LLC using the constraint |E|+|V|/|P| + k/|P|×Mem_max ≤ Size_LLC. A cost model estimates optimal partition size by balancing intra-partition processing cost against inter-partition coordination overhead. Second, strategic pivot query selection: KGraph pre-selects a small set of "pivot" queries whose results are memoized and made available to all CGQs. Six pivot selection strategies (S1–S6) spanning random, degree-sorted, and boundary-vertex criteria are evaluated; a decision tree trained on graph features (average degree, max degree, |V|) automatically selects the best strategy and pivot count for a given graph. The optimizer combines the cost model and decision tree. The execution engine extends ForkGraph's APIs with two new primitives—INITMEMOIZATIONSTORAGE and MEMOIZATIONFUNCTOR—that users implement to define memoization logic, with parallelism across pivot queries being embarrassingly parallel. Five application types are supported: SSSP, SSWP, SSR (traversal-based), DeepWalk, and PPR (random walk-based). Key Results: On 8 real-world graphs (4.6M–133.6M vertices, road networks to social/web graphs) against ForkGraph, KGraph achieves up to 12.5× speedup with an average of 4.1×. For traversal-based queries (SSSP, SSWP, SSR), KGraph outperforms ForkGraph in all cases, with SSSP showing up to 9.35× on Friendster. KGraph significantly outperforms Glign (a traversal-specialized baseline) on road networks while matching it on social networks. For random walk queries (DeepWalk, PPR), KGraph nearly doubles speeds on road networks but shows only ~20% improvement on social networks due to large boundary-vertex counts and the small-world topology. On synthetic R-MAT graphs scaling to 100M edges with densities up to 64, KGraph achieves 49.4× speedup over Ligra (memoization-augmented Ligra) with operation reduction ratios reaching 47.8–61.3% vs. Ligra's 15–22% at density 64. In the Grab Jakarta case study, applying KGraph reduced total query processing time from 6,519ms to 725ms (with only 8ms for memoization overhead), well within acceptable latency for real-time ride-hailing. Memory overhead for traversal-based pivot memoization remains modest (0.6–53.5% of graph size) but grows substantially for random walk applications (up to 384% for DeepWalk at α=2.2). Strengths: - Rigorous empirical motivation: the profiling study (operation reduction ratio distributions, query result overlapping distributions) provides principled justification for why and when memoization helps, distinguishing this from ad hoc engineering. - The partition-based approach elegantly recovers small-graph memoization benefits on large graphs by exploiting locality, with a theoretically grounded cost model and LLC-fit constraint. - The decision tree-based optimizer removes manual tuning, generalizing across road networks, social networks, and web graphs with a single trained model; the real Grab case study validates practical applicability at production scale. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The memory overhead for random walk applications (DeepWalk, PPR) is prohibitive at higher pivot counts—exceeding the graph size itself—limiting practical applicability for walk-based workloads on memory-constrained systems. - The performance gains on social networks are marginal for random walks (~20%), and the system's benefit is highly graph-topology-dependent; the decision tree mitigates but does not fully resolve the risk of poor configuration selection (e.g., wrong partition count can degrade performance by 2×). - The approach requires users to implement custom MEMOIZATIONFUNCTOR and INITMEMOIZATIONSTORAGE logic, introducing programming burden and potential for incorrect implementations that undermine memoization correctness or efficiency.
Citations
#112 — Moderately Innovative (55)

ChartEditBench: Evaluating Grounded Multi-Turn Chart Editing in Multimodal Language Models

Manav Nitin Kapadnis, Lawanya Baghel, Atharva Naik, Carolyn Rosé · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The multi-turn stateful evaluation protocol is a genuine contribution that reveals capability degradation (20-33%) invisible to single-turn benchmarks, and the hybrid evaluation combining programmatic assertions with visual difference enumeration is more interpretable than standard LLM-as-a-judge. However, as a benchmark paper the core contribution is dataset construction rather than algorithmic novelty, and the entirely synthetic generation pipeline and dependence on an unpublished model (Chart-R1) limit reproducibility and ecological validity.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing benchmarks for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) focus on single-turn chart generation or question answering — tasks where models either synthesize a chart from scratch or answer a factual query about a static image. This framing misses how real-world visualization workflows actually operate: analysts and developers iteratively refine existing chart specifications across multiple turns, maintaining shared context, tracking prior edits, and adapting to evolving requirements. No benchmark existed to measure this incremental, stateful editing capability. Additionally, standard evaluation approaches (LLM-as-a-judge, CLIP-based embeddings) suffer from narrow score distributions, high variance, and opacity about which aspects of an output succeeded or failed — making them ill-suited for diagnosing multi-turn failure modes. Methodology: The authors introduce ChartEditBench, a synthetic benchmark with 4,142 instances (20,710 total modification samples) spanning 37 chart types and 35 modification categories across two tasks: (1) Code-to-Code Chart Modification, where given original code and a target image the model must produce modified code, and (2) Natural Language Delta Modification, where the model receives a natural language instruction instead. Each instance undergoes N=5 sequential turns of progressively harder edits — from simple styling changes (colors, titles) at turn 1 to complex transformations (chart type conversion, dual axes) at turns 4–5. A fully automated three-stage pipeline generates charts, modification chains, and VQA pairs using LLMs against 37 matplotlib chart types, with a UniformDistributionManager ensuring balanced coverage. A 430-instance subset (2,400 turns) was human-verified by two expert annotators. Crucially, the benchmark uses a conversational evaluation protocol where each turn receives the model's own previous prediction as input, creating error-accumulation chains. Evaluation employs a hybrid composite metric combining: execution-based rendering success, programmatic assertion pass rates for instruction following and code quality, and a visually grounded similarity score where Chart-R1 (a fine-tuned VLM) enumerates explicit visual differences between predicted and ground-truth charts. Six models are evaluated: GPT-5-mini, Claude Haiku 4.5, Qwen3-VL (2B/8B/30B-A3B), and InternVL3-1B. Key Results: Claude Haiku 4.5 achieves the highest overall score (2.187/5.0) with particularly strong visual similarity (1.937). Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B is competitive (2.139 overall) using only 3B active parameters via MoE. GPT-5-mini leads on instruction following (1.623) but lags on visual similarity (1.521). InternVL3-1B scores only 1.345 overall with instruction following of 0.480. All models exhibit monotonic performance degradation across turns: Claude Haiku 4.5 drops from 2.46 to 1.97 (20% decline) while InternVL3-1B drops from 1.62 to 1.09 (33%). Visual similarity degrades most steeply (~35% average across models), while code quality drops only ~8% and instruction following remains roughly stable across turns — indicating accumulated errors corrupt visual output more than code structure. Style modifications (axis_style, series_label, axis_limit) yield scores of 2.2–2.8 for top models, while data-centric modifications (rolling_average, data_transformation) drop to 1.3–1.5. Rolling average failures exceed 80% low-score rates across all models except Claude Haiku 4.5 (58%). Proprietary models maintain 85–90% rendering success vs. 46–90% for open-source models. Strengths: - The multi-turn stateful evaluation protocol is a genuine methodological advance over one-shot benchmarks, revealing capability gaps (20–33% degradation) that single-turn evaluation completely masks. - The hybrid evaluation framework combining programmatic assertions, execution checks, and LLM-based visual difference enumeration is more interpretable and reliable than generic LLM-as-a-judge or CLIP-based metrics, with a principled separation between programmatic and LLM-judged instructions. - The dataset construction pipeline is rigorous: four-step code validation (AST parsing, sandboxed execution, render validation, structure validation), balanced modification type distribution via round-robin, and human verification with inter-annotator agreement via Cohen's κ. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is entirely synthetic — all charts and modification chains are LLM-generated, which may introduce systematic biases from the generating model's preferences and could fail to capture the diversity and messiness of real analyst workflows. - The evaluation relies on Chart-R1 (an anonymous, unpublished model) as the primary visual similarity judge, creating a dependency on an external artifact that cannot be independently verified or reproduced by the community. - The task formulation is restricted to matplotlib/Python code editing; it does not generalize to other visualization toolchains (Vega-Lite, D3, Plotly) that are equally common in practice, limiting ecological validity.
Recommended 2602.15758 PDF
#113 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Knowledge, Rules and Their Embeddings: Two Paths towards Neuro- Symbolic JEPA

Yongchao Huang, Hassan Raza · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The bidirectional formulation connecting energy-based rule constraints with Langevin-based rule discovery is conceptually elegant, and the EBC loss provides clean geometric interpretability. However, the empirical evaluation on a 303-patient UCI dataset with no neuro-symbolic baselines and an unimplemented latent-to-symbolic decoder leaves the core claims largely unsubstantiated — the framework is a promising sketch rather than a validated contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern self-supervised predictive architectures such as Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) excel at capturing statistical correlations from high-dimensional data but lack mechanisms to internalize verifiable symbolic logic, leaving them susceptible to spurious correlations and shortcut learning. Traditional rule-based inference systems (association rule mining, fuzzy logic) offer rigorous, interpretable logic but suffer from NP-hard combinatorial explosion and rigid discrete boundaries that prevent gradient-based optimization. No existing framework unifies self-supervised JEPA representation learning with discrete rule logic to enable bidirectional, generative, and zero-shot abductive reasoning. The paper addresses both directions simultaneously: using rules to improve JEPA latent geometry, and using the resulting continuous manifold to enable gradient-guided rule discovery. Methodology: The framework introduces two primary components. In Direction 1 (Rules for JEPA), the paper proposes Rule-Based JEPA (RbJEPA), which trains a JEPA purely on extracted symbolic rules by treating antecedents as contexts and consequents as targets with confidence/support weighting, and Rule-Informed JEPA (RiJEPA), which augments standard JEPA training with Energy-Based Constraints (EBC). The EBC loss — inspired by contrastive metric learning — minimizes energy E(A,C) = ||g(f_c(A)) − f_t(C)||² for valid rule pairs and maximizes it for corrupted pairs via a margin: L_EBC = Σ_valid E(A,C) + λΣ_invalid max(0, m − E(A,C_neg)). The combined objective is L_total = L_JEPA + βL_EBC. For real-world settings where raw data and symbolic rules are dimensionally distinct, the paper introduces a Multi-Modal Dual-Encoder Architecture with four separate encoders (data context/target, rule context/target) projecting into a shared 32-dimensional latent semantic space via a single modality-agnostic predictor. In Direction 2 (JEPA for Rules), the shaped energy landscape defines a rule manifold M_rule = {(z_c, z_t) | E(z_c,z_t) ≤ ε}. Novel rule discovery is enabled through gradient-guided Langevin dynamics: z^(k+1) = z^(k) − η∇_z E(z^(k)) + √(2ηT)ε, supporting three modes — unconditional joint generation, conditional forward inference, and abductive backward inference. A second paradigm (Marginal-Predictive Discovery) restricts diffusion to the context marginal and uses the deterministic predictor for consequent generation, with Metropolis-Hastings validation. Key Results: In a synthetic 3D simulation, RiJEPA assigns a mean energy of 12.713 to invalid OOD rule mappings versus 0.150 for classic JEPA — an 85× improvement in OOD discrimination — while maintaining low energy (0.006) for valid in-distribution rules. PCA visualization confirms that RiJEPA's latent space tightly clusters valid rules and explicitly separates invalid regions, whereas classic JEPA's representations are deeply entangled. In a clinical case study on the UCI Heart Disease dataset (303 patients, 14 features, binary coronary disease outcome), both classic JEPA and RiJEPA achieve 100% downstream linear probing accuracy. However, RiJEPA additionally achieves 100% zero-shot logical accuracy — classifying unseen patients purely by Euclidean distance to predefined symbolic "risk poles" without any fine-tuning — while classic JEPA is mathematically incapable of this task. RiJEPA demonstrates all four generative paradigms: unconditional clinical profile synthesis, forward inference from demographic priors, abductive root-cause reconstruction from outcomes, and marginal-predictive translation of raw patient vitals into symbolic diagnostic narratives. The training dynamics reveal a logic-accuracy trade-off: RiJEPA accepts a slightly higher raw data reconstruction MSE in exchange for satisfying symbolic constraints, eliminating spurious statistical shortcuts. Strengths: - The bidirectional formulation is conceptually elegant and internally consistent: the same energy landscape that enables rule-informed representation learning also enables gradient-guided rule discovery, creating a genuinely unified framework. - The EBC loss provides a clean geometric interpretation — valid logical transitions become low-energy basins, invalid mappings are repelled — which directly enables both zero-shot inference and Langevin-based generative discovery without requiring separate components. - The multi-modal dual-encoder design gracefully degrades: when rule inputs are masked, the architecture reduces to a standard JEPA, and the Fallback Latent Norm ablation (5.87) confirms numerical stability without symbolic input. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The empirical evaluation is severely limited in scale and breadth. The real-world experiment uses UCI Heart Disease with only 303 patients — a dataset considered a toy benchmark in modern clinical ML. The claimed 100% zero-shot accuracy on a small, clean binary classification task with an explicit anchor loss directly optimizing for the evaluation metric is not a meaningful demonstration of generalization; it is structurally guaranteed by the training objective. - No comparison against established neuro-symbolic baselines (Logic Tensor Networks, Differentiable ILP, Neural-Symbolic Concept Learners) is provided. The only baseline is vanilla JEPA, which makes it impossible to assess whether the proposed approach improves over the prior art or simply demonstrates that any form of supervised structure helps over pure self-supervision. - The latent-to-symbolic decoder D(z) — required to make generative rule discovery interpretable and the entire Direction 2 practically useful — is described theoretically but never implemented in experiments. The "discovered rules" in Table 5 are human-readable narratives, not formally validated symbolic rules, leaving the core claim of generative rule discovery unsubstantiated.
Search Results 2603.13265 PDF
#114 — Moderately Innovative (55)

InCoder-32B: Code Foundation Model for Industrial Scenarios (127 ▲ )

Jian Yang, Wei Zhang, Jiajun Wu, Junhang Cheng, Shawn Guo, Haowen Wang, Weicheng Gu, Yaxin Du, Joseph Li, Fanglin Xu, Yizhi Li, Lin Jing, Yuanbo Wang, Yuhan Gao, Ruihao Gong, Chuan Hao, Ran Tao, Aishan Liu, Tuney Zheng, Ganqu Cui, Zhoujun Li, Mingjie Tang, Chenghua Lin, Wayne Xin Zhao, Xianglong Liu, Ming Zhou, Bryan Dai, Weifeng Lv · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Mar 2026
Why this rank: InCoder-32B represents an impressive engineering effort in curating industrial code data across five heterogeneous domains and training with execution-grounded closed-loop repair trajectories. However, the methodology — data engineering plus supervised fine-tuning on a standard 32B transformer — is largely conventional, and the core contribution is scale and quality of domain-specific data rather than algorithmic novelty. The error analysis revealing systematic API hallucination failures (46% format errors in VeriScope, 47% linker errors in EmbedCGen) suggests the approach has not yet achieved deep domain understanding despite strong benchmark numbers.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: General-purpose code LLMs have achieved strong results on mainstream programming benchmarks but fail systematically on industrial software engineering tasks. Domains such as CUDA/Triton GPU kernel optimization, Verilog/RTL hardware design, embedded firmware development (ARM Cortex-M4), compiler optimization, and 3D CAD modeling impose requirements fundamentally different from conventional software: hardware-aware reasoning, strict resource and timing constraints, specialized language semantics, and execution correctness verified against real toolchains. Existing training corpora are dominated by general-purpose code (GitHub, StackOverflow), leaving industrial domains underrepresented. No prior work has attempted to unify intelligence across all five of these domains in a single model. Methodology: InCoder-32B is trained via a three-stage "Code-Flow" pipeline on a standard 32B decoder-only transformer. Stage 1 (Pre-training) collects industrial code from public repositories, technical literature via OCR, and domain-specific web data. It applies multi-level deduplication (hash, token-level near-duplicate, repository fork consolidation, cross-source) and validates all samples through AST comparison and recompilation. Training uses autoregressive language modeling combined with fill-in-the-middle (FIM) objectives on 4,096 GPUs. Stage 2 (Mid-training) progressively extends context from 8K to 32K to 128K tokens. The first sub-stage targets file-level tasks (e.g., RTL module completion); the second enables long-context reasoning over debugging sessions and multi-file designs. Domain-specific artifacts are injected — SDC timing constraint files, GPU profiling traces, memory sanitizer logs — alongside synthetic industrial QA pairs and agentic trajectories. Stage 3 (Post-training) constructs 2.5M SFT samples grounded in real execution environments: Icarus Verilog and Yosys for chip design, nvcc on NVIDIA A100 for CUDA, the official Triton compiler stack, ARM GCC plus Renode (a cycle-accurate STM32F407 simulator) for embedded firmware, and native x86-64 benchmarking for assembly optimization. Each task is decomposed into a structured instruction (natural language requirement, interface constraints, target platform, verification scripts), diverse candidate solutions are generated via template perturbation and cross-language migration, and candidates are verified by actual execution. Critically, failed solutions together with their full compiler/runtime feedback are used to generate repaired solutions, and the complete closed-loop repair trajectory — failed solution, execution feedback, and corrected solution — is included as additional training data. Key Results: On general code benchmarks, InCoder-32B achieves 94.5% on HumanEval, 91.8% on MBPP, 74.8% on SWE-bench Verified, 49.14% on LiveCodeBench, and 60.99% on BFCL — competitive with other 32B-class models and matching Kimi-K2-Instruct on HumanEval and MBPP. On industrial benchmarks, the gains are substantial. For chip design (RealBench), InCoder-32B achieves module-level Syn@1 of 74.8% and Func@1 of 62.7%, compared to Kimi-K2-Instruct (50.1% Syn@1, 22.2% Func@1) and DeepSeek-V3.2 (39.3% Syn@1, 17.2% Func@1), outperforming all open-weight models including those with 10–20× more parameters. On KernelBench (GPU kernel optimization), InCoder-32B achieves L2 pass rate of 36.0%, versus 28.0% for the next-best open model (Claude-Sonnet-4.6 at 28.0%) and 23.0% for GLM-5. On CAD-Coder (3D modeling), it achieves 82.0% compile rate and 53.5% IoU, topping all evaluated models including Claude-Sonnet-4.6 (77.0%/32.4%). Ablation confirms that scaling SFT data from 83M to 250M tokens yields consistent improvement across nearly all industrial benchmarks. Notably, closed-source Claude-Sonnet-4.6 still outperforms on EmbedCGen (79.0% vs. 35.2%) and TritonBench G-call (28.8% vs. 18.5%). Strengths: - First open-weight 32B model to unify industrial code intelligence across five heterogeneous domains, with execution-grounded training on real industrial toolchains (not proxy environments) - Closed-loop repair trajectories that include failed solutions with full compiler/runtime feedback are a technically motivated and experimentally validated design choice that meaningfully improves functional correctness - Strong chip design performance (RealBench module Func@1 62.7%) substantially outperforms all open models including frontier MoE architectures many times its size, demonstrating that targeted domain-specific data engineering can compensate for scale - Progressive context extension to 128K with domain-specific long-context data enables reasoning over realistic industrial artifacts (multi-file RTL designs, extended debugging sessions) Weaknesses / Limitations: - Significant performance gaps remain versus closed-source APIs on embedded systems (EmbedCGen 35.2% vs. Claude-Sonnet-4.6's 79.0%) and Triton optimization (G-call 18.5% vs. 28.8%), suggesting the training pipeline has not fully closed the gap on these domains - Error analysis reveals the model has broad domain vocabulary but has not fully internalized precise library conventions: 46% of VeriScope failures are format/output parse errors, 47% of EmbedCGen failures are linker errors from incorrect HAL/CMSIS API references, and 33% of TritonBench failures are NameErrors from incorrect Triton API usage — systematic failures that suggest surface-level memorization of domain syntax rather than deep internalization - Pre-training data scale and composition are deferred almost entirely to appendices, making it difficult to assess reproducibility or isolate contributions of pre-training versus post-training; the model architecture itself is standard and undifferentiated from base Qwen/DeepSeek-class transformers
Recommended 2603.16790 PDF
#115 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Qianfan-OCR: A Unified End-to-End Model for Document Intelligence (81 ▲ )

Daxiang Dong, Mingming Zheng, Dong Xu, Chunhua Luo, Bairong Zhuang, Yuxuan Li, Ruoyun He, Haoran Wang, Wenyu Zhang, Wenbo Wang, Yicheng Wang, Xue Xiong, Ayong Zheng, Xiaoying Zuo, Ziwei Ou, Jingnan Gu, Quanhao Guo, Jianmin Wu, Dawei Yin, Dou Shen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 55 as strong engineering with the moderately novel Layout-as-Thought mechanism, which elegantly recovers explicit layout analysis within the end-to-end paradigm while improving accuracy. The coordinate special token design is a practical innovation. However, the overall approach — a well-trained 4B VLM for document understanding — follows an established trajectory, and the core innovation has only been validated on one task type.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional OCR systems face a three-way trade-off between cost, accuracy, and capability. Pipeline-based systems chain layout detection, text recognition, and language comprehension as separate modules, introducing inter-stage error propagation and irreversible loss of visual context (spatial relationships, chart structures, formatting). Specialized two-stage OCR large models improve accuracy but add deployment complexity. General VLMs offer broad capabilities but underperform on structured document parsing. Industrial applications typically orchestrate heterogeneous components (detection model + OCR model + downstream LLM), increasing cost and limiting end-to-end optimization. The key gap is that existing end-to-end OCR models lack explicit layout analysis output — a functionality pipeline users rely on for element localization and type classification. Methodology: Qianfan-OCR is a 4B-parameter vision-language model built on the Qianfan-VL architecture: a Qianfan-ViT encoder (AnyResolution tiling up to 4K, 256 tokens/tile, up to 4096 tokens/page), a lightweight two-layer MLP cross-modal adapter, and Qwen3-4B as the language backbone (36 layers, 2560 hidden dims, GQA with 32 query / 8 KV heads, 32K–131K context). The model performs direct image-to-Markdown conversion and supports prompt-driven tasks spanning document parsing, table extraction, chart understanding, document QA, and key information extraction (KIE). The central technical innovation is Layout-as-Thought: an optional thinking phase triggered by ⟨think⟩ tokens where the model generates structured layout representations (bounding boxes, element types, reading order) within ⟨layout⟩...⟨/layout⟩ tags before producing final output. Bounding box coordinates are normalized to [0,999] and encoded as dedicated special tokens ⟨COORD_0⟩–⟨COORD_999⟩, reducing thinking output length by ~50% versus digit sequences. The layout phase serves dual purposes: recovering explicit layout analysis within the end-to-end paradigm, and improving accuracy on structurally complex documents by providing structural priors before generation. Training follows a four-stage progressive recipe over 2.85T tokens on 1,024 Baidu Kunlun P800 chips: Stage 1 — adapter-only alignment (50B tokens); Stage 2 — foundational OCR pretraining with full-parameter training, OCR-heavy data mixture (Document OCR 45%, Scene OCR 25%, Caption 15%, Specialized 15%) over 2T tokens; Stage 3 — domain-specific enhancement (800B tokens) across complex tables, formulas, charts, KIE, multilingual OCR, and document understanding, with a 70/30 domain-to-general data split; Stage 4 — instruction tuning via public data curation, reverse synthesis, and chart data mining. Six specialized data synthesis pipelines generate high-quality training data covering 25 layout categories, 192 languages, and 11 chart types. PaddleOCR-VL is used as the annotation engine for its finer label granularity (12 text element subtypes vs. MinerU's 4). Key Results: On OmniDocBench v1.5, Qianfan-OCR achieves 93.12 overall, ranking first among all end-to-end models and surpassing DeepSeek-OCR-v2 (91.09), Gemini-3 Pro (90.33), Qwen3-VL-235B (89.15), and Gemini-2.5 Pro (88.03). It also outperforms several pipeline systems including MinerU2.5 (90.67) and MonkeyOCR-pro-3B (88.85), narrowing the gap to the top pipeline system PaddleOCR-VL 1.5 (94.50). On OlmOCR Bench, Qianfan-OCR achieves 79.8, best among end-to-end models and competitive with PaddleOCR-VL (80.0). On OCRBench, it scores 880, highest among all models including Qwen3-VL-4B (873). On KIE benchmarks, it achieves a mean of 87.9 across five benchmarks, outperforming Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B (84.2, 50× more parameters), Gemini-3.1-Pro (79.2), and Qwen3-4B-VL (83.5). On document understanding, it leads on six of eight benchmarks including CharXiv_DQ (94.0), CharXiv_RQ (85.2), ChartQA (88.1), and ChartBench (85.9). Two-stage OCR+LLM pipelines score 0.0 on both CharXiv tasks, demonstrating the fundamental failure mode of discarding visual context. Throughput with W8A8 quantization reaches 1.024 pages/second on a single A100, comparable to PaddleOCR-VL (1.224 PPS). Ablation confirms the four-stage recipe yields +13.02% absolute improvement over Stage 1+4 alone. Strengths: - Layout-as-Thought is a well-motivated mechanism that simultaneously recovers a concrete functionality gap (explicit layout output) and provides measurable accuracy gains on complex documents; the entropy-based analysis elegantly characterizes when to enable it, offering actionable guidance. - The coordinate special token design (⟨COORD_0⟩–⟨COORD_999⟩) is an efficient engineering choice that halves thinking output length, directly reducing inference latency for layout reasoning — a practical detail with significant deployment impact. - The empirical evidence for end-to-end superiority is compelling and concrete: two-stage systems achieve literally 0.0 on both CharXiv tasks, making a strong case that discarding visual context is architecturally incompatible with spatial reasoning tasks. - The multi-stage training ablation is thorough and provides transferable insights — particularly that general-purpose data in Stage 3 acts as a regularizer preventing overfitting, a non-obvious finding with broad applicability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Layout-as-Thought has only been validated on OmniDocBench document parsing; its benefits for KIE, chart QA, and document understanding tasks remain undemonstrated, leaving the generality of the core innovation uncertain. - The model still trails the top pipeline system PaddleOCR-VL 1.5 by 1.38 points on OmniDocBench (93.12 vs. 94.50) and falls behind Qwen3-VL-4B on DocVQA (92.8 vs. 94.9) and OCRBenchv2-en (56.0 vs. 60.68), indicating that specialization comes with measurable costs on general tasks. - The Layout-as-Thought mechanism is generated via supervised fine-tuning in a rigid format, which the authors themselves acknowledge; without reinforcement learning, the model cannot adaptively decide when structural reasoning is needed versus counterproductive, requiring users to make this judgment manually based on document type.
Recommended 2603.13398 PDF
#116 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Scalable Mesh Partitioning and Robust Sub-Cell Geometry Treatment for the Ghost-Cell Immersed Boundary Method on Cartesian Meshes

hal.science · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The sliding-partition algorithm and automatic degenerate cell elimination address real practical pain points in immersed boundary CFD, and the demonstration at 50 billion cells far exceeds prior capabilities. The super-cell coarsening idea for resolving degenerate cells is a useful contribution. However, both algorithms are engineering extensions of known techniques (Anupindi partitioning, ray tracing), the sliding heuristic's advantage degrades at scale, and the lack of quantitative validation against analytical or experimental benchmarks limits confidence in accuracy.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Immersed Boundary Methods (IBMs) on Cartesian grids simplify mesh generation for CFD simulations with complex geometries by embedding surface representations into a fixed grid rather than constructing body-fitted meshes. However, two fundamental problems limit their practical use at scale. First, for geometries with small physical-to-bounding-box volume ratios — such as arterial networks where only 3.2% of bounding-box cells are inside the physical domain — classical Cartesian partitioning wastes the vast majority of computational resources on inactive outer cells. Existing solutions (Anupindi's partition removal, METIS-based unstructured partitioning) are either insufficient or fail to scale past ~50 million cells. Second, complex geometries produce "degenerate cells" — cells cut by more than one disconnected boundary segment in 2D or disconnected surface patch in 3D — which cause numerical instabilities in ghost-cell formulations. No existing tool could automatically eliminate degenerate cells for ghost-cell methods; practitioners were forced to resort to manual geometry modification or mesh refinement, neither of which is reliable. Methodology: Two independent algorithms are proposed and implemented in the open-source Notus CFD code. The first is a sliding-partition domain decomposition algorithm. Starting from the Anupindi method (remove partitions entirely outside the physical domain), the algorithm additionally slides partition boundary lines to push marginal partitions — those containing only a thin sliver of physical domain — fully outside, enabling their removal. A heuristic orders slide operations by minimizing the number of degrees of freedom locked by each move. The algorithm is applied to all integer factorizations of the processor count and the decomposition minimizing remaining partitions is selected. The second algorithm eliminates degenerate cells in two phases. In contour/surface reconstruction, ray tracing along grid edges identifies geometry intersection points. Cells with exactly two points receive a direct line segment; cells with more are resolved via recursive quadrant sub-ray tracing until all sub-cells contain at most two points, from which segments are assembled. In degenerate cell elimination, two cases are handled: cells with connected chains of segments are reduced to a single diagonal segment; cells with disconnected segments (tunneling or split configurations) are treated by expanding "super-cells" (3×3 in 2D, 3×3×3 in 3D, recursively expanded to 5×5, 7×7, etc.) that coarsen the geometry locally — replacing complex local topology with a simpler interpolated boundary — until all cells within the super-cell contain at most one segment or surface. In 3D, polygons on super-cell boundaries are triangulated using minimum-surface-area criterion to reconstruct interior intersection points and rebuild non-degenerate surface segments. Key Results: On the partitioning algorithm, for an arterial system geometry (3.2% physical volume fraction) starting from 8,192 partitions, the Anupindi method yields 856 remaining partitions while the proposed method yields 686 — a 20% further reduction — with inner-cell fraction rising from 30.9% to 38.6%. For the Lascaux Cave geometry at 512 initial partitions, the reduction is 30% (112 vs. 76 partitions). The advantage diminishes with partition count but remains approximately 10% even at 500,000 partitions. The algorithm scales to 50 billion cells distributed over 500,000 partitions with preprocessing completing in 15–19 minutes on 128 processors — compared to METIS, which requires 15 minutes just for 50 million cells and cannot handle meshes larger than that. On the degenerate cell elimination algorithm, 2D processing completes in under 5 seconds for 100-million-cell meshes with near-linear parallel efficiency to 256 processors. In 3D, the arterial system (150 million cells) completes in 170 seconds on 256 processors and under 2 minutes for 1.2 billion cells on 2,048 processors — a negligible fraction of total simulation time. For an arterial haemodynamics simulation, active cell count was reduced from 526 million to 14.4 million after partitioning, enabling a 686-core simulation where medium-sized arteries reach 60 cm/s. A porous medium simulation requiring ~2,000 super-cells to handle approximately 2,000 degenerate configurations was completed successfully on an 80-million-cell mesh. Strengths: - Addresses a genuine practical gap: the first algorithm to guarantee complete elimination of degenerate cells for ghost-cell IBM without manual intervention, applicable to arbitrary 2D and 3D closed geometries - Demonstrated at extreme scale (50 billion cells, 500,000 partitions) far beyond what METIS-based approaches can handle, with timing that remains negligible relative to simulation cost - Both algorithms produce rectangular/cuboidal partitions, preserving compatibility with massively parallel structured-grid linear solvers (hypre/BoomerAMG) that are essential for billion-cell simulations - Implemented in open-source Notus CFD, making the methods immediately deployable by practitioners Weaknesses / Limitations: - The sliding partition algorithm's relative benefit over Anupindi degrades from ~30% at small partition counts to ~10% at 500,000 partitions, and the heuristic ordering of slides is not proven optimal — the true combinatorial optimum may be significantly better but is intractable - The 3D degenerate cell elimination has poor parallel efficiency for geometries with geometrically concentrated degeneracies: the pumpkin geometry (deep sub-ray tracing near the stem) achieves only 9% parallel efficiency at 4,096 processors, and the super-cell elimination phase is inherently sequential, bounding scalability by Amdahl's law as processor counts grow - The algorithm cannot handle sub-grid-scale features (e.g., tunnels narrower than one cell width), which still require mesh refinement — a limitation not resolved and acknowledged only briefly - The 3D triangulation within large super-cells (approaching 25×25×25) can produce geometrically degenerate (long, thin) triangles that destabilize surface reconstruction; the proposed fallback (iterating over triangulations by surface area) is ad hoc and not guaranteed to succeed - The applications section provides no quantitative validation against analytical solutions or experimental benchmark data — flow fields are qualitatively reasonable but accuracy of the ghost-cell formulation under the modified (coarsened) geometry is not assessed
Search Results
#117 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Nemotron-Cascade 2: Post-Training LLMs with Cascade RL and Multi-Domain On-Policy Distillation (27 ▲ )

Zhuolin Yang, Zihan Liu, Yang Chen, Wenliang Dai, Boxin Wang, Sheng-Chieh Lin, Chankyu Lee, Yangyi Chen, Dongfu Jiang, Jiafan He, Renjie Pi, Grace Lam, Nayeon Lee, Alexander Bukharin, Mohammad Shoeybi, Bryan Catanzaro, Wei Ping · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 54 because while the efficiency results are remarkable (gold-medal competitions with 3B active parameters), the methodological contributions — sequential domain-wise RL and on-policy distillation from intermediate checkpoints — are engineering refinements of known techniques rather than conceptual breakthroughs. The optimal cascade ordering is empirically determined and model-specific, limiting transferable insight.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Post-training LLMs with reinforcement learning across diverse reasoning and agentic domains is challenging due to catastrophic forgetting, cross-domain interference, and training instability. Existing multi-domain RL approaches either train jointly (causing interference) or sequentially without principled stabilization. The gap this paper fills is how to scale domain-wise RL to cover a much broader set of tasks—math proof, competitive coding, instruction following, RLHF, long-context, and SWE agents—without degrading performance across domains as training progresses. Methodology: The paper builds on Cascade RL (sequential, domain-wise RL stages from Nemotron-Cascade 1) and introduces two key additions. First, Multi-Domain On-Policy Distillation (MOPD): intermediate teacher checkpoints (one per domain, selected from the best validation checkpoint within each Cascade RL stage) are used to distill back into the student via a token-level reverse-KL objective with truncated importance weighting. Because teachers come from the same SFT initialization, vocabulary and distribution shift are minimal. MOPD provides dense per-token training signal versus GRPO's sparse outcome reward, yielding faster convergence (52 steps to match RLHF's 160-step results on ArenaHard). Second, multi-domain RL stages are introduced where task formats and verification costs are similar (e.g., MCQA + tool-calling + structured output), enabling joint training without interference. The full Cascade RL order is: SFT → IF-RL → Multi-domain RL → MOPD → RLHF → Long-context RL → Code RL → SWE RL. Throughout, GRPO with strict on-policy training (importance ratio = 1, no KL term) and token-level loss normalization is used. The base model is Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B-Base (30B MoE, 3B activated). SFT data is meticulously curated across math (4.4M samples), code (4.3M), science (2.7M), general chat (10.2M+), instruction following, SWE agentic/agentless trajectories, and terminal agent tasks (490K). Key Results: Nemotron-Cascade-2-30B-A3B achieves gold-medal performance on IMO 2025 (35/42 pts), IOI 2025 (439.28/600), and ICPC World Finals 2025 (10/12 problems), making it the second open-weight model after DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale-671B-A37B (20× more parameters) to reach this level. On competitive coding benchmarks: LiveCodeBench v6 = 87.2 (88.4 with TIR), surpassing Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (74.6) and Qwen3.5-397B-A17B (83.6). On math: AIME 2025 = 92.4 (98.6 with TIR), HMMT Feb25 = 94.6, IMO ProofBench = 72.9 (within 8 points of DeepSeek-Math-V2-671B). On alignment: ArenaHard v2 = 83.5, IFBench = 82.9—best-in-class among compared models. SWE-bench Verified (OpenHands) = 50.2, below Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (69.2). MOPD reaches ArenaHard Hard Prompt 85.5 in 52 steps vs. RLHF reaching 80.7 in 160 steps. The model underperforms on knowledge-intensive (MMLU-Pro = 79.8 vs. Qwen3.5 = 85.3) and long-context tasks (AA-LCR = 39.1 vs. Qwen3.5 = 58.5). Strengths: - Remarkable intelligence density: gold-medal competition results with only 3B activated parameters, 20× fewer than the prior open-weight achiever, representing a genuine efficiency breakthrough. - MOPD is a principled and practically effective solution to capability drift: using intermediate checkpoints from the same pipeline as teachers is elegant and avoids external model dependencies while delivering 3× faster convergence than RLHF. - The Cascade RL framework's ordering principles (mitigate inter-domain interference, group non-conflicting tasks, use MOPD as stabilization point) are well-motivated and empirically validated across a comprehensive benchmark suite. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance on knowledge-intensive tasks (MMLU-Pro, GPQA-Diamond, HLE) and agentic benchmarks (SWE-bench, τ²-Bench, BFCL) lags Qwen3.5-35B-A3B considerably, and the paper acknowledges this is a pretraining data limitation—not addressed by RL post-training alone. - The optimal Cascade RL ordering is empirically determined and model-specific (the authors note it changed from Nemotron-Cascade 1 to 2), providing limited transferable guidance for other architectures or capability profiles. - IMO 2025 and IOI results rely heavily on test-time scaling (generate-verify-refine loops with up to 2000+ model generations per problem), making wall-clock inference costs at evaluation time very high and not representative of single-pass capabilities.
