#118 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Iterative Semantic Reasoning from Individual to Group Interests for Generative Recommendation with LLMs

Xiaofei Zhu, Jinfei Chen, Feiyang Yuan, Zhou Yang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The individual-to-group interest reasoning hierarchy is well-motivated by consumer behavior theory and the bidirectional CoT design is a sound architectural choice with strong ablation support. However, the framework is primarily an engineering composition of existing components (LightGCN, CoT prompting, contrastive distillation) rather than a methodological departure, and evaluation on only three Amazon product datasets with modest user counts leaves scalability and generalizability undemonstrated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Recommender systems must infer user preferences from historical interaction sequences. Recent LLM-based approaches improve on ID-only methods by encoding rich semantic representations of users and items, but they stop at semantic integration — they construct representations without reasoning through them. User behavior theory (means-end chain models, laddering theory) argues that genuine preference understanding requires step-wise inference: first mapping item attributes to explicit individual interests, then surfacing latent implicit interests shared by behaviorally similar user groups. No existing generative recommendation method models this individual-to-group reasoning pipeline or iteratively aligns the two levels of interest. Methodology: The paper proposes ISRF (Iterative Semantic Reasoning Framework), a three-module system built on top of a T5-small backbone LLM. (1) Individual Interest Reasoning (IIR): A Chain-of-Thought prompt drives DeepSeek-R1-14B to perform bidirectional reasoning over item attributes — forward reasoning produces a positive description of who would prefer the item, backward reasoning (conditioned on the positive) produces a negative description of who would not, and a final synthesis step derives key item features. The resulting LLM embeddings are dimensionality-reduced via PCA, frozen, and projected into the recommendation space via a two-layer adapter. LightGCN is then run on the user-item interaction graph using these item embeddings to produce explicit user interest representations. (2) Group Interest Reasoning (GIR): The same bidirectional CoT process is applied to users, sampling items from each user's history to generate positive/negative/synthesized interest descriptions. A user similarity matrix is constructed from cosine similarity of LLM-generated user embeddings, and the Top-K nearest neighbors define a sparse user relation graph. A second LightGCN pass on this graph yields group implicit interest representations. (3) Iterative Refinement (IR): For direct recommendation, a contrastive distillation loss uses the group representation as a teacher to supervise the individual sequential representation (stop-gradient on teacher). For sequential recommendation, a contrastive loss maximizes mutual information between group and individual representations. The joint objective combines standard autoregressive generation loss with the appropriate alignment loss. Inference uses beam search. Key Results: Experiments run on Amazon Sports, Beauty, and Toys datasets (19K–36K users, 12K–18K items) against 14 baselines spanning traditional, attention-based, GNN-based, and LLM-based methods. On sequential recommendation, ISRF improves over the strongest baseline (ELMRec) by 3.92%–10.37% across all datasets and metrics (H@5, N@5, H@10, N@10), all statistically significant (p < 0.05). On direct recommendation, gains over ELMRec are substantially larger: 7.52%–24.57%, with Sports showing H@10 of 0.7697 vs. ELMRec's 0.6479 (+18.8%). Ablation confirms each component contributes — removing item semantic reasoning (w/o Ise) causes a 24% drop in H@10 on Toys for direct recommendation; removing the adapter degrades performance by ~6%. Semantic variants show that bidirectional (positive+negative) reasoning consistently outperforms either polarity alone, with positive and negative views making comparable contributions. Computational overhead is modest: training takes ~50% more time per epoch than ELMRec on Sports (15m vs. 10m), with inference time essentially identical, on a single RTX 4090. Strengths: - The explicit individual→group interest reasoning hierarchy is well-motivated by established consumer behavior theory and produces a concrete, interpretable two-level architecture rather than a monolithic black-box embedding. - Bidirectional CoT (positive + negative semantic reasoning) is a technically sound design choice — the ablation confirms both polarities contribute roughly equally, and combining them outperforms either alone, suggesting genuine complementarity. - Gains on direct recommendation (up to 24.6% over SOTA) are large and consistent across all three datasets, with statistical significance reported — the result is not cherry-picked. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The user similarity graph construction requires computing all-pairs cosine similarity over LLM-generated user embeddings, which is O(|U|²). At 35K users this is manageable but the approach will not scale to industrial recommender systems with millions of users without significant engineering. - Evaluation is confined to three Amazon product datasets in a single narrow domain (consumer goods). There is no evidence that the individual-to-group reasoning hierarchy generalizes to domains with sparser user histories (e.g., news, music) or to cold-start users who have too little interaction history to generate meaningful CoT prompts. - The iterative refinement mechanism couples direct and sequential recommendation training — it is unclear how to apply ISRF in a pure sequential-only setting, and the gains on sequential recommendation (3–10%) are notably smaller than on direct recommendation (7–25%), suggesting the group interest module contributes less to next-item prediction than to candidate ranking.
Citations 2603.13934 PDF
#119 — Moderately Innovative (54)

LeJOT-AutoML: LLM-Driven Feature Engineering for Job Execution Time Prediction in Databricks Cost Optimization

Lizhi Ma, Yi-Xiang Hu, Yihui Ren, Feng Wu, Xiang-Yang Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: LeJOT-AutoML introduces a genuinely useful multi-agent architecture that automates feature engineering from weeks to minutes, with concrete safety gates and production deployment at Databricks. The discovery of qualitatively different predictive signals (temporal lags vs. static ratios) is a meaningful methodological insight. However, the accuracy gap versus manual engineering (R²=0.81 vs 0.91) and the complete absence of comparison against established AutoML baselines (H2O, Auto-sklearn, CAAFE) make it impossible to attribute gains to the LLM/MCP design versus standard automated approaches, keeping this in moderately innovative territory.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Databricks job orchestration systems reduce cloud costs by selecting resource configurations that minimize execution cost while satisfying latency constraints. Accurate execution-time prediction is central to this: underestimation violates SLOs, while overestimation causes over-provisioning. Existing pipelines rely on static, manually engineered features (~40 features) that systematically miss runtime effects — partition pruning reduces actual scan volume by orders of magnitude relative to table size, join skew concentrates work on straggler tasks, and shuffle amplification inflates compute costs beyond what input sizes suggest. These signals are distributed across execution logs, job scripts, and metadata stores, and capturing them manually requires months of domain-expert effort that lags behind evolving workloads. Methodology: LeJOT-AutoML is a three-agent AutoML pipeline that automates feature engineering using LLMs integrated with a Model Context Protocol (MCP) toolchain. The Feature Analyzer Agent (FAA) retrieves domain knowledge via retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over a Spark SQL and Databricks knowledge base, then synthesizes a structured feature specification list — not just feature names, but typed schemas with source, extraction plan, cost estimate, and refresh frequency. The Feature Extraction Agent (FExA) materializes features through a four-stage pipeline: static extraction from job scripts and metadata, runtime materialization via log parsers and a read-only SQL sandbox, normalization/encoding, and data-quality checks. The Feature Evaluation Agent (FEvA) assesses feature health (coverage, stability, distribution shift), feature utility (SHAP-style importance, redundancy via correlation screening), and end-to-end model impact (MAE/RMSE/R² deltas via ablations), emitting feedback to FAA/FExA for iterative refinement. Two safety gates — a code-completion checker (syntactic validity, approved library allowlist) and a data-leakage checker (temporal/semantic isolation from the target label) — guard all generated extractor code before execution. The final predictor is XGBoost trained on the validated feature matrix, with Qwen-235B as the agent reasoning backbone. Cost-aware feature selection at inference time solves a constrained optimization trading off prediction accuracy against per-feature extraction latency under a per-request budget. Key Results: AutoML generates 200+ features spanning log profiling, time-series statistics, and driver-node history, versus ~40 features from manual engineering. The top AutoML features are temporal lag signals (duration_seconds_lag_1 at 28.8% importance, vcpu_lag_1 at 24.6%), whereas manual top features are static resource ratios (vcpu_ratio at 16.2%, vmemory_ratio at 13.4%) — indicating the two pipelines identify qualitatively different predictive signals. On predictive accuracy, manual engineering achieves R²=0.91 / MAPE=19.49% / MAE=78.94 versus AutoML's R²=0.81 / MAPE=20.13% / MAE=123.29. However, AutoML completes three end-to-end iterations in 20–30 minutes versus approximately one month for manual engineering. The iterative feedback loop improves MAE from 247.95 to 145.64 and R² from 0.61 to 0.81 over three FEvA iterations. Integrated into LeJOT, AutoML achieves 19.01% cost savings versus 27.94% for manual ML, with lower throughput (0.08k/s vs 0.12k/s). Module-level timing shows FAA (~255s) and the data-leakage checker (~220s) dominate pipeline latency due to LLM reasoning cost. Strengths: - Reduces feature engineering cycle from weeks/months to 20–30 minutes — a practical order-of-magnitude improvement in development velocity that enables continuous adaptation under workload drift - Extracts runtime-derived features (shuffle amplification, partition pruning effectiveness, stage imbalance) that are structurally inaccessible to static analysis, capturing qualitatively different predictive signals than manual approaches - Safety gates (code-completion and data-leakage checkers) are concrete, deployable reliability mechanisms that make LLM-generated code safe to execute in an enterprise environment - Demonstrated in real Databricks production deployment with end-to-end cost savings (19.01%), not just held-out benchmark accuracy Weaknesses / Limitations: - AutoML accuracy (R²=0.81) meaningfully lags manual engineering (R²=0.91) due to the MCP toolchain's lack of configuration-change trajectories and pricing context — the authors acknowledge this but do not quantify how much of the gap is attributable to this specific missing signal versus LLM reasoning limitations - No comparison against established AutoML baselines (H2O AutoML, Auto-sklearn, CAAFE) — the only comparison is to hand-crafted features, making it impossible to assess whether the LLM/MCP design adds value beyond standard automated feature selection - Experiments run on a single workload type on a single machine (Intel Core Ultra 7, 32 GB RAM), with five runs and no statistical significance testing; scalability to diverse Databricks job types or larger job volumes is undemonstrated - LLM agent costs (FAA + data-leakage checker ≈ 475s per iteration) create non-trivial retraining latency; at three iterations the pipeline approaches 25 minutes, and the cost of LLM inference at production scale is not analyzed
Recommended 2603.07897 PDF
#120 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Cross-scale knowledge tracking: A joint time-frequency modeling approach for global cycles and local fluctuations

link.springer.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The slow/fast variable decomposition grounded in nonlinear dynamics theory is a coherent conceptual framing, and the CORAL alignment loss for cross-scale feature distribution matching is a practical contribution. However, the core components — Fourier transforms, wavelet transforms, hypergraph neural networks, GCNs — are all established tools being combined in a relatively expected manner, and statistical significance against the strongest baseline (AKT) is absent on two of four datasets.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge tracing (KT) — predicting students' future knowledge mastery from historical interaction sequences — is the core task in intelligent tutoring systems. Existing KT methods (DKT, SAKT, AKT, GKT, HiTSKT, etc.) share a fundamental flaw: they operate at a single temporal scale or rely on linear fusion of multi-scale features, failing to capture the nonlinear coupling between long-term learning trends ("slow variables," e.g., semester-level accumulation and periodic review cycles) and short-term behavioral fluctuations ("fast variables," e.g., pre-exam cramming or consecutive incorrect answers). The paper frames the learning process through nonlinear dynamics theory — slow variables provide stable evolutionary boundaries while fast variables trigger nonlinear perturbations that feed back to reshape long-term trajectories. Crucially, long-term and short-term features also exhibit mismatched statistical distributions, causing feature conflicts under naive fusion schemes. Methodology: The proposed WFTKT model has four tightly integrated modules. (1) FT-HGNN (long-term branch): the student's interaction sequence is Fourier-transformed and low-pass filtered (cutoff ~0.14 Hz, corresponding to a 7-day course cycle) to extract slow-variable global periodic features. These are then restored to the time domain via inverse FFT and processed by Hypergraph Neural Networks (HGNN) to model high-order, multi-point dependencies among knowledge concepts (e.g., cross-disciplinary concept chains). The FT and HGNN outputs are blended via a learnable scalar α. (2) WT-GCN (short-term branch): continuous wavelet transform is applied and high-pass filtered (cutoff ~0.04 Hz, ≈24-hour fluctuations) to isolate fast-variable transient components. After inverse wavelet transform, a directed Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) models how short-term perturbations diffuse locally across knowledge points. Two parallel GCN paths handle dependency and co-occurrence relations between concepts. (3) Dynamic Fusion Framework (DFF): replaces static concatenation with learned, time-step-adaptive weights over the long-term and short-term feature maps, using dual-path fusion (learning network and knowledge network) to simulate nonlinear slow–fast coupling. (4) CORAL alignment loss: minimizes the Frobenius norm between the covariance matrices of slow-variable and fast-variable features, forcing second-order statistical alignment and reducing distribution conflict before fusion. Total training loss is cross-entropy prediction loss plus CORAL loss weighted by a small coefficient (5×10⁻⁶). Key Results: Evaluated on four public KT benchmark datasets — ASSISTments2009, ASSISTments2017, Statics2011, KDDCup2010 — against nine baselines (DKT, DKVMN, SAKT, AKT, GKT, HawkesKT, CL4KT, QDCKT, HiTSKT). WFTKT achieves the highest AUC on all four: 0.8548 (+2.4% over AKT on Assist2009), 0.8148 (+6.0% over CL4KT on Assist2017), 0.8556 (+16.5% over GKT on Statics2011), 0.8239 (+1.1% over AKT on KDDCup2010). ACC improvements are substantial on Assist2017 (0.8437, +13.6% over CL4KT) and KDDCup2010 (0.8162, +14.5% over HiTSKT). RMSE is best or near-best on most datasets (0.3727 on KDDCup2010). CORAL ablation (WFTKT-CR) shows MMD between long/short features drops by 63–68% when CORAL is used; removing the wavelet branch (WFTKT-WR) causes a >10% AUC drop on Assist2017. A linear fusion control (WFTKT-Linear) lags WFTKT by 3–5% AUC and 5–8% RMSE, confirming that nonlinear coupling adds real value. Training time is 91.6 minutes for 50 epochs (ASSISTments2017 on RTX 2080), competitive with HiTSKT and CL4KT. Paired t-tests confirm significance at p<0.001 for most comparisons, though no significant edge over AKT on Assist2017 (p=0.057) and KDDCup2010 (p=0.613) is a notable caveat. Strengths: - The slow/fast variable framing grounded in nonlinear dynamics is coherent and principled, giving a clear theoretical rationale for each architectural choice (FT↔slow, WT↔fast, HGNN↔high-order structure, GCN↔local diffusion). - CORAL loss for second-order distribution alignment between feature scales is a well-motivated and practical solution to cross-scale feature conflict, empirically verified via MMD measurements showing >60% discrepancy reduction. - Ablation studies are thorough — four component removals plus a linear fusion control and an architecture swap experiment — convincingly isolating each module's contribution. - Consistent top-ranked AUC across all four datasets, spanning diverse educational contexts (short vs. long sequences, slow- vs. fast-variable-dominant scenarios). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The cutoff frequencies (0.14 Hz for FT, 0.04 Hz for WT) are empirically fixed and not personalized; the paper acknowledges that students with different review cycles would require different frequencies, and uniform parameters cause 4–6% AUC penalty for heterogeneous populations. Meta-learning or hypernetwork-based personalization is deferred to future work. - Computational complexity is non-trivial: HGNN is O(|V|²|E|) and adds 30–40% overhead over GCN in large-node scenarios; on CPU the single-inference time is 10–15 ms, potentially problematic for large-scale MOOCs. Lightweight alternatives are not yet implemented. - Statistical significance against AKT is absent on Assist2017 (p=0.057) and KDDCup2010 (p=0.613), suggesting the advantage in fast-variable-dominant scenarios with short sequences is marginal; the model also underperforms CL4KT and HawkesKT on ACC for Assist2009 and on RMSE for Assist2009, indicating the multi-module design can hurt on simple, low-periodicity tasks via mild overfitting.
Citations
#121 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Outcome-Aware Tool Selection for Semantic Routers: Latency-Constrained Learning Without LLM Inference

Huamin Chen, Xunzhuo Liu, Junchen Jiang, Bowei He, Xue Liu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: OATS presents a practical solution — outcome-aware centroid interpolation of tool embeddings — that is genuinely zero-cost at serving time and includes a sensible validation gate. The connection to GRPO reward normalization provides theoretical grounding. However, gains on the larger ToolBench benchmark are marginal (+1.7%) and don't beat BM25, the evaluation uses binary proxy labels rather than real production outcomes, and end-to-end task completion is never measured.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference gateways use semantic routers to select which tools to attach to requests before forwarding them to backend models. The critical constraint is that tool selection must complete within single-digit milliseconds on CPU, without GPU resources or LLM inference—at 10,000 requests/second with a 5 ms budget, tool selection alone consumes 50 CPU-seconds per second. The standard approach, static embedding similarity, satisfies the latency constraint but has fundamental accuracy limitations: tool descriptions are written once and never updated (description quality bottleneck), the system cannot learn from historical outcomes, each tool is represented as a single fixed vector, and embedding similarity rewards surface-level lexical overlap rather than functional relevance. On ToolBench, BM25 (pure lexical retrieval) actually outperforms dense embedding (NDCG@5 0.853 vs. 0.834), underscoring the weakness of static embeddings at scale. RL-based tool orchestration methods can learn from outcomes but require LLM inference and multi-turn rollouts, making them incompatible with router-level latency requirements. Methodology: The paper proposes Outcome-Aware Tool Selection (OATS), which incorporates outcome feedback into tool selection through offline embedding refinement with no changes to the serving path. The core mechanism (Stage 1, OATS-S1) operates as a periodic batch job: for each tool, outcome logs are partitioned into positive queries (where the tool was correctly selected) and negative queries (where it was retrieved but wrong—hard negatives). Each tool's embedding is then interpolated toward the centroid of its positive queries and away from its negative centroid via: ê_i = (1 − α)·e(d_i) + α·ē(Q⁺_i) − β·ē(Q⁻_i), with α=0.3 (attraction) and β=0.1 (repulsion), followed by L2 renormalization. The process iterates N=3 times with momentum (μ=0.5) to prevent oscillation, and a validation gate ensures the refined embeddings never degrade below the static baseline. At serving time, the path is identical to the static baseline—only the stored tool vectors differ. Two learned extensions are evaluated as ablations: a 2,625-parameter MLP re-ranker (OATS-S2) trained with binary cross-entropy on outcome-derived features (similarity gap, category, historical success rate, frequency, query length), and a 197K-parameter contrastive adapter (OATS-S3) trained with InfoNCE loss using hard-negative triplets mined from outcome logs. Both extensions run on CPU within the millisecond budget. The connection to RL is made explicit: the contrastive loss mirrors GRPO's normalized advantage computation but operates in embedding space, avoiding the cost of trajectory rollouts. Key Results: On MetaTool (199 tools, 1,287 test queries), OATS-S1 improves NDCG@5 from 0.869 to 0.940 (+8.2%) and Recall@1 from 0.716 to 0.830, with no added serving cost (p50: 3.53 ms vs. 3.66 ms baseline). On ToolBench (2,413 tools, 180 test queries), OATS-S1 improves NDCG@5 from 0.834 to 0.848 (+1.7%), though BM25 still leads at 0.853. The MLP re-ranker (OATS-S2) hurts on ToolBench (NDCG@5: 0.823, −1.1%) and adds nothing on MetaTool (0.869 = baseline), because 357 training queries spread across 2,413 tools yields fewer than 0.15 positive examples per tool. The contrastive adapter (OATS-S3) reaches NDCG@5 0.931 on MetaTool (+6.2%) and 0.841 on ToolBench (+0.7%), providing consistent but smaller gains than OATS-S1 on MetaTool. On the MetaTool "similar choices" subtask—the hardest split with overlapping tool functionality—OATS-S1 achieves 83.4% Recall@1 vs. 73.5% for Vicuna-7B and 69.1% for ChatGPT, while running ~1,000× faster on CPU. All OATS methods stay within single-digit millisecond p50 latency on both benchmarks. Strengths: - The core mechanism (embedding centroid interpolation) is genuinely zero-cost at serving time—no additional parameters, no code changes to the inference path, and it can degrade no worse than the static baseline due to the validation gate. This is a practical, deployable solution. - The paper honestly characterizes when the approach fails: the MLP re-ranker degrades performance below the ~10:1 data-to-tool ratio threshold, and gains on ToolBench are modest because dense embedding space becomes crowded at 2,413 tools. The deployment decision framework (when to use S1 vs. S2 vs. S3 based on tool count and data density) is actionable. - The connection drawn between centroid interpolation and RL reward normalization (GRPO) provides genuine theoretical grounding for why the approach works, situating a simple geometric operation within a broader learning framework. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The gains on ToolBench are marginal (+1.7% NDCG@5) and the method does not beat BM25 on that benchmark. The fundamental limitation—that centroid interpolation has less room to maneuver in a densely packed 2,413-tool embedding space—is acknowledged but not resolved, and the paper offers no principled solution for large tool sets beyond adding the contrastive adapter. - Evaluation uses binary ground-truth labels (match/no-match) as a proxy for production outcomes, which are richer and noisier (tools selected but unused, tools erroring, suboptimal tool use). The claim that richer signals yield stronger improvements is speculative; no experiments validate this. - The evaluation measures only retrieval quality (NDCG, Recall), not end-to-end task completion. Whether improved tool selection translates to better downstream LLM task success is unverified, and the benchmarks used (MetaTool, ToolBench) have known limitations in representing the diversity of real production tool workloads.
Search Results 2603.13426 PDF
#122 — Moderately Innovative (52)

SEFEL: A Simple Yet Effective Framework for Fast Event Linking

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: SEFEL delivers strong engineering value through its 38-55x inference speedup by eliminating cross-encoder reranking, and the LLM-based argument extraction is a practical improvement over brittle rule-based pipelines. However, the core architectural idea — replacing cross-encoder reranking with a bi-encoder plus contrastive learning — is a well-established pattern in entity linking, making this a competent adaptation rather than a novel paradigm. The +21.5 point out-of-KB gain is impressive but partly attributable to the synthetic data generation strategy rather than architectural innovation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Event linking associates event mentions in text with entries in a knowledge base (KB), enabling downstream tasks like question answering and KB enrichment. The task is hard due to diverse event expressions (same event, different surface forms), cross-sentence argument scattering, and the need to handle out-of-KB events (mentions with no KB entry). Prior work follows a two-stage retrieve-and-rank pipeline (bi-encoder retrieval + cross-encoder reranking), which is computationally expensive at inference because each mention must be cross-encoded against multiple candidates independently. The best prior method (ArgEveLINK) also relies on hand-crafted rule-based argument extraction pipelines that limit cross-domain generalization and produce noisy supervision for out-of-KB detection. Methodology: SEFEL is an end-to-end dual-tower (bi-encoder) framework that eliminates the cross-encoder reranking stage entirely. It has four components. First, an LLM (LLaMA-3.1-70B-Instruct) extracts event type and arguments from raw text without annotated data, replacing hand-crafted pipelines. Out-of-KB training samples are generated by perturbing extracted arguments via LLM-guided substitution and sentence rewriting, enriching the training set with synthetic NIL examples (~1% of training data). Second, the event linking base module builds argument-aware event mention representations by averaging the transformer embeddings of the event mention and its LLM-extracted arguments (Eq. 4), projecting both mention and candidate event embeddings into a shared space via linear transformations. A learnable NIL embedding serves as an explicit out-of-KB candidate. Candidate scoring fuses dot-product similarity with Wikipedia-derived event popularity priors via a learned linear layer, trained with cross-entropy (Eq. 8). Third, a contrastive learning module operates from two views: mention–mention (using a momentum-updated memory bank and dropout augmentation to pull embeddings of mentions referring to the same event together while pushing apart different events) and mention–candidate (pulling the mention embedding toward its gold event and away from other candidates). All loss terms are jointly optimized. At inference, scoring is a single forward pass through the bi-encoder with dot-product lookup — no reranking. Key Results: On the Wikipedia in-KB test set (19,267 instances), SEFEL achieves 83.64% accuracy, outperforming the best baseline ArgEveLINK by +3.59 points (80.05%). On the NYT out-of-KB test set (993 instances), SEFEL achieves 76.90%, a +21.5 point gain over ArgEveLINK (55.40%) and dramatically outperforming GPT-4o-mini (30.55%). For efficiency, SEFEL processes the Wikipedia test set in 446 seconds versus 24,612 for ArgEveLINK (~55× speedup) and 50,293 for GPT-4o-mini. On NYT: 59 seconds vs. 2,250 for ArgEveLINK (~38×). Ablations confirm every component contributes: removing event arguments drops Wikipedia accuracy to 82.22% and NYT to 56.53%; replacing LLM extraction with rule-based Unist+TagPrime drops NYT to 62.43%; removing contrastive learning (either view) consistently hurts both datasets. Event popularity is critical for out-of-KB detection (its removal drops NYT accuracy to 56.92%), while event description is critical for in-KB linking (its removal drops Wikipedia to 58.64%). Strengths: - The elimination of the cross-encoder reranking stage yields dramatic inference speedups (38–80×) without accuracy loss, making the system viable for large-scale deployment where prior methods are prohibitively slow. - LLM-based argument extraction without annotated data substantially generalizes beyond rule-based pipelines, evidenced by the 14-point NYT accuracy gap between SEFEL and its rule-based ablation (SEFELUni+Tag). - The unified scoring architecture with a learnable NIL embedding neatly handles both in-KB and out-of-KB scenarios in a single pass, avoiding threshold tuning or external classifiers for NIL detection. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The LLM used for argument extraction (LLaMA-3.1-70B) introduces significant offline preprocessing cost and a dependency on a large external model; this cost is not analyzed or compared against baselines, potentially understating total system overhead. - Out-of-KB synthetic training data constitutes only ~1% of training instances, and the sensitivity of the method to this fraction is not explored — it is unclear whether the out-of-KB gains would hold with more or fewer generated samples or on domains where LLM argument perturbation produces less realistic negatives. - Evaluation is limited to English Wikipedia-derived KBs and two datasets; generalization to other languages, domain-specific KBs, or event ontologies beyond Wikipedia page titles is not demonstrated despite the claimed cross-domain generalization advantage of LLM-based argument extraction.
Search Results
#123 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Reducing Human Effort in Ontology -Based Data Integration : An LLM-Assisted Mapping Pipeline

openreview.net · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: LAMP demonstrates that structured task decomposition with RAG retrieval can outperform larger models on ontology mapping, and the ablation study cleanly validates the design. However, the core ideas — decomposing LLM tasks, injecting global context, using vector similarity for retrieval — are well-established patterns applied to a specific domain, and the evaluation on a tiny benchmark (9-13 tables) leaves scalability and generalization unproven.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Ontology-Based Data Integration (OBDI) requires semantic mappings between heterogeneous data sources and a target ontology, a process that is labor-intensive, requires deep domain and ontology engineering expertise, and does not scale as data source diversity grows. Prior LLM-based approaches address this by issuing a single prompt to generate the entire mapping file, but this strategy degrades sharply on complex ontologies and large schemas due to token limitations, loss of coherence across mapping decisions, and hallucination. LAMP targets this gap by designing a structured, decomposed pipeline that keeps individual LLM subtasks small and coherent while maintaining a global view of the mapping problem. Methodology: LAMP (LLM-Assisted Mapping Pipeline) decomposes the mapping workflow into two sequential levels: (1) table-to-class mapping, where each relational table is matched to ontology classes via RAG over a ChromaDB vector database (cosine similarity over class-level chunks), and (2) column-to-property mapping, where columns of each table are mapped to data and object properties of the selected class. Three cross-cutting mechanisms reinforce quality: (a) term refinement — an LLM expands abbreviated table/column names and generates human-readable descriptions to improve retrieval quality; (b) enriched representations — ontology chunks include class names, descriptions, IRIs, data properties, and object properties, stored in Markdown key-value format shown empirically to suit LLMs better than XML/JSON/CSV; and (c) global context — a compact schema summary (all table/column names, all ontology class/property names) is injected into every prompt to maintain cross-task coherence and avoid contradictory decisions. The system is built on Python/Flask (backend), Angular 19 (frontend), LangChain, ChromaDB, Google Gemini 2.5 Flash (LLM), and gemini-embedding-001. Function-calling is required of the LLM to enforce structured output format. A human-in-the-loop component allows review and refinement of term expansions, class mappings, and property mappings before the final R2RML mapping file is generated and syntactically validated. Key Results: LAMP is evaluated on the BLINKG benchmark across three scenarios of increasing complexity: Scenario 1 (low complexity, close schema-ontology alignment), Scenario 2 (medium complexity, value normalization required), and Scenario 3 (high abstraction, minimal structural overlap). For class mapping, LAMP achieves F1 = 1.0 on Scenarios 1 and 2, and F1 = 0.80 on Scenario 3. For property mapping (data + object), F1 = 0.97, 0.99, and 0.48 respectively. Compared to the best BLINKG single-prompt baselines (Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o), LAMP — using the lighter Gemini 2.5 Flash — matches or exceeds both on Scenarios 2 and 3. On Scenario 3 class mapping, LAMP's 0.80 substantially outperforms Gemini 2.5 Pro (0.38) and GPT-4o (0.27); on Scenario 3 property mapping, LAMP's 0.48 more than triples GPT-4o's 0.12. An ablation study shows process decomposition is the single most critical feature: removing it drops Scenario 3 class F1 from 0.80 to 0.33 and property F1 from 0.48 to 0.13. Removing global context is second most impactful (class: 0.80→0.67, property: 0.48→0.40). Strengths: - The task decomposition principle is well-motivated and empirically validated — the ablation cleanly shows its dominant contribution, and the intuition (smaller, focused subtasks reduce hallucination and token overflow) is sound and transferable to other schema-matching problems. - Outperforming larger models (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o) using a lighter model (Gemini 2.5 Flash) via structured pipeline design is a compelling demonstration that prompt engineering and workflow design can compensate for raw model capacity. - The global context mechanism is a practically important contribution: injecting a compact but holistic schema summary into every prompt addresses the coherence problem introduced by decomposition, and the ablation confirms its significance. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation dataset (BLINKG) is small and narrow: Scenario 1 has only 13 tables and 30 columns across toy ontologies (Person, Sport, City); Scenario 2 has 10 tables; Scenario 3 has 9 tables. This makes it difficult to assess scalability or generalization to real enterprise OBDI deployments with hundreds of tables and large, inconsistent ontologies. - Performance collapses on Scenario 3 property mapping (F1 = 0.48), and the authors partially attribute this to ontology incompleteness rather than pipeline limitations — they manually corrected missing domain/range definitions before evaluation, raising concerns about how well LAMP handles real-world, imperfect ontologies without pre-curation. - The system is restricted to tabular data sources and R2RML as the output mapping language, excluding non-tabular sources (RDF graphs, APIs, document stores) and alternative standards (RML, YARRRML, SPARQL-based mappings), which limits practical applicability in heterogeneous integration environments.
Search Results
#124 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Retrieval-Feedback-Driven Distillation and Preference Alignment for Efficient LLM -based Query Expansion

Minghan Li, Guodong Zhou · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The retrieval-metric-driven DPO construction is the paper's key insight — using actual nDCG@10 differences as preference signals directly addresses the readability/retrievability mismatch in query expansion distillation. However, the overall framework is a competent assembly of well-known components (QLoRA distillation, DPO, BM25 scoring), and the student still meaningfully underperforms the teacher, suggesting the ceiling of this approach is already visible.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong effectiveness for generative query expansion — generating paragraph-level text that enriches sparse query representations and improves downstream retrieval. However, deploying models like DeepSeek-685B in production retrieval systems is impractical: API latency reaches ~4.7 s/query and resource cost is prohibitive for high-concurrency, low-latency applications. Small models (4B parameters) are fast and deployable but lack the semantic elaboration capability of large models, resulting in substantially weaker expansion quality. The gap between "readability" and "retrievability" — where language modeling objectives do not guarantee downstream nDCG@10 improvements — remains an unresolved challenge when using standard supervised fine-tuning for distillation. Methodology: The paper proposes a two-stage teacher–student framework. In the first stage, DeepSeek-V3.1-685B generates two expansions per training query: a zero-shot expansion (E1) targeting broad semantic coverage, and a few-shot expansion (E2) using four Query2Doc-style in-context examples for structured, detail-rich output. Both are used as supervision targets for QLoRA-based supervised fine-tuning (LoRA rank=4, 4-bit NF4 quantization) of a Qwen3-4B-Instruct student model, training it to generate paragraph-level expansions (60–100 words) under a unified zero-shot prompt at inference time — eliminating the need for few-shot demonstrations during deployment. In the second stage, the two teacher-generated expansions per query are each evaluated via BM25@1000 retrieval and scored by nDCG@10. Pairs where |ΔnDCG@10| ≥ 0.01 form chosen/rejected DPO training instances; the rest are discarded as ambiguous. Direct Preference Optimization then fine-tunes the SFT-initialized policy model against a frozen reference model copy, explicitly aligning generation preferences toward expansions that improve retrieval ranking. The full pipeline requires no online large-model calls during serving — only the 4B student is deployed. Key Results: On TREC DL19, SFT+DPO achieves nDCG@10 = 0.6536, representing 97% of the DeepSeek-685B teacher's zero-shot performance (0.6741) and a +14.78 pp absolute gain over BM25 (0.5058). On DL20 and DL21, the student achieves nDCG@10 of 0.5921 (+11.25 pp over BM25) and 0.5633 (+11.75 pp), respectively. On Chinese MIRACL-zh, the method achieves nDCG@10 = 0.3366 (+15.65 pp over BM25), demonstrating cross-lingual generalizability. DPO consistently adds +1.15 to +3.62 pp nDCG@10 on top of SFT alone across all four benchmarks. The distilled 4B model generates expansions in 0.103 s/query versus 4.726 s/query for the DeepSeek API — a 45.9× latency reduction — and 22× faster than a locally deployed OLMo-32B teacher, while using only 8.9 GB GPU memory versus 62+ GB for a 32B model. Ablation confirms that combining E1+E2 supervision is superior to either source alone, and that DPO-only without SFT initialization is unstable across datasets and retrievers. The framework generalizes to dense retrieval (BGE-base-en-v1.5), where SFT+DPO improves nDCG@10 by +2.10 pp on DL19 and +2.89 pp on DL20 over raw BGE. Strengths: - The practical motivation is well-founded: 45–49× inference speedup and 7× memory reduction make the method genuinely deployable in production retrieval systems, not just academically interesting. - The retrieval-metric-driven DPO construction is principled — using actual BM25 nDCG@10 differences as the preference signal directly couples training objectives with evaluation targets, addressing the readability/retrievability mismatch that plagues standard SFT distillation. - Evaluation is thorough across four benchmarks (English DL19/20/21 and Chinese MIRACL-zh), two retriever types (sparse BM25 and dense BGE), two teacher models (DeepSeek-685B and OLMo-32B), and includes ablations on LoRA rank, preference threshold Δ, and SFT data composition — providing a complete picture of where the gains come from. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The student still falls ~3% short of the teacher's zero-shot nDCG@10 on DL19 and further behind on MIRACL-zh, indicating a meaningful performance gap that the framework does not close — the teacher's richer semantic elaboration remains out of reach for a 4B model. - Preference pairs are constructed from only two fixed expansion candidates (zero-shot vs. few-shot), which limits preference signal diversity. A richer candidate pool (e.g., multiple sampled generations at varying temperatures) could yield more informative DPO training data and potentially larger gains. - The DPO threshold Δ and the specific BM25 retriever used during preference construction introduce retriever-specific bias: preferences are calibrated for BM25 term matching, which may not optimally align with dense retrieval objectives, partially explaining why gains under BGE are smaller and DPO-only is less stable in that setting.
Search Results 2603.13776 PDF
#125 — Moderately Innovative (51)

FIRM-MoE: Fine-GrainedExpert Decomposition for Resource-Adaptive MoE Inference

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: FIRM-MoE's decomposition of experts into independently loadable sub-expert linear layers is a reasonable engineering insight that directly addresses bandwidth waste from mispredicted whole-expert transfers. The MoL intersection strategy and Expert Utilization metric are sensible contributions. However, the overall approach combines several individually straightforward ideas (finer-grained loading, multi-layer prediction intersection, coordinate-wise search) without a unifying conceptual advance, and the modest 1.31× average throughput gain reflects incremental rather than transformative improvement.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs achieve high model capacity with sparse activation, but their large expert pools demand substantial GPU memory, making edge deployment on memory-constrained devices (e.g., single RTX 3090) impractical. Prior offloading approaches either suffer from low GPU utilization due to on-demand loading, or rely on whole-expert pre-fetching with prediction-based pre-loading — which wastes bandwidth when predictions are wrong and lacks adaptability to varying memory budgets. No prior work treats expert sub-components (individual linear layers within an expert) as independently schedulable units. Methodology: FIRM-MoE introduces three coordinated mechanisms. First, Expert Decomposition treats each expert's constituent weight matrices (W_gate, W_up, W_down) as independently loadable sub-experts, enabling partial pre-fetching of only the first n linear layers per predicted expert. This reduces wasted CPU-to-GPU transfers when predictions miss. Second, Meeting-of-Layers (MoL) improves expert prediction by aggregating routing decisions from the preceding P layers: each of the P layers independently predicts K candidate experts for layer l using that layer's hidden state and layer l's routing matrix, and the intersection (high-confidence set) is taken as the final prefetch set. This intersection strategy trades prediction accuracy for higher expert utilization (ratio of pre-fetched experts that are actually used), which is the operationally relevant metric under bandwidth constraints. Third, Hierarchical Expert Optimization and Pre-fetching (HEOP) adaptively tunes (K, P) per layer group (shallow/middle/deep) using a lightweight coordinate-wise hill-climbing search minimizing a loss L_T = α·n_unused + β·(n_miss − n_hit). The search space is partitioned by layer depth (reducing O(n³) to O(n)) and runs online during inference. The full system uses LRU caching on GPU and a multi-threaded pipeline overlapping computation with asynchronous CPU-to-GPU transfer. Key Results: Evaluated on five MoE models (Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, DeepSeek-MoE-16B-Base, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, OLMoE-1B-7B) on an RTX 3090 with TruthfulQA and ShareGPT benchmarks. Against six baselines (DeepSpeed-MII, MoE-Infinity, basic pre-fetching, Adap-MoE, Fiddler, Llama.cpp), FIRM-MoE achieves up to 1.5× decoding speedup and 2.85× memory savings. On Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B with cache=128 experts, it reaches 1.8× over the basic pre-fetching baseline; with cache=768, it maintains 1.6×. Crucially, going from 768 to 128 experts cached degrades FIRM-MoE performance by only ~30% while reducing memory by 2.85×. Ablation on Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B shows FG pre-fetching alone yields 1.24–1.41× speedup; MoL alone yields 1.21–1.27×; combining all components yields 1.29–1.65×. MoL achieves 90.5% expert utilization vs. 78.8% for top-4 selection, though at lower raw prediction accuracy (59.8%). E2E throughput on ShareGPT across 15 input/output length configurations shows an average 1.31× gain over Fiddler and Llama.cpp. Strengths: - The insight of treating individual linear layers within an expert as independently loadable units is simple, broadly applicable, and directly addresses the core inefficiency of wasted CPU-GPU bandwidth from mispredicted whole-expert transfers. - MoL's intersection-based multi-layer prediction is parameter-free and outperforms weighted voting alternatives, with a new Expert Utilization metric that better captures edge-deployment constraints than the standard Prediction Accuracy metric. - HEOP reduces search complexity from O(n³) to O(n) via layer-group partitioning and runs online without a separate profiling phase, making it practical without offline calibration data. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to batch size 1 (autoregressive decoding), which is the primary edge use case but does not cover batched inference where expert activation patterns and bandwidth bottlenecks differ substantially. - The granularity hyperparameter n (1 or 2 layers pre-fetched) is selected per model class based on a manual observation about model size, not automatically tuned by HEOP; this is a gap in the claimed "resource-adaptive" automation. - No model accuracy benchmarks are reported; the paper claims orthogonality to quantization but does not validate that the offloading mechanism itself introduces no accuracy regression across the diverse routing patterns induced by the TruthfulQA and ShareGPT distributions.
Search Results
#126 — Moderately Innovative (51)

Improving TabPFN's Synthetic Data Generation by Integrating Causal Structure

Davide Tugnoli, Andrea De Lorenzo, Marco Virgolin, Giovanni Cinà · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper cleanly diagnoses a real and underappreciated failure mode — collider-induced spurious correlations from arbitrary column ordering in autoregressive foundation models — and validates it with controlled experiments. However, the proposed fix (topological ordering and parent-only conditioning) is methodologically straightforward once the problem is identified, and the practically important discovered-CPDAG setting yields largely negative results due to PC-stable's poor orientation recall, significantly limiting real-world applicability where ground-truth DAGs are unavailable.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: TabPFN (Tabular Prior-Data Fitted Network) is a foundation model for tabular data that generates synthetic samples autoregressively — sequentially conditioning each feature on previously generated features in input column order. This ordering is arbitrary with respect to the true causal structure of the data. When the generation order conflicts with causal dependencies (e.g., a collider is generated before its parents), the model learns and propagates spurious conditional dependencies into the synthetic marginal distribution. This matters most in high-stakes domains like healthcare and drug development, where synthetic data must faithfully preserve causal effects to support valid downstream analyses such as treatment effect estimation. Prior causal generative models (DECAF, Causal-TGAN, DATGAN) address this by training from scratch on each dataset and explicitly using DAG structure, but no prior work had adapted a pre-trained foundation model for causally faithful generation. Methodology: The authors propose two complementary causal conditioning strategies layered on top of TabPFN's existing autoregressive generation mechanism. (1) DAG-aware conditioning: when a full DAG is known, each variable is conditioned exclusively on its causal parents (rather than all predecessors), and variables are generated in topological order. Formally, the conditioning set for variable x_{σ(i)} becomes C_DAG = {x_j : x_j → x_{σ(i)} in G}. (2) CPDAG-based conditioning: for the realistic partial-knowledge setting, a Completed Partially Directed Acyclic Graph (CPDAG) is used. Variables with at least one directed adjacent edge are conditioned on their known causal parents; remaining variables fall back to sequential conditioning on all predecessors. The minimal CPDAG is constructed by orienting only V-structures from the true DAG; the discovered CPDAG is learned from data via the PC-stable constraint-based algorithm (α=0.05), using KCI for continuous pairs, G² for discrete, and k-NN CMI for mixed-type pairs. Evaluation uses three metrics — Correlation Matrix Difference (CMD, Frobenius norm of correlation matrix discrepancy), k-Marginal Total Variation Distance (kMTVD, k=2), and Nearest-Neighbor Adversarial Accuracy (NNAA) — plus Average Treatment Effect (ATE) preservation. Experiments span a custom 4-variable collider SCM, six Microsoft CSuite benchmark datasets (4–12 nodes, diverse causal structures), and the 38-variable UVA/Padova T1DM glucose simulator (partial causal knowledge). Training sizes range from N=20 to N=1000 with 100 resampling iterations per configuration. Key Results: DAG-aware conditioning significantly improves CMD in 24 of 35 dataset×training-size configurations versus vanilla TabPFN with original ordering, with only 2 degradations (both on CSS at small N). When both methods use topological ordering (isolating the contribution of causal parent conditioning from column ordering alone), DAG-aware generation shows 20 significant improvements and no degradations in CMD. For ATE preservation, DAG-aware generation achieves 26 significant improvements across 42 configurations with only 2 degradations; on the custom collider SCM, it reduces absolute ATE error by approximately 1.23 units at N=20. Vanilla TabPFN with topological (vs. original) ordering yields 18 significant ATE improvements and 26 CMD improvements, confirming ordering alone matters substantially. Minimal CPDAG shows 15 CMD improvements and 3 degradations versus vanilla original ordering, and 23 ATE improvements with 2 degradations — the effectiveness depends on V-structure placement. Discovered CPDAG via PC-stable performs poorly overall (4 CMD degradations, 0 improvements), because PC leaves most edges undirected (direction recall ≤ 0.32 across all datasets and sizes), causing fallback to vanilla sequential conditioning and, on CMC where PC over-orients with low precision (~0.19), active degradation. On the 38-variable SGL dataset with partial causal information, improvements in distributional quality increase with training size, unlike the smaller SCMs. Strengths: - Cleanly diagnoses a fundamental and previously underappreciated failure mode of autoregressive foundation models: collider-induced spurious correlations from arbitrary column ordering, validated both theoretically and empirically with correlation coefficients of |ρ|≈0.15 in the vanilla model versus ≈0 for DAG-aware conditioning. - The DAG-aware and CPDAG-based strategies require no retraining or fine-tuning — they are pure inference-time modifications to TabPFN's conditioning context, making them immediately applicable to any TabPFN-based workflow. - Rigorous experimental design with 100 resampling iterations, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with Holm-Bonferroni correction, and Hodges-Lehmann effect size estimation across a diverse benchmark suite spanning controlled SCMs and realistic clinical simulators. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The CPDAG-based strategy is brittle in practice: PC-stable recovers useful orientations only under favorable conditions (sufficient sample size, correct variable types, no latent confounders), and incorrect orientations actively degrade performance — the discovered CPDAG results are largely negative, limiting the no-DAG-knowledge use case. - Evaluation of causal estimands is restricted to the ATE of a single pre-specified treatment-outcome pair per dataset; other causal quantities (ATT, CATE, interventional distributions over non-outcome variables) are not studied, leaving unclear how broadly the improvements generalize. - The CMD metric assumes monotonic dependence structure and is explicitly noted to be uninformative for datasets with multiplicative functional forms (e.g., CSS), creating a gap between the metric and the underlying causal fidelity being targeted.
Search Results 2603.10254 PDF
#127 — Moderately Innovative (51)

LVOmniBench: Pioneering Long Audio-Video Understanding Evaluation for Omnimodal LLMs (25 ▲ )

Keda Tao, Yuhua Zheng, Jia Xu, Wenjie Du, Kele Shao, Hesong Wang, Xueyi Chen, Xin Jin, Junhan Zhu, Bohan Yu, Weiqiang Wang, Jian Liu, Can Qin, Yulun Zhang, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Huan Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: LVOmniBench fills a genuine evaluation gap by being the first benchmark targeting long-form (10-90 min) joint audio-video comprehension, and the manual curation with unimodal ablation filtering is rigorous. The ASR ablation provides useful diagnostic insight into how models process audio. However, this is fundamentally a benchmark contribution rather than a methodological advance, the scale is modest (1,014 questions), and the multiple-choice format with 25% random baseline limits discriminative power.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Omnimodal large language models (OmniLLMs) capable of jointly processing audio, video, and text have advanced rapidly, yet all existing evaluation benchmarks assess them only on short clips (10 seconds to ~6 minutes average). Real-world video content routinely spans tens of minutes. This mismatch means current benchmarks cannot reveal whether OmniLLMs can perform the sustained cross-modal reasoning that practical applications demand—long-term memory across an extended session, precise temporal localization of audio-visual events, and fine-grained alignment of non-verbal acoustic cues with visual content. No prior benchmark combined (a) long-form video (>10 min), (b) joint audio-visual reasoning requirements, and (c) multi-dimensional difficulty stratification. Methodology: The authors construct LVOmniBench through a fully manual pipeline. Videos are sourced from YouTube under Creative Commons licenses, filtered to 10–90 minutes, and organized into 5 broad domains and 21 fine-grained subcategories (entertainment, lifestyle, DIY & cooking, record, film & TV). From an initial pool of >3,000 raw videos, 275 are retained after objective quality screening requiring dynamic content, rich audio-visual correlation, and absence of trivial unimodal shortcuts. Human annotators then generate 1,014 multiple-choice QA pairs (4 options each), each explicitly requiring joint audio-visual reasoning. A rigorous filtering pass uses Gemini to test each question in unimodal ablation modes (video-only, audio-only, text-only); questions solvable by a single modality are discarded or revised. Each surviving question is difficulty-stratified across three tiers (Low/Medium/High) using four dimensions: perceptual difficulty, informational granularity, temporal span, and inference complexity. Question types span nine subcategories: Attribute Perception, Music Perception, Counting, Event Understanding, Human-Centric Understanding, Sound Inference, Spatial Inference, Temporal Inference, and Logical Reasoning. The resulting benchmark has an average video duration of 2,069 seconds (34:29)—more than 6× longer than any prior audio-visual benchmark. Evaluation uses maximum permissible frames per model (up to 768 frames for Qwen/SALMONN models; 1 FPS for Gemini with deep thinking mode enabled). Key Results: Thirteen models are evaluated spanning proprietary OmniLLMs (Gemini 2.0/3.0 Flash and Pro), open-source OmniLLMs (Qwen2.5-Omni-7B, Qwen3-Omni-30B, MiniCPM-o 4.5, Ming-Omni-2.0-100B, video-SALMONN 2+ 7B, VideoLLaMA2-7B), video-only LLMs (Qwen3-VL-8B/30B), and audio-only LLMs (Qwen2-Audio). Gemini 3.0 Pro achieves the highest accuracy at 65.8% overall, with 79.3% on Low-difficulty and 45.0% on High-difficulty questions. Gemini 3.0 Flash reaches 59.0%. Among open-source OmniLLMs, Qwen3-Omni-30B leads at 35.8%, while most others fall below 35% and near random-chance (25%) on High-difficulty tasks. Qwen3-VL-30B (video-only) achieves only 36.3%, notably lower than its performance on prior long-video-only benchmarks, confirming audio is essential. An ablation replacing raw audio with ASR transcripts shows that open-source models (e.g., Qwen3-Omni: +7.1 pp with ASR) improve substantially, revealing they cannot effectively process raw audio, while Gemini 3.0 Flash does not improve (+12.8 pp with raw audio vs. +5.8 pp with ASR), indicating raw audio carries non-verbal signals (emotion, intonation, musicality) that text cannot capture. Error analysis of 153 Gemini 3.0 Flash mistakes finds: Perception errors 34% (60% visual, 40% audio), Reasoning errors 23.5%, Localization errors 19.6%, Cross-modal semantic gap 19%, OCR/emotional errors 4%. Strengths: - Fills a genuine and significant gap: the only benchmark specifically targeting long-form (10–90 min) joint audio-video comprehension, with average duration 6–20× longer than all prior benchmarks. - Fully manual curation and annotation—every video and QA pair was human-selected and human-verified, avoiding hallucination artifacts common in LLM-generated benchmarks. - Principled quality control via unimodal ablation filtering ensures every question genuinely requires both audio and video, preventing the unimodal shortcuts that plague most AV benchmarks. - Multi-dimensional difficulty stratification and nine-category question taxonomy enable granular, hierarchical analysis rather than a single aggregate score. - The ASR ablation experiment provides actionable diagnostic insight, distinguishing models that process audio semantically from those that extract richer non-verbal signals. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small by modern standards (275 videos, 1,014 questions), which limits statistical power for fine-grained subcategory analysis and may make it susceptible to overfitting by future models trained on similar content. - Video sourcing is restricted to YouTube/Creative Commons, which biases content toward specific creator demographics and vlog-style formats (entertainment and lifestyle dominate), potentially underrepresenting professional, scientific, or structured long-form content. - The benchmark currently uses multiple-choice format exclusively with four options; this inflates baseline accuracy (25% random), and does not test open-ended generation or temporal grounding with timestamps, underrepresenting important real-world capabilities. - Difficulty labels are static annotations made at construction time using a single model (Gemini) for unimodal filtering; they may not align well with the actual difficulty distribution experienced by future, stronger models.

KP7XMC7UlQ

KP7XMC7UlQ
Why this rank: Scores 51 for the well-grounded factorized policy formulation that elegantly solves credit assignment in behavior trees, with formal theoretical justification. The dual use of expert labels for initialization and continuous regularization is principled. However, the scope is narrow (simple grid-world environments), the BT structures are hand-designed, and the approach addresses a relatively niche intersection of VLM distillation and behavior tree execution.

#129 — Moderately Innovative (48)

coDrawAgents: A Multi-Agent Dialogue Framework for Compositional Image Generation

Chunhan Li, Qifeng Wu, Jia-Hui Pan, Ka-Hei Hui, Jingyu Hu, Yuming Jiang, Bin Sheng, Xihui Liu, Wenjuan Gong, Zhengzhe Liu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: coDrawAgents applies multi-agent dialogue with visual grounding to compositional image generation, achieving strong GenEval results (0.94 overall). The Visual Chain-of-Thought with incremental canvas grounding is a reasonable design choice, and the ablation cleanly decomposes contributions. However, the framework's heavy dependence on GPT-5 for spatial reasoning limits reproducibility and scientific insight, and the lack of human evaluation or pixel-level feedback weakens the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Text-to-image (T2I) generation models consistently fail at faithfully composing scenes with multiple objects — misplacing items, miscounting, and conflating attributes. Prior approaches fall into two inadequate camps: single-agent methods that burden one model with parsing, planning, and verification (making early spatial errors unrecoverable), and multi-agent pipelines that are fixed and sequential without visual grounding or iterative negotiation. The core challenges are threefold: (1) global layout planning over all objects simultaneously scales quadratically in relational complexity, (2) planning without access to a partially-rendered canvas forces models to hallucinate spatial context, and (3) diffusion models commit to coarse global structure early in denoising with no explicit error-correction loop thereafter. Methodology: coDrawAgents is a training-free, plug-and-play multi-agent dialogue framework with four specialized roles that engage in closed-loop interaction. The Interpreter (LLM with chain-of-thought) first classifies the prompt as simple or compositionally complex, routing simple cases directly to a T2I painter (layout-free mode). For complex scenes it decomposes the prompt into attribute-rich object descriptors, ranks them by semantic salience, and groups equal-priority objects for joint processing. The Planner (GPT-5 as MLLM) implements a divide-and-conquer strategy via a novel Visual Chain-of-Thought (VCoT): at each iteration it receives the global prompt, current-priority object descriptions, prior layouts, the partially-rendered canvas, and object grounding correspondences. VCoT proceeds through three reasoning steps — canvas state analysis, context-aware planning, and physics constraint enforcement — to incrementally propose layouts grounded in the actual evolving scene rather than imagination. The Checker performs a two-stage check-then-refine at each iteration: first validating size/scale/placement at both object and global levels, then reviewing all prior layouts cross-iteration to catch overlaps, occlusion ordering errors, and scale drift. The Painter uses Flux (T2I) in layout-free mode and 3DIS-Flux (L2I) in layout-aware mode, incrementally compositing newly confirmed objects onto the canvas to supply visual context for subsequent rounds. Agents iterate once per semantic priority level, and the final canvas is the generated image. Key Results: On GenEval, coDrawAgents achieves an overall score of 0.94, surpassing GPT Image 1 [High] (0.84), UniWorld-V1 (0.80), and SD3-Medium (0.74). Gains are largest on the hardest sub-tasks: Position 0.95 vs. next-best 0.75, Counting 0.94 vs. 0.85, Color Attribution 0.81 vs. 0.70. On DPG-Bench (dense multi-object prompts), coDrawAgents achieves 85.17 overall, topping DALL-E 3 (83.50), FLUX.1-dev (83.84), and OmniGen2 (83.57), with particularly strong Relation score (92.92). Ablation on DPG-Bench isolates each contribution: layout-free baseline scores 77.60; adding layout-aware planning raises this to 82.61; adding visual context grounding to 84.51; adding the Checker yields the final 85.17. Efficiency analysis shows average agent invocations of 1.00/1.52/1.62/1.95 (Interpreter/Planner/Checker/Painter) per image despite an average of 2.79 objects, demonstrating that semantic grouping substantially reduces calls. Strengths: - Closed-loop multi-agent dialogue with visual grounding is a principled solution to the three identified failure modes of prior work; the VCoT physics constraint module is a non-trivial contribution for preventing physically implausible placements. - Plug-and-play design (any T2I and L2I model can be swapped in without retraining) gives the framework a long runway as base models improve. - Ablation study cleanly decomposes the contribution of each component, and the efficiency analysis (Table 3) provides evidence that semantic grouping keeps inference costs competitive despite iterative multi-agent overhead. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework depends critically on GPT-5 for the Planner's VCoT; substituting weaker MLLMs would degrade spatial reasoning quality significantly, and the closed-source dependency limits reproducibility and cost transparency. - Error accumulation across iterations remains a real risk: the Checker operates on layout coordinates without re-rendering, so it may approve placements that look correct as bounding boxes but produce visual artifacts after L2I synthesis; there is no pixel-level feedback loop. - Evaluation relies entirely on MLLM judges (GenEval, DPG-Bench scoring protocol), which share failure modes with the Planner/Checker themselves; human perceptual studies or adversarially chosen prompts testing unusual attribute combinations are absent.
Citations 2603.12829 PDF
#130 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Sink-Aware Pruning for Diffusion Language Models

Aidar Myrzakhan, Tianyi Li, Bowei Guo, Shengkun Tang, Zhiqiang Shen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The observation that attention sinks in diffusion language models exhibit high temporal variance — contrasting sharply with AR models — is a genuine and previously overlooked insight supported by clean quantitative analysis. However, the resulting method is a straightforward reweighting of existing pruning scores, and absolute accuracy gains are often sub-1 percentage point, raising questions about practical significance outside aggressive compression regimes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) generate text via iterative denoising over multiple timesteps, incurring substantially higher inference cost than autoregressive (AR) LLMs. Existing post-training pruning methods for LLMs — notably Wanda and SparseGPT — were designed for AR models and implicitly assume that attention sink tokens (a small set of positions absorbing disproportionate attention mass) are stable, structurally critical anchors that must be preserved. This paper challenges that assumption: it shows that sink positions in DLMs shift substantially across denoising timesteps (high temporal variance), whereas AR sinks are spatially concentrated and temporally stable. Naively applying AR-derived "always keep sinks" heuristics to DLMs thus misidentifies transient, functionally marginal positions as essential, wasting pruning budget. Methodology: The paper introduces two complementary variance statistics to quantify sink dynamics: spatial variance (how unevenly attention concentrates across positions on average over the trajectory) and temporal variance (how much the attention-weighted centroid of the sink set shifts across diffusion timesteps). Empirically, DLMs exhibit temporal variance orders of magnitude higher than AR models (e.g., LLaDA: 0.0173 vs. Llama3.1: 3.4e-6), confirming sinks are transient in diffusion generation. Building on this, the authors propose Sink-Aware Pruning: a three-step modification applied on top of existing pruning criteria (Wanda or SparseGPT). First, per-token attention mass is aggregated across all layers and heads at each diffusion timestep over a calibration set. Second, a soft sink score is computed via a sigmoid over how much each token's attention mass exceeds the mean, then averaged across a uniform set of timesteps to produce a step-invariant sink estimate. Third, a per-position down-weighting factor ω = 1 − φ̄(j) is applied to the input activations before computing importance scores, suppressing sink positions' contribution to Wanda's weight-activation product or SparseGPT's empirical Hessian. Pruning then proceeds identically to the base method. The approach requires no retraining and adds only lightweight calibration overhead. Key Results: Sink-Aware Pruning is evaluated on LLaDA-8B, Dream-7B, LLaDA-1.5-8B, and MMaDA-8B across 8 benchmarks (MMLU, ARC-C, PIQA, WinoGrande, GSM8K, HellaSwag, GPQA, RACE) at sparsity levels of 25%, 50%, and 75% for unstructured pruning, and at structured pruning ratios of 0.3 and 0.5. For unstructured pruning on LLaDA at 50% sparsity, Sink-Aware improves over Wanda baseline by +0.92 pp average accuracy (53.18 vs. 52.70) and over SparseGPT by +0.18 pp (52.36→52.54). Gains are most pronounced at 75% sparsity: on LLaDA, Sink-Aware (Wanda) achieves 30.94 avg vs. 29.99 for Wanda (+0.95 pp) and 32.57 vs. 32.57 for SparseGPT. On LLaDA-1.5 at 75% sparsity with Wanda, the gain reaches +1.86 pp. For structured pruning on LLaDA at ratio 0.5, Sink-Aware achieves PIQA 0.6037, WinoG 0.5724, ARC-E 0.5279, ARC-C 0.2362 vs. the LLaDA-structure baseline of 0.5898/0.5572/0.4853/0.2039. The method consistently matches or exceeds baselines across all models and sparsity levels; gains are smaller at 25% sparsity and larger at moderate-to-high sparsity where misidentifying critical weights is most costly. Strengths: - Identifies a genuine and previously overlooked mismatch between AR-derived pruning heuristics and DLM attention dynamics, supported by rigorous quantitative variance analysis across multiple model families. - The method is a clean, lightweight plug-in correction to existing pruning pipelines (Wanda, SparseGPT) with no architectural changes, no retraining, and negligible added computation beyond calibration. - Evaluation is thorough: four DLM backbones, eight diverse benchmarks, three sparsity levels for unstructured pruning, two for structured, with consistent comparisons under identical calibration data. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Gains are often modest in absolute terms (fractions of a percentage point), particularly at low sparsity (25%), raising questions about practical significance in non-aggressive compression regimes. - Sink statistics are estimated from a fixed calibration distribution (WikiText-2); distribution shift could reduce reliability, and the sensitivity of sink identification to calibration data choice is not studied. - The paper evaluates only post-training pruning without recovery fine-tuning; combining sink-aware scoring with lightweight post-pruning adaptation (e.g., LoRA) is left as future work, and the method's interaction with quantization is unexplored.
Recommended 2602.17664 PDF
#131 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Tell as You Want: Customizing Image Narrative with Knowledge and Thoughts

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: T⁴ defines a reasonable new task (customized image narratives with expertise-level adaptation) and achieves competitive results against larger closed-source models with a well-structured pipeline. However, each component — two-stage RAG, structured CoT, expertise-level prompting — is individually well-established, and the combination, while effective, does not produce surprising synergies. The coarse three-level personalization lacks quantitative evaluation, and the synthetic training data bounds generalization.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard image captioning models describe visible surface-level content (objects, attributes, spatial relationships) but fail to provide the deeper contextual knowledge users often need—fine-grained taxonomic categories, functional properties, historical context, or domain-specific facts. Moreover, current systems produce fixed outputs with no adaptation to user expertise level or specific interests. This limits utility in real applications like museum exhibit guides, children's education, or expert-facing tools. The paper addresses both gaps: knowledge depth and personalization. Methodology: The authors introduce "customized image narrative generation" as a new task, and propose T⁴ (Tailor, reTrieve, Think, Tell), a four-stage cascade built on Llama-3.2-11B-Vision with full-parameter fine-tuning. (1) Tailor: Given an image, topic, user expertise level (Kid/Student/Scholar), and optional text or visual region prompts (bounding boxes, points), a query predictor generates candidate questions the user is likely to ask. Knowledge base passages appropriate to the user's expertise level are retrieved first to reduce hallucination in unfamiliar domains. (2) Retrieve: Two-stage RAG—coarse document retrieval via EVA-CLIP image embeddings (cosine similarity), followed by fine-grained passage retrieval using the multimodal retriever PreFLMR conditioned on both image and query. (3) Think and Tell: A three-stage chain-of-thought generation: a Captioning stage describing query-relevant image content, a Sub-QA stage decomposing the query into sequential sub-questions (beginning with fine-grained entity identification), and a Conclusion stage synthesizing a fluent narrative. Structured XML-like tags delimit DESCRIPTION, ENTITY, ANSWER, and reasoning stages. Training data (ImgNarr-23K, ~23K samples from InfoSeek and Encyclopedic-VQA) was synthetically generated using Gemini-2.5-Flash. The knowledge base uses Wikipedia passages rewritten by GPT-4o at three reading levels. Key Results: On the ImgNarr-23K narrative generation benchmark, T⁴ achieves 47.4/56.1 (QA/Entity) on InfoSeek and 39.3/28.1 on E-VQA, outperforming Gemini-2.5-Flash+RAG (44.9/51.6 and 39.4/17.9) and all open-source baselines. On knowledge-based VQA (zero-shot transfer, no additional fine-tuning), T⁴ achieves 44.9% on InfoSeek and 39.3% on E-VQA, surpassing the prior SoTA ReflectiVA (40.1% and 35.5%) despite using the same retriever stack. With ground-truth documents, T⁴ reaches 58.4% (InfoSeek) and 73.4% (E-VQA), matching or exceeding ReflectiVA's oracle performance. Ablations confirm both CoT and RAG contribute independently, with their combination yielding best results; fine-tuning further adds substantial gains over zero-shot RAG. Strengths: - The two-stage retrieval (image-level document retrieval → query-conditioned passage retrieval) is well-motivated and empirically validated; using EVA-CLIP for coarse retrieval and PreFLMR for fine-grained reranking is a principled design. - The structured three-stage CoT (Caption → Sub-QA → Conclusion) with tagged outputs enables clean extraction for downstream VQA without additional fine-tuning, demonstrating strong task transfer. - Achieves competitive or superior performance to much larger closed-source models (Gemini-2.5-Flash) using only ~10K training samples and an 11B-parameter open model. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The ImgNarr-23K dataset is synthetically generated by Gemini-2.5-Flash from ground-truth documents, meaning training data quality and diversity are bounded by the teacher model and the two source datasets (InfoSeek, E-VQA). Generalization to truly open-domain or out-of-distribution specialized domains is unverified. - User expertise personalization is coarse (three levels) and evaluated only qualitatively; there is no quantitative benchmark measuring whether generated narratives are actually appropriate for Kids vs. Scholars, nor user studies confirming perceived utility. - The knowledge base must be pre-constructed with level-appropriate documents, which requires either pre-existing level-tagged corpora or GPT-4o rewrites at scale—a non-trivial deployment cost that may limit applicability in new specialized domains.
Search Results
#132 — Moderately Innovative (48)

From Representation to Clusters: A Contrastive Learning Approach for Attributed Hypergraph Clustering

Li Ni, Shuaikang Zeng, Lin Mu, Longlong Lin · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 48 because end-to-end contrastive clustering is a well-explored paradigm for regular graphs, and CAHC extends it to hypergraphs with a hyperedge-level contrastive loss — a reasonable but largely expected contribution. The negative sampling strategy's failure on large hyperedges reveals a structural limitation that the paper acknowledges but does not resolve.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Attributed hypergraph clustering seeks to partition nodes into groups using both hypergraph structure (high-order relationships via hyperedges) and node features, without supervision. Existing contrastive learning methods decouple this into two stages: first learn node embeddings, then apply k-means. This two-stage pipeline lacks direct clustering supervision during representation learning, allowing clustering-irrelevant features to pollute the embeddings, which degrades downstream cluster quality. The paper addresses this gap by proposing CAHC, the first end-to-end attributed hypergraph clustering method that simultaneously learns representations and cluster assignments. Methodology: CAHC has two sequential stages. In Stage 1 (representation learning), two augmented views of the hypergraph are generated via node feature masking and membership relation masking (Bernoulli-sampled binary masks). A shared multi-head attention-based hypergraph neural network (HGNN) encodes both views into node embeddings. The encoder is trained with two complementary contrastive objectives: a hyperedge-level loss that maximizes similarity for nodes connected by real hyperedges while minimizing it for artificially constructed negative hyperedges (formed by randomly replacing nodes), and a node-level NT-Xent loss that pulls together representations of the same node across views. In Stage 2 (cluster assignment learning), k-means initializes cluster centroids from the Stage 1 embeddings. The model then jointly optimizes representation learning and a clustering loss that minimizes the discrepancy between soft cluster assignments (cosine similarity-based membership degrees) and hard pseudo-labels (argmax of soft assignments).
