For each row i, the Euclidean norm ||a_i|| is computed and a dynamic threshold θ_i = max(τ||a_i||, ε) is derived from a relative drop tolerance τ and absolute floor ε=10^{-12}. All off-diagonal entries below θ_i are discarded; the top 2×maxfill survivors per row are retained with diagonal preservation enforced. This produces a filtered intermediate matrix A' that is sparser than the original Jacobian. A' is then factorized using standard ILU(0)—which is stable because numerically unstable small entries have been removed—yielding approximate L̃Ũ factors used as a left preconditioner for BiCGSTAB. The entire pipeline is implemented in CSR format with OpenMP parallelization; the two-phase filtering design (size determination then population) enables embarrassingly parallel row-wise execution. SPIRIT is integrated into the SkyNet nuclear network code as a drop-in solver. Key tuned parameters for r-process: τ=10^{-32}, maxfill=100, ε=10^{-12}, BiCGSTAB tolerance=10^{-13}. Key Results: On 500 r-process Jacobians (7836×7836), SPIRIT reduces average BiCGSTAB iterations to ~5 with residuals below 10^{-6}, versus hundreds of iterations and residuals of ~7–10 for ILUT/ILU(0). Preconditioning time drops from 314–737 ms (ILUT/ILU(0)) to 35–45 ms for SPIRIT. In full SkyNet r-process simulations, total runtime falls from 400 s (MKL) to 163 s with SPIRIT(BiCGSTAB)—a 2.45× speedup—while the matrix inversion fraction drops from 67.7% to 16.3%. SPIRIT(BiCGSTAB) outperforms Pardiso (589 s) by 3.6× and Trilinos/KLU (9062 s) by 55×. Final abundance accuracy is excellent: mean-normalized abundance error of 8.7×10^{-7} and log-ratio metric F=4.05×10^{-5} over A=120–210 (the astrophysically critical mass window), comparable to MKL-vs-Pardiso agreement. Deviations appear only for A>250, where r-process contributions are astrophysically minor. The GMRES-coupled variant (212 s total) performs worse in accuracy (mean error 3.42×10^{-3}) despite similar per-iteration speed, making BiCGSTAB the recommended pairing. For smaller test cases (trivial networks, matrix solver), SPIRIT is competitive; for the X-ray burst (686×686, 97.5% sparse), SPIRIT is slower than MKL and shows larger deviations (MAE 3.2×10^{-4} vs MKL's 7.67×10^{-10}). Strengths: - Novel pre-factorization filtering with row-norm-derived dynamic thresholds achieves stability where ILU(0) diverges and outperforms ILUT by orders of magnitude, addressing a genuine gap in preconditioner design for ill-conditioned astrophysical systems. - Demonstrated 2.5× end-to-end speedup over the incumbent MKL solver in production SkyNet runs, with physical fidelity (abundance errors ~10^{-6}) far below the inherent nuclear physics uncertainties—directly enabling large-scale Monte Carlo uncertainty quantification campaigns. - Open-source (GPL v3), embarrassingly parallel CSR implementation with simple tuning, and broad applicability beyond nucleosynthesis to any large sparse ill-conditioned linear system. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance degrades for moderately sparse, smaller systems: the X-ray burst case (686×686, 97.5% sparse) runs slower than MKL and Pardiso and shows significantly elevated abundance errors (MAE 3.2×10^{-4}), suggesting the dual-threshold filter is tuned for the extreme sparsity (~99.76%) of r-process Jacobians and does not generalize cleanly. - The GMRES-coupled variant exhibits poor accuracy (mean error 3.42×10^{-3}, log-ratio 0.101) despite acceptable runtime, limiting SPIRIT to BiCGSTAB pairings in practice and reducing solver flexibility. - Benchmarks were conducted on a modest laptop (AMD Ryzen 5 3550H, 4 cores, 8 GB RAM) without GPU utilization, and no strong- or weak-scaling study across HPC node counts is presented, leaving scalability to large parallel architectures uncharacterized. - The additional Newton-Raphson iterations induced by SPIRIT's looser internal convergence partially offsets the matrix inversion speedup (theoretical ~155 s vs. actual ~163 s), and this interaction with SkyNet's time-step control logic is specific to that code's convergence criteria, potentially complicating portability to other network solvers.
Search Results
#147 — Moderately Innovative (44)

F2LLM-v2: Inclusive, Performant, and Efficient Embeddings for a Multilingual World (21 ▲ )

Ziyin Zhang, Zihan Liao, Hang Yu, Peng Di, Rui Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 44 as a solid multilingual embedding effort with commendable openness and breadth (282 languages), but the methodology — contrastive learning, structured pruning, knowledge distillation, MRL — is entirely composed of established techniques. The gains are concentrated on underserved language leaderboards with few competitors, and the model underperforms frontier systems on the most-tracked benchmarks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Text embedding models are fundamental to semantic search, RAG, classification, and clustering. The field has shifted from encoder-based (BERT, XLM-R) to decoder-based LLM embeddings, but two critical gaps persist. First, an English-centric bias dominates both training data and benchmark evaluation — the MTEB-Multilingual benchmark has 35 of 131 tasks in English alone, and many language-specific leaderboards are essentially empty (the Polish benchmark had a single model with complete results before this work). Second, a transparency gap exists: top performers like Gemini-Embedding and Qwen3-Embedding are closed-source or open-weight without disclosed training data or methodology, hindering reproducibility. F2LLM-v2 directly addresses both gaps by building a fully open, linguistically inclusive family of embedding models. Methodology: F2LLM-v2 trains 8 models (80M to 14B) on a curated corpus of 60 million samples from 157 publicly available sources, spanning 282 natural languages and 40+ programming languages — with deliberate emphasis on underserved mid- and low-resource languages rather than benchmark-targeted data. All model architectures are based on the Qwen3 decoder transformer, using the final EOS token hidden state as the sequence representation. Training follows a two-stage pipeline: Stage 1 uses 27M samples from 7 large-scale retrieval datasets (without instructions) to build a semantic foundation; Stage 2 uses 18M samples with task-specific instruction prefixes applied to queries to sharpen performance on classification, reranking, and paraphrase detection. Data is consolidated into three canonical formats — retrieval (query, positive, hard negatives), clustering (anchor, same-class positive, hard negative), and two-way classification — enabling a unified contrastive learning objective. Hard negatives are mined using Qwen3-Embedding-8B. The three smallest models (80M, 160M, 330M) are obtained by structured pruning of the 0.6B model along hidden size, MLP intermediate size, and number-of-layers dimensions, using activation norms on calibration data for row/column pruning and simple prefix retention for layer pruning. Pruned models are then trained with an MSE-based embedding distillation loss from a teacher (the immediately larger model), which is also applied to the 0.6B and 1.7B models in Stage 2. Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) is applied throughout both stages, with minimum dimension 8, enabling flexible embedding truncation at inference time. Key Results: F2LLM-v2-14B achieves state-of-the-art on 11 of 17 evaluated MTEB benchmarks (including European, Scandinavian, Indic, German, Polish, Japanese, Dutch, Persian, and Vietnamese), and ranks in the top 10 on all 17. Smaller models also excel: F2LLM-v2-0.6B scores 66.47 average across 17 benchmarks versus Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B at 64.02 — a +2.45 gap — while F2LLM-v2-330M (64.41 avg) substantially outperforms EmbeddingGemma-0.3B (59.55 avg). The MRL ablation shows the 330M model at its full 896-dimensional embedding is competitive with 8B/14B models truncated to 32 dimensions, enabling practitioners to dynamically trade off storage and compute. Knowledge distillation consistently improves pruned models: the 80M model gains +4.67 points (58.04 vs. 53.37), and benefits are observed across all five distilled model sizes. The code benchmark is a particular strength, with all sizes outperforming similarly-sized Qwen3-Embedding and EmbeddingGemma models. Strengths: - Genuinely broad multilingual coverage driven by real-world data availability (282 natural languages with meaningful representation) rather than benchmark optimization, setting new SOTA on 10 language-specific leaderboards that were previously almost uncontested - Fully open release — models, training code, data, and intermediate checkpoints — addressing the transparency gap that characterizes most frontier embedding research - Efficient model family via principled pruning + knowledge distillation: the 80M model achieves 58.04 average score, and MRL enables continuous performance-cost tradeoffs without retraining Weaknesses / Limitations: - Despite the breadth of coverage, English still dominates the training corpus at 28.7% of samples, and the long tail of low-resource languages (many with only 800 samples) is unlikely to yield strong per-language performance; the paper does not report per-language accuracy for these truly low-resource cases - Knowledge distillation is not applied to the three largest models (4B, 8B, 14B) due to resource constraints, leaving open whether it would further improve performance at scale; the comparison between distilled and non-distilled larger models is therefore absent - The 14B model ranks only 6th on the core MTEB-Multilingual benchmark (score 68.74) and 10th on English (73.08), well behind models like Gemini-Embedding and Qwen3-Embedding on the high-resource benchmarks that the community most tracks — the gains are concentrated in underserved language leaderboards with few competing submissions
Recommended 2603.19223 PDF
#148 — Moderately Innovative (44)

GASN: a graph attention and self-attention network for blockchain service reliability prediction

link.springer.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 44 for combining GATv2 dynamic attention with self-attention interaction modeling for blockchain QoS prediction. The theoretical grounding of GATv2's advantage over static GAT attention is a meaningful contribution, but the approach is a relatively standard combination of existing GNN components applied to a small dataset (100×200), and performance gains are inconsistent across conditions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As Blockchain as a Service (BaaS) platforms proliferate in cloud environments, users face the challenge of selecting reliable peer nodes from thousands of candidates. Quality of Service (QoS) prediction—specifically predicting the success rate of user-peer interactions—is the core technical problem. The fundamental difficulty is extreme data sparsity: users interact with only a tiny fraction of available peers, leaving the reliability matrix largely unobserved. Existing collaborative filtering, matrix factorization, and GNN-based approaches struggle under high sparsity because they fail to propagate informative features across weakly connected graph structures or capture the complex nonlinear, context-dependent dependencies between users and peers. Methodology: GASN is a two-stage deep learning framework that decouples graph-based representation learning from interaction modeling. In the first stage, two heterogeneous graphs are constructed—a User-View Graph and a Peer-View Graph—where nodes include users/peers as well as geographic context nodes (region, autonomous system). These graphs are processed by a multi-layer GATv2 attention mechanism. The key architectural innovation is a "swapped-order" attention computation: the linear transformation is applied before the nonlinearity (LeakyReLU), rather than after, producing dynamic (query-dependent) attention weights as opposed to the static weights of standard GAT. This is formally shown to be a universal approximator over neighbor ranking functions. The final embedding from the last graph attention layer is taken as the representation. In the second stage, user and peer embeddings are concatenated and fed into a multi-head self-attention module (with MLP pre-processing, layer normalization, and dropout) to model fine-grained cross-feature interactions, replacing the inner-product or MLP interaction layers used in prior work. A fully connected layer then produces the predicted success rate. The model is trained end-to-end with L1 loss and Adam optimization. Key Results: Experiments use a 100×200 user-peer interaction dataset spanning 21 countries with over 2 million service records. Four QoS constraint scenarios (varying MaxBlockBack and MaxRTT) and four sparsity levels (1%–4% observed entries) are evaluated. GASN consistently achieves the lowest MAE and RMSE among nine baselines including memory-based CF (UPCC, IPCC, UIPCC), matrix factorization (PMF, MF), neural CF (MNCF), and GNN-based methods (GraphMF, QoSGNN, RIGCN). The reported aggregate improvement over the best baselines is up to 32.6% reduction in MAE and 22.9% in RMSE. Gains are most pronounced at 1%–2% density. For example, on the 100–5000 dataset at 1% density, GASN achieves MAE=0.1691 vs. the best baseline QoSGNN at 0.1917 (11.8% reduction); at 2% density MAE=0.1005 vs. RIGCN at 0.1492 (32.6% reduction). Ablation studies confirm each component contributes: self-attention outperforms inner-product interaction especially under sparsity, GATv2 outperforms vanilla GAT, and multi-layer attention outperforms single-layer. Hyperparameter analysis shows optimal performance at 2 attention layers and embedding dimension d=32, beyond which over-smoothing and overfitting degrade results. Strengths: - The GATv2 "swapped-order" design is theoretically grounded: the paper proves standard GAT produces static attention (neighbor rankings are query-independent), while GATv2 achieves dynamic attention that adapts per query node, which is particularly important for personalized reliability prediction. - The two-stage decoupled design—graph representation followed by self-attention interaction—is well-motivated and empirically validated; each component contributes meaningfully per the ablation study. - Evaluation is thorough: 9 baselines spanning multiple paradigms, 4 datasets with 4 sparsity levels each, plus ablation and hyperparameter sensitivity analysis. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is extremely small by modern standards (100 users × 200 peers), which makes it hard to assess whether GASN would scale or maintain its advantage on graphs with millions of nodes; the reported complexity analysis is theoretical and not benchmarked empirically in terms of wall-clock training time against baselines. - The graph is modeled as static, ignoring temporal dynamics of blockchain networks where peer availability and network conditions change continuously—a limitation the authors acknowledge but do not address. - Performance improvements on several conditions (e.g., 12–1000 at 4% density: MAE 0.0909 vs. RIGCN 0.0893) are not consistent wins; in a few settings GASN is outperformed on RMSE, suggesting the model may not robustly dominate under all conditions despite headline claims.
Search Results
#149 — Moderately Innovative (43)

Semantics for Smart Building Systems

pure.tue.nl · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Combines semantic web standards (Brick/SHACL/SPARQL) with MPC-based EV charging in a real deployed system, which is a competent engineering integration. However, the individual components — ontology-based building data access, microservice MPC decomposition, and smart charging optimisation — are well-established in the literature. Portability is validated on only one real building, and poor forecasting accuracy undermines the quantitative claims without a sensitivity analysis.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Smart buildings increasingly deploy data-driven energy management applications such as Model Predictive Control (MPC)-based Demand Side Management (DSM). However, two fundamental barriers impede at-scale deployment: (i) a data accessibility problem — heterogeneous building management systems (BMS, SCADA, IoT sensors) expose data through incompatible schemas and proprietary interfaces; and (ii) an application portability problem — each building has unique system configurations, making it expensive and time-consuming to re-deploy control applications across buildings. Prior work on portable building applications focused exclusively on simple rule-based analytics, leaving the far more complex MPC-based control domain unaddressed. This PhD thesis directly attacks both barriers with a semantic web-based solution. Methodology: The thesis proposes a three-part framework. First, a Reference System Architecture integrates heterogeneous data sources via standardised metadata schemas (extensions of the Brick ontology using OWL/RDF) and a uniform SPARQL + REST interface for querying both time-series sensor data and semantic graph data; the architecture follows a service-oriented pattern validated through a full end-to-end implementation in a real TU/e campus building. Second, a Semantics-Driven Portability Service uses SHACL shape constraints to automatically validate whether a target building's semantic model satisfies the data requirements of a specific control algorithm, and then auto-configures the data retrieval bindings (SPARQL queries) at deployment time — removing reliance on building-specific naming conventions or custom integration code. Third, an MPC-based Supervisory Controller for EV Smart Charging is decomposed into seven modular REST microservices (load forecasting, PV forecasting, state estimation, optimisation, scheduling, real-time feedback, and building interface), each capable of semantic self-configuration. The optimisation formulates a constrained quadratic programme over a rolling 24-hour horizon with 10-minute intervals, minimising grid peak demand subject to contractual demand limits, EV departure times and state-of-charge targets, and PV self-consumption. The system integrates with a live BMS via MQTT and REST. Key Results: The MPC controller was deployed in a real PV- and EV-integrated office building at TU/e and validated over three experimental periods spanning January–August 2025. Key quantitative findings: (1) Peak Power Reduction (PPR): 16.2% mean PPR in experimental controlled charging vs. uncontrolled baseline; simulation under tighter contractual demand constraints (41 kW vs. 61 kW) shows PPR up to 45%. (2) Economic savings: simulation demonstrates cost reductions from energy arbitrage and demand charge avoidance, with controlled charging favouring off-peak and PV-abundant periods. (3) EV charging fulfilment: the controller achieves near-complete energy delivery against targets, with shortfall mainly caused by early unannounced departures or EV battery management system overrides capping current below the planned setpoint. (4) Forecasting accuracy: building load MAE = 2.64 kW (MAPE 27.24%); PV production MAE = 1.32 kW (MAPE 37.01%) — acknowledged as limiting factors for optimisation quality. (5) Semantic portability: the portability service successfully validates and auto-configures the real building and a synthetic virtual building, generating human-readable SHACL violation reports that identify missing sensors or incomplete timeseries references before deployment. Strengths: - End-to-end real-world deployment and longitudinal experimental validation (months of live operation across three test periods) rather than purely simulation-based results — rare in the MPC-for-buildings literature. - The modular microservice decomposition with semantic self-configuration cleanly separates the optimisation logic from building-specific plumbing, providing a credible and deployable portability mechanism that goes significantly beyond prior metadata-standardisation approaches. - Rigorous use of semantic web standards (OWL, SHACL, SPARQL, Brick ontology extensions) gives the framework a solid interoperability foundation aligned with emerging industry standards. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Semantic portability is validated on only one real building plus one synthetic virtual building; demonstrating transfer to structurally different building types (residential, HVAC-heavy, multi-zone) is deferred to future work, leaving generalisation claims largely unsubstantiated. - Forecasting accuracy is poor (MAPE >27% for load, >37% for PV), which the authors acknowledge degrades optimisation quality but do not quantify how much of the achievable peak reduction is lost to forecast error — a missing sensitivity analysis. - The MPC supports only unidirectional EV charging; Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) and other flexible loads (heat pumps, HVAC, BESS) are explicitly out of scope, limiting the system to a narrow DSM use case compared to comprehensive building energy management.
