#150 — Moderately Innovative (42)

Large Language Models for Graph Reasoning: Capabilities, Limitations and Future Directions

authorea.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The four-paradigm taxonomy and 2×2 limitation framework provide useful organizational structure for the rapidly growing Graph-LLM literature, and the post-2025 temporal focus captures emerging directions missed by prior surveys. However, this is a survey with no original experiments — all quantitative claims are synthesized from heterogeneous cited evaluations — and structural quality issues (duplicate tables, missing affiliations, no peer review) weaken its authority as a reference work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph-structured data underlies critical scientific and industrial systems—social networks with 1.8 trillion edges, knowledge graphs with 100+ billion triplets, drug-target interaction networks—yet the dominant paradigm (GNNs) suffers from two compounding failures: limited semantic depth (nodes are reduced to numerical vectors, losing linguistic context) and generalization rigidity (task-specific training does not transfer across graph distributions). LLMs offer emergent symbolic reasoning and zero-shot generalization, but the Graph-LLM integration landscape grew by 300%+ in publications between 2024 and late 2025 without a unifying analytical framework. This survey fills that gap by systematically analyzing 157 papers (2022–2026), characterizing both capabilities and fundamental architectural incompatibilities. Methodology: The paper contributes a four-paradigm taxonomy organized by the LLM's functional role and depth of modality alignment. LLM-as-Enhancer (68% of literature): GNN remains the primary engine; LLM generates semantic node embeddings fed into GNN (e.g., GraphSAGE). Modalities stay architecturally isolated. LLM-as-Predictor (~10%): Graph is fully linearized into the prompt; LLM reasons zero-shot via In-Context Learning, but context-window constraints and lack of topological priors limit performance on Level 3–4 tasks (cycle detection, isomorphism). Graph-LLM Alignment (~10%): Structural encodings are projected into the LLM's embedding space via cross-attention or soft prompts (e.g., GraphTranslator, UniGraph); requires costly joint training. Multi-Agent Graph Reasoning (12%): An Explorer Agent navigates via BFS/DFS tool APIs, a Reasoner Agent performs logical deduction on retrieved subgraphs, and a Verifier Agent cross-checks conclusions against raw topology—enabling reasoning over arbitrarily large graphs by decoupling exploration from inference. The survey also formalizes graph linearization strategies (adjacency list, edge list, natural language, GML), characterizes their structural fidelity vs. token efficiency tradeoffs, and introduces a 2×2 limitation taxonomy along Architectural/Representational × Local/Global axes. Key Results: LLM-enhanced node features improve classification accuracy 10–15% over Word2Vec on OGB-arXiv. GPT-4 achieves 60–70% zero-shot accuracy on graph isomorphism and cycle detection (NLGraph benchmark) without task-specific training. Chain-of-Thought prompting outperforms direct prompting by 20%+ in F1 on 2WikiMultiHopQA. Self-Consistency CoT reduces topological hallucinations by ~15%. Structural hallucination rates reach 15–40% on multi-hop benchmarks as graph density increases. Linearization format changes cause 20–35% accuracy swings on identical tasks—diagnostic evidence that LLMs process graphs as text patterns rather than structural objects. GraphRAG-style dual-stream architectures (SF-GPT) reduce structural hallucination rates by 40%+ vs. direct CoT baselines. Domain experts preferred LLM-generated explanations 2:1 over GNNExplainer subgraph masks in clinical decision support settings. Multi-agent systems incur 5–10× inference cost overhead vs. single-pass methods. Strengths: - The four-paradigm taxonomy is well-motivated and practically grounded, with clear architectural diagrams and systematic comparison tables covering structural fidelity, semantic capability, scalability, and training cost across all paradigms. - The 2×2 limitation framework (Architectural/Representational × Local/Global) is a genuine conceptual contribution that organizes disparate failure modes—Structural Hallucination, Permutation Sensitivity, Linearization Fragility, Scalability Wall—into a coherent analytical structure rather than an ad-hoc list. - The survey's temporal focus (post-2025) captures emerging directions (test-time scaling, multi-agent orchestration, equivariant adapters, GFMs) that prior surveys missed, and the research roadmap including TRL estimates and the Universal Graph Vocabulary problem provides actionable guidance. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The quantitative benchmarking is largely synthesized from cited papers rather than from original experiments; performance numbers are drawn from heterogeneous evaluation setups (different graph sizes, linearization formats, prompting strategies), making cross-paradigm comparisons unreliable and potentially reflecting format optimization rather than genuine capability differences—exactly the reproducibility crisis the paper itself identifies. - The paper lacks author affiliations and has not been peer-reviewed (posted as a preprint), and the writing exhibits structural inconsistencies (Table 2 appears twice with different content; Figure caption labels are mismatched), raising concerns about rigor and completeness that limit its authority as a definitive reference.
Search Results
#151 — Moderately Innovative (41)

98$\times $ Faster LLM Routing Without a Dedicated GPU: Flash Attention, Prompt Compression, and Near-Streaming for the vLLM Semantic Router

Xunzhuo Liu, Bowei He, Xue Liu, Andy Luo, Haichen Zhang, Huamin Chen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The 98× speedup is achieved through competent engineering composition of three known techniques — Flash Attention porting to AMD ROCm, classical NLP prompt compression, and streaming body handling — each targeting a distinct bottleneck. The surprising finding that compression improves classification accuracy via denoising is noteworthy. However, the individual stages are largely standard engineering rather than research contributions, and the benchmark methodology (N=10 runs, no confidence intervals) is weak.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM serving systems increasingly require system-level routing layers for safety classification, domain routing, and PII detection before requests reach GPU inference. These routers must add minimal latency while sharing GPU resources with vLLM inference instances rather than requiring a dedicated accelerator. Three compounding bottlenecks make this hard at long-context (8K–32K tokens): (1) standard scaled dot-product attention (SDPA) materializes an O(n²) attention mask—at 8K tokens, three concurrent classifiers need ~4.5 GB for attention masks alone, exceeding the ~718 MB left by vLLM; (2) CPU-based ONNX inference takes 4.9 s at 8K tokens; and (3) Envoy's BUFFERED body mode introduces O(n) serialization overhead that scales with payload size. Methodology: The paper presents three staged optimizations that compose multiplicatively. Stage 1 implements a custom CK Flash Attention operator for ONNX Runtime on AMD ROCm (gfx942/MI300X), filling a gap where ORT only ships FA kernels for NVIDIA CUDA. The approach rewrites ONNX graphs via pattern-matching on SDPA subgraphs, replacing them with a single CKFlashAttention node that calls AMD's Composable Kernel FMHA kernels. A key engineering challenge is mapping ModernBERT's alternating local (128-token sliding window) and global attention layers to CK's sliding-window parameters, and converting the O(n²) 2D attention mask to a 1D padding bias. This reduces memory from O(n²) to O(n). Stage 2 applies a neural-inference-free prompt compression pipeline combining four classical NLP signals—TextRank (graph centrality via PageRank), U-shaped position weighting parameterized as w_pos(i) = 1 - d·sin(πi/(n-1)), TF-IDF information density, and novelty scoring (inverse centroid similarity to surface outlier adversarial content)—into a composite score s(i) = 0.20·r_TR + 0.40·w_pos + 0.35·r_TF-IDF + 0.05·r_nov, compressing all inputs to ~512 tokens with no LLM or model call. Stage 3 implements a near-streaming Envoy ext proc body handler with a three-state machine (init → passthrough/accumulate): requests with an explicit model field bypass body inspection entirely via zero-copy gjson/sjson field extraction, while 'auto' model requests incrementally precompute NLP state during chunk delivery. Key Results: The three stages compose to a cumulative 98× improvement at 8K tokens: ONNX CPU baseline 4,918 ms → GPU+FA 127 ms (38.7×) → add compression 62 ms (2.0×) → add near-streaming 50 ms (1.2×). At 16K tokens the system achieves 108 ms end-to-end, a regime where CPU backends cannot operate. FA eliminates OOM failures at 8K–32K tokens where SDPA fails entirely, and provides 3.3× speedup at the 4K crossover point. Compression reduces 16K-token jailbreak classification from 126.6 ms to 10.4 ms (12×), with a compression overhead of only 19 ms vs. 240 ms saved. Surprisingly, compression improves classification accuracy: domain accuracy rises from 53.1% to 61.2% and PII detection from 78.5% to 92.4%, consistent with a denoising effect from the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon—though jailbreak detection decreases from 70.8% to 56.6% because compression prioritizes domain-representative over outlier sentences. Total GPU footprint stays under ~800 MB, enabling co-location with vLLM on the same GPU and eliminating the need for a dedicated accelerator (estimated savings of ~$281K/year for a 32-node cluster). Strengths: - The staged decomposition is principled and each optimization targets a distinct bottleneck; the multiplicative composition is clean and well-characterized across the full 500–32K token range. - The prompt compression finding that accuracy improves under compression is non-obvious and well-explained mechanistically (denoising via 'lost in the middle' effect), adding scientific interest beyond the latency contribution. - The CK Flash Attention ONNX integration fills a genuine gap in the AMD/ONNX Runtime ecosystem and is released open-source with prebuilt models on HuggingFace, making the contribution immediately deployable. - Cost analysis (Table XIV) concretely grounds the engineering motivation: co-location eliminates a ~$3,500–$10,100/year/node GPU line item. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark methodology is weak: N=10 sequential requests with only 2–3 independent runs is insufficient for statistical significance; no confidence intervals or variance analysis is reported beyond a vague '<3% coefficient of variation' claim. - Jailbreak detection degrades from 70.8% to 56.6% under compression—a meaningful drop for a security-critical signal—and the paper's claim that 'in production, jailbreak runs on the full uncompressed prompt' is inconsistently explained relative to the stated design where compression precedes all classifiers. - The accuracy evaluation corpus (384 Wikipedia-derived synthetic cases) is far removed from production distributions; real RAG payloads, code review contexts, and adversarial jailbreak inputs are substantially different, and the accuracy claims may not generalize. - Stage 1 is AMD ROCm-specific by necessity, limiting its novelty since NVIDIA deployments already have FA through cuDNN; Stages 2 and 3 are hardware-agnostic but are classical engineering optimizations rather than research contributions. - Prompt compression uses character-length heuristics rather than the actual model tokenizer for token budget control, acknowledged as a limitation but potentially impactful for precise boundary cases.
Search Results 2603.12646 PDF
#152 — Moderately Innovative (41)

BISTRO: a general purpose oracle for macroeconomic time series

bis.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: BISTRO demonstrates that domain-specific fine-tuning of an existing foundation model (MOIRAI) on macroeconomic data yields substantial improvements at longer horizons, and the conditional forecasting via covariate path conditioning is operationally useful for central banks. The contribution is primarily one of careful engineering and domain application rather than methodological novelty — fine-tuning a pre-trained model on domain data is standard practice — and the inability to support causal inference limits its value for the counterfactual policy analysis that is central banking's core use case.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional macroeconomic forecasting requires building a bespoke model for each task — specifying functional form, estimating parameters, and validating — all for a single variable or country context. This is labor-intensive and inflexible: every change in the variable set, sample length, or frequency demands a new modeling cycle. The success of large language models (LLMs) as general-purpose zero-shot learners motivates asking whether a single foundational model, trained once on a broad macroeconomic corpus, can serve as a flexible forecasting tool for central banks without task-specific re-estimation. Existing general-purpose time series foundational models (e.g., MOIRAI) are trained on heterogeneous data spanning healthcare, energy, and finance, making them poorly calibrated for the specific distributional characteristics of macroeconomic series, especially at longer horizons and with covariates. Methodology: BISTRO (BIS Time-series Regression Oracle) fine-tunes the MOIRAI transformer architecture — itself built on the patch-based adaptation of the attention mechanism from NLP — on the BIS macroeconomic dataset comprising 4,925 time series across 63 economies from 1970–2024 (training window 1984–2024). Two key architectural constraints differentiate BISTRO from MOIRAI: (1) frequency is fixed to daily with a patch size of 32 days, accommodating monthly/quarterly series via last-value-carry-forward to enforce real-time publication lag discipline; (2) fine-tuning targets macroeconomic series exclusively, enabling the attention weights to learn macro-relevant co-movement patterns (e.g., oil-inflation, policy rate-inflation). The model produces probabilistic forecasts over a user-specified prediction window (PDT) given a context window (CTX) of historical data. At inference, weights are frozen — no re-estimation occurs — making it a single-pass foundational forecaster. The workflow supports both unconditional (baseline) and conditional (scenario) forecasts, where future paths for covariates can be fixed to explore counterfactuals. Benchmarks are AR(1) and the unmodified MOIRAI. Key Results: The headline qualitative result is BISTRO's ability to anticipate the persistence of the 2021–22 US inflation surge: starting from June 2021, BISTRO correctly projects a high and sustained inflation trajectory while AR(1) and MOIRAI mechanically revert toward the historical mean. Quantitative evaluation uses relative RMSFE (R-RMSFE) against AR(1) across four held-out windows (1995, 2005, 2015, 2023–24) not in the training sample. For the US, BISTRO achieves R-RMSFE of 0.946 at 3-month, 0.804 at 6-month, and 0.481 at 12-month horizons (vs. MOIRAI's 1.241, 1.335, 1.349 at the same horizons) — a dramatic improvement especially at long horizons. Euro area results are similar directionally (0.898 at 12 months vs. MOIRAI's 1.689). Other advanced economies show mixed results closer to benchmark. EMEs show systematic improvement at medium term. In conditional scenario analysis with oil price shocks (−3%, +3%, +9%), BISTRO correctly captures non-linear, asymmetric pass-through to inflation (larger positive shocks have disproportionate effects; negative shocks have smaller pass-through), while MOIRAI's attention weights on oil prices are effectively zero, producing identical forecasts across all scenarios. The Taylor rule exercise shows BISTRO correctly infers that the inflation-policy rate link depends on the length of historical context provided (12-year low-inflation regime vs. 37-year multi-regime history), while MOIRAI is insensitive to context length. Strengths: - Domain-specific fine-tuning decisively outperforms the general-purpose MOIRAI at longer horizons (12-month R-RMSFE of 0.481 for the US vs. 1.349), validating the hypothesis that macro-specific training is necessary for meaningful covariate integration - No re-estimation at inference: the frozen-weight foundational model generalizes across countries, variables, and frequencies without any user-side parameter fitting, dramatically lowering deployment cost - Conditional forecasting via covariate path conditioning is operationally clean — researchers can impose scenario paths for any variable without modifying model structure, enabling straightforward scenario/stress analysis Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 2021–22 inflation surge evaluation is partially compromised by training sample overlap: the inflation series was in the training data (albeit in month-on-month form without an "inflation" label), making this a weaker out-of-sample test than advertised - BISTRO captures historical correlations only and explicitly cannot support causal/structural inference: conditioning on policy rates yields a spurious positive correlation with inflation (policy tightening appears inflationary), undermining its usefulness for the counterfactual policy exercises central banks most need - Performance for other advanced economies (e.g., UK, New Zealand in 2023–24) is notably weak, with large errors relative to AR(1), and results for EMEs at the 12-month horizon barely beat the benchmark (R-RMSFE 0.969), suggesting uneven generalization across institutional and data-quality contexts
Search Results
#153 — Moderately Innovative (41)

A dual-branch graph neural network architecture for drug-target binding affinity prediction

nature.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The dual-branch GCN+GraphSAGE architecture with JK pooling is a reasonable engineering combination, and the ablation study confirms each component contributes. However, the individual building blocks (GCN, GraphSAGE, JK networks) are all well-established, and the gains over single-branch baselines are marginal on the larger KIBA dataset. The absence of modern graph transformers and 3D-aware GNNs from the comparison pool makes it difficult to assess relevance against the current state of the art.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Predicting drug-target binding affinity (DTA) is a critical bottleneck in drug discovery, where traditional experimental pipelines take 13+ years and cost ~$2.6B per approved drug, with only 10–20% success rates. Existing computational GNN approaches rely on single-branch message-passing architectures or shallow/handcrafted molecular descriptors that struggle to capture the full structural and functional complexity of drug molecules. There is also a gap between benchmark-level performance and practical drug-repurposing utility — particularly for emerging threats like COVID-19. This paper proposes a dual-branch GNN that combines complementary neighborhood aggregation strategies to produce richer molecular embeddings. Methodology: The proposed model is a dual-branch molecular encoder that combines three GNN components: (1) a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) branch using symmetric normalized adjacency convolution, (2) a GraphSAGE branch using inductive neighborhood aggregation (mean/max/LSTM), and (3) a Jumping Knowledge (JK) layer implemented via bidirectional LSTM that adaptively pools node embeddings across all GNN layers to capture both shallow and deep structural representations. The outputs of the GCN and GraphSAGE branches are concatenated before being fed into the JK-LSTM, then passed through a sum+max readout and an MLP to produce the final molecule-level embedding. Drug molecules are represented as graphs via SMILES→RDKit conversion (atoms as nodes, bonds as edges with type labels). For target encoding, the Explainable Substructure Partition Fingerprint (ESPF) is used, which automatically identifies chemically meaningful substructures. The final affinity score is produced by a joint MLP classifier over the concatenated drug and target embeddings. The model is evaluated against 45 baseline combinations spanning 9 drug encoders (GCN, GIN variants, AttentiveFP, MPNN, Transformer, NeuralFP, MixHop) and 8 target encoders (AAC, CNN, Conjoint-triad, ESPF, etc.) on the Davis and KIBA kinase inhibitor datasets, using MSE, Pearson correlation, and Concordance Index (CI) as metrics. Key Results: On the Davis dataset, the proposed model with ESPF target encoding achieves the best composite score across all 46 evaluated combinations: MSE = 33.98 (vs. 35.24 for GCN-ESPF, the next best), Pearson = 76.49 (vs. 76.19), and CI = 85.41 (vs. 84.41). The worst-performing combination (Transformer-PseudoAAC) reaches MSE = 81.31, underscoring the critical role of encoding quality. On the larger, noisier KIBA dataset, the proposed model achieves MSE = 0.3564, Pearson = 71.92, and CI = 77.54 — competitive but not strictly first (NeuralFP-AAC achieves MSE = 0.3532), reflecting diminishing returns on larger benchmarks. An ablation study confirms that both the dual-branch drug encoder and ESPF target encoding contribute independently; substituting either degrades all three metrics. A COVID-19 drug repurposing case study against SARS-CoV-2 3CL-pro ranks Remdesivir (EUA-approved) and Glecaprevir highly, with Spearman ρ = 0.536 and Kendall τ = 0.401 agreement with AutoDock Vina docking scores, and an enrichment factor of 11.4 when identifying top-ranked compounds. Strengths: - The dual-branch design combining GCN (spectral) and GraphSAGE (inductive) with JK pooling is a principled architectural choice that captures complementary neighborhood information; ablation confirms each component contributes measurably. - Comprehensive empirical comparison against 45 baselines across two standard benchmarks provides strong evidence of advantage over individual GNN backbones and non-graph encoders. - The COVID-19 case study with cross-validation against physical docking (Spearman ρ = 0.536) adds translational credibility beyond benchmark metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance gains on KIBA are marginal (not strictly best on MSE) and well within standard deviation overlap with top baselines, raising questions about whether the architectural complexity is fully justified on large, noisy datasets. - The comparison baseline pool, while broad in encoding combinations, is limited to relatively dated GNN architectures; modern methods like graph transformers (e.g., Graphormer), 3D-geometry-aware GNNs (e.g., DimeNet++, SchNet), or pre-trained large molecular models are absent, making it unclear whether the gains hold against the current state of the art. - Only 15 training epochs and fixed hyperparameters across all methods may disadvantage some baselines; the lack of a held-out test set protocol (only cross-validated averages are reported) limits confidence in generalization claims. - The COVID-19 repurposing case study uses Imunovir as the top-ranked drug, but the paper itself notes it was not validated as a potent SARS-CoV-2 3CL-pro inhibitor in major studies, and the binding score comparison uses inconsistent scales (arbitrary model output vs. kcal/mol), weakening the translational argument.
Citations
#154 — Moderately Innovative (41)

Accelerating Suffix Jailbreak attacks with Prefix-Shared KV-cache

Xinhai Wang, Shaopeng Fu, Shu Yang, Liangyu Wang, Tianhang Zheng, Di Wang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 41 as a competent systems-level optimization that exploits an obvious structural redundancy — shared prefixes in suffix jailbreak attacks — with a clean lazy broadcasting mechanism. The practical impact is real (eliminates OOM failures, enables wider search), but the conceptual contribution is straightforward engineering rather than methodological novelty. The approach is narrowly scoped to static-prefix attacks and evaluated only on 7-8B models.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Suffix jailbreak attacks — which search for adversarial token sequences that cause LLMs to bypass safety guardrails — require evaluating thousands of candidate suffixes per run, making them prohibitively expensive. A naïve batch forward pass redundantly recomputes the same prefix KV vectors for every candidate; a standard KV cache avoids recomputation but must physically duplicate the prefix cache N times to match batch dimension, exploding memory and forcing small batch sizes. Production inference systems (vLLM, SGLang) address memory via paged/radix scheduling but are strictly forward-pass and lack gradient support, disqualifying them for white-box gradient-based attacks (GCG, AdvPrompter). No existing tool simultaneously supports gradient access and efficient prefix sharing. Methodology: The paper proposes Prefix-Shared KV Cache (PSKV), a plug-and-play PyTorch-native optimization that exploits a structural property of suffix attacks: all K·q candidate prompts in a batch share an identical prefix (the harmful instruction). PSKV computes the prefix KV cache exactly once before the attack loop begins, then applies a lazy, layer-wise expansion strategy: during each layer's forward pass it transiently broadcasts the singleton prefix cache to match the current batch size, uses it, and immediately discards the expansion. This keeps resident memory for the prefix at O(N_p) independent of batch size, versus O(N_cand · N_p) for standard duplication. For multi-instruction batched attacks with variable-length prefixes, PSKV introduces a suffix-centric alignment strategy: left-pad instructions and right-pad targets so all suffixes are column-aligned, enabling a single dense batched tensor operation and allowing prefix cache sharing across the entire B·K·q candidate dimension. The method requires no changes to attack logic and preserves gradient flow throughout. Key Results: Evaluated across six suffix attacks (GCG, GCQ, Zhu's AutoDAN, BEAST, AmpleGCG, AdvPrompter) on five 7–8B parameter LLMs (Vicuna-7B, Llama-2-7B, Llama-3-8B, Mistral-7B, Qwen2.5-7B) using HarmBench. PSKV achieves 1.4×–2.04× wall-clock speedup over the no-acceleration baseline, with the largest gains on Zhu's AutoDAN (up to 1.95×, e.g., 88→45 min on Llama-3-8B) due to that attack's large number of inference steps per iteration. Memory reduction vs. standard KV cache is 1.28×–3.68× for gradient-based attacks on MHA models (e.g., GCG on Vicuna: 100→27 GB), and prevents OOM failures that plague standard KV cache on GCQ, AutoDAN, and BEAST (which crash without PSKV). Attack Success Rate is preserved — minor fluctuations across conditions are within noise from stochastic search. On the gradient-free BEAST attack, PSKV reaches within ~50% of SGLang latency while retaining gradient support that SGLang lacks. Scalability experiments show peak memory stabilizes at ~40.8 GB for GCG on Llama-3 as search width scales 16→64, demonstrating that marginal memory cost per candidate is negligible. Strengths: - Identifies a concrete structural redundancy (shared prefix in suffix attacks) and exploits it with a clean, minimal mechanism — lazy layer-wise broadcasting — that neither changes attack logic nor requires framework modification. - Demonstrates genuine practical impact: eliminates OOM failures that make standard KV cache unusable for high-width beam/genetic search attacks, enabling broader search spaces previously infeasible on 80 GB A100s. - Covers both attack families (optimization-based and model-based) and five diverse architectures (MHA and GQA), with thorough analysis of why gains vary across model types (MHA vs. GQA attention, vocabulary size, dual-model AdvPrompter overhead). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach is restricted to attacks where the prefix is truly static across a batch; any attack that conditions the prefix on candidate-specific information would require redesign. - Speedup for AdvPrompter is modest (~1.3×–1.9×) because training overhead is not reduced — PSKV only accelerates the inference phase, and the paper does not address training-time KV redundancy. - The evaluation uses only 7–8B parameter models on two A100s; scalability behavior on larger models (30B+) or distributed multi-GPU setups with larger effective batch sizes is not characterized, leaving open how the layer-wise expansion overhead interacts with tensor parallelism.
Search Results 2603.13420 PDF
#155 — Moderately Innovative (41)

Unifying Ontology Construction and Semantic Alignment for Deterministic Enterprise Reasoning at Scale

preprints.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: This paper makes a genuine mathematical contribution by generalizing u-word shortening techniques from 2-dimensional to arbitrary d-dimensional permutations, overcoming non-trivial obstacles in extending twin permutation definitions. However, the core methodology (Eulerian path compression via incomparable elements) is a direct adaptation of existing 2D techniques rather than a fundamentally new approach, and the gap between achievable and optimal lengths remains uncharacterized.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: A universal word (u-word) for d-dimensional permutations of length n is a 2-dimensional word with d−1 rows such that every size-n window is order-isomorphic to exactly one d-dimensional n-permutation, and all such permutations are covered exactly once. The trivial u-word has length (n!)^(d−1) + n − 1, which grows factorially. The central question is whether shorter u-words exist by exploiting incomparable elements — entries that are treated as equal and resolved independently in each row via linear extensions of partially ordered sets. The paper extends prior work on shortening u-cycles and u-words for ordinary (2-dimensional) permutations to the general d-dimensional setting, filling a gap left by Kitaev, Potapov, and Vajnovszki (2019), whose techniques did not generalize directly to d > 2 due to obstacles around the definition of "twin permutations" in higher dimensions. Methodology: The key technical tool is incomparable elements introduced independently per row (rather than per column), which strictly generalizes the 2-dimensional approach and yields many more achievable lengths. The proof constructs a clustered graph of overlapping d-dimensional n-permutations, where clusters group permutations sharing the same (d−1)×(n−1) signature (reduced form of the leftmost n−1 columns). The graph is Eulerian by construction, and any Eulerian path corresponds to a u-word of length (n!)^(d−1) + n − 1. Compression is achieved by identifying i-twin classes — equivalence classes of permutations of "type i" (exactly i non-monotone rows differing by ±1 at the last position) within the same cluster. Each i-twin class of size 2^i contributes 2^i parallel edges in the clustered graph. Removing a cycle of length n−1 formed by 2^i parallel edges reduces the u-word length by n−1, while keeping the graph balanced and strongly connected (hence still Eulerian). The maximum achievable compression sums over all i from 1 to d−1, using the formula derived in Lemma 3.3 for the number of disjoint cycles by type. The resulting formula for the minimum achievable length is (n!)^(d−1) + n − 1 − i(n−1) for i up to (2^(d−1)/(n−1))·[(1+(n−1)!)^(d−1) − (1+(n−1)!/2)^(d−1)]. Key Results: The main result (Theorem 1.1) proves existence of u-words for d-dimensional n-permutations of length (n!)^(d−1) + n − 1 − i(n−1) for all valid i in the stated range. For d=2 this recovers the Kitaev–Potapov–Vajnovszki result exactly. Concrete examples are constructed: for 3-dimensional 3-permutations, a u-word of length 18 is exhibited under maximum compression (down from the trivial length of (3!)^2 + 3 − 1 = 38), and a u-word of length 22 is shown for a non-maximum compression setting. A u-cycle of length 16 is also exhibited for 3-dimensional 3-permutations, and the paper conjectures (Question 1) that u-cycles of correspondingly shortened lengths also exist. The paper further provides a lower bound of 2^((2^(d−1)/(n−1))·[...]) on the number of distinct shortened u-words constructible by the approach, though this is acknowledged to be far from tight. Strengths: - The generalization from d=2 to arbitrary d is non-trivial; the authors carefully identify and overcome the obstacles (twin permutation definitions, row-independent incomparability) that prevent naive extension of prior 2D techniques. - The proof is constructive: explicit Eulerian paths and their translations into u-words are given for small cases, making the existence result concrete and verifiable. - The paper honestly characterizes the limits of its own approach, noting that allowing incomparable elements at closer distances than n−1 could yield even shorter u-words (as demonstrated for n=4, d=2 where length 17 is achievable vs. the theorem's minimum of 21). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The result is purely existential; no efficient algorithm for constructing shortened u-words for general n and d is provided or analyzed for computational complexity. - The gap between the achievable lengths in Theorem 1.1 and the true minimum u-word length is uncharacterized and potentially large, as acknowledged for even the d=2 case — the approach does not capture all possible compression strategies. - The paper raises three open questions (existence of correspondingly short u-cycles, characterization of all achievable lengths, tight upper bounds on the number of u-words) but resolves none of them, leaving the combinatorial landscape substantially incomplete.
Search Results 2603.1005 PDF
#156 — Incremental (38)

Orla: A Library for Serving LLM -Based Multi-Agent Systems

Rana Shahout, Hayder Tirmazi, Minlan Yu, Michael Mitzenmacher · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Orla provides a clean architectural separation between workflow-level policy and request-level serving, filling a genuine gap. However, as a 4-page demo paper with narrow evaluation (two datasets, 5 problems for cache management), rudimentary routing (binary simple/complex classification with no accuracy analysis), and no comparison against the most relevant prior system (ThunderAgent), the contribution remains incremental and insufficiently validated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Serving LLM-based agentic systems requires handling heterogeneous multi-stage workflows that combine inference calls, tool invocations, and coordination across potentially different models and backends. Existing LLM serving engines (vLLM, SGLang) optimize at the level of individual requests — through continuous batching, prefill/decode scheduling, and KV cache management — but are blind to workflow structure. Orchestration frameworks like LangGraph and AutoGen compose stages at the application level but treat the serving layer as a black box, unable to make decisions about model routing, scheduling priority, or KV cache lifecycle across stage boundaries. The result is a gap between what agentic workloads need (workflow-level coordination) and what today's infrastructure provides (request-level optimization). Methodology: Orla is a lightweight serving library that sits between application-level orchestration frameworks and LLM inference backends. Developers define workflows as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) of stages; Orla handles execution coordination through three components. The stage mapper assigns each stage to a specific model and backend — either via explicit developer-specified mappings or dynamic routing through a pluggable interface. The provided `OneBitStageMapper` uses a lightweight LLM call to classify requests as "simple" or "complex" and routes accordingly. The workflow orchestrator executes the DAG, enforcing stage dependencies, dispatching ready stages concurrently, and propagating context between stages via developer-defined builder functions. Scheduling operates at two levels: a stage scheduler selects which stage queue to serve (FCFS or priority), and a request scheduler orders requests within that queue. The memory manager coordinates KV cache lifecycle at workflow granularity rather than request granularity, using signals from the orchestrator (stage transitions, backend switches, workflow completion) to decide when to preserve or evict cached state. Three built-in policies handle common cases: preserve on small context increments within the same backend, flush at workflow/backend boundaries, and flush under memory pressure. All backends are accessed through a uniform OpenAI-compatible HTTP interface, making backend substitution a single configuration change. Key Results: Evaluated on SWE-bench Lite (2,294 requests) for stage mapping and DAG-MATH (5 problems, 2,894 requests) for cache management, on a single machine with one NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPU (96 GB VRAM). For stage mapping, routing 956 of 2,294 requests to Qwen3-4B and the rest to Qwen3-8B reduces wall-clock time by 38% and mean completion time by 60% relative to a single-model vLLM baseline serving Qwen3-8B for all requests, while also reducing estimated inference cost by 35%. For cache management, preserving KV cache across stages within a workflow (flush-per-workflow) improves the full TTFT distribution compared to flushing after every inference call (flush-per-request), with gains most visible at lower percentiles of the CDF. Strengths: - Clean architectural separation of workflow-level policy from request-level execution, filling a genuine gap between orchestration frameworks and serving backends - Plug-and-play backend design via a uniform OpenAI-compatible interface enables the same workflow code to run on SGLang, vLLM, Ollama, or a simulated backend — practically useful for both production and research settings - Open-source release with a working demo that runs on a laptop CPU via Docker, lowering the barrier to experimentation Weaknesses / Limitations: - This is a short system/demo paper (4 pages of content); the evaluation is narrow — only two datasets, and the cache management experiment covers just 5 problems. Scalability under high concurrency, multi-GPU deployments, or diverse workflow topologies is not addressed. - The `OneBitStageMapper`'s binary simple/complex classification is rudimentary. There is no analysis of routing accuracy, misclassification cost, or how it performs relative to more principled complexity estimators (e.g., embedding-based or prompt-length-based routing). - No head-to-head comparison against the most relevant prior system, ThunderAgent, which also addresses KV cache thrashing in agentic workloads. The paper acknowledges the relationship but does not quantify relative gains.
Search Results 2603.13605 PDF
#157 — Incremental (38)

Multi-label Text Classification with Deep Learning Models

trepo.tuni.fi · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The thesis provides a thorough empirical investigation and the PDBR-CNN framework is a reasonable contribution, but the core finding — that label frequency distribution governs BR vs. LD performance — is more confirmatory than surprising. The exclusive focus on CNN backbones without fine-tuned transformer baselines limits the relevance of conclusions in the current era.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-label text classification (MLTC) assigns multiple, potentially co-occurring labels to documents and is central to applications such as medical diagnosis, legal tagging, and news categorization. The dominant paradigm — binary relevance (BR) — treats labels independently, ignoring inter-label dependencies, while algorithm-adaptation methods that model dependencies lack clear guidelines and are often compared against weak BR baselines. This creates a gap: deep-learning BR methods are underexplored, and the claimed superiority of label-dependency (LD) methods over optimized BR remains unverified. The thesis systematically investigates whether a properly optimized deep-learning BR architecture can close or reverse this gap. Methodology: The thesis progresses through four studies. First, a word-and-sentence-level CNN (ws-CNN) under a BR framework is evaluated against SVM and LR baselines on MIMIC-III clinical discharge summaries, establishing that deep BR outperforms traditional BR. Second, a controlled comparison pits a binary-relevance CNN (B-CNN) against algorithm-adaptation CNNs — including a novel Implicit-Threshold CNN (IT-CNN) and Adaptive-Threshold CNN (AT-CNN) — on 1,013 subclass combinations, revealing that B-CNN dominates at the cost of linear runtime scaling. Third, an optimized BR-CNN with class-imbalance weight scaling (W_n=1/N_n, W_p=α/N_p) is benchmarked against BERT-BL, LBA, LACO, and traditional BR methods on four datasets (AAPD, Reuters-21578, MIMIC-III, RCV1-v2). Fourth, the Partial Dependency Binary Relevance CNN (PDBR-CNN) is introduced: a four-stage framework consisting of (1) BR base classifiers, (2) F-score-based meta-class extraction to identify the most correlated auxiliary labels, (3) multi-label meta-level CNN classifiers trained on class subsets of incrementally higher order (2N to 5N), and (4) per-class model selection via validation F1. PDBR is model-agnostic and tested with CNN, GRU, and LSTM backbones across four word embeddings (GloVe, Word2Vec, fastText, BERT). Key Results: On MIMIC-III, BR-CNN outperforms LACO by +12.4% micro-F1 and +11.4% macro-F1 and surpasses all tested novel architectures. On AAPD, BR-CNN beats LACO by +0.1% micro-F1 and +5.1% macro-F1. On Reuters-21578 and RCV1-v2 — which feature extreme long-tailed distributions (26 classes with <2 samples) — BR-CNN trails the best LD methods by 1.8–17%, confirming that label frequency balance is the decisive moderator. PDBR-CNN (5N) achieves state-of-the-art results across all three benchmark datasets: micro-F1 of 0.775/0.924/0.762 on AAPD/Reuters-21578/MIMIC-III, outperforming LACO, BERT-BL, LSAN, traditional stacking, and classifier chains. Performance improves monotonically with correlation order N. PDBR generalizes across GRU and LSTM backbones, though CNN consistently ranks best. Ablation confirms that weight scaling contributes 0.9–20.2% macro-F1 gain depending on class imbalance severity, and pre-trained embeddings contribute 5–10%. Strengths: - Rigorous empirical framing: the same backbone (Kim CNN) is used for both BR and multi-label variants, ensuring fair comparisons rather than architecture-confounded contrasts typical in prior literature. - PDBR-CNN elegantly bridges BR and LD methods without external feature engineering or chain order sensitivity, using F-score-based meta-class selection to identify the most informative auxiliary labels per target class. - The finding that label frequency distribution (not the BR/LD distinction per se) governs which paradigm wins provides actionable, dataset-specific guidance missing from previous work. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The thesis focuses exclusively on flat multi-label text datasets with ≤103 classes; scalability to extreme multi-label settings (thousands of classes) is not addressed, and PDBR's O(N×L) classifier count grows quadratically with the number of correlation orders and classes. - Comparison with transformer-based BR baselines (e.g., BR-BERT) is absent; BERT embeddings are explored only as frozen feature inputs rather than fine-tuned end-to-end, likely underestimating BERT's potential within the BR framework and limiting the strength of conclusions about LD versus BR superiority in the modern LLM era.
Search Results
#158 — Incremental (38)

OFF-SETT: A Semantic Framework for Annotating Trends in Spatiotemporal Data

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: OFF-SETT is a competently engineered semantic framework that applies established W3C standards (RDF Data Cube, GeoSPARQL, OWL-Time) to spatiotemporal trend annotation. The three-layer trajectory model is clean and the BEAST-based breakpoint detection is appropriate, but the core contribution is an integration and application of existing ontological machinery to a new domain rather than a fundamentally new approach. The scale of validation (373 municipalities, two datasets) is modest.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Territorial monitoring requires analyzing large volumes of spatiotemporal (ST) data—satellite-derived indices, population counts, land use—that are increasingly available through open data initiatives. Despite availability, these datasets remain fragmented, domain-siloed, and technically demanding to interpret, restricting meaningful analysis to specialists. No ontology existed specifically for semantically describing trends and breakpoints in ST series. The gap is a principled, domain-agnostic methodology that transforms raw time series into human-interpretable, interoperable knowledge artifacts accessible to urban planners, policymakers, and citizens. Methodology: The paper introduces the SETT ontology (SEmantic Trajectory of Territory) and the OFF-SETT framework (Ontological Framework For SETT). SETT adopts a three-layer trajectory model: (1) raw trajectories represented as RDF Data Cube observations indexed along spatial, temporal, and thematic dimensions; (2) structured trajectories that segment raw series into episodes and transition points annotated with slope direction and steepness; and (3) thematic trajectories that enrich segments with domain-specific labels (e.g., "Warming," "Greening," "Urban Loss") using SKOS concept schemes. The framework reuses W3C standards—OWL-Time, GeoSPARQL, RDF Data Cube—to ensure interoperability with the Linked Open Data (LOD) cloud. The OFF-SETT pipeline comprises four steps: data preprocessing (outlier handling, spatial aggregation of Landsat pixels to municipality boundaries), CSV-to-RDF Data Cube conversion, breakpoint detection and semantic annotation using the BEAST algorithm (Bayesian Estimator of Abrupt Change, Seasonality, and Trend), and publication via a GraphDB SPARQL endpoint. BEAST decomposes each series into trend, seasonal, and residual components and estimates breakpoint locations probabilistically. Semantic intensity categories (Weak/Moderate/Strong trends) are assigned using data-driven Jenks natural breaks optimization. The system was validated on 373 municipalities in France and Switzerland over 1985–2022 using two heterogeneous datasets: population counts (INSEE/FSO) and five Landsat-derived spectral indices (NDVI, NDBI, NDWI, NDSI, LST). Key Results: The framework generated over 12.1 million RDF triples for raw data cubes and 17.6 million triples total when including semantic trajectory annotations. SPARQL queries against the published knowledge graph correctly retrieve multi-episode territorial trajectories; for the municipality of Meyrin, the system identifies two LST episodes ("Slight warming 1987–2002" and "Very slight warming 2003–2022") and two demographic episodes ("Moderate population growth" followed by "Strong population growth"), consistent with known urbanization history. Breakpoint frequency analysis of NDVI across 373 municipalities identifies 2003 as a major disruption year, consistent with the 2003 European heatwave, and the early 1990s (1988–1992) as a cross-indicator transition period. The Traces-Viz web application provides interactive map-based exploration of annotated trajectories via SPARQL queries, making results accessible to non-expert users without technical knowledge of RDF or SPARQL. Strengths: - The multi-layer ontology design cleanly separates raw data representation, structural segmentation, and thematic interpretation, enabling modular extension and algorithm substitution at any layer. - Reuse of established W3C standards (RDF Data Cube, OWL-Time, GeoSPARQL, SKOS) ensures genuine interoperability with the broader LOD cloud and facilitates linking territorial indicators to external resources such as DBpedia, Wikidata, and GeoNetwork. - The use of BEAST with its Bayesian posterior probabilities over breakpoint locations is methodologically appropriate: it avoids arbitrary threshold-based segmentation, handles both seasonal and non-seasonal series within a unified framework, and provides uncertainty estimates that are preserved as metadata. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The preprocessing step (spatial aggregation, outlier handling) remains semi-automated and analyst-dependent, limiting scalability and reproducibility without expert intervention; this is a significant barrier to fully automated deployment at continental scale. - BEAST is more sensitive to abrupt breakpoints than to gradual inflections, meaning that slowly evolving territories—typical of France and Switzerland—may have meaningful trend changes systematically missed, biasing the trajectory descriptions toward dramatic events. - The thematic vocabulary (domain-specific labels per indicator) is largely hand-crafted and context-dependent, requiring manual effort for each new indicator domain; there is no automated mechanism for vocabulary induction or cross-domain consistency checking, which constrains generalization claims.
Search Results
#159 — Incremental (35)

An epoch-weighted privacy budget allocation framework for fine-tuning large language models

scis.scichina.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: EW-FT combines two individually reasonable ideas — epoch-weighted privacy budget allocation and autoencoder-based dimensionality reduction — on top of the existing DP-forward framework. The epoch-weighting motivation is empirically grounded, but gains over DP-forward are modest (1-3%), experiments are restricted to BERT-base with only 3 epochs, and no comparison to recent DP fine-tuning methods (LoRA-based, SLDP-FT) is provided.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs on private data exposes training samples to membership inference, extraction, and attribute inference attacks. Existing differentially private fine-tuning methods (DP-SGD and its variants) suffer from three key limitations: (1) they operate in a centralized (CDP) paradigm requiring a trusted aggregator, making them unsuitable for distributed settings; (2) they apply uniform noise across all training epochs, ignoring the well-established observation that model sensitivity to perturbations increases as optimization progresses toward convergence — wasting privacy budget early and under-protecting late stages; and (3) high-dimensional embeddings require disproportionately large noise to satisfy DP guarantees (curse of dimensionality), degrading utility. The gap is a principled, epoch-aware, forward-pass LDP mechanism that simultaneously addresses budget allocation and dimensionality. Methodology: EW-FT (Epoch-Weighted Fine-Tuning) integrates three components. First, the framework applies LDP noise to hidden embeddings during the forward pass rather than gradient perturbation, enabling distributed privacy without a trusted third party (following DP-forward). The model is split into a user-controlled pre-noise component and a service-provider-controlled post-noise component; the user clips embeddings to fixed F-norm C (sensitivity = 2C) before adding Gaussian noise. Second, the Epoch-Weighted Privacy Budget Allocation (EWPBA) algorithm distributes the total privacy budget ε non-uniformly across epochs. Fine-tuning is divided into two stages: a rapid exploration stage (first half of epochs) with larger noise multipliers σ (lower precision needed), and a precision adjustment stage (second half) with smaller σ (finer gradient control needed). A binary-search procedure over a central noise axis σ_beg finds a valid noise multiplier list satisfying the overall (ε, δ)-DP constraint tracked via the PRV Accountant. The step distance S hyperparameter controls how fast noise ramps down. Third, a stacked autoencoder is inserted between the pre-noise and post-noise layers to compress hidden embeddings to a lower-dimensional space (default 768×64) before noise injection; decoders reconstruct the representation afterward. This reduces effective noise magnitude proportional to the dimensionality reduction, mitigating the curse of dimensionality. The three modules are independently tunable. Key Results: Experiments use BERT-base-uncased on SST-2 (67K train), QNLI (104K train), and IMDB (25K train) with baseline DP-forward at matched (ε, δ) budgets. At ε=8, EW-FT achieves 88.07% on SST-2 vs. 85.21% for DP-forward (+2.86%), 78.37% on IMDB vs. 76.01% (+2.36%), and 83.31% on QNLI vs. 81.91% (+1.40%). Average gains across all ε values are +2.8%, +2.2%, and +1.8% on SST-2, IMDB, and QNLI respectively. Ablation shows EWPBA alone and autoencoder alone each contribute positively, with deeper encoder layers benefiting more from EWPBA while shallower layers benefit more from the autoencoder. The 768×64 autoencoder outperforms smaller (768×4) and larger (768×256) variants, and step distance S=2 performs best. EW-FT consistently outperforms DP-forward across all tested encoder perturbation layers and all privacy budgets, while remaining significantly below the non-private baseline (e.g., 92.89% non-private vs. 88.42% EW-FT on SST-2 at ε=8). Strengths: - The two-stage epoch weighting is well-motivated by empirical evidence (Table 1 shows 8.58% accuracy swing from injecting identical noise at epoch 1 vs. epoch 2), grounding the design in observed model behavior rather than heuristic intuition. - The PRV Accountant integration for non-uniform σ lists is technically sound: EWPBA correctly computes exact (ε, δ) composition across heterogeneous per-epoch noise via a modified ComposePRV′ algorithm, not a loose union bound. - Forward-pass LDP injection is architecturally compatible with distributed/federated settings and avoids the memory and computational overhead of per-sample gradient clipping in DP-SGD. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to BERT-base fine-tuned with only E=3 epochs, a narrow regime; it is unclear whether the two-stage epoch split and EWPBA budget schedule generalize to larger models (GPT-scale), longer training runs, or generative tasks — the paper explicitly acknowledges this limitation. - The accuracy gap to non-private fine-tuning remains large (2–5% on most tasks at ε=8), and the gains over DP-forward are modest (1–3%), raising questions about practical significance given the added complexity of the stacked autoencoder and EWPBA tuning (two additional hyperparameters: dim and S). - The comparison baseline is restricted to DP-forward; no comparison to other recent forward-pass methods (e.g., SLDP-FT) or parameter-efficient DP methods (LoRA-based) is provided, making it difficult to assess where EW-FT stands in the broader landscape.
Citations
#160 — Incremental (35)

Empirical Economics Letters

fredonia.edu · genai manufacturing - new results
Why this rank: The end-to-end integration of avatar facilitation with agentic post-meeting workflow automation is a reasonable applied contribution, and the quantified engagement and retention gains are concrete. However, the study is a single-persona, single-company pilot with 28 participants over 8 weeks — essentially a proof-of-concept with no novelty control, making this an incremental extension of existing avatar and workflow automation technologies.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Hybrid and remote work environments create substantial challenges for business communication — specifically in sustaining engagement, retaining information, and ensuring workflow continuity across distributed teams. Traditional video conferencing degrades over time in engagement and produces meeting outcomes with diffuse accountability. This paper addresses the gap between passive AI tools (recordings, transcripts) and active AI-mediated communication by proposing and evaluating a system in which a lifelike digital human avatar autonomously facilitates structured business meetings. Methodology: The study uses a mixed-methods experimental design with 28 sales professionals over an 8-week period (45 sessions, 22 minutes each). The system integrates three components: (1) an AI-generated digital avatar built with HeyGen/Similar's Creator Plan to replicate the appearance and voice of a senior sales manager, (2) Zoom videoconferencing for real-time interaction, and (3) an N8N/Zapier-based agentic workflow automation layer for multi-step post-meeting processing including transcription, summary generation, email follow-ups, and calendar events. Quantitative measures included speaking time ratios, turn-taking frequency, response latency, and pre/post knowledge assessments. Qualitative data came from semi-structured interviews and expert evaluations of avatar-human conversation quality. Engagement was measured on a 1–10 scale across four dimensions: attention, participation, question response, and note-taking. Key Results: Avatar-led meetings showed a 23% increase in active listening behaviors (p < .01) and a 27.7% improvement in post-meeting information retention scores (avatar: M=8.3, SD=1.2 vs. traditional: M=6.5, SD=1.8; p < .001). Avatar-led sessions generated 31% more actionable items with clear ownership. Engagement variance was significantly lower in avatar-led meetings (SD=0.14 vs. SD=0.31 for traditional), indicating more sustained attention throughout. The agentic workflow achieved 92.4% transcription accuracy (Word Error Rate), 83.7% summary relevance as rated by experts, and 76.2% autonomous completion of follow-up tasks. User perception ratings were highest for efficiency (M=6.8) and helpfulness (M=6.1), but naturalness (M=3.9) and comfort (M=3.9) scored significantly lower, with 73% of participants noting the avatar maintained better focus on business objectives than human facilitators. Strengths: - Demonstrates concrete, quantified gains in information retention and engagement consistency using a real working system rather than a hypothetical framework - The combination of agentic post-meeting automation with avatar facilitation is novel and addresses end-to-end meeting lifecycle, not just the interaction phase - Mixed-methods design captures both behavioral metrics and subjective experience, revealing the tension between functional effectiveness and perceived naturalness Weaknesses / Limitations: - The experiment is extremely narrow in scope: one avatar persona, one organizational role (sales manager), one human counterpart (Shane), and one company context — severely limiting generalizability - The 76.2% autonomous workflow completion rate, while presented positively, means roughly 1 in 4 follow-up tasks still required human intervention, which undermines the automation value proposition for production use - No control for novelty effects; the 8-week duration is insufficient to determine whether engagement gains persist as participants habituate to the avatar format
Search Results
#161 — Incremental (35)

A Conceptual Framework for Building and Exploring Semantic Views of Enterprise Knowledge Graphs

journals-sol.sbc.org.br · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The four-layer design pattern systematizes known data integration challenges into a clean reusable architecture with formal specifications. But this is primarily a reorganization and formalization of established techniques (R2RML mappings, owl:sameAs linking, conflict resolution) with only a two-source case study and no quantitative evaluation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Enterprise Knowledge Graphs (EKGs) have become central to organizational data integration and analytics, yet the field lacks a formally defined, reusable architectural model for structuring the semantic views they produce. Existing work offers mapping languages, linking techniques, and integration workflows, but leaves the resulting data and metadata in ad hoc, heterogeneous representations that are hard to reproduce, maintain, or extend incrementally. Without a canonical, implementation-independent structure, organizations cannot systematically represent exported data, identity links, unified entities, and fused information. The paper targets this gap with the research question: how can a reusable architectural pattern organize semantic view data and metadata into well-defined layers that support both consistent integration and incremental construction? Methodology: The paper introduces DDP_SV, a Data Design Pattern for Semantic Views of EKGs. The core pattern decomposes the semantic view into two interconnected knowledge graphs. The Data Graph is organized into four hierarchical layers: (1) Exported Views, which map raw source data to a shared Semantic View Ontology (SVO) vocabulary via R2RML or RML rules; (2) Linkset Views, which establish owl:sameAs identity links between semantically equivalent entities across exported views using match functions; (3) Unification Views, which consolidate equivalent IRIs into canonical IRIs via normalization functions; and (4) Fusion Views, which resolve property-level conflicts among unified resources using declarative Property Fusion Assertions (PFAs) and conflict resolution functions such as KeepSingleValueByReputation(). The Metadata Graph mirrors this four-layer structure, capturing data lineage and view specifications. Each layer is given a formal tuple-based specification with associated state-derivation semantics. Built atop DDP_SV is a five-step incremental construction methodology following a pay-as-you-go strategy: integrate sources one at a time by (i) extending the SVO, (ii) constructing the exported view, (iii) building linkset views, (iv) designing unification views, and (v) implementing fusion views. Finally, a graphical tool, ContextEKG_Explorer, allows users to navigate the same entity across exported, unification, and fusion view contexts, surfacing data lineage and discrepancies interactively. The approach is validated with SV_Music, a music domain EKG integrating DBpedia and MusicBrainz. Key Results: The paper's contributions are primarily conceptual and architectural rather than empirical. The SV_Music case study demonstrates that the framework correctly handles ontology alignment (three class correspondences identified between DBpedia and MusicBrainz), construction of three linkset views (LV_Record, LV_Track, LV_MusicArtist) using attribute similarity, three unification views using canonical IRI concatenation, and three fusion views resolving conflicts in svm:labelName and dc:date via KeepSingleValueByReputation(). The semantic portal is publicly accessible with both SPARQL query and GUI access. No quantitative scalability experiments, precision/recall measurements for entity linking, or user studies evaluating ContextEKG_Explorer are reported. Strengths: - The four-layer architecture maps cleanly onto the four canonical challenges of semantic data integration (structural heterogeneity, semantic equivalence, identifier consolidation, conflict resolution), making the design principled rather than arbitrary. - The formal tuple-based specifications and state-derivation semantics for each view type provide implementation-independent precision, enabling reuse across toolchains (R2RML, RML, Silk, SPARQL). - The pay-as-you-go incremental methodology is structurally embedded in the pattern itself — each layer is modular and declarative, so new sources can be added without reprocessing prior sources, which is a meaningful improvement over process-level formulations of pay-as-you-go in prior work. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The validation is limited to a two-source, domain-specific case study with no quantitative evaluation of entity linking quality, conflict resolution accuracy, query performance, or scalability to realistic enterprise data lake sizes; empirical validation with real users is explicitly acknowledged as future work. - The framework places the burden of defining match functions, normalization functions, and conflict resolution strategies on the user without automated support — the degree of manual effort required for ontology alignment and linkset construction in realistic heterogeneous settings is not analyzed.
Search Results
#162 — Incremental (34)

Improving LLM Performance Through Black-Box Online Tuning: A Case for Adding System Specs to Factsheets for Trusted AI

Yonas Atinafu, Henry Lin, Robin Cohen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The SLO-first goodput objective is the right framing, and the finding that speculative decoding can hurt tail latency is practically useful. However, the evaluation is critically limited — a single small model on one GPU — and the headline result reduces to simply disabling speculative decoding, a conclusion reachable without any automated controller. The split focus between a systems contribution and a policy argument (Factsheets) leaves both underdeveloped.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interactive LLM services are dominated by tail latency: default serving configurations either underutilize GPUs or push the system past a queueing inflection point where p99 latency spikes and a minority of users suffer extreme delays. Three serving-stack knobs — client concurrency, batch size (max_num_seqs), and speculative decoding parameters — interact in workload- and hardware-dependent ways that are not captured by average-throughput metrics. Existing auto-tuning systems (OtterTune, SCOOT) either ignore speculative decoding as a tunable knob, require internal instrumentation, or rely on expensive Bayesian optimization. The paper also observes that AI Factsheets for Trusted AI currently omit system performance and sustainability metrics, leaving adopters uninformed about deployment-time reliability. Methodology: The paper presents SLO-Tuner, a black-box online controller that maximizes goodput — the rate of requests meeting an explicit p99 SLO — using only end-to-end latency measurements with no internal instrumentation. The controller hill-climbs over a knob vector K = {concurrency, max_num_seqs, speculative width, spec on/off}, evaluating the current configuration and a fixed neighborhood of one-hop neighbors per iteration and moving only if the score S(K) = goodput(K) − λ·max(0, p99(K) − SLO) − hw_cost(K) improves by at least δ=0.02 or if the current p99 violates the SLO. The search runs for a fixed budget (8 iterations on live vLLM), requiring no random restarts. A lightweight discrete-event simulator models FCFS batching, prefill/decode phases, and speculative draft/verifier dynamics; it is used for cheap pre-deployment stress-testing and trend guidance, while live measurements provide correctness. A thin adapter maps the logical knob vector to stack-specific CLI flags, enabling portability across serving stacks (vLLM, MLX) without code changes to the controller logic. Key Results: On TinyLlama-1.1B served via vLLM with a 1.2 s p99 SLO, SLO-Tuner reduces p99 from ≈1.36 s to ≈0.70 s and increases goodput from ≈8.1 rps to ≈15.0 rps — roughly halving tail latency and nearly doubling SLO-satisfying throughput. The critical finding is that speculative decoding is monotonically harmful for this SLO: disabling speculation (width 0) achieves 13.87 rps at p99 0.77 s, while width 16 degrades to 4.53 rps with p99 2.00 s. Concurrency shows a sharp knee: goodput peaks around 8–10 concurrent threads and collapses beyond 10 despite raw throughput continuing to rise. Batch size benefits up to ≈11–13 sequences before p99 crosses the SLO boundary. The simulator reproduces qualitative trends (same knob ordering) but differs in absolute latency by 0.5–0.6 s versus live vLLM. A portability check on Apple Silicon (MLX, Qwen-4B/0.6B) confirms the simulator's directional guidance transfers across hardware. Tuning wall time is bounded at ≈40–45 minutes on an L40S GPU. Strengths: - The SLO-first goodput objective is the right target for tail-latency-constrained services, and the empirical finding that wide speculative decoding can reduce goodput while hurting p99 is a practically important, non-obvious result that contradicts the default assumption that speculation is always beneficial. - The black-box design — interacting only through OpenAI-compatible APIs and public CLI flags — is genuinely portable: it requires zero instrumentation changes to the serving stack and can run alongside existing autoscalers rather than replacing them. - The simulator/live division of labor (simulator for cheap trend guidance, live measurements for correctness) is pragmatically sound and reduces the number of expensive live segments needed. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation scope is severely limited: only one small model (TinyLlama-1.1B) is tested on live vLLM, on a single GPU, with a single synthetic prompt and closed-loop steady arrivals. The reported headline result (1.36 s → 0.70 s) is essentially just disabling speculative decoding — a conclusion reachable by manual inspection without any controller. It is unclear whether the hill-climb adds value beyond this trivial fix. - The paper reports a single tuning trajectory with no variance analysis, no comparison against random search or Bayesian optimization under the same segment budget, and no multi-seed runs, making it impossible to assess robustness or whether the results would replicate. - The paper is split between a systems contribution (SLO-Tuner) and a policy argument (Factsheets should include performance metrics), and neither half receives sufficient depth. The Factsheet discussion is speculative and qualitative, with no concrete proposal for what metrics, formats, or thresholds should be standardized.
